$$ D[2,2] = \min(D[1,2]+1,\ D[2,1]+1,\ D[1,1]+2) = \min(3,\ 2,\ 2) = 2 $$
$$ D[2,3] = \min(D[1,3]+1,\ D[2,2]+1,\ D[1,2]+2) = \min(3,\ 3,\ 4) = 3 $$
$$ D[2,4] = \min(D[1,4]+1,\ D[2,3]+1,\ D[1,3]+0) = \min(4,\ 4,\ 2) = 2 $$
(U = U, sub_cost = 0)
$$ D[2,5] = \min(D[1,5]+1,\ D[2,4]+1,\ D[1,4]+2) = \min(5,\ 3,\ 5) = 3 $$
$$ D[2,6] = \min(D[1,6]+1,\ D[2,5]+1,\ D[1,5]+2) = \min(6,\ 4,\ 6) = 4 $$
$$ D[2,7] = \min(D[1,7]+1,\ D[2,6]+1,\ D[1,6]+2) = \min(7,\ 5,\ 7) = 5 $$
$$ D[2,8] = \min(D[1,8]+1,\ D[2,7]+1,\ D[1,7]+2) = \min(8,\ 6,\ 8) = 6 $$

Continuing this process systematically through the remaining rows, we obtain:

**Final Filled DP Matrix:**

| | # | S | A | T | U | R | D | A | Y |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **#** | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
| **S** | 1 | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
| **U** | 2 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
| **N** | 3 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
| **D** | 4 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 4 | 5 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
| **A** | 5 | 4 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 5 |
| **Y** | 6 | 5 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 |

**Final Answer:**

$$ D[6,8] = 4 $$

**Conclusion:** The minimum edit distance between **"SUNDAY"** and **"SATURDAY"** is **4**. The corresponding backtrace operations are typically: insert `A`, insert `T`, substitute `N` → `R` (cost 2), insert `A`.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the initialization:** The first row and first column must be filled with $0, 1, 2, 3, \dots$ before computing interior cells. Examiners deduct **2 marks** if omitted.
> 2. **Wrong substitution cost:** When the substitution cost is **2** (not 1), students often mistakenly use 1. Re-read the question carefully.
> 3. **Skipping the diagonal transition:** The recurrence has THREE branches. Writing only two loses **1 mark**.
> 4. **No unit or boundary condition stated:** Always state base conditions explicitly: $D[i,0] = i$, $D[0,j] = j$.
> 5. **In n-gram problems:** Forgetting to include `<s>` (start) and `</s>` (end) tokens loses **1 mark**.
> 6. **Confusing stemming with lemmatization:** Stemming produces stems (often non-words); lemmatization produces lemmas (dictionary forms). Examiners test this distinction.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ **Pipeline order:** Raw Text → Sentence Segmentation → Word Tokenization → Normalization → Stop Word Removal → Stemming/Lemmatization → Clean Tokens → Vectorization.
- ✅ **Tokenization** splits text into words/sentences; the core unit of NLP.
- ✅ **Normalization** = case folding + accent removal + Unicode standardization.
- ✅ **Stemming** = crude rule-based suffix chopping (Porter algorithm, fast, lossy).
- ✅ **Lemmatization** = dictionary-aware morphological reduction to lemma (WordNet, slow, accurate).
- ✅ **Stop Words** = high-frequency low-information tokens; removed in classical IR but kept in transformers.
- ✅ **Edit Distance Formula:** $D[i,j] = \min(D[i-1,j]+1,\ D[i,j-1]+1,\ D[i-1,j-1]+\text{sub\_cost})$.
- ✅ **Substitution cost** = $0$ if characters match, else $1$ (Levenshtein) or $2$ (weighted variant).
- ✅ **Bigram Probability:** $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = C(w_{i-1}, w_i) / C(w_{i-1})$.
- ✅ **Trigram Probability:** $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}, w_{i-2}) = C(w_{i-2}, w_{i-1}, w_i) / C(w_{i-2}, w_{i-1})$.
- ✅ **Applications:** Spell checkers (edit distance), search engines (tokenization + stemming), speech recognition (n-gram LMs), bioinformatics (DNA alignment).
- ✅ **Always include `<s>` and `</s>`** when computing n-gram probabilities over sentences.
- ✅ **KTU Mark Distribution:** Module 1 typically carries **15–20 marks** in the End Semester Exam, with edit distance being the most frequently asked 14-mark problem.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Empirical Laws
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT741 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Empirical Laws

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Empirical Laws in NLP?

In Natural Language Processing, **Empirical Laws** refer to the *mathematically verifiable statistical regularities* observed when natural language text is analyzed at scale. They are called "empirical" because they were derived by *counting* occurrences in real corpora, rather than by deductive linguistic reasoning. They form the **mathematical foundation of corpus linguistics** and underpin almost every modern NLP system, from search engines to language models.

The three foundational empirical laws covered in KTU Module 1 are:

| Law | Author / Year | What it Describes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Zipf's Law** | George Kingsley Zipf (1949) | Relationship between a word's *frequency* and its *rank* |
| **Heap's Law** | Harold Stanley Heaps (1978) | Relationship between *vocabulary size* and *corpus size* |
| **Mandelbrot's Law** | Benoit Mandelbrot (1954) | A *generalization* of Zipf's law with better low-rank fit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** These three laws are *assessable* topics. You are expected to state, derive, plot, and apply them. Memorize the equations, the typical parameter ranges, and the linearization tricks (log-log plots).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you walk into a popular city café:

* **A handful of items** (latte, cappuccino) account for **most of the orders**.
* **Many items** (single-origin pour-overs, seasonal specials) are ordered *rarely*.
* The menu **keeps growing** as the chef experiments, but *very slowly* — most new words/items you hear are repeats.

This is precisely how language behaves in any text corpus:

* A **few words** ("the", "of", "is", "and") dominate the frequency table.
* **Most words** appear only **once or twice** (these are called *hapax legomena*).
* Vocabulary **grows sub-linearly** as you read more text — you keep *re-using* familiar words.

Empirical laws mathematically *quantify* this everyday observation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (Board-Exam Ready):**
> An empirical law in NLP is a *power-law type* statistical relationship — derived from corpus analysis — that captures a structural property of natural language, such as the rank-frequency distribution of words (Zipf), the growth of vocabulary with corpus size (Heap), or a refined rank-frequency model (Mandelbrot).

## 1.3 Why Do These Laws Exist? (The "Why")

* **Zipf's Law** is often justified by the *Principle of Least Effort* — speakers balance their effort (re-using short, common words) against the listener's need for *unambiguity* (using rarer, more specific words).
* **Heap's Law** reflects *diminishing returns*: as corpus grows, the probability of encountering a *truly new* word drops steadily.
* **Mandelbrot's Law** adds a *rank-shift* parameter to better model the long tail and high-frequency "knee" of the distribution.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Zipf's Law — The Rank–Frequency Law

### 2.1.1 Statement

If we list every unique word (type) in a corpus in *descending order of frequency*, the **frequency of the word at rank $r$ is inversely proportional to its rank**.

### 2.1.2 Mathematical Form

The general form of Zipf's law is a **power law**:

$$f(r) = c \cdot r^{-\alpha}$$

Where:
* $f(r)$ = frequency of the word at rank $r$
* $c$ = a positive constant (≈ frequency of the most common word)
* $\alpha$ = exponent, typically $\approx 1$ for English (range $0.9 \le \alpha \le 1.1$)
* $r$ = rank (integer: 1, 2, 3, ...)

> [!NOTE]
> **Idealized Zipf**: When $\alpha = 1$, the second most frequent word occurs **half** as often as the first, the third occurs **one-third** as often, and so on. This is the famous *harmonic* drop-off.

### 2.1.3 Linearization Trick (Log-Log Plot)

Taking the logarithm of both sides:

$$\log f(r) = \log c - \alpha \cdot \log r$$

This is the equation of a **straight line** with:
* **Slope** = $-\alpha$
* **Intercept** = $\log c$

Plotting $\log f(r)$ vs $\log r$ yields a straight line — this is the standard *empirical verification* of Zipf's law.

### 2.1.4 The Standard $c = 0.1 \cdot N$ Heuristic

For a corpus of $N$ total tokens, the most frequent word typically appears about $10\%$ of the time, so:

$$c \approx 0.1 \cdot N$$

So $f(1) \approx 0.1N$, $f(2) \approx 0.05N$, $f(3) \approx 0.033N$, etc.

## 2.2 Heap's Law — The Vocabulary Growth Law

### 2.2.1 Statement

The **vocabulary size (number of unique types)** grows as a power-law function of the **corpus size (number of tokens)**.

### 2.2.2 Mathematical Form

$$V(N) = K \cdot N^{\beta}$$

Where:
* $V$ = vocabulary size (number of unique words)
* $N$ = corpus size (number of total tokens)
* $K$ = a positive constant (typically between **10 and 100**)
* $\beta$ = exponent, typically between **0.4 and 0.7** (often $\approx 0.5$ for English)

### 2.2.3 Linearization Trick

Taking the logarithm:

$$\log V = \log K + \beta \cdot \log N$$

A plot of $\log V$ vs $\log N$ is a **straight line** with slope $\beta$ and intercept $\log K$.

### 2.2.4 Sub-linearity

Because $0 < \beta < 1$, vocabulary grows **faster than logarithmically but slower than linearly**. This is the *diminishing returns* phenomenon.

## 2.3 Mandelbrot's Law — The Refined Rank–Frequency Law

### 2.3.1 Statement

Mandelbrot observed that Zipf's pure power law **fails for very high-frequency words** (the "head") and **very rare words** (the "tail"). He introduced a more flexible form:

$$f(r) = \frac{C}{(r + \rho)^{\alpha}}$$

Where:
* $C > 0$ = scaling constant
* $\rho \ge 0$ = rank-shift parameter (handles the high-frequency "knee")
* $\alpha$ = exponent (typically between $1.0$ and $1.5$)

### 2.3.2 Relationship to Zipf

If we set $\rho = 0$ and $\alpha = 1$, Mandelbrot's law **reduces exactly to Zipf's law**. So Mandelbrot's is a **strict generalization**.

---

## 2.4 📋 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready Table)

| Law | Equation | Parameters & Typical Values | Linearized Form | Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Zipf** | $f(r) = c \cdot r^{-\alpha}$ | $c > 0$, $\alpha \approx 1$ | $\log f = \log c - \alpha \log r$ | Predict word frequency from rank |
| **Heap** | $V(N) = K \cdot N^{\beta}$ | $K \in [10, 100]$, $\beta \in [0.4, 0.7]$ | $\log V = \log K + \beta \log N$ | Estimate vocabulary / corpus size |
| **Mandelbrot** | $f(r) = C / (r + \rho)^{\alpha}$ | $C>0$, $\rho \ge 0$, $\alpha \in [1.0, 1.5]$ | $\log f = \log C - \alpha \log(r+\rho)$ | Better fit than pure Zipf |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units & Conventions**: All logarithms above are base-10 by convention in KTU textbooks (matching the original Zipf plots). Use $\log_{10}$ unless specifically told otherwise. Frequencies $f(r)$ and vocabulary $V$ are always *positive integers* in practice — the law gives *expected* (real-valued) values.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Law | NLP / Engineering Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| Zipf | (i) Design of **Huffman coding** for text compression — most weight on rare words. (ii) Stop-word filtering (top ~100 words cover ~50% of text). (iii) **Search engine ranking** (TF-IDF is partly inspired by inverse-rank thinking). (iv) Language model smoothing (e.g., *Good-Turing*). |
| Heap | (i) **Capacity planning** for search indexers. (ii) Estimating the size of dictionaries for spell-checkers / MT systems. (iii) Deciding when a corpus is "large enough" for a task. |
| Mandelbrot | (i) **Information-theoretic models** of language. (ii) Smoothing in $n$-gram language models. (iii) Designing IR indexes with better trade-offs. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivations below are written out **exhaustively** with no skipped algebraic steps, in the style expected by KTU board examiners.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of Zipf's Law (Log-Linearization)

### Step 1: Start from the empirical observation

Empirically, for many languages and corpora, plotting frequency $f(r)$ vs rank $r$ on a log-log scale produces an approximately straight line. This straight-line behavior is the signature of a **power law**.

### Step 2: Hypothesize a power-law form

The simplest family of functions whose log-log plot is linear is:

$$f(r) = c \cdot r^{k}$$

We will determine the exponent $k$ from empirical data.

### Step 3: Take the logarithm of both sides

Applying $\log_{10}$ to both sides:

$$\log f(r) = \log(c \cdot r^{k}) = \log c + k \log r$$

### Step 4: Match to the standard line equation $y = mx + b$

Let:
* $y = \log f(r)$
* $x = \log r$
* $m = k$ (slope)
* $b = \log c$ (intercept)

So:

$$y = m \cdot x + b$$

### Step 5: Identify the empirical slope

For English, empirical data (e.g., Brown corpus) gives a slope of approximately $-1$ on the log-log plot. Therefore:

$$m = k = -1$$

### Step 6: Rewrite in canonical form

Substituting $k = -1$:

$$f(r) = c \cdot r^{-1} = \frac{c}{r}$$

This is the **idealized Zipf's law**. The general form keeps $\alpha$ as a free parameter:

$$f(r) = c \cdot r^{-\alpha}$$

with $\alpha \approx 1$ for natural English text. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Heap's Law (Log-Linearization)

### Step 1: Start from the empirical observation

Plotting vocabulary size $V$ against corpus size $N$ on a log-log scale also yields a straight line — meaning the relationship is again a **power law**.

### Step 2: Hypothesize a power-law form

$$V(N) = K \cdot N^{\gamma}$$

### Step 3: Take the logarithm

$$\log V = \log(K \cdot N^{\gamma}) = \log K + \gamma \log N$$

### Step 4: Identify slope and intercept

* Slope $= \gamma$
* Intercept $= \log K$

### Step 5: Empirical values from the literature

For English text (e.g., the Brown corpus, the Google Web Trillion Word Corpus), regression gives:
* $\gamma = \beta \in [0.4, 0.7]$
* $K \in [10, 100]$

So:

$$V(N) = K \cdot N^{\beta} \quad \text{with} \quad 0.4 \le \beta \le 0.7 \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: How Mandelbrot's Law Generalizes Zipf's Law

### Step 1: Start with Zipf's idealization

$$f(r) = \frac{c}{r^{\alpha}}$$

### Step 2: Recognize the failure mode

Empirically, the pure Zipf form **under-predicts the most frequent words** and **over-predicts very rare words**. Mandelbrot proposed a *shifted* denominator to absorb this bias.

### Step 3: Introduce the rank-shift $\rho$

Add a non-negative offset $\rho$ to the rank:

$$f(r) = \frac{C}{(r + \rho)^{\alpha}}$$

### Step 4: Show that Zipf is a special case

Set $\rho = 0$ and $\alpha = 1$:

$$f(r) = \frac{C}{(r+0)^{1}} = \frac{C}{r}$$

which is exactly Zipf's law with $c = C$. So Mandelbrot's law **contains Zipf's law as a special case**. $\blacksquare$

### Step 5: Linearization for empirical fitting

Take the log:

$$\log f = \log C - \alpha \log(r + \rho)$$

The slope of $\log f$ vs $\log(r+\rho)$ is $-\alpha$ and intercept is $\log C$.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Examples (Board-Exam Style)

### Example 1 — Applying Zipf's Law

**Problem:** A corpus has $N = 1{,}000{,}000$ tokens. The most frequent word (rank 1) has frequency $70{,}000$. Using Zipf's law with $\alpha = 1$, predict the frequencies of words at ranks 2, 10, 50, and 100.

**Solution:**

The constant $c = f(1) = 70{,}000$. So $f(r) = 70{,}000 / r$.

| Rank $r$ | Calculation | Predicted Frequency |
| :---: | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | $70{,}000/1$ | $70{,}000$ |
| 2 | $70{,}000/2$ | $35{,}000$ |
| 10 | $70{,}000/10$ | $7{,}000$ |
| 50 | $70{,}000/50$ | $1{,}400$ |
| 100 | $70{,}000/100$ | $700$ |

**Insight:** Notice that the *top 2 words* alone account for $105{,}000$ tokens, i.e., $\approx 10.5\%$ of the entire million-token corpus.

---

### Example 2 — Applying Heap's Law

**Problem:** For a certain English corpus, Heap's law parameters are $K = 50$ and $\beta = 0.5$. Compute vocabulary size for $N = 1{,}000{,}000$ and $N = 4{,}000{,}000$. What is the percentage increase in $V$ when the corpus is doubled twice (from 1M to 4M)?

**Solution:**

Using $V = 50 \cdot N^{0.5}$:

$$V(1{,}000{,}000) = 50 \cdot (1{,}000{,}000)^{0.5} = 50 \cdot 1000 = 50{,}000$$

$$V(4{,}000{,}000) = 50 \cdot (4{,}000{,}000)^{0.5} = 50 \cdot 2000 = 100{,}000$$

Percentage increase:

$$\Delta V\,(\%) = \frac{100{,}000 - 50{,}000}{50{,}000} \times 100 = 100\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> When $\beta = 0.5$, quadrupling the corpus *doubles* the vocabulary. In general, multiplying $N$ by a factor of $2^{1/\beta}$ multiplies $V$ by a factor of 2.

---

### Example 3 — Verifying Zipf Log-Linearity

**Problem:** A mini-corpus yields the following rank-frequency pairs: $(1, 1000), (10, 100), (100, 10)$. Verify that Zipf's law holds and find $c$ and $\alpha$.

**Solution:**

Take $\log_{10}$:

| $r$ | $f(r)$ | $\log r$ | $\log f$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 1000 | 0 | 3.0 |
| 10 | 100 | 1 | 2.0 |
| 100 | 10 | 2 | 1.0 |

The points lie exactly on the line $\log f = 3 - \log r$, i.e., $\log f = 3 - 1 \cdot \log r$.

So $c = 10^{3} = 1000$ and $\alpha = 1$. $\checkmark$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Zipf's Law | Heap's Law | Mandelbrot's Law |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Variables related | Frequency ↔ Rank | Vocabulary ↔ Corpus size | Frequency ↔ Shifted Rank |
| Type of growth | $1/r$ (hyperbolic) | $N^{\beta}$ (sub-linear) | $1/(r+\rho)^{\alpha}$ (refined) |
| Parameters | $c, \alpha$ | $K, \beta$ | $C, \rho, \alpha$ |
| Number of parameters | 2 | 2 | 3 |
| Empirical fit quality | Good | Good | **Excellent** (best) |
| Log-log linearization | Yes | Yes | Yes (on $r+\rho$) |
| Reduces to Zipf? | — | — | Yes (when $\rho=0, \alpha=1$) |
| Primary use | Word frequency prediction | Vocabulary estimation | Language modelling, IR |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams below are **architectural / functional flow schematics** designed to convey the *logic* of empirical-law application in NLP pipelines, since the underlying math is best understood as a process flow rather than a physical object.

## 4.1 Master Workflow: From Raw Corpus to Empirical Law Verification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    raw["Raw Text Corpus"] --> tok["Step 1: Tokenization split into words"]
    tok --> count["Step 2: Count word frequencies"]
    count --> sort["Step 3: Sort by frequency descending"]
    sort --> rank["Step 4: Assign integer rank 1, 2, 3 ..."]
    
    rank --> zbranch{"Step 5: Which law?"}
    zbranch -->|"Frequency vs Rank"| zipf["Apply Zipf: f = c * r power -alpha"]
    zbranch -->|"Vocab vs Tokens"| heap["Apply Heap: V = K * N power beta"]
    zbranch -->|"Refined fit"| mandel["Apply Mandelbrot: f = C over r+rho power alpha"]
    
    zipf --> loglogZ["Log-log plot slope = -alpha"]
    heap --> loglogH["Log-log plot slope = beta"]
    mandel --> loglogM["Log-log plot on r+rho slope = -alpha"]
    
    loglogZ --> verify["Step 6: Verify straight line fit"]
    loglogH --> verify
    loglogM --> verify
    
    verify --> apps["Step 7: Apply to NLP tasks IR, LM, Compression, Indexing"]
```
