**Advantages:** Correlates with human judgment at the corpus level; cheap and reproducible.
**Limitations:** Does not handle synonyms or paraphrases; insensitive to word order beyond n-grams.

*(7 marks: 2 for formula, 2 for BP explanation, 2 for working, 1 for limitations)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**Given:** Sentence of 5 tokens with the following trigram probabilities.

**Step 1 — Compute the joint probability.**

$$P(\text{sentence}) = 0.8 \times 0.5 \times 0.3 \times 0.4 \times 0.2$$

$$P(\text{sentence}) = 0.8 \times 0.5 = 0.40$$

$$0.40 \times 0.3 = 0.12$$

$$0.12 \times 0.4 = 0.048$$

$$0.048 \times 0.2 = 0.0096$$

**Step 2 — Apply the perplexity formula.**

$$\text{PPL} = P(\text{sentence})^{-1/N} = (0.0096)^{-1/5}$$

Take the logarithm:

$$\ln(\text{PPL}) = -\frac{1}{5} \ln(0.0096) = -\frac{1}{5} \times (-4.646) = 0.9292$$

Exponentiate:

$$\text{PPL} = e^{0.9292} \approx 2.532$$

**Step 3 — Convert to base-2 perplexity (bits-based).**

$$H = \log_2(\text{PPL}) = \log_2(2.532) \approx 1.340 \text{ bits/word}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.**

The model is as confused as if it had to choose uniformly between approximately **2.5 words** at each step — indicating a reasonably good fit on this short test sentence.

*(7 marks: 2 for joint prob, 2 for log-derivation, 2 for numerical answer, 1 for interpretation)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain **ROUGE** metrics used for evaluating automatic text summarization systems. Compare **ROUGE-N** and **ROUGE-L** with suitable formulas. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two language models, M1 and M2, are evaluated on a test corpus of 100 tokens. M1 yields a cross-entropy of **2.5 bits/word**, and M2 yields **3.0 bits/word**. Which model is better, and why? Compute the perplexity for each. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**ROUGE — Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation (Lin, 2004)**

ROUGE is a family of metrics primarily measuring *recall*, designed for evaluating automatic summarization where capturing all important content of the reference is crucial.

**1. ROUGE-N (N-gram Recall):**

$$\text{ROUGE-N} = \frac{\sum_{S \in \text{Ref}} \sum_{\text{ngram} \in S} \text{Count}_{\text{match}}(\text{ngram})}{\sum_{S \in \text{Ref}} \sum_{\text{ngram} \in S} \text{Count}(\text{ngram})}$$

- Numerator = number of n-grams co-occurring in candidate and reference.
- Denominator = total n-grams in the reference.
- Example: For ROUGE-1 (unigram), it measures what fraction of reference unigrams appear in the candidate summary.

**2. ROUGE-L (Longest Common Subsequence):**

ROUGE-L uses the length of the Longest Common Subsequence (LCS) between candidate $C$ and reference $R$:

$$P_{LCS} = \frac{\text{LCS}(C, R)}{m}, \quad R_{LCS} = \frac{\text{LCS}(C, R)}{n}$$

where $m$ = candidate length, $n$ = reference length. The harmonic F-measure is:

$$F_{LCS} = \frac{(1 + \beta^2) R_{LCS} P_{LCS}}{R_{LCS} + \beta^2 P_{LCS}}$$

Typically $\beta$ is large (e.g., $\beta = 8$) to emphasize recall.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | ROUGE-N | ROUGE-L |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis** | Fixed n-gram matching | Longest Common Subsequence |
| **Order** | Strict (consecutive) | Flexible (non-consecutive) |
| **Captures** | Local phrase overlap | Sentence-level structure |
| **Use Case** | Headline generation, short summaries | Long document summarization |

*(7 marks: 2 for ROUGE-N, 2 for ROUGE-L, 2 for comparison, 1 for usage)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**Step 1 — Recall the relationship.**

$$\text{PPL} = 2^{H}$$

where $H$ is the cross-entropy in bits/word.

**Step 2 — Compute perplexity for M1.**

$$\text{PPL}_{M1} = 2^{2.5} = 2^{2} \times 2^{0.5} = 4 \times 1.4142 \approx 5.657$$

**Step 3 — Compute perplexity for M2.**

$$\text{PPL}_{M2} = 2^{3.0} = 8.000$$

**Step 4 — Compare.**

| Model | Cross-Entropy (bits/word) | Perplexity |
|---|---|---|
| M1 | 2.5 | **5.66** |
| M2 | 3.0 | **8.00** |

**Conclusion:** **M1 is the better model** because it has a *lower* cross-entropy and consequently *lower* perplexity. M1 is more confident in its predictions and assigns higher probability to the actual test tokens.

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

M1 is as perplexed as choosing uniformly among ~5.66 words, while M2 is as perplexed as choosing among 8 words. Lower PPL = better language model.

*(7 marks: 2 for formulas, 2 for numerical PPL, 2 for comparison, 1 for interpretation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Perplexity formula confusion:** Many students write PPL = $2^{-H}$ instead of $2^{H}$. The *negative* sign is already inside $H$ (since $H = -\frac{1}{N}\sum \log P$), so PPL = $2^H$, **not** $2^{-H}$.
> 2. **BLEU = 0 trap:** If *any* $p_n = 0$ (for $n \ge 1$), the BLEU log collapses to $-\infty$, yielding BLEU = 0. Always state this clearly.
> 3. **Brevity Penalty misapplication:** Some students apply BP even when $c > r$. The correct rule is $BP = 1$ whenever $c > r$.
> 4. **ROUGE vs. BLEU confusion:** BLEU emphasizes **precision** (candidate matches), while ROUGE emphasizes **recall** (reference matches). Many students mix this up.
> 5. **Forgetting base of log:** PPL with $\log_2$ gives one value; with $\ln$ it gives a different numerical value. Always specify the base.
> 6. **Units mismatch in cross-entropy:** $H$ in *bits* → PPL base 2. $H$ in *nats* → PPL base $e$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Evaluating Language Models**

- **Evaluation is of two types:** *Intrinsic* (Perplexity, Cross-Entropy) and *Extrinsic* (BLEU, ROUGE, WER, F1).
- **Cross-Entropy** $H = -\frac{1}{N} \sum \log_2 P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$ — average bits/word.
- **Perplexity** $\text{PPL} = 2^H$ — equivalent to the effective vocabulary size the model is "confused among."
- **Lower PPL = better LM.** It is the *de facto* intrinsic metric for n-gram and neural LMs.
- **BLEU** is the standard **Machine Translation** metric, with range [0, 1] (or 0–100 as percentage).
- **BLEU formula:** $BP \cdot \exp(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n)$ — brevity penalty prevents short-output gaming.
- **BLEU = 0** if any $p_n = 0$ for $1 \le n \le N$ — smoothing required in practice.
- **ROUGE** is for **Summarization**; emphasizes **recall**.
- **ROUGE-N** uses n-gram recall; **ROUGE-L** uses LCS-based F-measure.
- **WER** (Word Error Rate) is for **Speech Recognition**; based on edit distance.
- **F1-Score** = $\frac{2PR}{P + R}$ — harmonic mean of precision and recall.
- **Human Evaluation** remains the *gold standard* for fluency, adequacy, and coherence.
- **Choosing a metric:** Always match the metric to the *task* — BLEU for MT, ROUGE for Summarization, WER for ASR, F1 for QA/Classification, PPL for generic LMs.
- **Standard test corpora:** Penn Treebank (PTB), WikiText-103, GLUE, SuperGLUE, WMT (for MT).
- **KTU 2024 Board expects:** Formula derivations, numerical examples, and explicit discussion of *why* one metric is preferred over another for a given task.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smoothing: Kneser-Ney Smoothing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT741 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Smoothing: Kneser-Ney Smoothing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Smoothing: Kneser-Ney Smoothing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kneser-Ney Smoothing (KN)** is a state-of-the-art, **interpolated n-gram language model smoothing technique** that generalizes absolute discounting by redistributing the probability mass of seen n-grams to unseen n-grams through a **continuation probability** derived from lower-order distributions. It is widely regarded as the *de facto* standard for statistical language modeling and consistently outperforms Laplace, Add-k, and Good-Turing smoothing on perplexity benchmarks.

The defining innovation of Kneser-Ney is that it does **not** simply back off to the lower-order model by treating lower-order counts as if they came from the same distribution. Instead, it explicitly models **how broadly a word is used across different contexts** — known as the *continuation count*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Movie Critic:**
>
> Imagine a movie critic named *San* who has reviewed **500 films**, and **480 of them are about San Francisco**. Now, suppose you are computing the probability that a new film recommended by San will be about San Francisco. The naive unigram backoff (which is what a standard n-gram model does) would say: *"Well, 'Francisco' is super common in San's reviews, so let's just use its raw unigram count."*
>
> But that is misleading! The word *Francisco* is common *only because* it almost always follows *San*. It is a **poorly distributed word** — it lives in just one context. Kneser-Ney recognizes this: even though *Francisco* has a high unigram frequency, it has a **low continuation probability** because it does not appear in many *different* contexts.
>
> The lesson: **frequency alone does not equal distributional richness**.

### 1.3 Why Standard Smoothing Fails

| Smoothing Method | Core Flaw That Kneser-Ney Fixes |
|---|---|
| **Laplace / Add-k** | Overestimates probability of unseen events uniformly. |
| **Good-Turing** | Reassigns mass to unseen events but does not redistribute based on context. |
| **Standard Backoff (Katz)** | Treats lower-order counts using the *same* distribution as the unigram — ignores context-specific overdispersion. |
| **Absolute Discounting** | Subtracts a fixed constant $D$ but still uses raw lower-order probabilities. |

The Kneser-Ney solution: **subtract $D$ from seen counts, then redistribute the discounted mass using a continuation probability that counts the number of *distinct preceding contexts* of each word.**

### 1.4 The Two Variants of Kneser-Ney

1. **Interpolated Kneser-Ney** — Always combines the higher-order discounted probability with the lower-order continuation probability. This is the **most commonly used** form in practice.
2. **Non-Interpolated (Plain) Kneser-Ney** — Pure backoff: use the discounted probability if the count is high enough, otherwise back off to the continuation probability.

The **Modified Kneser-Ney** extension uses **multiple discount values** ($D_1$, $D_2$, $D_{3+}$) based on the count of the n-gram, yielding the version that is essentially **the best-performing smoothing technique for n-gram language modeling** in 2024-era KTU curricula.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Building Up to Kneser-Ney — Conceptual Step Ladder

- **Step 1: Counting is Naive.** A raw n-gram model assigns zero probability to any n-gram never seen in training, making it unusable for real text.
- **Step 2: Smoothing Steals from the Rich.** We shift some probability mass from seen n-grams to unseen n-grams by *discounting* the seen counts.
- **Step 3: Interpolation Beats Pure Backoff.** Pure backoff wastes information — even for a seen n-gram, the lower-order model can provide useful signal. Hence we **interpolate** (weighted sum) between the higher- and lower-order distributions.
- **Step 4: Discounting Should Be Count-Dependent.** Not all counts should be discounted equally. Counts of 1 are more "noisy" than counts of 5. So Modified KN uses **three discount buckets**.
- **Step 5: Lower-Order Distribution Should Reflect Context Diversity.** This is the **key Kneser-Ney insight**. The unigram backoff $P(w)$ counts how often $w$ occurs, but we need to count **how many different contexts $w$ appears in**. A word like *Francisco* is frequent but contextually narrow.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below use $w_i$ as the predicted word and $w_{i-n+1}^{i-1}$ as the context of length $n-1$. Counts are denoted $c(\cdot)$.

| # | Formula Name | Equation (rendered) | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Interpolated KN — Higher Order | $P_{KN}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \dfrac{\max\bigl(c(w_{i-1},w_i) - D,\, 0\bigr)}{c(w_{i-1})} + \lambda(w_{i-1}) \cdot P_{cont}(w_i)$ | Discounted bigram probability + weighted lower-order term |
| 2 | Backoff Weight $\lambda$ | $\lambda(w_{i-1}) = \dfrac{D}{c(w_{i-1})} \cdot N_{1+}(w_{i-1} \bullet)$ | Fraction of mass reserved for continuation, scaled by number of unique successors |
| 3 | Continuation Probability (Numerator) | $N_{1+}(\bullet\, w_i) = \bigl\vert \{ w_{j-1} : c(w_{j-1}, w_i) > 0 \} \bigr\vert$ | Number of distinct preceding words for $w_i$ |
| 4 | Continuation Probability (Denominator) | $\displaystyle\sum_{w'} N_{1+}(\bullet\, w') = \sum_{w'} \bigl\vert \{ w_{j-1} : c(w_{j-1}, w') > 0 \} \bigr\vert$ | Total number of (preceding, word) type pairs |
| 5 | Continuation Probability (Full) | $P_{cont}(w_i) = \dfrac{N_{1+}(\bullet\, w_i)}{\displaystyle\sum_{w'} N_{1+}(\bullet\, w')}$ | Probability of $w_i$ as a "novel continuation" |
| 6 | Modified KN Discount (1-gram) | $D(c) = \begin{cases} D_1 & \text{if } c = 1 \\ D_2 & \text{if } c = 2 \\ D_{3+} & \text{if } c \ge 3 \end{cases}$ | Three-bucket discount heuristic |
| 7 | Empirical Estimate of $D$ | $D = \dfrac{n_1}{n_1 + 2 n_2}$ | Where $n_k$ = number of n-grams with count exactly $k$ |
| 8 | Total Discounted Mass | $Y = \dfrac{n_1}{n_1 + 2 n_2}$ | Singleton ratio from Good-Turing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notational Safeguard:** In all rendered equations above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes the *cardinality* of a set (number of distinct elements). This is the *only* place where vertical bars appear inside math, and they have been replaced in tabular contexts to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility

Kneser-Ney smoothing is foundational in:

- **Speech Recognition Systems** (acoustic + language model pipelines)
- **Machine Translation** (statistical and NMT hybrid scoring)
- **Autocomplete and Predictive Typing** (mobile keyboards, search engines)
- **Optical Character Recognition (OCR)** post-processing
- **Information Retrieval** (query likelihood language models)
- **Code Autocompletion Tools** in IDEs

It remains the **gold-standard baseline** against which newer neural language models (RNNs, Transformers) are measured using **perplexity (PPL)**.

### 2.4 Why Modified Kneser-Ney Is the Industry Standard

The Modified KN version uses empirically tuned discount values, typically:

$$D_1 \approx 0.9,\quad D_2 \approx 1.1,\quad D_{3+} \approx 1.1$$

> [!NOTE]
> These are not magic numbers — they are derived from leave-one-out statistics on large corpora. The rationale: **counts of 1 are discounted more aggressively** because they are statistically the most likely to be artifacts, while counts $\geq 2$ are discounted less aggressively because they are more likely to be genuine.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Standard Interpolated Kneser-Ney Formula

We start from the **absolute discounting** intuition. Suppose we have a bigram count $c(w_{i-1}, w_i)$. Absolute discounting says:

> "If we have seen this bigram, reduce its count by a small constant $D$ and redistribute $D$ per seen bigram to the unseen mass."

**Step 1 — Define the discounted bigram probability:**

$$P_{abs}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \frac{\max\bigl(c(w_{i-1}, w_i) - D,\, 0\bigr)}{c(w_{i-1})}$$

**Conversion Logic:** We take the original MLE count $c(w_{i-1}, w_i)$, subtract the discount $D$, and clamp at zero (so we never have negative probability). Then we normalize by the total unigram count of $w_{i-1}$ to keep it a valid probability.

**Step 2 — The mass we have freed up:**

For every context $w_{i-1}$, the total mass subtracted from its successors is:

$$\text{Freed Mass} = \sum_{w : c(w_{i-1}, w) > 0} \frac{D}{c(w_{i-1})} = \frac{D \cdot N_{1+}(w_{i-1} \bullet)}{c(w_{i-1})}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Each of the $N_{1+}(w_{i-1} \bullet)$ distinct successor words has contributed exactly $D / c(w_{i-1})$ to the freed mass. Multiplying gives the total.

**Step 3 — Define the backoff weight $\lambda(w_{i-1})$:**

$$\lambda(w_{i-1}) = \frac{D \cdot N_{1+}(w_{i-1} \bullet)}{c(w_{i-1})}$$

**Conversion Logic:** This is exactly the freed mass. It is the amount of probability we now need to push down to the lower-order (unigram) model.

**Step 4 — The full interpolated Kneser-Ney formula:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{KN}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) 
&= \frac{\max\bigl(c(w_{i-1}, w_i) - D,\, 0\bigr)}{c(w_{i-1})} \;+\; \lambda(w_{i-1}) \cdot P_{cont}(w_i) \\
&= \frac{\max\bigl(c(w_{i-1}, w_i) - D,\, 0\bigr)}{c(w_{i-1})} \;+\; \frac{D \cdot N_{1+}(w_{i-1} \bullet)}{c(w_{i-1})} \cdot P_{cont}(w_i)
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The bigram probability is the discounted observed portion, plus a weighted share of the lower-order continuation probability. The weights ensure the total sums to 1.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Continuation Probability

This is **the heart of Kneser-Ney**. We want a lower-order distribution that measures *contextual diversity* rather than raw frequency.

**Step 1 — Define the number of distinct preceding contexts for a word $w_i$:**

$$N_{1+}(\bullet\, w_i) = \bigl\vert \{ w_{j-1} : c(w_{j-1}, w_i) > 0 \} \bigr\vert$$

**Conversion Logic:** This counts how many *different* words have appeared immediately before $w_i$ in the training corpus. A high number means $w_i$ is *versatile*; a low number means $w_i$ is *contextually narrow* (like *Francisco*).

**Step 2 — The total number of (preceding word, current word) type pairs:**

$$Z = \sum_{w'} N_{1+}(\bullet\, w') = \sum_{w'} \bigl\vert \{ w_{j-1} : c(w_{j-1}, w') > 0 \} \bigr\vert$$

**Conversion Logic:** We sum the distinct predecessor counts over *all* word types. This is the total number of unique (left-context, word) combinations observed.

**Step 3 — The continuation probability:**

$$P_{cont}(w_i) = \frac{N_{1+}(\bullet\, w_i)}{Z} = \frac{\bigl\vert \{ w_{j-1} : c(w_{j-1}, w_i) > 0 \} \bigr\vert}{\displaystyle\sum_{w'} \bigl\vert \{ w_{j-1} : c(w_{j-1}, w') > 0 \} \bigr\vert}$$

**Conversion Logic:** This is just a normalized count of how many contexts $w_i$ can appear in. Words that appear in many contexts get higher continuation probability — this is the *opposite* of what raw unigram frequency would do for contextually narrow words.

### 3.3 Generalization to N-Gram Order

For an n-gram model, the formula generalizes recursively. For the highest order (n-gram) and lower order (n-1)-gram, we have:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{KN}(w_i \mid w_{i-n+1}^{i-1}) 
&= \frac{\max\bigl(c_{\text{context}}(w_i) - D(c_{\text{context}}(w_i)),\, 0\bigr)}{c_{\text{context}}} \\
&\quad + \lambda(w_{i-n+1}^{i-1}) \cdot P_{KN}(w_i \mid w_{i-n+2}^{i-1})
\end{aligned}$$

Where the **continuation** recursively uses the n-1 order model with $N_{1+}(\bullet^{n-1} w_i)$ — the number of distinct $(n-1)$-gram contexts in which $w_i$ has appeared.

### 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (Numerical)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example — Bigram Kneser-Ney**
>
> Consider the following training corpus:
>
> *"I am Sam. Sam I am. I do not like green eggs and ham."*
>
> From this corpus, we extract the relevant bigram statistics for the word **"am"**:
>
> - $c(\text{"I"}) = 3$  (unigram count)
> - $c(\text{"I", "am"}) = 2$  (bigram count)
> - $c(\text{"am"}) = 2$  (unigram count)
> - $c(\text{"Sam", "am"}) = 1$  (bigram count)
> - Distinct predecessors of "am": $\{\text{"I", "Sam"}\}$ → $N_{1+}(\bullet\,\text{am}) = 2$
> - Suppose the total $Z = 14$ (sum of $N_{1+}$ over all word types)
> - Let $D = 0.75$ for simplicity (one discount bucket)

**Step 1 — Compute $P_{KN}(\text{am} \mid \text{I})$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{KN}(\text{am} \mid \text{I}) 
&= \frac{\max(c(\text{I, am}) - D,\, 0)}{c(\text{I})} + \lambda(\text{I}) \cdot P_{cont}(\text{am}) \\
&= \frac{\max(2 - 0.75,\, 0)}{3} + \frac{0.75 \cdot N_{1+}(\text{I}\, \bullet)}{c(\text{I})} \cdot P_{cont}(\text{am}) \\
&= \frac{1.25}{3} + \frac{0.75 \cdot 2}{3} \cdot \frac{2}{14} \\
&= 0.4167 + 0.5 \cdot 0.1429 \\
&= 0.4167 + 0.0714 \\
&= 0.4881
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion Logic:**
- We discounted the seen count $2$ by $D = 0.75$ to get $1.25$, then normalized by the context count $3$.
- The backoff weight $\lambda(\text{I})$ is $(0.75 \times 2) / 3 = 0.5$ — this means $0.5$ of the probability mass gets redirected to the unigram-level model.
- The continuation probability $P_{cont}(\text{am}) = 2/14 = 0.1429$ — the word *am* appears in 2 distinct predecessor contexts, out of 14 total (predecessor, word) pairs in the vocabulary.

**Step 2 — Compare with a plain backoff model:**

A naive backoff would use $P_{abs}(\text{am}) = c(\text{am}) / N = 2/24 = 0.0833$, ignoring contextual diversity. The Kneser-Ney model gives $\text{am}$ a much higher score here, **reflecting the fact that *am* is contextually versatile** (it follows both "I" and "Sam"). This is a feature, not a bug.

### 3.5 Modified Kneser-Ney Discount Estimation

For Modified KN, the discount for count $c$ is:

$$D(c) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } c = 0 \\ D_1 & \text{if } c = 1 \\ D_2 & \text{if } c = 2 \\ D_{3+} & \text{if } c \ge 3 \end{cases}$$

The empirical formula for the discounts uses $Y = n_1 / (n_1 + 2n_2)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= 1 - 2 Y \cdot \dfrac{n_2}{n_1} \\
D_2 &= 2 - 3 Y \cdot \dfrac{n_3}{n_2} \\
D_{3+} &= 3 - 4 Y \cdot \dfrac{n_4}{n_3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Each discount value is calibrated so that, on average, the mass subtracted from a particular count bucket equals the amount that would have been predicted by Good-Turing smoothing. This is the elegant **bridge between Good-Turing and Kneser-Ney**.

### 3.6 Summary Comparison Table — All Major Smoothing Techniques

| Technique | Handles Zeros? | Uses Context? | Interpolation? | KN Improvement |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MLE (No Smoothing)** | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | Baseline |
| **Laplace (Add-1)** | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | Mass over-uniformly distributed |
| **Add-k** | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | Better than Add-1, still uniform |
| **Good-Turing** | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | Smart reallocation, no context |
| **Katz Backoff** | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | Good but context-blind at unigram level |
| **Interpolated KN** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | **Distributional unigram** |
| **Modified Interpolated KN** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | **State-of-the-art baseline** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Kneser-Ney Smoothing

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **algorithmic flow** of computing a Kneser-Ney smoothed n-gram probability. Note that all node IDs are purely alphanumeric (no reserved keywords) and labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Target Ngram w context w]
    B[Lookup Count c context w]
    C{Is Count Greater Than Zero}
    D[Apply Discount D c using Modified KN buckets]
    E[Normalize by c context]
    F[Compute Lambda Backoff Weight]
    G[Lookup Continuation Count N1+ of w]
    H[Compute Continuation Probability P cont w]
    I[Compute Lower Order KN Probability recursively]
    J[Combine Higher Order and Lower Order]
    K[Output Final P KN w context w]
    L[End Return Probability]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C -- Yes --> D
    C -- No --> F
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I --> J
    F --> J
    J --> K
    K --> L
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The process starts by looking up the count of the target n-gram in the training corpus.
- If the count is non-zero, the **Modified KN discount** is applied (subtracting $D_1$, $D_2$, or $D_{3+}$ depending on count magnitude).
- Regardless of whether the count is zero, the **backoff weight $\lambda$** is computed using the number of distinct successors.
- The continuation probability is computed by counting the distinct predecessor contexts.
- The lower-order Kneser-Ney probability is computed recursively (for higher-order n-grams).
- The final output is the interpolated sum of the discounted higher-order probability and the weighted lower-order probability.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Relationship Between Continuation and Raw Frequency

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Word w in Corpus]
    N[Raw Unigram Frequency c w]
    O[Distinct Predecessor Contexts N1+ bullet w]
    P[Continuation Probability P cont w]

    M --> N
    M --> O
    N --> Q[Standard Backoff Uses c w directly]
    O --> P
    P --> R[Kneser Ney Backoff uses P cont w]
    Q --> S[Overestimates Contextually Narrow Words]
    R --> T[Correctly Weights Contextual Diversity]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The left branch shows the **standard backoff** approach, which uses raw unigram counts.
- The right branch shows the **Kneser-Ney** approach, which replaces raw counts with continuation probabilities.
- This is the **conceptual core** of Kneser-Ney: replacing frequency with *distributional richness*.

### 4.3 Functional Pipeline Diagram — Modified KN Discount Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Input Ngram Count c]
    X1{Count equals 1}
    X2{Count equals 2}
    X3{Count greater than or equal 3}
    Y1[Apply Discount D1 typically 0.9]
    Y2[Apply Discount D2 typically 1.1]
    Y3[Apply Discount D3plus typically 1.1]
    Z[Discounted Count c minus D c]

    X --> X1
    X1 -- Yes --> Y1
    X1 -- No --> X2
    X2 -- Yes --> Y2
    X2 -- No --> X3
    X3 -- Yes --> Y3
    Y1 --> Z
    Y2 --> Z
    Y3 --> Z
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Modified Kneser-Ney uses a three-bucket discount scheme.
- Counts of 1 receive the **largest discount** ($D_1$) because they are most likely to be statistical noise.
- Counts of 2 receive a smaller discount ($D_2$).
- Counts $\geq 3$ receive a uniform discount ($D_{3+}$).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(3 Marks)** Differentiate clearly between **backoff** and **interpolation** in the context of n-gram language model smoothing. State which approach is used in Kneser-Ney smoothing and justify your answer with a one-sentence reason.

**Model Answer (3 Marks Allocation):**

| # | Element | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Backoff** — When the higher-order n-gram has zero (or insufficient) count, fall back to the lower-order n-gram probability. Only one model is used at a time. | 1 |
| 2 | **Interpolation** — Always combine the higher-order and lower-order n-gram probabilities using a weighted sum, regardless of whether the higher-order count is non-zero. | 1 |
| 3 | **KN uses interpolation** because interpolating allows the model to leverage lower-order context even when higher-order counts are reliable, reducing variance. | 1 |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**
**(3 Marks)** Define **continuation probability** in Kneser-Ney smoothing. Why is it preferred over the raw unigram probability as a backoff distribution?

**Model Answer (3 Marks Allocation):**

| # | Element | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Definition:** $P_{cont}(w_i) = N_{1+}(\bullet\, w_i) \,/\, \sum_{w'} N_{1+}(\bullet\, w')$ where $N_{1+}(\bullet\, w_i)$ is the number of distinct preceding contexts of $w_i$. | 1 |
| 2 | **Reason 1:** It measures *contextual diversity* — how broadly a word is used across different preceding words. | 1 |
| 3 | **Reason 2:** Raw unigram frequency overweights contextually narrow words (e.g., *Francisco* after *San*); continuation probability correctly downweights them. | 1 |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

---

## Question A (14 Marks) — *[Choose either A or B]*

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Interpolated Kneser-Ney smoothing formula** for a bigram language model from the principle of absolute discounting. Define and explain each term in the final equation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Given the following tiny corpus:
*"the cat sat on the mat. the cat sat on the rug. the dog sat on the mat."*

Compute the **Kneser-Ney smoothed probability** $P_{KN}(\text{“rug”} \mid \text{“on the”})$ as a trigram probability. Assume discount $D = 0.75$ for all counts and the standard interpolated KN formula. Show all intermediate steps.

---

### Model Solution for Q.A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks Model Solution

| Step | Content | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Start with absolute discounting principle:** observed bigram counts are reduced by a constant $D$ and the freed mass is redistributed to unseen bigrams. | 1 |
| 2 | **Write the discounted bigram probability:** $\frac{\max(c(w_{i-1}, w_i) - D, 0)}{c(w_{i-1})}$. Explain why we use $\max$ (to clamp at zero). | 1 |
| 3 | **Compute the freed mass per context:** $\frac{D \cdot N_{1+}(w_{i-1} \bullet)}{c(w_{i-1})}$ — this becomes the backoff weight $\lambda$. | 1 |
| 4 | **Define the continuation probability $P_{cont}$** as the proportion of distinct preceding contexts of a word. | 1 |
| 5 | **Write the full interpolated formula:** $P_{KN}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \frac{\max(c - D, 0)}{c(w_{i-1})} + \lambda(w_{i-1}) \cdot P_{cont}(w_i)$. | 1 |
| 6 | **Explain the role of interpolation:** even with a high bigram count, the lower-order continuation contributes, reducing variance. | 1 |
| 7 | **Conclude:** justify why this is superior to Katz backoff (which fails to use $P_{cont}$). | 1 |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks Numerical Solution

**Step 1 — Extract trigram and bigram counts from the corpus:**

| N-gram | Count |
|---|---|
| ("on the", "rug") | 1 |
| ("on the", "mat") | 2 |
| ("on the", total) | 3 |
| ("the", "rug") | 1 |
| ("the", "mat") | 2 |
| ("rug", preceded by distinct words) | 1 (only "the") |
| ("mat", preceded by distinct words) | 1 (only "the") |

**Step 2 — Apply the formula with $D = 0.75$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{KN}(\text{rug} \mid \text{on the}) 
&= \frac{\max(c(\text{on the, rug}) - D, 0)}{c(\text{on the})} + \lambda(\text{on the}) \cdot P_{cont}(\text{rug}) \\
&= \frac{\max(1 - 0.75, 0)}{3} + \frac{0.75 \cdot N_{1+}(\text{on the} \bullet)}{c(\text{on the})} \cdot P_{cont}(\text{rug})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute each component:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\max(1 - 0.75, 0)}{3} &= \frac{0.25}{3} = 0.0833 \\
N_{1+}(\text{on the} \bullet) &= \text{count of distinct successors of "on the"} = 2 \text{ (rug, mat)} \\
\lambda(\text{on the}) &= \frac{0.75 \times 2}{3} = 0.5 \\
P_{cont}(\text{rug}) &= \frac{N_{1+}(\bullet\, \text{rug})}{\sum_{w'} N_{1+}(\bullet\, w')}
\end{aligned}$$

Assuming a small vocabulary of {rug, mat} only: $N_{1+}(\bullet\, \text{rug}) = 1$, $N_{1+}(\bullet\, \text{mat}) = 1$, total $Z = 2$.

$$P_{cont}(\text{rug}) = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

**Step 4 — Combine:**

$$P_{KN}(\text{rug} \mid \text{on the}) = 0.0833 + 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.0833 + 0.25 = 0.3333$$

**Step 5 — State the result clearly with units:**

> The Kneser-Ney smoothed probability of *"rug"* following *"on the"* is **0.3333** (dimensionless, since probabilities are unitless).

| Sub-step | Marks |
|---|---|
| Correct trigram count extraction | 1 |
| Apply discounting formula correctly | 1 |
| Compute $\lambda$ correctly | 1 |
| Compute $P_{cont}$ correctly | 1 |
| Recursive use of lower-order model (big continuation) | 1 |
| Combine via interpolation | 1 |
| Final numerical answer | 1 |

---

## Question B (14 Marks) — *[Alternative Choice]*

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Modified Kneser-Ney smoothing** technique. How does it improve over the standard Kneser-Ney smoothing? Use the three-bucket discount scheme in your answer.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **Laplace (Add-1)**, **Good-Turing**, and **Kneser-Ney** smoothing techniques across the following dimensions: (i) handling of zero counts, (ii) use of context, (iii) interpolation vs backoff, (iv) computational complexity, and (v) typical perplexity performance on benchmark corpora.

---

### Model Solution for Q.B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks Model Solution

| Element | Content | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Standard KN uses a single discount $D$** for all non-zero n-gram counts. This is a simplification because in practice, counts of 1 are much less reliable than counts of 5. | 1 |
| 2 | **Modified KN introduces three discount buckets:** $D_1$ (for $c=1$), $D_2$ (for $c=2$), $D_{3+}$ (for $c \geq 3$). | 1 |
| 3 | **Empirical discount values:** typically $D_1 \approx 0.9$, $D_2 \approx 1.1$, $D_{3+} \approx 1.1$, derived from $n_1, n_2, n_3, n_4$ statistics. | 1 |
| 4 | **Why $D_1 < D_2$:** Counts of 1 are the noisiest and most likely to be artifacts, so they are discounted *less* in absolute terms (because we already know they are unreliable). Actually $D_1$ is *smaller* than $D_2$ in some implementations, but the *effective* discount is larger for low counts. | 1 |
| 5 | **Worked intuition:** A count of 1 represents a single observation that may never recur. Discounting it by 0.9 leaves only 0.1, which is then redistributed. A count of 3 is more reliable and receives a smaller effective discount. | 1 |
| 6 | **Formula for empirical estimation** of $D_1, D_2, D_{3+}$ using $Y = n_1 / (n_1 + 2 n_2)$ and related ratios. | 1 |
| 7 | **Conclusion:** Modified KN is the de facto state-of-the-art smoothing for n-gram language models and is the standard baseline in the KTU NLP curriculum. | 1 |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks Comparison Table (1.4 Marks per Row × 5 Rows)

| Dimension | Laplace (Add-1) | Good-Turing | Kneser-Ney |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Handling of zero counts** | Adds 1 to every count uniformly. Simple but over-smooths. | Reassigns mass based on singleton counts $n_1$. | Discounts seen counts and uses $P_{cont}$ for unseen. |
| **(ii) Use of context** | Context-agnostic (same $k$ for all orders). | Context-agnostic (operates on counts globally). | Fully context-aware via continuation counts. |
| **(iii) Interpolation vs Backoff** | Neither; pure additive smoothing. | Neither; pure mass reallocation. | Interpolated by default. |
| **(iv) Computational Complexity** | $O(V)$ per n-gram; $V$ = vocab size. | $O(V)$ per n-gram; requires $n_1, n_2$ stats. | $O(V)$ for backoff weights + recursion. |
| **(v) Perplexity on PTB** | ~500+ PPL (poor). | ~300–350 PPL (better). | **~140 PPL (best in class)** for bigram, lower for trigram. |

| Sub-part | Marks |
|---|---|
| Five dimensions correctly identified and tabulated | 5 |
| Conceptual clarity in each cell | 1 |
| Concluding remark on KN superiority | 1 |

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Kneser-Ney Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the `max(·, 0)` clamp** — Students often write $c(w_{i-1}, w_i) - D$ without clamping at zero, leading to negative probabilities. Examiners deduct **1 full mark** for this.
> 2. **Confusing continuation count with raw unigram count** — A very common error is to use $c(w_i)$ instead of $N_{1+}(\bullet\, w_i)$ in the continuation probability. This destroys the entire Kneser-Ney advantage.
> 3. **Missing the interpolation step** — Writing only the backoff version and forgetting to add the lower-order term will cost **at least 2 marks** out of 7.
> 4. **No mention of the three-bucket discount in Modified KN** — If the question says "Modified Kneser-Ney" and the student writes a single $D$ value, expect to lose **2–3 marks**.
> 5. **Failing to define $\lambda$ explicitly** — Examiners look for the backoff weight formula as a separate expression. Just embedding it inline loses a mark.
> 6. **Unit / normalization oversight** — In numerical problems, students forget to verify that probabilities sum to 1. A quick sanity check at the end earns bonus partial credit in borderline cases.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Kneser-Ney Smoothing**
>
> - **Smoothing is essential** because raw n-gram models assign zero probability to unseen sequences, making them unusable for real text generation or scoring.
> - **Absolute discounting** subtracts a constant $D$ from each seen n-gram count, freeing probability mass for unseen events.
> - **The Kneser-Ney insight** is the **continuation probability**: instead of using raw lower-order counts, count the number of *distinct preceding contexts* of each word.
> - **Formula (Bigram):** $P_{KN}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \dfrac{\max(c - D, 0)}{c(w_{i-1})} + \lambda(w_{i-1}) \cdot P_{cont}(w_i)$.
> - **Backoff weight:** $\lambda(w_{i-1}) = \dfrac{D \cdot N_{1+}(w_{i-1} \bullet)}{c(w_{i-1})}$.
> - **Continuation:** $P_{cont}(w_i) = \dfrac{N_{1+}(\bullet\, w_i)}{\sum_{w'} N_{1+}(\bullet\, w')}$.
> - **Interpolated KN** is always preferred over non-interpolated (plain) KN in practice.
> - **Modified KN** uses **three discount values** $D_1, D_2, D_{3+}$ for counts of 1, 2, and $\geq 3$ respectively — typically empirically derived.
> - **Discount estimation** uses $Y = n_1 / (n_1 + 2 n_2)$ and similar ratios.
> - **Real-world example:** "Francisco" appears often but only in one context ("San Francisco") — high unigram frequency, low continuation probability. KN handles this correctly.
> - **Application areas:** speech recognition, machine translation, autocomplete, OCR, IR.
> - **Performance:** KN achieves the best perplexity among classical smoothing methods and remains the **gold-standard baseline** for evaluating neural LMs.
> - **Recursion:** The continuation probability itself recursively uses the lower-order Kneser-Ney model for n-gram orders $\geq 3$.
> - **The `max(c - D, 0)` clamp** is non-negotiable — never produce negative probabilities.
> - **Modified KN's three-bucket scheme** is the single most important practical extension of standard KN for KTU exam purposes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Perplexity's Relation to Entropy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT741 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Perplexity's Relation to Entropy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Information Theory** and **Natural Language Processing (NLP)**, **Perplexity (PP)** is formally defined as the exponential of the **cross-entropy** between the empirical distribution of a test sequence and the probability distribution estimated by a language model. It quantifies the *uncertainty* or *confusion* a model exhibits when predicting the next token in a sequence.

For a sequence of tokens $W = (w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_N)$ drawn from a vocabulary $V$, the perplexity of a language model $M$ over the sequence $W$ is given by:

$$
PP_M(W) = P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_N)^{-1/N}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Perplexity is a normalized, exponentiated measure of the average negative log-likelihood of a sequence. It is the standard intrinsic evaluation metric for probabilistic language models in Module 1 of PEITT741.

The closely related quantity, **Entropy $H(P)$**, measures the *intrinsic average information content* (in bits or nats) of a probability distribution $P$:

$$
H(P) = -\sum_{x \in V} P(x) \log_b P(x)
$$

where the base $b$ determines the unit (base 2 → bits, base $e$ → nats).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **multiple-choice quiz** where you are asked to guess the next word in a sentence. The choices come from a vocabulary of $N$ words.

- If the model is **completely clueless** (uniform random guessing over $N$ words), then at every step, the model is as confused as if it had to pick uniformly among **$N$** options. Its perplexity is therefore exactly $N$.
- If the model is **highly confident and accurate**, it narrows the "effective" number of choices down to a small set. The perplexity drops.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Meaning of Perplexity:** It is the *effective branching factor* of the language model. A perplexity of 100 means the model is, on average, as puzzled as a random guesser choosing uniformly among 100 alternatives.

Think of entropy as the **theoretical average information per symbol** measured in raw bits, and perplexity as that same information content **converted into "how many choices" the model effectively faces** at each step. They are mathematically intertwined — perplexity is simply the exponential of entropy.

### 1.3 Key Metrics at a Glance

- **Entropy $H(P)$** is measured in **bits** (or **nats**).
- **Perplexity $PP$** is a **dimensionless scalar** (always $\geq 1$).
- For a deterministic distribution (one outcome with probability 1), $H = 0$ and $PP = 1$ (no confusion).
- For a uniform distribution over $N$ outcomes, $H = \log_b N$ and $PP = N$ (maximum confusion).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold Constants & Standard Metrics:** The natural logarithm base $e \approx 2.718$ and base-2 logarithm are the two canonical bases. The convention $PP = 2^H$ is the most prevalent in NLP literature and is the default expected in KTU examinations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Decomposition of the Perplexity-Entropy Relationship

The relationship between perplexity and entropy emerges from three cascading definitions in information theory:

- **Step 1 — Shannon Entropy as Average Information Content:**
  Every random variable has an average information (surprisal) cost. The lower the probability of an outcome, the higher its *self-information* $I(x) = -\log_b P(x)$. Entropy averages this across the distribution.

- **Step 2 — Cross-Entropy as Model-vs-Truth Divergence:**
  When a model $Q$ approximates the true distribution $P$, the cross-entropy $H(P, Q)$ captures the average number of bits needed to encode samples from $P$ using a code optimized for $Q$. It always satisfies $H(P, Q) \geq H(P)$.

- **Step 3 — Perplexity as Exponentiated Cross-Entropy:**
  Exponentiating the cross-entropy (or entropy, in the ideal self-modeling case) converts the information-theoretic "bits per word" into a count of equally-likely alternatives. This is the perplexity.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why':** The reason perplexity is defined as an *exponential* of entropy (rather than entropy itself) is historical and psychological. Shannon's original intuition was that the entropy of a distribution over English text is roughly 1.3 bits per character, but humans prefer to reason in terms of "how many choices" — i.e., $2^{1.3} \approx 2.5$ equally-likely next characters — which is far more intuitive.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Base Convention** | **Range / Units** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Self-Information | $I(x)$ | $-\log_b P(x)$ | $b = 2$ or $e$ | $\geq 0$, in bits/nats |
| Shannon Entropy | $H(P)$ | $-\sum_x P(x)\log_b P(x)$ | $b = 2$ → bits | $\geq 0$, in bits/nats |
| Joint Entropy | $H(X, Y)$ | $-\sum_{x,y} P(x,y)\log_b P(x,y)$ | $b = 2$ | $\geq 0$, in bits |
| Conditional Entropy | $H(Y \mid X)$ | $\sum_x P(x) H(Y \mid X=x)$ | $b = 2$ | $\geq 0$, in bits |
| Cross-Entropy | $H(P, Q)$ | $-\sum_x P(x)\log_b Q(x)$ | $b = 2$ | $\geq H(P)$, in bits |
| KL Divergence | $D_{KL}(P \mid\mid Q)$ | $\sum_x P(x)\log_b \frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}$ | $b = 2$ | $\geq 0$, in bits |
| Perplexity (Model on Sequence) | $PP_M(W)$ | $P(w_1,\ldots,w_N)^{-1/N}$ | $b = 2$ | $\geq 1$, dimensionless |
| Perplexity (in terms of Cross-Entropy) | $PP$ | $b^{H(P,Q)}$ | $b = 2$ or $e$ | $\geq 1$, dimensionless |
| Perplexity (in terms of Entropy) | $PP$ | $b^{H(P)}$ | $b = 2$ or $e$ | $\geq 1$, dimensionless |
| Average Negative Log-Likelihood | $ANLL$ | $-\frac{1}{N}\sum_i \log_b P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$ | $b = 2$ | $\geq 0$, in bits/nats |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Table Note:** In the row "Perplexity (in terms of Cross-Entropy)", the variable $b$ is the same base used in the log of the cross-entropy. The absolute-value delimiter $\vert \cdot \vert$ has been written as plain text to preserve KTU markdown compatibility.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Language Model Benchmarking:** Perplexity is the *de facto* intrinsic metric for evaluating $n$-gram, neural, and transformer-based LMs on held-out corpora. Lower perplexity → better predictive distribution.
- **Speech Recognition Decoders:** Weighted finite-state transducers (WFSTs) use perplexity-derived language model scores to re-rank acoustic hypotheses.
- **Machine Translation Evaluation:** Cross-entropy-per-bit and perplexity are used in Minimum Translation Edit Rate (MTER) pipelines and LM adaptation.
- **Loss Function in Neural LMs:** Training objectives such as the standard log-loss in RNNs/Transformers are directly equivalent to minimizing perplexity.
- **Information-Theoretic Source Coding:** The Shannon source coding theorem establishes entropy as the theoretical limit of compression — perplexity simply rephrases that limit in terms of branching factor.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Perplexity from Joint Probability of a Sequence

We start from the formal definition of perplexity for a sequence $W = (w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_N)$:

$$
PP_M(W) = P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_N)^{-1/N}
$$

Taking the **base-2 logarithm** of both sides:

$$
\log_2 PP_M(W) = -\frac{1}{N} \log_2 P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_N)
$$

By the chain rule of probability, the joint probability decomposes as:

$$
P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_N) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_{i-1})
$$

Substituting this into the log expression:

$$
\log_2 PP_M(W) = -\frac{1}{N} \log_2 \left[ \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_{<i}) \right]
$$

Applying the **logarithm of a product** identity, $\log(\prod a_i) = \sum \log(a_i)$:

$$
\log_2 PP_M(W) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2 P(w_i \mid w_{<i})
$$

Recognizing the right-hand side as the **average negative log-likelihood (ANLL)** of the model on the sequence:

$$
\log_2 PP_M(W) = \text{ANLL}_M(W)
$$

Exponentiating both sides (base 2):

$$
PP_M(W) = 2^{\text{ANLL}_M(W)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion of Derivation 1:** Perplexity is mathematically equivalent to 2 raised to the average negative log-likelihood of the model on the test sequence. This is the form typically computed in practice for $n$-gram and neural LMs.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Cross-Entropy as the Bridge from Perplexity

The **empirical cross-entropy** of model $Q$ (our language model) under the true data-generating distribution $P$ for a sequence of length $N$ is defined as:

$$
H(P, Q) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2 Q(w_i \mid w_{<i})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $P$ here is the empirical (one-hot) distribution observed in the test data; the inner sum is exactly the same quantity that appeared in Derivation 1.

Equating the right-hand side of the cross-entropy expression with the right-hand side of the perplexity log-expression:

$$
H(P, Q) = \log_2 PP_M(W)
$$

Therefore, exponentiating both sides:

$$
PP_M(W) = 2^{H(P, Q)}
$$

This is the **canonical perplexity-entropy relation** expected in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Perplexity in Terms of Shannon Entropy (Self-Modeling Case)

If the model $Q$ is a *perfect* estimator of the true distribution $P$ (i.e., $Q = P$), then cross-entropy collapses to Shannon entropy:

$$
H(P, P) = -\sum_x P(x) \log_2 P(x) = H(P)
$$

Substituting into the perplexity relation:

$$
PP_P = 2^{H(P)}
$$

For a **uniform distribution** over $N$ outcomes, $P(x) = 1/N$ for all $x$, so:

$$
H(P) = -\sum_{x=1}^{N} \frac{1}{N} \log_2 \frac{1}{N} = -\log_2 \frac{1}{N} = \log_2 N
$$

Therefore:

$$
PP_P = 2^{\log_2 N} = N
$$

This confirms the earlier intuition: for a uniform distribution, perplexity equals the number of outcomes.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A trigram language model assigns the following probabilities to a test sentence $W = $ "the cat sat": $P(\text{the} \mid \langle s \rangle) = 0.5$, $P(\text{cat} \mid \text{the}) = 0.4$, $P(\text{sat} \mid \text{cat}) = 0.2$. Compute the perplexity of the model on this sentence.

**Step 1 — Compute the joint probability:**

$$
P(W) = 0.5 \times 0.4 \times 0.2 = 0.04
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the perplexity formula with $N = 3$:**

$$
PP_M(W) = (0.04)^{-1/3}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the exponent:**

$$
(0.04)^{-1/3} = \left(\frac{1}{0.04}\right)^{1/3} = (25)^{1/3} \approx 2.924
$$

**Step 4 — Verify via the cross-entropy route:**

$$
H(P, Q) = -\frac{1}{3}\left[ \log_2(0.5) + \log_2(0.4) + \log_2(0.2) \right]
$$

$$
= -\frac{1}{3}\left[ -1 - 0.3219 - 2.3219 \right]
$$

$$
= -\frac{1}{3}\left[ -3.6438 \right] = 1.5479 \text{ bits}
$$

Exponentiating:

$$
PP_M(W) = 2^{1.5479} \approx 2.924
$$

Both methods yield the same result, confirming the perplexity–entropy relation.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Concepts to Quantities

| **Concept** | **Connected Quantity** | **Relationship** | **Use in NLP** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Self-Information $I(x)$ | Surprisal of one symbol | $\log_b$ of inverse probability | Token-level loss |
| Entropy $H(P)$ | True average information | Exponent of self-info, weighted | Theoretical lower bound |
| Cross-Entropy $H(P, Q)$ | Avg. bits using model $Q$ | Lower bound = entropy | Training loss for LMs |
| Perplexity $PP$ | Exponential of cross-entropy | Dimensionless branching factor | Standard LM evaluation |
| KL Divergence $D_{KL}$ | Excess bits due to $Q$ | $H(P,Q) - H(P)$ | Distillation, analysis |
| Average Negative Log-Likelihood | Empirical cross-entropy | Per-token loss average | PyTorch/TensorFlow loss |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: Perplexity-Entropy Computational Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional data flow** that connects a raw text sequence, the language model, and the resulting perplexity-entropy computations:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Test Corpus W] --> B[Tokenization Stage]
    B --> C[Sequence w1 w2 ... wN]
    C --> D[Language Model Q]
    D --> E[Per-Token Probabilities Pwi_given_prev]
    E --> F[Negative Log-Likelihood Sum]
    F --> G[Average ANLL Computation]
    G --> H{Normalization Base}
    H -->|Base 2| I[Cross Entropy in Bits]
    H -->|Base e| J[Cross Entropy in Nats]
    I --> K[Exponentiate 2 to the H]
    J --> L[Exponentiate e to the H]
    K --> M[Perplexity PP]
    L --> M[Perplexity PP]
    G --> N[Ideal Self Modeling Case]
    N --> O[Shannon Entropy H of P]
    O --> P[Perplexity equals 2 to the H of P]
    M --> Q[Final Scalar Evaluation Metric]
    P --> Q[Final Scalar Evaluation Metric]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Hierarchy: From Information to Perplexity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Info[Self Information I x] --> Ent[Shannon Entropy H P]
    Ent --> Cross[Cross Entropy H P Q]
    Cross --> Perp[Perplexity 2 to the H P Q]
    Ent --> PerpSelf[Perplexity 2 to the H P]
    Perp --> Eval[Language Model Evaluation]
    PerpSelf --> Eval
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The first diagram traces the *operational pipeline* from raw corpus input to the final perplexity scalar. The second diagram shows the *conceptual hierarchy* — moving upward from raw self-information to the practical evaluation metric. Notice that **cross-entropy** is the immediate precursor to perplexity in both views.

### 4.3 Distribution-to-Perplexity Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Distribution Type] --> B[Uniform over N outcomes]
    A --> C[Deterministic one outcome]
    A --> D[Peaked at one outcome]
    B --> E[Perplexity equals N]
    C --> F[Perplexity equals 1]
    D --> G[Perplexity between 1 and N]
    E --> H[Maximum Confusion]
    F --> I[Zero Confusion]
    G --> J[Partial Confidence]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical Takeaway:** This mapping is essential for KTU 2024 examination answers. Always state the *type* of distribution first, derive entropy, then exponentiate to obtain perplexity. Examiners award full marks only when both entropy *and* perplexity values are computed with proper units.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**
*Define perplexity in the context of language models. Show that for a uniform distribution over $N$ possible words, the perplexity equals $N$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Perplexity of a language model $M$ on a test sequence $W = (w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_N)$ is defined as the reciprocal of the geometric mean of the per-token probabilities assigned by the model:

$$
PP_M(W) = \left[ \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_{<i}) \right]^{-1/N} = 2^{H(P, Q)}
$$

**Derivation for Uniform Distribution (1.5 marks):** For a uniform distribution, $P(x) = 1/N$ for every outcome $x$. The Shannon entropy is:

$$
H(P) = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{N} \log_2 \frac{1}{N} = \log_2 N
$$

Exponentiating:

$$
PP = 2^{H(P)} = 2^{\log_2 N} = N
$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*State and explain the mathematical relationship between cross-entropy and perplexity. Why is perplexity preferred over cross-entropy for reporting language model performance?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Relationship (1.5 marks):** The perplexity $PP$ of a language model is the base-$b$ exponentiation of the cross-entropy $H(P, Q)$ between the true distribution $P$ and the model distribution $Q$:

$$
PP = b^{H(P, Q)}
$$

where $b = 2$ gives perplexity in terms of "binary choices" (the standard NLP convention).

**Preference Justification (1.5 marks):**
- Perplexity is a **dimensionless scalar**, easier to interpret than entropy's units of "bits/word".
- It directly maps to the *effective branching factor* — the average number of equally-likely choices the model faces at each step.
- Lower bound of 1 corresponds to a perfect model, making it intuitive for model comparison.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Convention:** Part B questions in the End Semester Examination carry 14 marks with internal choice. Each sub-part (a) and (b) carries 7 marks. Students are expected to attempt either **Question A** OR **Question B** in full.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Adapted]**
*(a)* Derive the formula expressing **perplexity of a language model** on a test sequence in terms of the **average negative log-likelihood** of the sequence. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A unigram language model is built over a vocabulary of $V = 4$ words $\{a, b, c, d\}$ with the following empirical probabilities estimated from a corpus:

$$
P(a) = 0.5, \quad P(b) = 0.25, \quad P(c) = 0.125, \quad P(d) = 0.125
$$

Compute the **Shannon entropy**, **perplexity**, and **cross-entropy** of this distribution with itself. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer for (a):**

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of perplexity for a sequence of length $N$:**

$$
PP_M(W) = P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_N)^{-1/N}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the logarithm base 2 to both sides:**

$$
\log_2 PP_M(W) = -\frac{1}{N} \log_2 P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_N)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the chain rule of probability:**

$$
P(w_1, \ldots, w_N) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_{<i})
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and apply $\log(\prod) = \sum \log$:**

$$
\log_2 PP_M(W) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2 P(w_i \mid w_{<i})
$$

**Step 5 — Identify the right-hand side as the average negative log-likelihood (ANLL):**

$$
\log_2 PP_M(W) = \text{ANLL}_M(W)
$$

**Step 6 — Exponentiate to isolate perplexity:**

$$
PP_M(W) = 2^{\text{ANLL}_M(W)}
$$

**Assumption:** The model assigns non-zero probability to every token in the sequence (zero-probability tokens make perplexity undefined / infinite).

**Model Answer for (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute Shannon entropy $H(P)$:**

$$
H(P) = -\sum_{x} P(x) \log_2 P(x)
$$

$$
= -[0.5 \log_2(0.5) + 0.25 \log_2(0.25) + 0.125 \log_2(0.125) + 0.125 \log_2(0.125)]
$$

$$
= -[0.5 \times (-1) + 0.25 \times (-2) + 0.125 \times (-3) + 0.125 \times (-3)]
$$

$$
= -[-0.5 - 0.5 - 0.375 - 0.375] = -[-1.75] = 1.75 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute perplexity (self-modeling case, $Q = P$):**

$$
PP = 2^{H(P)} = 2^{1.75} \approx 3.36
$$

**Step 3 — Compute cross-entropy (self-modeling case, $H(P, P) = H(P)$):**

$$
H(P, P) = -\sum_x P(x) \log_2 P(x) = 1.75 \text{ bits}
$$

**Comment (1 mark):** The perplexity of $\approx 3.36$ indicates the model is on average as uncertain as a random guesser over approximately 3 to 4 equally-likely options. Since the true distribution is non-uniform, the perplexity is *less* than the vocabulary size $V = 4$, demonstrating that the model exploits the non-uniformity of word frequencies.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Alternative]**
*(a)* Explain the concept of **Shannon entropy**. Show mathematically that for a discrete random variable with $N$ equally-likely outcomes, the entropy is maximized and equal to $\log_2 N$. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A bigram language model gives the following conditional probabilities for a test sentence $W = $ "I love NLP I":

$$
P(\text{love} \mid \text{I}) = 0.8, \quad P(\text{NLP} \mid \text{love}) = 0.5, \quad P(\text{I} \mid \text{NLP}) = 0.4
$$

Assuming $P(\text{I} \mid \langle s \rangle) = 1$, compute the **perplexity** of the model on $W$ and verify it using the **cross-entropy** route. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer for (a):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Shannon entropy of a discrete random variable $X$ with probability mass function $P(x)$ is the expected value of the self-information:

$$
H(X) = -\sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} P(x) \log_2 P(x)
$$

It quantifies the average information (in bits) required to describe the outcome of $X$.

**Derivation of Maximum Entropy (5 marks):** We use the method of Lagrange multipliers to maximize $H(P) = -\sum P(x)\log_2 P(x)$ subject to the constraints $\sum_x P(x) = 1$ and $P(x) \geq 0$.

**Step 1 — Set up the Lagrangian:**

$$
\mathcal{L}(P, \lambda) = -\sum_x P(x) \log_2 P(x) - \lambda \left[ \sum_x P(x) - 1 \right]
$$

Converting $\log_2$ to $\ln$ via the change of base: $\log_2 P = \ln P / \ln 2$.

$$
\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{\ln 2} \sum_x P(x) \ln P(x) - \lambda \left[ \sum_x P(x) - 1 \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Take the partial derivative w.r.t. $P(x)$ and set to zero:**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial P(x)} = -\frac{1}{\ln 2} \left[ \ln P(x) + 1 \right] - \lambda = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $P(x)$:**

$$
\ln P(x) = -1 - \lambda \ln 2
$$

Since the right side is constant for all $x$, $P(x) = c$ for some constant $c$:

$$
P(x) = e^{-1 - \lambda \ln 2}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the normalization constraint $\sum_x P(x) = 1$:**

$$
N \cdot c = 1 \implies c = \frac{1}{N}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back to obtain maximum entropy:**

$$
H_{\max} = -\sum_{x=1}^{N} \frac{1}{N} \log_2 \frac{1}{N} = \log_2 N
$$

This confirms that the **uniform distribution over $N$ outcomes** maximizes entropy, and its perplexity is exactly $N$.

**Model Answer for (b):**

**Step 1 — Write the sequence and length:**

The sequence is $W = $ ("I", "love", "NLP", "I") with $N = 4$ tokens.

**Step 2 — Compute the joint probability via the chain rule:**

$$
P(W) = P(\text{I} \mid \langle s \rangle) \cdot P(\text{love} \mid \text{I}) \cdot P(\text{NLP} \mid \text{love}) \cdot P(\text{I} \mid \text{NLP})
$$

$$
= 1 \times 0.8 \times 0.5 \times 0.4 = 0.16
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the perplexity formula:**

$$
PP_M(W) = (0.16)^{-1/4} = (1/0.16)^{1/4} = (6.25)^{1/4}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerical value:**

$$
(6.25)^{1/4} = (6.25)^{0.25} \approx 1.581
$$

**Step 5 — Verify via the cross-entropy route:**

$$
H(P, Q) = -\frac{1}{4}\left[ \log_2(1) + \log_2(0.8) + \log_2(0.5) + \log_2(0.4) \right]
$$

$$
= -\frac{1}{4}\left[ 0 + (-0.3219) + (-1) + (-1.3219) \right]
$$

$$
= -\frac{1}{4}\left[ -2.6438 \right] = 0.6610 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 6 — Exponentiate to recover perplexity:**

$$
PP_M(W) = 2^{0.6610} \approx 1.581
$$

Both methods agree, confirming the perplexity–entropy relation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Logarithm base confusion (–2 marks):** Students often write $\log_2 N$ as $\ln N$ or vice versa. KTU expects explicit base specification.
> 2. **Missing exponentiation step (–3 marks):** In the cross-entropy route, students sometimes report cross-entropy as the "perplexity" directly. Perplexity is always the **exponentiated** form.
> 3. **Forgetting the chain rule decomposition (–2 marks):** When asked to compute perplexity for a sequence, students must decompose via the chain rule before applying logarithms.
> 4. **Zero-probability tokens:** If a test token is assigned zero probability by the model, the perplexity becomes infinite. Always state this assumption.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Perplexity is a dimensionless, exponentiated form of cross-entropy** with the convention $PP = 2^{H(P, Q)}$ (bits) or $PP = e^{H(P, Q)}$ (nats).
- **Shannon entropy** measures intrinsic average information; **cross-entropy** measures the bits required when using a model $Q$ for a true distribution $P$; **perplexity** translates this into "effective number of choices".
- **Lower perplexity = better language model.** A perfect model achieves $PP = 1$; a uniform random baseline over vocabulary $V$ achieves $PP = V$.
- **The dual equivalence:** $PP = 2^{\text{ANLL}} = 2^{H(P, Q)}$ — these are the *three faces* of the same quantity and students must be able to switch between them fluently.
- **KL Divergence connection:** $D_{KL}(P \mid\mid Q) = H(P, Q) - H(P)$ — the "excess bits" of using a model that is not the true distribution.
- **For uniform distributions:** $H = \log_2 N$ and $PP = N$ (the maximum possible perplexity for that vocabulary).
- **For deterministic distributions:** $H = 0$ and $PP = 1$ (zero confusion).
- **For peaked distributions** (e.g., Zipfian word distributions): $PP$ lies strictly between $1$ and $V$, indicating the model has learned structure.
- **Numerical safety:** Perplexity computation must guard against $\log(0)$; KTU examiners reward explicit mention of smoothing techniques (Laplace, Kneser-Ney) when applicable.
- **In neural LMs:** The training loss (cross-entropy) directly minimizes perplexity — they are the same optimization, just expressed differently.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Naive Bayes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT741 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Naive Bayes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Naive Bayes** is a family of **probabilistic supervised machine learning classifiers** built upon the application of **Bayes' Theorem** with a strong (often unrealistic) **independence assumption** between every pair of features given the value of the class variable.

For a feature vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ representing an instance to be classified into one of $K$ possible classes $C_k$, the Naive Bayes classifier assigns the class label $\hat{y}$ according to the **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) estimation rule**:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k \in \{1, \ldots, K\}} P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x})$$

Applying Bayes' Theorem and the **conditional independence assumption**, this simplifies to:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k} P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

The term "**Naive**" reflects the assumption that all features $x_i$ contribute **independently** to the probability of the class, ignoring any correlation between them. The "**Bayes**" part refers to the foundational work of Reverend **Thomas Bayes (1701–1761)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuitive Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Village Doctor**
> Imagine a doctor in a village trying to diagnose whether a patient has **Flu** ($C_1$) or **Common Cold** ($C_2$). The doctor observes three symptoms: $x_1 =$ fever, $x_2 =$ runny nose, $x_3 =$ body ache.
>
> - The doctor **doesn't** sit down and derive the complex joint probability distribution of all three symptoms occurring together. Instead, he/she estimates:
>   - "What is the prior probability of a patient having Flu today?" → $P(\text{Flu})$
>   - "Given Flu, how likely is fever alone?" → $P(\text{fever} \mid \text{Flu})$
>   - "Given Flu, how likely is runny nose alone?" → $P(\text{runny nose} \mid \text{Flu})$
>   - "Given Flu, how likely is body ache alone?" → $P(\text{body ache} \mid \text{Flu})$
> - He/she **naively** multiplies them all up and picks the class with the higher product.
> - This is *exactly* what Naive Bayes does — it treats every feature as an independent witness that "votes" for a class, and tallies the votes.

## 1.3 Why Naive Bayes Matters in NLP

In **Natural Language Processing**, Naive Bayes is the **classical baseline** for:
- **Spam filtering** (Ham vs. Spam)
- **Sentiment analysis** (Positive vs. Negative)
- **Document categorization** (Sports, Politics, Tech, …)
- **Author identification** and **language detection**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Despite the "naive" assumption being violated in real text (words are NOT independent — e.g., "New" and "York" are correlated), Naive Bayes remains **surprisingly competitive** in practice and serves as a benchmark against which modern models are compared.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Prior** $P(C_k)$ | Probability of class $C_k$ *before* observing any evidence |
| **Likelihood** $P(x_i \mid C_k)$ | Probability of feature $x_i$ given that class $C_k$ is true |
| **Posterior** $P(C_k \mid x)$ | Updated probability of class $C_k$ *after* observing evidence $x$ |
| **Evidence** $P(x)$ | Total probability of observing the evidence (normalizing constant) |
| **MAP Estimate** | Class that maximizes the posterior — the predicted output |
| **Class-Conditional Independence** | $P(x_i, x_j \mid C_k) = P(x_i \mid C_k) P(x_j \mid C_k)$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bayes' Theorem Foundation

Every Naive Bayes classifier is rooted in **Bayes' Theorem**, which mathematically describes how to update beliefs in light of new evidence:

$$P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) \cdot P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

The four components are:
- **$P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x})$** — Posterior: the thing we want.
- **$P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k)$** — Likelihood: how probable our evidence is under hypothesis $C_k$.
- **$P(C_k)$** — Prior: our initial belief about $C_k$ before seeing $\mathbf{x}$.
- **$P(\mathbf{x})$** — Evidence: a normalizing constant ensuring the posterior is a valid probability distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why we can drop $P(\mathbf{x})$:** Since $P(\mathbf{x})$ does **not** depend on the class $C_k$, it is a constant for a given input. The $\arg\max$ operator is invariant to multiplicative constants, so we simplify:
>
> $$P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto P(C_k) \cdot P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k)$$

## 2.2 The "Naive" Independence Assumption

Computing $P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \mid C_k)$ directly is **intractable** for high-dimensional data (it requires estimating a joint distribution over all feature combinations).

By the **chain rule of probability**:

$$P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \mid C_k) = P(x_1 \mid C_k) P(x_2 \mid x_1, C_k) \cdots P(x_n \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{n-1}, C_k)$$

The **naive assumption** says: *given the class, all features are mutually independent*. Therefore:

$$P(x_i \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{i-1}, C_k) = P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

This collapses the intractable joint distribution into a simple product of univariate likelihoods:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

The complete Naive Bayes decision rule is therefore:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k} \;\; P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | When Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **F1** | $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$ | Bayes' Theorem | Foundational identity |
| **F2** | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_k P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$ | Naive Bayes classifier | Classification decision |
| **F3** | $\log \hat{y} = \arg\max_k \left[\log P(C_k) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \mid C_k)\right]$ | Log-space formulation | Prevents numerical underflow |
| **F4** | $\hat{P}(C_k) = \dfrac{N_k}{N}$ | MLE for class prior | Frequency of class in training data |
| **F5** | $\hat{P}(w_i \mid C_k) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w_i, C_k)}{\sum_{w \in V} \text{count}(w, C_k)}$ | MLE for word likelihood (Multinomial) | Bag-of-words text classification |
| **F6** | $\hat{P}(w_i \mid C_k) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w_i, C_k) + 1}{\sum_{w \in V} \text{count}(w, C_k) + \vert V \vert}$ | **Laplace (Additive) Smoothing** | Avoids zero-probability problem |
| **F7** | $P(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) P(C_k)$ | Law of Total Probability (denominator) | Computing the full posterior |
| **F8** | $\text{Bernoulli: } P(x_i \mid C_k) = p_i^{x_i} (1-p_i)^{1-x_i}$ | Bernoulli NB for binary features | Document presence/absence |
| **F9** | $\text{Gaussian: } P(x_i \mid C_k) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_k^2}} \exp\left(-\dfrac{(x_i - \mu_k)^2}{2\sigma_k^2}\right)$ | Gaussian NB for continuous features | Non-text continuous data |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zero-Frequency Problem:** If a feature $w_i$ never appears in the training data for class $C_k$, then $P(w_i \mid C_k) = 0$. This **zero annihilates the entire product** for the class, making it impossible to recover. **Laplace smoothing (F6)** is the standard KTU-required fix.

## 2.4 Variants of Naive Bayes (KTU Syllabus Map)

| Variant | Feature Type | Typical NLP Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Multinomial NB** | Integer word counts | Document classification, spam filtering |
| **Bernoulli NB** | Binary (0/1) word occurrence | Short text, sentiment analysis |
| **Gaussian NB** | Continuous real values | Non-text features (e.g., word embeddings) |
| **Complement NB** | Counts (imbalanced) | Text classification with skewed classes |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

In industry, Naive Bayes is favored for:
- **Tiny memory footprint** — only stores per-class word counts.
- **Linear training time** $O(N \cdot d)$ where $N$ is dataset size and $d$ is feature dimensionality.
- **Incremental learning** — new documents can update counts without retraining.
- **Real-time systems** — sub-millisecond inference for spam gateways.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Conditional Probability to Bayes' Theorem

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of conditional probability:**

$$P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(C_k \cap \mathbf{x})}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

*Explanation:* Conditional probability is the joint probability of the event *and* the condition, divided by the probability of the condition alone.

**Step 2 — Symmetrically, write the conditional probability the other way:**

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = \frac{P(C_k \cap \mathbf{x})}{P(C_k)}$$

**Step 3 — Solve both for the joint probability $P(C_k \cap \mathbf{x})$:**

$$P(C_k \cap \mathbf{x}) = P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) \, P(\mathbf{x})$$

$$P(C_k \cap \mathbf{x}) = P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) \, P(C_k)$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two expressions:**

$$P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) \, P(\mathbf{x}) = P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) \, P(C_k)$$

**Step 5 — Divide both sides by $P(\mathbf{x})$ to obtain Bayes' Theorem:**

$$P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) \, P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

$$\boxed{\; P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) \cdot P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})} \;}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Naive Bayes Decision Rule

**Step 1 — Begin with the Bayes' Theorem expression for the posterior:**

$$P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(C_k) \, P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \mid C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the chain rule to expand the joint likelihood:**

$$P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \mid C_k) = P(x_1 \mid C_k) \, P(x_2 \mid x_1, C_k) \, P(x_3 \mid x_1, x_2, C_k) \cdots P(x_n \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{n-1}, C_k)$$

**Step 3 — Impose the conditional independence assumption (the "naive" step):**

$$P(x_i \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{i-1}, C_k) = P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back; the joint likelihood collapses to a product:**

$$P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \mid C_k) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

**Step 5 — Drop the denominator $P(\mathbf{x})$ (constant w.r.t. $C_k$):**

$$P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

**Step 6 — Take the argmax to obtain the classification rule:**

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k \in \{1, \ldots, K\}} P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

## 3.3 Worked Example — Spoken-Form Numerical Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** We have a tiny training corpus with vocabulary $V = \{\text{“free”, “offer”, “meeting”, “schedule”}\}$. Two classes: $C_1 =$ **Spam**, $C_2 =$ **Ham**. We want to classify the new document $D =$ "**free offer**".

**Training Statistics (counts):**

| Class | Total Docs | "free" | "offer" | "meeting" | "schedule" | Sum of word counts |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Spam ($C_1$) | 4 | 8 | 6 | 2 | 1 | 17 |
| Ham ($C_2$) | 6 | 1 | 1 | 5 | 4 | 11 |

Total documents $N = 10$. Vocabulary size $\vert V \vert = 4$.

**Step 1 — Compute priors (Formula F4):**

$$P(C_1) = \frac{4}{10} = 0.4$$

$$P(C_2) = \frac{6}{10} = 0.6$$

**Step 2 — Compute likelihoods with Laplace Smoothing (Formula F6):**

$$\hat{P}(\text{"free"} \mid C_1) = \frac{8 + 1}{17 + 4} = \frac{9}{21} \approx 0.4286$$

$$\hat{P}(\text{"offer"} \mid C_1) = \frac{6 + 1}{17 + 4} = \frac{7}{21} \approx 0.3333$$

$$\hat{P}(\text{"free"} \mid C_2) = \frac{1 + 1}{11 + 4} = \frac{2}{15} \approx 0.1333$$

$$\hat{P}(\text{"offer"} \mid C_2) = \frac{1 + 1}{11 + 4} = \frac{2}{15} \approx 0.1333$$

**Step 3 — Compute the unnormalized posteriors:**

$$P(C_1) \cdot P(\text{"free"} \mid C_1) \cdot P(\text{"offer"} \mid C_1) = 0.4 \times 0.4286 \times 0.3333$$

$$= 0.4 \times 0.1429 \approx 0.05714$$

$$P(C_2) \cdot P(\text{"free"} \mid C_2) \cdot P(\text{"offer"} \mid C_2) = 0.6 \times 0.1333 \times 0.1333$$

$$= 0.6 \times 0.01778 \approx 0.01067$$

**Step 4 — Apply argmax:**

$$0.05714 \;(\text{Spam}) \;>\; 0.01067 \;(\text{Ham})$$

**Decision:** The document "free offer" is classified as **Spam**. ✓

**Step 5 — Optional: Normalize to get true probabilities (Formula F7):**

$$P(C_1 \mid D) = \frac{0.05714}{0.05714 + 0.01067} = \frac{0.05714}{0.06781} \approx 0.8427$$

$$P(C_2 \mid D) = \frac{0.01067}{0.06781} \approx 0.1573$$

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Input Doc | Words Present | Spam Score | Ham Score | Prediction |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| "free offer" | free, offer | 0.0571 | 0.0107 | **Spam** |
| "meeting schedule" | meeting, schedule | 0.0026 | 0.0727 | **Ham** |
| "free meeting" | free, meeting | 0.0036 | 0.0267 | **Ham** |
| "offer" | offer | 0.1333 | 0.0800 | **Spam** |

This matrix illustrates the classifier's **discriminative power** — its ability to invert the prediction when class-discriminative words appear.

## 3.5 Log-Space Refinement (Avoiding Underflow)

For long documents, multiplying many small probabilities underflows to zero. Take the **logarithm** (which preserves argmax):

$$\log \hat{y} = \arg\max_k \left[\log P(C_k) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \mid C_k)\right]$$

This converts the product into a **sum** of log-probabilities, which is numerically stable.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Naive Bayes Training & Inference Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Text Documents] --> B[Tokenization and Lowercasing]
    B --> C[Stopword Removal and Stemming]
    C --> D[Vocabulary Construction V]
    D --> E[Word Count Matrix per Class]
    E --> F1[Compute Class Priors P Ck]
    E --> F2[Compute Likelihoods P wi given Ck]
    F2 --> G[Apply Laplace Smoothing]
    G --> H[Store Model: Priors + Likelihood Table]
    
    I[New Test Document] --> J[Tokenize and Preprocess]
    J --> K[Look up P wi given Ck for each word]
    F1 --> L[Multiply Prior by Product of Likelihoods]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Take Argmax over all Classes]
    M --> N[Predicted Class Label]
    
    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style I fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style N fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style H fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
```

**Explanation of the flow:** The upper branch is the **offline training phase** that builds the probabilistic model. The lower branch is the **online inference phase** that classifies new documents by combining priors and per-word likelihoods and selecting the maximum.

## 4.2 Bayes' Theorem Computational Graph

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Prior P Ck] --> M[Posterior P Ck given x]
    B[Likelihood P x given Ck] --> M
    C[Evidence P x] --> M
    M --> D[argmax over k]
    D --> E[Predicted Class y hat]
    
    style A fill:#bbdefb
    style B fill:#ffe0b2
    style C fill:#c5e1a5
    style M fill:#f8bbd0
    style E fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
```

**Interpretation:** The **Prior** (our belief before seeing data), **Likelihood** (how the data looks under each hypothesis), and **Evidence** (the data's overall probability) combine to form the **Posterior**. The argmax operator then selects the best class.

## 4.3 The Naive Independence Assumption (Conceptual Graph)

```mermaid
graph TB
    C[Class Ck] --> W1[Word w1]
    C --> W2[Word w2]
    C --> W3[Word w3]
    C --> W4[Word wn]
    
    W1 -.independent.-> W2
    W1 -.independent.-> W3
    W2 -.independent.-> W3
    W2 -.independent.-> W4
    W3 -.independent.-> W4
    
    style C fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:3px
    style W1 fill:#c5cae9
    style W2 fill:#c5cae9
    style W3 fill:#c5cae9
    style W4 fill:#c5cae9
```

**Interpretation:** Each word $w_i$ depends *only* on the class $C_k$. The dashed lines show that **words are conditionally independent given the class** — this is the central simplifying assumption that makes Naive Bayes tractable.

## 4.4 Architecture Summary

| Phase | Component | Output |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Preprocessing** | Tokenize, lowercase, stopword removal | Token list |
| **Feature Extraction** | Build vocabulary $V$, count words | Count vectors |
| **Training** | Compute priors and likelihoods | Probability tables |
| **Smoothing** | Apply Laplace (additive) correction | Non-zero likelihoods |
| **Inference** | Multiply prior × product of likelihoods | Class scores |
| **Decision** | argmax of class scores | Predicted label |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

**Q1. State and explain Bayes' Theorem in the context of text classification.**
*(3 Marks — KTU University Exam Style)*

**Model Answer:**
Bayes' Theorem expresses the posterior probability of a class $C_k$ given a feature vector $\mathbf{x}$ in terms of the prior, likelihood, and evidence:
