$$P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) \cdot P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

In text classification, $C_k$ is a document category (e.g., Spam/Ham), $\mathbf{x}$ is the bag-of-words representation, $P(C_k)$ is the prior probability of the class in the training corpus, $P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k)$ is the probability of generating those words given the class, and $P(\mathbf{x})$ is the normalizing evidence term. The theorem allows us to **invert** $P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k)$ into the desired $P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x})$.

> *(Examiner Note: Award full 3 marks for the formula + clear identification of all four terms + a one-line interpretation.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

**Q2. What is the "naive" assumption in the Naive Bayes classifier? Why is it called "naive"?**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The "naive" assumption is that **all features (words) are conditionally independent of each other given the class label**. Mathematically:

$$P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \mid C_k) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

It is called "naive" because in real-world text, this assumption is **almost always violated** — words have strong correlations (e.g., "New York", "machine learning", "United States"). However, despite this unrealistic assumption, the classifier often produces **surprisingly accurate results**, which makes it a powerful yet simple baseline.

> *(Examiner Note: Award 1.5 marks for stating the assumption mathematically and 1.5 marks for explaining why it's called "naive".)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Laplace smoothing term in the denominator.** Students frequently write $\frac{\text{count}+1}{\text{total}}$ instead of $\frac{\text{count}+1}{\text{total}+\vert V \vert}$ — this is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Not converting the product to log-space** for long documents. Examiners may give a 14-mark question that includes a long document — failing to mention log-space loses **2 marks**.
> 3. **Not normalizing the final probabilities** when asked to give $P(C_k \mid D)$. The raw product is only **proportional** to the posterior; you must divide by $P(D)$ to get a true probability.
> 4. **Mixing up priors and likelihoods.** Priors are computed from **document counts**; likelihoods are computed from **word counts** — a common confusion.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Derive the Naive Bayes classification rule from first principles using Bayes' Theorem and the conditional independence assumption. Clearly state the mathematical formulation and explain each term. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

We start with Bayes' Theorem:

$$P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

For a document represented by words $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$, the joint likelihood is expanded using the chain rule:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = P(x_1 \mid C_k) \cdot P(x_2 \mid x_1, C_k) \cdot P(x_3 \mid x_1, x_2, C_k) \cdots P(x_n \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{n-1}, C_k)$$

Imposing the **conditional independence assumption** (the "naive" step):

$$P(x_i \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{i-1}, C_k) = P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

The likelihood collapses to:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

Substituting back and noting that $P(\mathbf{x})$ is constant w.r.t. $C_k$:

$$P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

The classification rule (MAP estimate) becomes:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k} P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

**Term Explanations:**
- $P(C_k)$: Prior — fraction of training documents in class $C_k$.
- $P(x_i \mid C_k)$: Likelihood — probability of word $x_i$ appearing in class $C_k$.
- $\prod$: Product over all words in the document.
- $\arg\max$: Returns the class $C_k$ that yields the highest score.

> *(Examiner Note: 3 marks for the derivation steps, 2 marks for the final formula, 2 marks for the term explanations.)*

---

**(b)** Consider a training set with 6 documents: 3 belong to class **Sports** and 3 to class **Politics**. The vocabulary is $V = \{$ "**goal**", "**match**", "**election**", "**policy**" $\}$. The word count tables are:

| Class | "goal" | "match" | "election" | "policy" | Total Words |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Sports | 4 | 5 | 0 | 1 | 10 |
| Politics | 1 | 0 | 6 | 3 | 10 |

Classify the document $D = $ "**goal election**" using Multinomial Naive Bayes with Laplace smoothing. Show all calculations. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Priors:**

$$P(\text{Sports}) = \frac{3}{6} = 0.5$$

$$P(\text{Politics}) = \frac{3}{6} = 0.5$$

**Step 2 — Likelihoods with Laplace Smoothing ($\vert V \vert = 4$):**

$$P(\text{goal} \mid \text{Sports}) = \frac{4 + 1}{10 + 4} = \frac{5}{14} \approx 0.3571$$

$$P(\text{election} \mid \text{Sports}) = \frac{0 + 1}{10 + 4} = \frac{1}{14} \approx 0.0714$$

$$P(\text{goal} \mid \text{Politics}) = \frac{1 + 1}{10 + 4} = \frac{2}{14} \approx 0.1429$$

$$P(\text{election} \mid \text{Politics}) = \frac{6 + 1}{10 + 4} = \frac{7}{14} = 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Unnormalized Posterior for Sports:**

$$P(\text{Sports} \mid D) \propto 0.5 \times 0.3571 \times 0.0714 = 0.5 \times 0.0255 \approx 0.01276$$

**Step 4 — Unnormalized Posterior for Politics:**

$$P(\text{Politics} \mid D) \propto 0.5 \times 0.1429 \times 0.5 = 0.5 \times 0.07143 \approx 0.03571$$

**Step 5 — Argmax Decision:**

$$0.03571 \;(\text{Politics}) \;>\; 0.01276 \;(\text{Sports})$$

$$\boxed{\; \hat{y} = \text{Politics} \;}$$

**Step 6 — Normalized probabilities (optional but recommended):**

$$P(\text{Politics} \mid D) = \frac{0.03571}{0.03571 + 0.01276} = \frac{0.03571}{0.04847} \approx 0.7368$$

$$P(\text{Sports} \mid D) \approx 0.2632$$

> *(Examiner Note: 1 mark for priors, 2 marks for smoothed likelihoods, 2 marks for the posterior products, 1 mark for argmax, 1 mark for correct final answer.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **zero-frequency problem** in Naive Bayes. How is it solved using **Laplace (additive) smoothing**? Write the mathematical formulation and discuss its effect on the probability estimates. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**The Zero-Frequency Problem:**
In Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE), the word probability is computed as:

$$\hat{P}(w_i \mid C_k) = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, C_k)}{\sum_{w \in V} \text{count}(w, C_k)}$$

If a word $w_i$ never appears in any training document of class $C_k$, then $\text{count}(w_i, C_k) = 0$, giving $P(w_i \mid C_k) = 0$. Since the Naive Bayes decision rule uses a **product** of likelihoods, this single zero **annihilates the entire product** for that class, making it impossible for the classifier to ever choose $C_k$ — no matter how strong the other evidence is.

**Laplace Smoothing Solution:**
Add a pseudo-count of 1 to every word count. The smoothed estimator becomes:

$$\hat{P}(w_i \mid C_k) = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, C_k) + 1}{\sum_{w \in V} \text{count}(w, C_k) + \vert V \vert}$$

where $\vert V \vert$ is the vocabulary size. This guarantees that **no probability is ever zero**.

**Generalized Form (Add-$\alpha$ Smoothing):**

$$\hat{P}(w_i \mid C_k) = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, C_k) + \alpha}{\sum_{w \in V} \text{count}(w, C_k) + \alpha \vert V \vert}$$

**Effect on Probability Estimates:**
1. **Eliminates zeros** — every word has a small but non-zero probability.
2. **Reduces high probabilities** — frequent words see their estimates slightly lowered.
3. **Slight bias, large variance reduction** — the bias introduced is usually small compared to the gain in generalization.
4. **Critical for unseen words** at test time — a new word in the test document is not catastrophic.

> *(Examiner Note: 2 marks for identifying the problem, 3 marks for the formula and explanation, 2 marks for the effect on estimates.)*

---

**(b)** A movie review dataset contains 5 reviews labelled **Positive** and 3 labelled **Negative**. The word count tables are:

| Class | "good" | "bad" | "boring" | "amazing" | Total |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Positive | 6 | 1 | 0 | 4 | 11 |
| Negative | 0 | 4 | 3 | 1 | 8 |

Classify the review $R = $ "**good amazing**" using **Multinomial Naive Bayes with Laplace smoothing** ($\alpha = 1$). Use log-probabilities to avoid underflow. Show all calculations. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Priors (Formula F4):**

$$P(\text{Pos}) = \frac{5}{8} = 0.625$$

$$P(\text{Neg}) = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375$$

**Step 2 — Smoothed Likelihoods ($\vert V \vert = 4$):**

$$P(\text{good} \mid \text{Pos}) = \frac{6 + 1}{11 + 4} = \frac{7}{15} \approx 0.4667$$

$$P(\text{amazing} \mid \text{Pos}) = \frac{4 + 1}{11 + 4} = \frac{5}{15} \approx 0.3333$$

$$P(\text{good} \mid \text{Neg}) = \frac{0 + 1}{8 + 4} = \frac{1}{12} \approx 0.0833$$

$$P(\text{amazing} \mid \text{Neg}) = \frac{1 + 1}{8 + 4} = \frac{2}{12} \approx 0.1667$$

**Step 3 — Log-Space Posterior for Positive:**

$$\log P(\text{Pos} \mid R) = \log 0.625 + \log 0.4667 + \log 0.3333$$

$$= -0.4700 + (-0.7608) + (-1.0986) = -2.3294$$

**Step 4 — Log-Space Posterior for Negative:**

$$\log P(\text{Neg} \mid R) = \log 0.375 + \log 0.0833 + \log 0.1667$$

$$= -0.9808 + (-2.4849) + (-1.7918) = -5.2575$$

**Step 5 — Argmax Decision:**

$$-2.3294 \;(\text{Positive}) \;>\; -5.2575 \;(\text{Negative})$$

$$\boxed{\; \hat{y} = \text{Positive} \;}$$

**Step 6 — Verification via direct product (for sanity check):**

$$P(\text{Pos}) \times P(\text{good}\mid\text{Pos}) \times P(\text{amazing}\mid\text{Pos}) = 0.625 \times 0.4667 \times 0.3333 \approx 0.0972$$

$$P(\text{Neg}) \times P(\text{good}\mid\text{Neg}) \times P(\text{amazing}\mid\text{Neg}) = 0.375 \times 0.0833 \times 0.1667 \approx 0.0052$$

The same decision: **Positive**. ✓

> *(Examiner Note: 1 mark for priors, 2 marks for smoothed likelihoods, 2 marks for log-space computation, 1 mark for argmax, 1 mark for final classification.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Naive Bayes**

- ✅ **Bayes' Theorem:** $P(C_k \mid x) = \dfrac{P(x \mid C_k) P(C_k)}{P(x)}$ — know all four terms by name.
- ✅ **Naive Assumption:** Features are **conditionally independent given the class** — words "know nothing" of each other once the class is known.
- ✅ **Decision Rule:** $\hat{y} = \arg\max_k P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$ — pick the class with the **highest product**.
- ✅ **Prior = Document Frequency**, **Likelihood = Word Frequency** — never mix them up.
- ✅ **Laplace Smoothing** is **mandatory** for unseen words: add 1 to the numerator, $\vert V \vert$ to the denominator.
- ✅ **Log-Space Trick:** Use $\log$ to convert product → sum for numerical stability on long documents.
- ✅ **Multinomial NB** uses word **counts**; **Bernoulli NB** uses word **presence/absence (binary)**.
- ✅ **Gaussian NB** applies when features are **continuous** (e.g., word embedding dimensions).
- ✅ The denominator $P(\mathbf{x})$ is **constant** for a given input — drop it during argmax.
- ✅ Despite the "naive" name, Naive Bayes is **fast, memory-efficient, and a strong baseline** for many NLP tasks including spam filtering, sentiment analysis, and document categorization.
- ✅ **Common pitfalls:** zero-frequency, forgetting smoothing denominator, mixing priors with likelihoods, not normalizing for true probabilities.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Text Classification and Sentiment: Naive Bayes Classifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT741 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Text Classification and Sentiment: Naive Bayes Classifiers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Text Classification and Sentiment: Naive Bayes Classifiers

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Text Classification** is a fundamental supervised learning task in Natural Language Processing (NLP) in which a document $d$ belonging to a fixed set of classes $C = \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_k\}$ is assigned a class label $\hat{c} \in C$ based on the linguistic features extracted from its textual content.

**Naive Bayes Classifier (NBC)** is a probabilistic generative classifier derived from **Bayes' Theorem** that computes the posterior probability of a class given a document, under the simplifying **conditional independence assumption** that every feature (typically a word/token) contributes independently to the class likelihood. Formally, for a document $d$ with feature vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$:

$$P(c \mid d) = \frac{P(c) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)}{P(d)}$$

The classifier selects the class with the **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** probability:

$$\hat{c} = \underset{c \in C}{\arg\max}\; P(c \mid d)$$

**Sentiment Analysis** (a sub-field of text classification) is the computational study of opinions, sentiments, and emotions expressed in text, often framed as a binary (positive/negative) or multi-class (positive, negative, neutral) classification problem.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spam Detective" Intuition

Imagine you are a **postmaster in 1995** trying to decide whether each incoming letter is *spam* or *legitimate* (ham). You have a gut feeling based on two clues: (1) does it contain words like *"free", "offer", "winner"*? (2) does it contain words like *"meeting", "schedule", "project"*?

You don't analyze the *grammar* or *style* — you simply **count suspicious keywords** and weigh them. If a letter has many spam words, you mark it spam. If it has many ham words, you let it through.

**Naive Bayes works exactly this way!**
- It treats each word as an **independent clue**.
- It learns from past labeled letters (training set) how often each word appears in spam vs. ham.
- For a new letter, it **multiplies the probabilities** of seeing each word under each class and picks the class with the highest combined score.
- The "naive" part is the assumption that the occurrence of "free" doesn't depend on "winner" — even though in real language, these words often co-occur.

---

### 1.3 Why Naive Bayes for NLP? (Syllabus Highlight)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this classifier dominates introductory NLP:**
> - **Training is O(N · |V|)** — extremely fast and scalable.
> - Works remarkably well on **bag-of-words** and **bag-of-n-grams** representations.
> - Requires **less training data** than discriminative models like SVMs.
> - Naturally handles **high-dimensional sparse** text data.
> - Forms the **probabilistic backbone** of classical spam filters, sentiment analyzers, and document categorizers.

**Historical Context**: Naive Bayes variants were the workhorses of text classification in the 1990s–2000s (spam filtering systems, news categorization at Reuters, early sentiment systems). They remain in the KTU 2024 syllabus as the **conceptual foundation** of probabilistic NLP before students progress to neural classifiers.

---

### 1.4 Types of Naive Bayes Models (Used in Text Classification)

| Variant | Feature Assumption | Typical NLP Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Multinomial Naive Bayes (MNB)** | Word **counts** (multinomial distribution) | Document classification, spam filtering |
| **Bernoulli Naive Bayes (BNB)** | Word **presence/absence** (binary) | Short-text classification, sentiment |
| **Gaussian Naive Bayes (GNB)** | Continuous features (Gaussian) | Rarely used in pure NLP; used with TF-IDF or embeddings |
| **Complement Naive Bayes (CNB)** | Adapted from MNB for imbalanced data | Text classification with class imbalance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the **KTU 2024 ESE**, the **Multinomial Naive Bayes** formulation is the **default** expected in derivations, numerical examples, and problem statements, as it aligns with the bag-of-words model central to Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Probabilistic Foundation — Bayes' Theorem

The entire Naive Bayes framework rests on **Thomas Bayes' Theorem (1763)**, which inverts conditional probabilities:

$$P(c \mid d) = \frac{P(d \mid c) \cdot P(c)}{P(d)}$$

Where:
- $P(c \mid d)$ = **Posterior** — probability of class $c$ given that we observed document $d$.
- $P(d \mid c)$ = **Likelihood** — probability of generating document $d$ if we *assume* the class is $c$.
- $P(c)$ = **Prior** — probability of class $c$ irrespective of any document (estimated from training set class frequencies).
- $P(d)$ = **Evidence** — probability of the document itself; acts as a normalizing constant since $d$ is fixed during classification.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" — Engineering Utility:** Bayes' Theorem lets us *reason backward* — from observed evidence (words in a document) to a hidden cause (the class). This is the central act of inference in NLP.

---

## 2.2 The Naive Bayes Assumption — "Naive" Means Independent

For a document $d$ represented as a sequence of word features $(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$, the true joint likelihood is intractable:

$$P(d \mid c) = P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \mid c)$$

The **conditional independence assumption** (the "naive" part) declares that *every word's occurrence is statistically independent of every other word, given the class*:

$$P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$$

**Why is this "naive"?** In real text, words are highly correlated (e.g., "New" and "York" almost always co-occur; "ice" follows "cream"). Yet, **empirically**, the assumption produces surprisingly accurate classifiers because the model only needs the *ranking* of class probabilities to be correct — not the exact probabilities.

---

## 2.3 The Multinomial Naive Bayes Formulation (Step by Step)

### Step 1 — Document Representation
A document $d$ is represented as a **bag of words**: a vector of word counts $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{|V|})$ where $x_i$ is the count of word $w_i$ in $d$, and $V$ is the vocabulary.

### Step 2 — Class Prior
The prior is the relative frequency of class $c$ in the training corpus:

$$P(c) = \frac{N_c}{N}$$

Where $N_c$ is the number of training documents with class $c$, and $N$ is the total number of training documents.

### Step 3 — Word Likelihood (Maximum Likelihood Estimate)
For a word $w_i$ and class $c$:

$$\hat{P}(w_i \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, c)}{\sum_{w \in V} \text{count}(w, c)}$$

The numerator is the number of times word $w_i$ appears across *all* training documents of class $c$. The denominator is the **total word count** of class $c$ (i.e., the size of the class's word "bag").

### Step 4 — Document Likelihood (Naive Assumption)
$$P(d \mid c) = P(|d|) \cdot |d|! \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{|V|} \frac{\hat{P}(w_i \mid c)^{x_i}}{x_i!}$$

For classification, since $P(|d|)$ and $|d|!$ are independent of $c$, they are absorbed into the normalization:

$$P(d \mid c) \propto \prod_{i=1}^{|V|} \hat{P}(w_i \mid c)^{x_i}$$

### Step 5 — MAP Classification Rule

$$\boxed{\;\hat{c} = \underset{c \in C}{\arg\max}\; P(c) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{|V|} \hat{P}(w_i \mid c)^{x_i}\;}$$

---

## 2.4 Smoothing — Laplace (Add-One) Smoothing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Zero-Frequency Problem:** If a word $w_i$ never appears in training documents of class $c$, then $\hat{P}(w_i \mid c) = 0$. Since the product contains a zero, the *entire* posterior becomes zero, regardless of how strong the other evidence is. This is **catastrophic for unseen words** — which is the rule, not the exception, in real text.

**Laplace Smoothing (Add-One)** solves this by pretending every word was seen at least once:

$$\hat{P}(w_i \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, c) + 1}{\sum_{w \in V} \text{count}(w, c) + \vert V \vert}$$

**Generalized Add-$\alpha$ Smoothing:**

$$\hat{P}(w_i \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, c) + \alpha}{\sum_{w \in V} \text{count}(w, c) + \alpha \vert V \vert}$$

Where $\alpha \geq 0$. Common choices: $\alpha = 1$ (Laplace), $\alpha < 1$ (Lidstone), $\alpha = 0.01$ to $0.1$ in modern implementations.

---

## 2.5 Log-Space Computation (Avoiding Underflow)

For a long document $d$ with many word occurrences, the product $\prod P(w_i \mid c)^{x_i}$ underflows in floating-point arithmetic. We take the **monotonic logarithm** to convert products into sums:

$$\log P(c \mid d) \propto \log P(c) + \sum_{i=1}^{|V|} x_i \cdot \log \hat{P}(w_i \mid c)$$

The classification rule in log-space:

$$\boxed{\;\hat{c} = \underset{c \in C}{\arg\max}\;\left[\log P(c) + \sum_{i=1}^{|V|} x_i \cdot \log \hat{P}(w_i \mid c)\right]\;}$$

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P(c \mid d) = \dfrac{P(d \mid c) P(c)}{P(d)}$ | **Bayes' Theorem** — foundation of NBC |
| 2 | $\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} P(c) \prod_{i} P(x_i \mid c)$ | **MAP classification rule** (raw product form) |
| 3 | $\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} \left[\log P(c) + \sum_i x_i \log P(x_i \mid c)\right]$ | **MAP classification rule** (log-space, avoids underflow) |
| 4 | $P(c) = N_c / N$ | **Class prior** from training set frequencies |
| 5 | $\hat{P}(w_i \mid c) = \text{count}(w_i,c) \,/\, \sum_{w \in V} \text{count}(w, c)$ | **MLE of word likelihood** (no smoothing) |
| 6 | $\hat{P}(w_i \mid c) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w_i,c) + \alpha}{\sum_{w \in V} \text{count}(w,c) + \alpha \vert V \vert}$ | **Laplace / Lidstone smoothed** likelihood |
| 7 | $\log \hat{P}(w_i \mid c) = \log\!\big(\text{count}(w_i,c) + \alpha\big) - \log\!\big(\sum_w \text{count}(w,c) + \alpha \vert V \vert\big)$ | Log-likelihood for **fast log-space training** |
| 8 | $P(d \mid c) \propto \prod_i P(w_i \mid c)^{x_i}$ | Document likelihood under MNB (ignoring multinomial coefficient) |
| 9 | $H(c) = -\sum_c P(c) \log P(c)$ | Class entropy (sometimes appears in advanced NBC analysis) |
| 10 | $\text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | **Evaluation** of the trained classifier |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on notation:** In every KTU exam formula, $\vert V \vert$ denotes the vocabulary size (cardinality). Always write it as `\vert V \vert` in LaTeX to avoid markdown parsing errors.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Email spam filtering** (Gmail, Outlook — historically Naive Bayes-based).
- **News article categorization** (Reuters, AP, BBC classification — early benchmarks).
- **Sentiment analysis on product reviews** (Amazon, Yelp, IMDb reviews — 2-class or 3-class).
- **Author attribution and language identification**.
- **Medical text classification** (radiology reports, EHR note classification).
- **Multilingual text routing** in enterprise document management systems.

Naive Bayes is the **probabilistic stepping stone** in NLP: even after being superseded by deep learning, it remains the first classifier students must master because every concept — priors, likelihoods, MAP, smoothing, log-space — reappears in HMMs, CRFs, and Bayesian neural networks.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example

## 3.1 Worked Example: Binary Sentiment Classification with MNB

This is a **board-exam-style numerical**. Master this style; nearly every KTU numerical on Naive Bayes follows the same skeleton.

### 3.1.1 Training Corpus (Toy Dataset)

We have a small labeled corpus of movie reviews for a **binary sentiment classifier** (Positive = $c_+$ vs. Negative = $c_-$).

| # | Class | Review Text (Tokenized) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pos** | "great movie loved it" |
| 2 | **Pos** | "great plot great acting" |
| 3 | **Pos** | "loved the acting great film" |
| 4 | **Neg** | "boring plot hated it" |
| 5 | **Neg** | "boring movie hated acting" |
| 6 | **Neg** | "hated it boring film" |

We must **classify a new test review**: $d_{\text{test}} = $ "great movie" — is it positive or negative?

---

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Build the Vocabulary

$$V = \{\text{great, movie, loved, it, plot, acting, film, boring, hated}\}$$

$$\vert V \vert = 9$$

---

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Compute Class Priors

From the training set: 3 positive and 3 negative documents. Total $N = 6$.

$$P(c_+) = \frac{3}{6} = 0.5$$

$$P(c_-) = \frac{3}{6} = 0.5$$

---

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Compute Word Counts per Class

**Positive class word counts** (concatenating all positive docs):

| Word | Count in $c_+$ |
|---|---|
| great | 4 |
| movie | 1 |
| loved | 2 |
| it | 1 |
| plot | 1 |
| acting | 2 |
| film | 1 |
| boring | 0 |
| hated | 0 |
| **Total** | **12** |

**Negative class word counts:**

| Word | Count in $c_-$ |
|---|---|
| great | 0 |
| movie | 1 |
| loved | 0 |
| it | 2 |
| plot | 1 |
| acting | 1 |
| film | 1 |
| boring | 3 |
| hated | 3 |
| **Total** | **12** |

---

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Apply Laplace Smoothing ($\alpha = 1$)

Using the formula:

$$\hat{P}(w_i \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, c) + 1}{\sum_w \text{count}(w, c) + \vert V \vert} = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, c) + 1}{12 + 9} = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, c) + 1}{21}$$

**Smoothed likelihoods for $c_+$:**

| Word | $\text{count}+1$ | $\hat{P}(w \mid c_+)$ |
|---|---|---|
| great | $4+1=5$ | $5/21 \approx 0.2381$ |
| movie | $1+1=2$ | $2/21 \approx 0.0952$ |
| loved | $2+1=3$ | $3/21 \approx 0.1429$ |
| boring | $0+1=1$ | $1/21 \approx 0.0476$ |

**Smoothed likelihoods for $c_-$:**

| Word | $\text{count}+1$ | $\hat{P}(w \mid c_-)$ |
|---|---|---|
| great | $0+1=1$ | $1/21 \approx 0.0476$ |
| movie | $1+1=2$ | $2/21 \approx 0.0952$ |
| loved | $0+1=1$ | $1/21 \approx 0.0476$ |
| boring | $3+1=4$ | $4/21 \approx 0.1905$ |

---

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Score the Test Document $d_{\text{test}} = $ "great movie"

The test document vector is $\mathbf{x} = \{x_{\text{great}} = 1,\; x_{\text{movie}} = 1,\; \text{all other } x_i = 0\}$.

**Score for $c_+$ (positive):**

$$P(c_+) \cdot P(\text{great} \mid c_+) \cdot P(\text{movie} \mid c_+)$$

$$= 0.5 \times \frac{5}{21} \times \frac{2}{21}$$

**Substitute the numerical values:**

$$= 0.5 \times 0.2381 \times 0.0952$$

$$= 0.5 \times 0.02267$$

$$= 0.01134$$

**Score for $c_-$ (negative):**

$$P(c_-) \cdot P(\text{great} \mid c_-) \cdot P(\text{movie} \mid c_-)$$

$$= 0.5 \times \frac{1}{21} \times \frac{2}{21}$$

**Substitute the numerical values:**

$$= 0.5 \times 0.0476 \times 0.0952$$

$$= 0.5 \times 0.00453$$

$$= 0.00227$$

---

### 3.1.7 Step 6 — MAP Decision

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c \in \{+,-\}} P(c \mid d_{\text{test}})$$

Since $P(c_+ \mid d_{\text{test}}) \propto 0.01134$ and $P(c_- \mid d_{\text{test}}) \propto 0.00227$:

$$\hat{c} = c_+ \quad (\text{Positive})$$

The ratio $\dfrac{P(c_+ \mid d_{\text{test}})}{P(c_- \mid d_{\text{test}})} = \dfrac{0.01134}{0.00227} \approx 5.0$, so the model is **5× more confident** in "Positive" than "Negative" — exactly what we expect given the strong presence of *"great"* in positive training reviews.

---

### 3.1.8 Step 7 — Verification in Log-Space

$$\log P(c_+) + \log \hat{P}(\text{great} \mid c_+) + \log \hat{P}(\text{movie} \mid c_+)$$

$$= \log(0.5) + \log(5/21) + \log(2/21)$$

$$= -0.6931 + (-1.4351) + (-2.3514)$$

$$= -4.4796$$

**For $c_-$:**

$$= \log(0.5) + \log(1/21) + \log(2/21)$$

$$= -0.6931 + (-3.0445) + (-2.3514)$$

$$= -6.0890$$

**Decision:** $-4.4796 > -6.0890$ → **Positive** ✓ (matches Step 6).

---

## 3.2 General MNB Classification Pipeline (Derivation Summary)

For a training corpus $\mathcal{D} = \{(d_1, c_1), \ldots, (d_N, c_N)\}$ with vocabulary $V$:

### 3.2.1 Training Phase

**Compute class priors:**

$$P(c_j) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}[c_i = c_j]$$

**Compute word counts per class:**

$$n_{w,c_j} = \sum_{i : c_i = c_j} \text{count}(w, d_i)$$

**Compute class-cumulative word counts:**

$$n_{c_j} = \sum_{w \in V} n_{w, c_j}$$

**Compute smoothed word log-likelihoods** (precompute for speed):

$$\log \hat{P}(w \mid c_j) = \log(n_{w, c_j} + \alpha) - \log(n_{c_j} + \alpha \vert V \vert)$$

### 3.2.2 Inference Phase

For a new document $d$ with count vector $\mathbf{x}$:

$$\text{score}(c_j) = \log P(c_j) + \sum_{w \in V} x_w \cdot \log \hat{P}(w \mid c_j)$$

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c_j} \text{score}(c_j)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the product (or sum in log-space) is the right thing to maximize:** The denominator $P(d)$ is *constant across all classes* for a given test document. Therefore, maximizing the posterior $P(c \mid d)$ is equivalent to maximizing the joint $P(c) P(d \mid c)$, which is what the formula above does. This is a **frequently tested KTU concept**.

---

## 3.3 Bernoulli Naive Bayes (For Comparison)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU often asks: *"Differentiate between Multinomial and Bernoulli Naive Bayes."* Memorize this distinction.

In **Bernoulli NB**, each document is represented as a **binary vector** $\mathbf{b} = (b_1, \ldots, b_{|V|})$ where $b_i \in \{0, 1\}$ indicates whether word $w_i$ is **present at least once** in the document.

The likelihood becomes:

$$P(d \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{|V|} \left[ b_i \cdot \hat{P}(w_i \mid c) + (1 - b_i) \cdot (1 - \hat{P}(w_i \mid c)) \right]$$

With smoothing:

$$\hat{P}(w_i \mid c) = \frac{\text{doc-count}(w_i, c) + \alpha}{N_c + 2\alpha}$$

**Key differences**:

| Property | Multinomial NB | Bernoulli NB |
|---|---|---|
| Document representation | **Word counts** | **Word presence/absence** |
| Handles word repetition | **Yes** (count matters) | **No** (binary) |
| Better for | Long documents, multiple occurrences | Short documents, sentiment |
| Penalizes absent words | **No** | **Yes** (via $(1 - \hat{P})$ term) |
| Training data needs | Moderate | Larger (needs to see absent words) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End MNB Text Classification Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Text Corpus] --> B[Tokenization]
    B --> C[Lowercasing and Stopword Removal]
    C --> D[Vocabulary Construction V]
    D --> E[Bag of Words Count Vector x]
    E --> F{Training Phase}
    F --> G[Compute Class Priors P of c]
    F --> H[Compute Word Likelihoods P of w given c]
    H --> I[Apply Laplace Smoothing alpha]
    I --> J[Store Log Likelihoods for Fast Lookup]
    J --> K[Trained MNB Model]
    K --> L{Inference Phase}
    L --> M[Convert Test Document to Count Vector]
    M --> N[Compute Log Score for Each Class]
    N --> O[Apply Argmax over Classes]
    O --> P[Predicted Class Label c hat]
    G --> N
    J --> N
```

## 4.2 Bayes' Theorem Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Observed Document d] --> B[Compute P of c]
    A --> C[Compute P of d given c]
    A --> D[Compute P of d]
    B --> E[Numerator: P of c times P of d given c]
    C --> E
    D --> F[Posterior: P of c given d]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Argmax over c]
    G --> H[Predicted Class]
```

## 4.3 Laplace Smoothing Mechanism (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Word Count count of w and c] --> B{Is count zero?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Add alpha: 0 plus 1]
    B -- No --> D[Add alpha: n plus 1]
    C --> E[Numerator: count plus alpha]
    D --> E
    F[Total Class Word Count] --> G[Add alpha times V size: n_c plus alpha V]
    G --> H[Denominator]
    E --> I[Divide: Numerator by Denominator]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Smoothed Probability P hat of w given c]
```

## 4.4 MNB vs. BNB Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MNB[Multinomial Naive Bayes]
        M1[Document] --> M2[Word Count Vector x1 to xV]
        M2 --> M3[Score: log P of c plus sum xi log P of w given c]
    end
    subgraph BNB[Bernoulli Naive Bayes]
        N1[Document] --> N2[Binary Vector b1 to bV]
        N2 --> N3[Score: log P of c plus sum bi log P of w given c plus 1-bi log 1 minus P of w given c]
    end
    M3 --> O[Argmax]
    N3 --> O
    O --> P[Final Class Prediction]
```

## 4.5 Sentiment Analysis Workflow (End-to-End Application View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Customer Reviews] --> B[Text Preprocessing]
    B --> C[Tokenize and Clean]
    C --> D[Build Vocabulary from Training Set]
    D --> E[Train MNB on Labeled Reviews]
    E --> F[Trained Sentiment Model]
    F --> G{New Review Arrives}
    G --> H[Vectorize using same Vocabulary]
    H --> I[Score: Pos and Neg]
    I --> J{Pos greater than Neg}
    J -- Yes --> K[Output: Positive Sentiment]
    J -- No --> L[Output: Negative Sentiment]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme Style]

**State Bayes' Theorem and explain its role in the Naive Bayes text classifier. Why is the Naive Bayes assumption called "naive"?** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Bayes' Theorem states:

$$P(c \mid d) = \frac{P(d \mid c) \cdot P(c)}{P(d)}$$

In Naive Bayes text classification, this theorem is used to compute the **posterior probability** of a class $c$ given a document $d$. The classifier computes this for every class and picks the one with the highest posterior (the **MAP estimate**):

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} P(c) \cdot P(d \mid c)$$

The assumption is called **"naive"** because it treats all word features as **conditionally independent given the class** — i.e., the presence of one word is assumed to be unrelated to the presence of any other word, given the class label. In real text, this is rarely true (e.g., "San Francisco" co-occurs), but the simplification works well in practice.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme Style]

**What is Laplace smoothing and why is it essential in Naive Bayes text classification?** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Laplace smoothing** (or **add-one smoothing**) modifies the maximum likelihood estimate of word probabilities to avoid zero counts:

$$\hat{P}(w_i \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, c) + 1}{\sum_{w \in V} \text{count}(w, c) + \vert V \vert}$$

It is **essential** because of the **zero-frequency problem**: in real text, any test document is almost certain to contain a word that **never appeared** in the training set for a particular class. Without smoothing, $\hat{P}(w_i \mid c) = 0$ for that word, which makes the *entire* product (and hence the posterior) zero — completely eliminating that class regardless of the strength of other evidence. Laplace smoothing assigns a small non-zero probability to unseen words, making the classifier robust to vocabulary mismatch between training and test data.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the Naive Bayes classification rule from first principles using Bayes' Theorem. Clearly state the conditional independence assumption and justify why the denominator $P(d)$ can be dropped during classification. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Given the following small training corpus for a binary sentiment classifier (Positive / Negative):

- $D_1$ (Pos): "good movie loved it"
- $D_2$ (Pos): "good story loved acting"
- $D_3$ (Pos): "loved good film"
- $D_4$ (Neg): "bad movie hated it"
- $D_5$ (Neg): "bad plot hated acting"
- $D_6$ (Neg): "hated bad story"

Classify the test document $d_{\text{test}} = $ "good movie" using **Multinomial Naive Bayes with Laplace smoothing** ($\alpha = 1$). Show all intermediate calculations. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Bayes' Theorem:** For a document $d$ with feature vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \ldots, x_n)$ and class $c$:

$$P(c \mid d) = \frac{P(d \mid c) \cdot P(c)}{P(d)}$$

**Step 2 — Posterior is what we want to maximize:**

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} P(c \mid d) = \arg\max_{c} \frac{P(d \mid c) \cdot P(c)}{P(d)}$$

**Step 3 — Drop the denominator:** The term $P(d)$ is the probability of the document under *no class assumption* — it is **constant for a fixed document $d$** regardless of which class we are evaluating. Since it does not depend on $c$, it does not affect the argmax:

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} P(d \mid c) \cdot P(c)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the conditional independence assumption:** Let the document be a sequence of word features. The true joint likelihood $P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \mid c)$ is intractable. The Naive Bayes assumption states that all features are conditionally independent given the class:

$$P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$$

**Step 5 — Final Naive Bayes classification rule:**

$$\boxed{\;\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c \in C} P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)\;}$$

**Justification for dropping $P(d)$:** For a given test document $d$, the value of $P(d)$ is identical for all candidate classes. The argmax operation only compares relative values, so any constant multiplicative factor (like $1/P(d)$) cancels out. This is a standard "proof" type step that KTU expects.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Vocabulary:**

$$V = \{\text{good, movie, loved, it, story, acting, film, bad, plot, hated}\}, \quad \vert V \vert = 10$$

**Step 2 — Class Priors:** 3 positive and 3 negative training docs.

$$P(c_+) = \frac{3}{6} = 0.5, \qquad P(c_-) = \frac{3}{6} = 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Word Counts per Class:**

| Word | Count in $c_+$ | Count in $c_-$ |
|---|---|---|
| good | 4 | 0 |
| movie | 1 | 1 |
| loved | 3 | 0 |
| it | 1 | 1 |
| story | 1 | 1 |
| acting | 1 | 1 |
| film | 1 | 0 |
| bad | 0 | 3 |
| plot | 0 | 1 |
| hated | 0 | 3 |
| **Total** | **12** | **11** |

**Step 4 — Smoothed Likelihoods (denominator = $n_c + \vert V \vert$):**

For $c_+$: denominator = $12 + 10 = 22$.

For $c_-$: denominator = $11 + 10 = 21$.

| Word | $\hat{P}(w \mid c_+) = (c+1)/22$ | $\hat{P}(w \mid c_-) = (c+1)/21$ |
|---|---|---|
| good | $5/22 \approx 0.2273$ | $1/21 \approx 0.0476$ |
| movie | $2/22 \approx 0.0909$ | $2/21 \approx 0.0952$ |

**Step 5 — Score the test document "good movie":**

**Score for $c_+$:**

$$P(c_+) \cdot \hat{P}(\text{good} \mid c_+) \cdot \hat{P}(\text{movie} \mid c_+) = 0.5 \times \frac{5}{22} \times \frac{2}{22}$$

$$= 0.5 \times \frac{10}{484} = 0.5 \times 0.02066 = 0.01033$$

**Score for $c_-$:**

$$P(c_-) \cdot \hat{P}(\text{good} \mid c_-) \cdot \hat{P}(\text{movie} \mid c_-) = 0.5 \times \frac{1}{21} \times \frac{2}{21}$$

$$= 0.5 \times \frac{2}{441} = 0.5 \times 0.00454 = 0.00227$$

**Step 6 — Decision:**

Since $0.01033 > 0.00227$, the model predicts:

$$\hat{c} = c_+ \quad \text{(Positive sentiment)}$$

The ratio is approximately $4.55 : 1$ in favor of Positive, dominated by the strong association of "good" with the positive class.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Multinomial Naive Bayes (MNB)** and **Bernoulli Naive Bayes (BNB)** with respect to their feature representation, likelihood formulation, and typical use cases in text classification. Mention the role of Laplace smoothing in both. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A spam filter is trained on the following 5 emails:

- $E_1$ (Spam): "offer free money"
- $E_2$ (Spam): "free offer click"
- $E_3$ (Spam): "money free offer"
- $E_4$ (Ham): "meeting project schedule"
- $E_5$ (Ham): "project meeting report"

Using **Multinomial Naive Bayes with $\alpha = 1$**, classify the email $e_{\text{test}} = $ "free offer". Show the complete computation of priors, likelihoods, and posterior scores. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Comparison of MNB and BNB (7 Marks):**

| Aspect | Multinomial Naive Bayes | Bernoulli Naive Bayes |
|---|---|---|
| **Feature** | Word **count** $x_i \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$ | Word **presence** $b_i \in \{0, 1\}$ |
| **Document model** | Multinomial distribution over $V$ | Multiple Bernoulli trials, one per word |
| **Likelihood** | $P(d \mid c) \propto \prod_i P(w_i \mid c)^{x_i}$ | $P(d \mid c) = \prod_i [b_i P(w_i \mid c) + (1-b_i)(1 - P(w_i \mid c))]$ |
| **Counts word repetition** | **Yes** (power $x_i$) | **No** (presence only) |
| **Absent-word contribution** | **Absent words contribute nothing** | **Absent words contribute via $(1 - P(w_i \mid c))$** |
| **Best for** | Long documents, classical spam filtering, news classification | Short documents, sentiment polarity, where absence is informative |
| **Computational cost** | $O(\vert d \vert)$ per class | $O(\vert V \vert)$ per class |

**Role of Laplace smoothing in both:** Both variants suffer from the zero-frequency problem. In MNB, $\text{count}(w, c) = 0$ makes the product zero. In BNB, both $P(w_i \mid c) = 0$ and $1 - P(w_i \mid c)$ become problematic for unseen words. Laplace smoothing (with parameter $\alpha = 1$ typically) adds $\alpha$ to every count before normalization, ensuring that no probability is ever exactly zero. For MNB, the denominator grows by $\alpha \vert V \vert$; for BNB, the denominator grows by $2\alpha$ (since each class contributes one $\alpha$).

---

**(b) Spam Filter Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Vocabulary:**

$$V = \{\text{offer, free, money, click, meeting, project, schedule, report}\}, \quad \vert V \vert = 8$$

**Step 2 — Priors:** 3 spam and 2 ham documents. Total $N = 5$.

$$P(\text{spam}) = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6, \qquad P(\text{ham}) = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4$$

**Step 3 — Word Counts:**

| Word | Count in Spam | Count in Ham |
|---|---|---|
| offer | 3 | 0 |
| free | 3 | 0 |
| money | 2 | 0 |
| click | 1 | 0 |
| meeting | 0 | 2 |
| project | 0 | 2 |
| schedule | 0 | 1 |
| report | 0 | 1 |
| **Total** | **9** | **6** |

**Step 4 — Smoothed Likelihoods:**

For Spam: denominator = $9 + 8 = 17$.

For Ham: denominator = $6 + 8 = 14$.

| Word | $\hat{P}(w \mid \text{spam})$ | $\hat{P}(w \mid \text{ham})$ |
|---|---|---|
| free | $(3+1)/17 = 4/17 \approx 0.2353$ | $(0+1)/14 = 1/14 \approx 0.0714$ |
| offer | $(3+1)/17 = 4/17 \approx 0.2353$ | $(0+1)/14 = 1/14 \approx 0.0714$ |

**Step 5 — Score the test email "free offer":**

**Score for Spam:**

$$P(\text{spam}) \cdot \hat{P}(\text{free} \mid \text{spam}) \cdot \hat{P}(\text{offer} \mid \text{spam}) = 0.6 \times \frac{4}{17} \times \frac{4}{17}$$

$$= 0.6 \times \frac{16}{289} = 0.6 \times 0.05536 = 0.03322$$

**Score for Ham:**

$$P(\text{ham}) \cdot \hat{P}(\text{free} \mid \text{ham}) \cdot \hat{P}(\text{offer} \mid \text{ham}) = 0.4 \times \frac{1}{14} \times \frac{1}{14}$$

$$= 0.4 \times \frac{1}{196} = 0.4 \times 0.00510 = 0.00204$$

**Step 6 — Decision:**

Since $0.03322 \gg 0.00204$, the email is classified as **Spam**.

The ratio is approximately $16.3 : 1$ in favor of Spam, dominated by the prior and the strong likelihood of "free" and "offer" under the spam class.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Naive Bayes Exam Answers (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Forgetting to add $\vert V \vert$ to the denominator** when applying Laplace smoothing. KTU specifically deducts 1 mark for this. The denominator is $\sum_w \text{count}(w, c) + \alpha \vert V \vert$, NOT just $\sum_w \text{count}(w, c) + \alpha$.
> 2. **Mixing up MNB and BNB** formulas in the likelihood term. MNB uses a power $x_i$; BNB uses the binary combination $[b_i P + (1-b_i)(1-P)]$.
> 3. **Forgetting to compute the prior** $P(c)$. The argmax is over $P(c) \times \text{likelihood}$, not just the likelihood. Many students drop the prior in numericals and lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Not showing log-space conversion** for longer documents where the product underflows. KTU expects explicit mention of the log transformation for documents with $> 5$ features.
> 5. **Not justifying dropping $P(d)$** in the derivation question. The examiner expects the statement: *"Since $P(d)$ is independent of $c$, it does not affect the argmax."*
> 6. **Confusing $n_{w,c}$ (word count in class)** with $\text{doc-count}(w, c)$ (number of documents in class $c$ containing word $w$) — they are equal only for MNB; BNB uses the latter.
> 7. **Missing the conclusion sentence** after the argmax. Always end with: *"Therefore, the test document is classified as class $c_j$."*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Naive Bayes for Text Classification — Rapid Revision Summary:**

- **Bayes' Theorem** inverts $P(c \mid d)$ from the tractable $P(d \mid c)$ and $P(c)$.
- The **"naive" assumption** = all word features are conditionally independent given the class. It is mathematically false but empirically effective.
- The **MAP classification rule** is $\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} P(c) \prod_{i} P(w_i \mid c)^{x_i}$.
- **Drop the denominator** $P(d)$ because it is constant across classes for a fixed test document.
- **Class prior** $P(c) = N_c / N$ (relative frequency in training set).
- **MLE of word likelihood** (no smoothing): $\hat{P}(w \mid c) = \text{count}(w,c) / \sum_w \text{count}(w, c)$.
- **Laplace (add-one) smoothing** prevents the zero-frequency problem: add $\alpha$ to every numerator and $\alpha \vert V \vert$ to the denominator.
- **Log-space computation** turns products into sums: $\log P(c) + \sum_i x_i \log \hat{P}(w_i \mid c)$ — essential for numerical stability.
- **Multinomial NB** uses **word counts**; best for long documents.
- **Bernoulli NB** uses **word presence/absence**; best for short texts and sentiment; penalizes absent words.
- **Sentiment analysis** is a canonical NLP application: binary (Pos/Neg) or multi-class (Pos/Neu/Neg).
- **Standard evaluation metrics**: accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score.
- **Strengths of NBC**: fast training, low data requirements, handles high-dimensional sparse text, interpretable.
- **Weaknesses of NBC**: independence assumption is unrealistic; probability estimates are poor (only rankings are reliable); zero-frequency problem requires smoothing.
- **Real-world deployment**: spam filters, news categorization, sentiment analyzers, medical text classification, author attribution.
- **Exam tip**: For numericals, always show (1) vocabulary, (2) priors, (3) word counts, (4) smoothed likelihoods, (5) scores, (6) argmax decision. Each missing step costs marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Training the Naive Bayes Classifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT741 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Training the Naive Bayes Classifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Naive Bayes Classifier — Training** is the statistical learning procedure in which a probabilistic model is induced from a labeled training corpus $\mathcal{D} = \{(d_1, c_1), (d_2, c_2), \dots, (d_N, c_N)\}$ by estimating the **class prior probabilities** $P(c)$ and the **feature likelihoods** $P(w \mid c)$ for every word $w$ in the vocabulary $V$ and every class $c \in \mathcal{C}$, using the **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** principle under the **conditional independence assumption** (the "naive" assumption).

Mathematically, the training objective is to find the parameter set $\hat{\theta}$ that maximizes the **log-likelihood** of the observed training data:

$$\hat{\theta} = \arg\max_{\theta} \; \mathcal{L}(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) = \arg\max_{\theta} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(c_i \mid d_i; \theta)$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition — "The Coin-Counting Detective"**

Imagine a **detective** who has been handed a stack of **labeled letters** — some marked *"Spam"* and others marked *"Not Spam"*. The detective's job is to **learn what spam looks like** by counting.

1. First, the detective flips through the pile and **counts how many letters are spam vs. not-spam** → this gives the **prior probability** $P(\text{Spam})$.
2. Then, inside each pile, the detective **counts how often each word appears** — e.g., *"free"* appears 80 times in 100 spam letters → $P(\text{"free"} \mid \text{Spam}) = 0.8$.
3. Once these counts are stored, any **new letter** can be classified by computing a probability score.

**"Naive"** means the detective naively assumes that the presence of one word (e.g., *"free"*) does **not affect** the presence of another word (e.g., *"money"*) within the same class. This simplification makes the math extremely fast and surprisingly effective in NLP.

## 1.3 Core Components in Training

| Component | Symbol | Role in Training |
|---|---|---|
| Training Corpus | $\mathcal{D}$ | The labeled dataset used for estimation |
| Class Set | $\mathcal{C} = \{c_1, c_2, \dots, c_{\vert \mathcal{C} \vert}\}$ | All possible output labels |
| Vocabulary | $V$ | The set of all unique words observed |
| Prior | $P(c)$ | How common each class is overall |
| Likelihood | $P(w \mid c)$ | How likely each word is given a class |
| Count Matrix | $\text{count}(w, c)$ | Raw frequency of word $w$ in class $c$ |
| Laplace Smoothing Constant | $\alpha$ | Prevents zero probabilities (default $\alpha = 1$) |

## 1.4 Key Properties

> [!NOTE]
> - **Generative Model**: Naive Bayes is a *generative* classifier — it models how data is *generated* per class.
> - **Closed-Form Solution**: Training has **no iterative optimization** (no gradient descent) — parameters are computed by **direct counting**.
> - **Linear Training Time**: $\mathcal{O}(\vert \mathcal{D} \vert \cdot \vert V \vert)$, making it extremely scalable.
> - **Parameter Sparsity**: Only $\vert \mathcal{C} \vert + \vert \mathcal{C} \vert \cdot \vert V \vert$ parameters need to be stored.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Naive Bayes Training

### Pillar 1 — The Bayes' Theorem Foundation

Naive Bayes classification is built upon **Bayes' Theorem**:

$$P(c \mid d) = \frac{P(c) \cdot P(d \mid c)}{P(d)}$$

For a test document $d$ containing words $w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n$, the **most probable class** is:

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c \in \mathcal{C}} P(c) \cdot P(d \mid c) = \arg\max_{c \in \mathcal{C}} P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid c)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The denominator $P(d)$ is dropped because it is **constant** for all classes and does not affect the $\arg\max$.

### Pillar 2 — The "Naive" Conditional Independence Assumption

The "naive" assumption states that **given the class, all word features are mutually independent**:

$$P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid c)$$

- **Why "naive"?** In real text, words are highly correlated ("New" and "York"). The assumption is mathematically convenient but rarely true.
- **Why it still works?** Even with violated assumptions, the **ranking** of classes often remains correct.

### Pillar 3 — Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)

MLE chooses the parameter values that **make the observed data most probable**:

$$\hat{P}(c) = \frac{N_c}{N} \quad , \quad \hat{P}(w \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(w, c)}{\sum_{w'} \text{count}(w', c)}$$

where:
- $N$ = total number of training documents
- $N_c$ = number of training documents in class $c$
- $\text{count}(w, c)$ = total occurrences of word $w$ across all documents of class $c$

## 2.2 The Zero-Probability Problem & Laplace Smoothing

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Issue**: If a word $w$ never appeared in the training data for class $c$, then $\hat{P}(w \mid c) = 0$, which **zeros-out the entire product** regardless of how strong the other evidence is.

**Laplace Smoothing** (also called *additive smoothing*) resolves this:

$$\hat{P}_{\text{Lap}}(w \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(w, c) + \alpha}{\sum_{w' \in V} \text{count}(w', c) + \alpha \cdot \vert V \vert}$$

For the priors, Laplace smoothing is **not typically applied** in standard Naive Bayes text classification.

## 2.3 The Log-Space Trick

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In practice, the raw product $\prod_{i} P(w_i \mid c)$ causes **numerical underflow** when document length is large (product of many small probabilities $\to 0$).

The standard solution is to take the **logarithm**, converting products into sums:

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c \in \mathcal{C}} \left[ \log P(c) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(w_i \mid c) \right]$$

This preserves the $\arg\max$ because $\log$ is a **monotonically increasing** function.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Used In |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\hat{P}(c) = \dfrac{N_c}{N}$ | MLE estimate of class prior | Class probability estimation |
| 2 | $\hat{P}(w \mid c) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w, c)}{\sum_{w' \in V} \text{count}(w', c)}$ | MLE estimate of word likelihood | Feature probability estimation |
| 3 | $\hat{P}_{\text{Lap}}(w \mid c) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w, c) + \alpha}{\sum_{w'} \text{count}(w', c) + \alpha \cdot \vert V \vert}$ | Laplace-smoothed likelihood | Avoiding zero probabilities |
| 4 | $\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} P(c) \cdot \prod_{i} P(w_i \mid c)$ | Naive Bayes decision rule | Classification |
| 5 | $\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} \left[ \log P(c) + \sum_{i} \log P(w_i \mid c) \right]$ | Log-space decision rule | Numerical stability |
| 6 | $P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid c)$ | Conditional independence assumption | Model formulation |
| 7 | $\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(c_i \mid d_i; \theta)$ | Log-likelihood objective | MLE derivation |
| 8 | $\hat{\theta} = \arg\max_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta \mid \mathcal{D})$ | MLE optimization statement | Theoretical foundation |

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities are **dimensionless probabilities** in the range $[0, 1]$. Sums of probabilities over a complete sample space equal **1**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Naive Bayes is the workhorse behind:
- **Spam detection** in email gateways (Gmail, Outlook).
- **Sentiment analysis** of product reviews (positive/negative).
- **Document categorization** in news aggregators (Reuters, BBC).
- **Medical diagnosis** from patient symptom reports.
- **Author identification** in forensic linguistics.

Its **O(N · |V|) training** and **O(|C| · |d|) inference** make it the **fastest probabilistic classifier** in production NLP systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Example

## 3.1 Derivation of MLE for Class Priors

We model the class label of each document as a **Categorical distribution**. The likelihood of observing the class sequence in the training set is:

$$P(\mathcal{C} \mid \theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(c_i; \theta) = \prod_{c \in \mathcal{C}} P(c)^{N_c}$$

The log-likelihood is:

$$\log P(\mathcal{C} \mid \theta) = \sum_{c \in \mathcal{C}} N_c \cdot \log P(c)$$

Using a **Lagrange multiplier** $\lambda$ for the constraint $\sum_c P(c) = 1$:

$$\mathcal{J} = \sum_{c \in \mathcal{C}} N_c \log P(c) + \lambda \left(1 - \sum_{c \in \mathcal{C}} P(c)\right)$$

Setting $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{J}}{\partial P(c)} = 0$:

$$\frac{N_c}{P(c)} - \lambda = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; P(c) = \frac{N_c}{\lambda}$$

Substituting into the constraint $\sum_c P(c) = 1$:

$$\sum_{c} \frac{N_c}{\lambda} = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{N}{\lambda} = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda = N$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\hat{P}(c) = \frac{N_c}{N}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of MLE for Word Likelihoods

Within each class $c$, word occurrences are modeled as a **Multinomial distribution** over vocabulary $V$. The likelihood of observing word $w$ exactly $\text{count}(w, c)$ times is:

$$P(\text{counts} \mid c) = \frac{(\sum_w \text{count}(w, c))!}{\prod_w \text{count}(w, c)!} \prod_{w \in V} P(w \mid c)^{\text{count}(w, c)}$$

Taking the log (and noting the multinomial coefficient is constant w.r.t. parameters):

$$\log P = \sum_{w \in V} \text{count}(w, c) \cdot \log P(w \mid c) + \text{const}$$

Applying the same Lagrange technique with $\sum_w P(w \mid c) = 1$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial P(w \mid c)} \left[ \sum_{w'} \text{count}(w', c) \log P(w' \mid c) - \lambda \left(\sum_{w'} P(w' \mid c) - 1\right) \right] = 0$$

$$\frac{\text{count}(w, c)}{P(w \mid c)} = \lambda \;\Rightarrow\; P(w \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(w, c)}{\lambda}$$

Using $\sum_w P(w \mid c) = 1$ gives $\lambda = \sum_{w'} \text{count}(w', c)$, hence:

$$\boxed{\hat{P}(w \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(w, c)}{\sum_{w' \in V} \text{count}(w', c)}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example — End-to-End Naive Bayes Training

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **complete worked example** of the training process. Study every step — KTU frequently tests such numerical problems for 7 marks.

### Setup

Consider the following **mini training corpus** with two classes $C = \{\text{pos}, \text{neg}\}$:

| Doc | Text | Class |
|:---:|---|:---:|
| $d_1$ | great product love it | pos |
| $d_2$ | great deal excellent | pos |
| $d_3$ | poor service hate it | neg |
| $d_4$ | hate hate bad product | neg |

**Vocabulary** $V$ (case-folded, with stop-words removed for clarity):
$$V = \{\text{great}, \text{product}, \text{love}, \text{it}, \text{deal}, \text{excellent}, \text{poor}, \text{service}, \text{hate}, \text{bad}\}, \quad \vert V \vert = 10$$

### Step 1 — Compute Class Priors $P(c)$

- Total documents $N = 4$
- Documents in pos class $N_{\text{pos}} = 2$
- Documents in neg class $N_{\text{neg}} = 2$

$$\hat{P}(\text{pos}) = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5 \quad , \quad \hat{P}(\text{neg}) = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5$$

### Step 2 — Build the Word Count Matrix $\text{count}(w, c)$

| Word $w$ | $\text{count}(w, \text{pos})$ | $\text{count}(w, \text{neg})$ |
|---|:---:|:---:|
| great | 2 | 0 |
| product | 1 | 1 |
| love | 1 | 0 |
| it | 1 | 1 |
| deal | 1 | 0 |
| excellent | 1 | 0 |
| poor | 0 | 1 |
| service | 0 | 1 |
| hate | 0 | 3 |
| bad | 0 | 1 |
| **Total $\sum_w$** | **7** | **8** |

### Step 3 — Compute Word Likelihoods $P(w \mid c)$ (Raw MLE)

Using $\hat{P}(w \mid c) = \dfrac{\text{count}(w, c)}{\sum_{w'} \text{count}(w', c)}$:

$$\hat{P}(\text{great} \mid \text{pos}) = \frac{2}{7} \approx 0.286 \quad , \quad \hat{P}(\text{great} \mid \text{neg}) = \frac{0}{8} = 0$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Zero observed!** $\hat{P}(\text{great} \mid \text{neg}) = 0$. This is the **zero-probability problem**. We will fix it in Step 4.

### Step 4 — Apply Laplace Smoothing ($\alpha = 1$)

$$\hat{P}_{\text{Lap}}(w \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(w, c) + 1}{\sum_{w'} \text{count}(w', c) + \vert V \vert}$$

- Denominator for pos: $7 + 10 = 17$
- Denominator for neg: $8 + 10 = 18$

| Word $w$ | $P_{\text{Lap}}(w \mid \text{pos})$ | $P_{\text{Lap}}(w \mid \text{neg})$ |
|---|:---:|:---:|
| great | $(2+1)/17 = 3/17 \approx 0.1765$ | $(0+1)/18 = 1/18 \approx 0.0556$ |
| product | $(1+1)/17 = 2/17 \approx 0.1176$ | $(1+1)/18 = 2/18 \approx 0.1111$ |
| love | $(1+1)/17 = 2/17 \approx 0.1176$ | $(1+1)/18 = 1/18 \approx 0.0556$ |
| it | $(1+1)/17 = 2/17 \approx 0.1176$ | $(1+1)/18 = 2/18 \approx 0.1111$ |
| deal | $(1+1)/17 = 2/17 \approx 0.1176$ | $(1+1)/18 = 1/18 \approx 0.0556$ |
| excellent | $(1+1)/17 = 2/17 \approx 0.1176$ | $(1+1)/18 = 1/18 \approx 0.0556$ |
| poor | $(0+1)/17 = 1/17 \approx 0.0588$ | $(1+1)/18 = 2/18 \approx 0.1111$ |
| service | $(0+1)/17 = 1/17 \approx 0.0588$ | $(1+1)/18 = 2/18 \approx 0.1111$ |
| hate | $(0+1)/17 = 1/17 \approx 0.0588$ | $(3+1)/18 = 4/18 \approx 0.2222$ |
| bad | $(0+1)/17 = 1/17 \approx 0.0588$ | $(1+1)/18 = 2/18 \approx 0.1111$ |
| **Sum** | **17/17 = 1.0000** | **18/18 = 1.0000** |

> [!NOTE]
> Both probability distributions sum to **1.0**, confirming they are valid probability mass functions.

### Step 5 — Verify the Trained Model on a Test Document

Let the test document be $d_{\text{test}} = $ "great hate". Apply the **log-space decision rule**:

$$c^* = \arg\max_{c \in \{\text{pos}, \text{neg}\}} \left[ \log P(c) + \log P(\text{great} \mid c) + \log P(\text{hate} \mid c) \right]$$

**For class = pos:**

$$S_{\text{pos}} = \log(0.5) + \log(3/17) + \log(1/17)$$
$$S_{\text{pos}} = (-0.6931) + (-1.7346) + (-2.8332)$$
$$S_{\text{pos}} = -5.2609$$

**For class = neg:**

$$S_{\text{neg}} = \log(0.5) + \log(1/18) + \log(4/18)$$
$$S_{\text{neg}} = (-0.6931) + (-2.8904) + (-1.5041)$$
$$S_{\text{neg}} = -5.0876$$

**Decision:**

$$S_{\text{neg}} = -5.0876 > S_{\text{pos}} = -5.2609 \;\Rightarrow\; \hat{c} = \text{neg}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The test document "great hate" is correctly classified as **negative**, even though it contains the positive word "great". The strong "hate" signal dominates due to its much higher likelihood under the neg class (4/18 vs. 1/17).

## 3.4 Summary of the Training Algorithm (Pseudocode Logic)

```
ALGORITHM: Train_Naive_Bayes(Corpus D, Vocabulary V, Classes C)
INPUT : Labeled training corpus D, vocabulary V, set of classes C, smoothing α
OUTPUT: Prior table P(c) and likelihood table P(w|c)

1.  FOR each class c in C:
2.       N_c ← count of documents in D belonging to class c
3.       P(c) ← N_c / |D|
4.       total_c ← 0
5.       FOR each word w in V:
6.            count(w, c) ← total occurrences of w in all docs of class c
7.            total_c ← total_c + count(w, c)
8.       FOR each word w in V:
9.            P(w|c) ← (count(w, c) + α) / (total_c + α·|V|)
10. RETURN (P(c) for all c, P(w|c) for all w, c)
```

Time complexity: $\mathcal{O}(\vert \mathcal{D} \vert \cdot \vert V \vert)$ — **one pass through data**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Naive Bayes Training Pipeline — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Labeled Corpus D] --> B[Tokenization and Lowercasing]
    B --> C[Stop-word Removal and Vocabulary Construction V]
    C --> D[Document Count per Class N_c]
    D --> E[Compute Class Priors P of c = N_c / N]
    C --> F[Per-Class Word Count Matrix count w c]
    F --> G{Laplace Smoothing alpha}
    G -->|alpha = 1| H[Smoothed Likelihood P of w given c]
    G -->|alpha = 0| I[Raw MLE Likelihood]
    H --> J[Trained Model: Prior Table and Likelihood Table]
    I --> J
    E --> J
    J --> K[Log-Space Normalization]
    K --> L[Ready for Inference]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style J fill:#d4edda
    style L fill:#fff3cd
    style G fill:#f8d7da
```

## 4.2 Parameter Estimation Flow (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input
        D1[Corpus D]
        V1[Vocabulary V]
    end

    subgraph Stage1[Stage 1: Document-Level Counting]
        D1 --> P1[Count documents per class]
        P1 --> Prior[Prior P c = N_c / N]
    end

    subgraph Stage2[Stage 2: Word-Level Counting]
        D1 --> P2[Count word occurrences per class]
        V1 --> P2
        P2 --> Raw[Raw MLE P w c]
    end

    subgraph Stage3[Stage 3: Smoothing]
        Raw --> Smooth[Apply Laplace: count+alpha / total+alpha times V]
        Smooth --> Smoothed[Smoothed P w c]
    end

    subgraph Output[Output: Trained Classifier]
        Prior --> Model[Trained Naive Bayes Model]
        Smoothed --> Model
    end

    style Stage1 fill:#fff8dc
    style Stage2 fill:#e6f3ff
    style Stage3 fill:#ffe6e6
    style Output fill:#d4edda
```

## 4.3 Zero-Probability Problem & Laplace Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Word w never appeared in class c training data] --> Q[count w c = 0]
    Q --> R[Raw MLE P w c = 0]
    R --> S[Entire document score for class c = 0]
    S --> T[Document wrongly classified due to single unseen word]

    P2[Solution: Laplace Smoothing alpha = 1] --> Q2[count w c + 1 = 1]
    Q2 --> R2[Smoothed P w c = 1 / total + V]
    R2 --> S2[No zero probability; model robust to unseen words]
    S2 --> T2[Correct classification preserved]

    style T fill:#f8d7da
    style T2 fill:#d4edda
```

## 4.4 Inference vs. Training Comparison (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TRAINING_PHASE
        T1[Labeled Corpus] --> T2[Counting Phase]
        T2 --> T3[Parameter Tables: P c and P w c]
    end

    subgraph INFERENCE_PHASE
        I1[Test Document d] --> I2[Tokenize and Look Up in V]
        I2 --> I3[Compute Score per Class using log P c + sum log P w c]
        I3 --> I4[arg max to select predicted class]
    end

    T3 -.pre-computed.-> I3

    style TRAINING_PHASE fill:#e1f5ff
    style INFERENCE_PHASE fill:#fff3cd
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Q:** Define the **Naive Bayes assumption** used in text classification. Why is it called "naive"?

**Model Answer:**

The **Naive Bayes assumption** states that **given the class label, all word features in a document are mutually independent**. Mathematically:

$$P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid c)$$

It is called **"naive"** because in real natural language text, words are **highly correlated** (e.g., "New" and "York" almost always co-occur). The assumption that words are conditionally independent given the class is therefore unrealistic. However, despite this simplifying assumption, Naive Bayes yields surprisingly strong performance in many NLP tasks, which is why it is called "naive" rather than "optimal".

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Q:** What is the **zero-probability problem** in Naive Bayes? How is it resolved?

**Model Answer:**

The **zero-probability problem** occurs when a word $w$ that appears in a **test document** was **never observed** in the training data for a particular class $c$. In raw MLE, this gives $\hat{P}(w \mid c) = 0$, which causes the **entire product** $\prod_i P(w_i \mid c)$ to become zero, effectively eliminating that class from consideration — even if the other words in the document strongly favor it.

**Resolution — Laplace Smoothing (additive smoothing):** A small constant $\alpha$ (typically $\alpha = 1$) is added to **every** word count, and the denominator is adjusted accordingly:

$$\hat{P}_{\text{Lap}}(w \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(w, c) + \alpha}{\sum_{w'} \text{count}(w', c) + \alpha \cdot \vert V \vert}$$

This ensures that no probability is ever exactly zero, and the resulting distribution still sums to 1.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — End-Semester Model, Module 1]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q: (a)** Explain the **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** approach for training a Naive Bayes text classifier. Derive the formulas for the class prior $\hat{P}(c)$ and the word likelihood $\hat{P}(w \mid c)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the training corpus below, compute the **class priors** and the **word likelihoods with Laplace smoothing** ($\alpha = 1$) for a Naive Bayes classifier. Use the vocabulary $V = \{\text{Chinese}, \text{Beijing}, \text{Tokyo}, \text{Japan}\}$. **(7 marks)**

| Doc | Text | Class |
|:---:|---|:---:|
| 1 | Chinese Beijing Chinese | c |
| 2 | Chinese Chinese Tokyo | c |
| 3 | Tokyo Japan Japanese | nc |

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Setup:** Given a training corpus $\mathcal{D} = \{(d_1, c_1), \dots, (d_N, c_N)\}$ with vocabulary $V$ and class set $\mathcal{C}$, Naive Bayes training estimates two parameter sets: the **priors** $P(c)$ and the **likelihoods** $P(w \mid c)$.

**Step 2 — Log-likelihood formulation:** MLE maximizes the log-probability of the observed class labels given the documents:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \log \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(c_i \mid d_i; \theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(c_i; \theta) + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log \prod_{j} P(w_{ij} \mid c_i; \theta)$$

**Step 3 — Derivation of $\hat{P}(c)$:** The first sum, when expanded, becomes $\sum_{c} N_c \log P(c)$. Subject to the constraint $\sum_c P(c) = 1$ (Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$):

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial P(c)} \left[\sum_c N_c \log P(c) + \lambda\left(1 - \sum_c P(c)\right)\right] = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{N_c}{P(c)} = \lambda$$

Substituting into the constraint gives $\lambda = N$, hence:

$$\boxed{\hat{P}(c) = \frac{N_c}{N}}$$

**Step 4 — Derivation of $\hat{P}(w \mid c)$:** Similarly, treating word occurrences within class $c$ as a multinomial distribution and applying MLE with constraint $\sum_w P(w \mid c) = 1$:

$$\boxed{\hat{P}(w \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(w, c)}{\sum_{w' \in V} \text{count}(w', c)}}$$

This is the **closed-form MLE solution** — no iterative optimization is required. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Build vocabulary:** $V = \{\text{Chinese}, \text{Beijing}, \text{Tokyo}, \text{Japan}\}$, so $\vert V \vert = 4$.

**Step 2 — Class priors:** $N = 3$ total documents; $N_c = 2$ (class c), $N_{nc} = 1$ (class nc).

$$\hat{P}(c) = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.6667, \quad \hat{P}(nc) = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.3333$$

**Step 3 — Build word count matrix:**

| Word $w$ | $\text{count}(w, c)$ | $\text{count}(w, nc)$ |
|---|:---:|:---:|
| Chinese | 3 | 0 |
| Beijing | 1 | 0 |
| Tokyo | 1 | 1 |
| Japan | 0 | 1 |
| **Total** | **5** | **2** |

**Step 4 — Apply Laplace smoothing** with $\alpha = 1$:

- Denominator for $c$: $5 + 1 \cdot 4 = 9$
- Denominator for $nc$: $2 + 1 \cdot 4 = 6$

| Word $w$ | $P_{\text{Lap}}(w \mid c)$ | $P_{\text{Lap}}(w \mid nc)$ |
|---|:---:|:---:|
| Chinese | $(3+1)/9 = 4/9 \approx 0.4444$ | $(0+1)/6 = 1/6 \approx 0.1667$ |
| Beijing | $(1+1)/9 = 2/9 \approx 0.2222$ | $(0+1)/6 = 1/6 \approx 0.1667$ |
| Tokyo | $(1+1)/9 = 2/9 \approx 0.2222$ | $(1+1)/6 = 2/6 \approx 0.3333$ |
| Japan | $(0+1)/9 = 1/9 \approx 0.1111$ | $(1+1)/6 = 2/6 \approx 0.3333$ |
| **Sum** | **1.0000** | **1.0000** |

> The trained model is fully specified by the two priors and the two rows of the smoothed likelihood table. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**Q: (a)** Discuss the **concept of Laplace smoothing** in Naive Bayes. Why is it necessary? Show mathematically that the smoothed distribution still sums to **1**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following test document: $d_{\text{test}} = $ "Tokyo Japan". Using the model trained in Question A(b), classify $d_{\text{test}}$ using the **log-space** decision rule. Show all computations. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Why Laplace smoothing is needed:** In Maximum Likelihood Estimation, a word $w$ that never appeared in class $c$ during training produces $\hat{P}(w \mid c) = 0$. During classification, the product $\prod_i P(w_i \mid c)$ becomes zero, making the class $c$ impossible to select — a catastrophic outcome caused by a single unseen word.

**Step 2 — Mathematical formulation:** Add a small constant $\alpha \geq 0$ to every count:

$$\hat{P}_{\text{Lap}}(w \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(w, c) + \alpha}{\sum_{w' \in V} \text{count}(w', c) + \alpha \cdot \vert V \vert}$$

**Step 3 — Proof that the distribution sums to 1:**

$$\sum_{w \in V} \hat{P}_{\text{Lap}}(w \mid c) = \sum_{w \in V} \frac{\text{count}(w, c) + \alpha}{\sum_{w' \in V} \text{count}(w', c) + \alpha \cdot \vert V \vert}$$

Since the denominator is **constant** with respect to $w$, it factors out:

$$= \frac{\sum_{w \in V} \left[\text{count}(w, c) + \alpha\right]}{\sum_{w' \in V} \text{count}(w', c) + \alpha \cdot \vert V \vert} = \frac{\sum_{w \in V} \text{count}(w, c) + \alpha \cdot \vert V \vert}{\sum_{w' \in V} \text{count}(w', c) + \alpha \cdot \vert V \vert} = 1$$

**Q.E.D.** The smoothed distribution is a valid probability mass function. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Recall from Question A(b):**
- $\hat{P}(c) = 2/3$, $\hat{P}(nc) = 1/3$
- $\hat{P}(\text{Tokyo} \mid c) = 2/9$, $\hat{P}(\text{Tokyo} \mid nc) = 2/6 = 1/3$
- $\hat{P}(\text{Japan} \mid c) = 1/9$, $\hat{P}(\text{Japan} \mid nc) = 2/6 = 1/3$

**Step 2 — Log-space score for class $c$:**

$$S_c = \log(2/3) + \log(2/9) + \log(1/9)$$
$$S_c = \log(2) - \log(3) + \log(2) - \log(9) + \log(1) - \log(9)$$
$$S_c = (\log 2 + \log 2) - (\log 3 + \log 9 + \log 9)$$
$$S_c = \log 4 - (\log 3 + 2 \log 9) = 0.6021 - (1.0986 + 2 \cdot 2.1972)$$
$$S_c = 0.6021 - 5.4930 = -4.8909$$

**Step 3 — Log-space score for class $nc$:**

$$S_{nc} = \log(1/3) + \log(1/3) + \log(1/3)$$
$$S_{nc} = 3 \log(1/3) = -3 \log 3 = -3 \cdot 1.0986 = -3.2958$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**

$$S_{nc} = -3.2958 > S_c = -4.8909 \;\Rightarrow\; \hat{c} = \text{nc}$$

> The test document "Tokyo Japan" is classified as **nc (not-China)**. This is correct, since both words have a **stronger likelihood** under the $nc$ class (1/3 each) than under $c$ (2/9 ≈ 0.222 and 1/9 ≈ 0.111). **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Naive Bayes Training Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the smoothing denominator $\alpha \cdot \vert V \vert$** — students often write only $(\text{count} + \alpha)/(\text{total} + \alpha)$ and lose **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Not verifying that probabilities sum to 1** — KTU examiners frequently award 1 mark for explicitly stating and checking this.
> 3. **Using raw product instead of log-space** for long documents — causes underflow and incorrect answers.
> 4. **Confusing $\text{count}(w, c)$ (total word occurrences across docs) with document frequency** (number of docs containing $w$). The former is correct for **Multinomial NB**; the latter is used in **Bernoulli NB**.
> 5. **Failing to define $V$ explicitly** in worked examples — graders deduct for ambiguity.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Naive Bayes Training**

- **Core Objective:** Find $\hat{\theta}$ that maximizes $\log P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta)$ subject to the conditional independence assumption.
- **Three Estimation Steps:**
  1. **Priors:** $\hat{P}(c) = N_c / N$
  2. **Likelihoods:** $\hat{P}(w \mid c) = \text{count}(w, c) / \sum_{w'} \text{count}(w', c)$
  3. **Smoothing:** Add $\alpha$ to numerator, $\alpha \cdot \vert V \vert$ to denominator.
- **The "Naive" Assumption:** Words are conditionally independent given the class. Enables the product factorization $P(d \mid c) = \prod_i P(w_i \mid c)$.
- **Decision Rule:** $\hat{c} = \arg\max_c \left[ \log P(c) + \sum_i \log P(w_i \mid c) \right]$.
- **Why log-space?** Prevents numerical underflow in long documents.
- **Two NB Variants:** *Multinomial NB* (uses word counts; used in this module) vs. *Bernoulli NB* (uses binary presence/absence).
- **Complexity:** Training is $\mathcal{O}(\vert \mathcal{D} \vert \cdot \vert V \vert)$; inference is $\mathcal{O}(\vert \mathcal{C} \vert \cdot \vert d \vert)$.
- **Zero-Probability Fix:** Always use Laplace smoothing in practice (set $\alpha = 1$ as default).
- **MLE Is Closed-Form:** No gradient descent, no epochs, no learning rate — the parameters are computed by **direct counting**.
- **Smoothing Proof Skill:** Be ready to show $\sum_w P_{\text{Lap}}(w \mid c) = 1$ — it is a frequently asked 2-mark sub-question.
- **Classification Pipeline:** Train (count → priors → likelihoods) → Test (tokenize → score per class → arg max).
- **Vocabulary $\vert V \vert$:** Must be computed **after** tokenization, **before** smoothing. It is the cardinality of the *union* of all unique word types in the corpus.
- **Real-World Deployments:** Spam filtering, sentiment analysis, document classification, medical NLP, author attribution.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation: Precision, Recall, F-measure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT741 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Evaluation: Precision, Recall, F-measure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** and **Information Retrieval (IR)**, evaluation metrics are quantitative measures used to assess the performance of a model against a ground-truth reference. The three cornerstone metrics mandated by the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for Module 1 are **Precision**, **Recall**, and the **F-measure**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEITT741 - Module 1):**
> Evaluation metrics form the empirical backbone of every NLP pipeline. Whether the task is *text classification*, *named entity recognition*, *machine translation*, or *information retrieval*, the same three metrics — Precision, Recall, and F-measure — are the universally accepted yardsticks.

### The Confusion Matrix (Foundation of All Evaluation)

Before defining the metrics, every student must internalize the **Confusion Matrix**, a 2×2 contingency table that captures the four possible outcomes of any binary classification or retrieval decision.

| | **Actually Relevant (Positive)** | **Actually Non-Relevant (Negative)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Predicted / Retrieved (Positive)** | **True Positive (TP)** | **False Positive (FP)** — *Type I Error* |
| **Predicted / Not Retrieved (Negative)** | **False Negative (FN)** — *Type II Error* | **True Negative (TN)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition — Precision (P):**
> Precision is the fraction of **retrieved/predicted positive instances** that are **truly relevant**. It answers the question: *"Of everything the system declared positive, how much was correct?"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition — Recall (R):**
> Recall (also called **Sensitivity** or **True Positive Rate**) is the fraction of **truly relevant instances** that were **successfully retrieved/predicted**. It answers the question: *"Of all the things that should have been found, how many did the system actually find?"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition — F-measure (F):**
> The **F-measure** (specifically **F1-score** when β = 1) is the **harmonic mean of Precision and Recall**. It provides a single scalar score that balances both metrics and is robust against arithmetic-mean distortion caused by extreme values.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **fishing trawler** casting a net into a sea full of two kinds of fish — **Salmon (the targets)** and **Tuna (by-catch)**.

- **Precision** is like asking: *"Of all the fish I hauled up in my net, what fraction are actually Salmon?"* A high-precision net is a *clean net* — it has very little Tuna (by-catch / False Positives).
- **Recall** is like asking: *"Of all the Salmon swimming in the sea, what fraction did my net manage to catch?"* A high-recall net is a *wide net* — it captures almost every Salmon (minimizes False Negatives / escaped Salmon).
- **F-measure** is the **balanced score** that punishes a fisherman who achieves a perfect 100% on one metric by utterly failing the other. It represents a *well-rounded trawler*.

This analogy maps directly onto NLP tasks: a search engine with high precision gives you few irrelevant results, while a search engine with high recall rarely misses a relevant document. The F-measure rewards systems that excel at **both simultaneously**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Formulation

### 2.1.1 Precision (P)

Precision is defined as the ratio of correctly predicted positive observations to the total predicted positive observations.

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

**Range:** $[0, 1]$ where **1.0** = perfect precision, **0.0** = every retrieved item is wrong.

### 2.1.2 Recall (R)

Recall is the ratio of correctly predicted positive observations to all actual positives in the dataset.

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

**Range:** $[0, 1]$ where **1.0** = every relevant item was found.

### 2.1.3 Accuracy (Auxiliary Metric)

Although the syllabus emphasizes P/R/F, examiners frequently compare with **Accuracy**:

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Accuracy is **misleading for imbalanced datasets** (e.g., 99% non-spam emails). A naive "always-no-spam" classifier yields 99% accuracy but 0% recall on the spam class. Precision and Recall are preferred.

### 2.1.4 F-Measure (General Form)

The **Fβ-measure** weights Precision and Recall asymmetrically:
