$$F_\beta = \left(1 + \beta^2\right) \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{\left(\beta^2 \cdot P\right) + R}$$

- When **β = 1** → **F1-score** (equal weight to P and R).
- When **β > 1** → Recall is emphasized (e.g., β = 2 for medical diagnosis).
- When **β < 1** → Precision is emphasized (e.g., β = 0.5 for spam filtering).

### 2.1.5 F1-Score (The Default)

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$$

Equivalently, the harmonic mean:

$$F_1 = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}\right)}$$

## 2.2 Why Harmonic Mean and Not Arithmetic Mean?

The harmonic mean is **penalizing**. Suppose a system has P = 1.0 but R = 0.01.

- Arithmetic mean = **0.505** (looks deceptively decent).
- Harmonic mean (F1) = **0.0198** (correctly reflects catastrophic failure).

This is because the harmonic mean is dominated by the **smaller** value, forcing both metrics to be high for a high F1.

## 2.3 Micro vs. Macro Averaging (Multi-Class Extension)

For multi-class problems (e.g., NER with tags B-PER, I-LOC, O, ...):

**Macro-F1**: Compute F1 per class, then average. Treats all classes equally.

$$\text{Macro-}F_1 = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{C} \vert} \sum_{c \in \mathcal{C}} F_{1,c}$$

**Micro-F1**: Pool all TP, FP, FN across classes, then compute a single F1. Dominated by frequent classes.

$$\text{Micro-}F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \sum_c TP_c}{2 \cdot \sum_c TP_c + \sum_c FP_c + \sum_c FN_c}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Metric** | **Formula** | **Range** | **What It Penalizes** | **Engineering Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Precision (P) | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | False Positives | Spam filters, legal search, medical screening |
| Recall (R) | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | False Negatives | Cancer detection, fraud recall, IR recall |
| Accuracy | $\dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | All errors equally | Balanced datasets only |
| F1-Score | $\dfrac{2PR}{P+R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Imbalance between P & R | General NLP benchmarks |
| Fβ-Score | $(1+\beta^2)\dfrac{PR}{\beta^2 P + R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Weighted imbalance | Domain-specific tuning |
| Macro-F1 | $\dfrac{1}{\vert \mathcal{C}\vert}\sum_c F_{1,c}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Rare-class neglect | CoNLL NER benchmarks |
| Micro-F1 | Pooled TP/FP/FN then F1 | $[0, 1]$ | Frequent-class dominance | Large-scale text classification |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Search Engines (Google, Bing):** Use Precision@k for top-k snippets; use Recall for completeness of result sets.
- **Healthcare NLP:** High Recall is critical — missing a tumor mention is unacceptable; some extra flags (lower Precision) are tolerable.
- **Chatbots / RAG Systems:** F1 is reported for intent classification and slot-filling tasks.
- **Machine Translation (MT):** BLEU is precision-oriented; ROUGE is recall-oriented; both are reported in KTU Module 4.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Harmonic Mean for F1?

**Starting Premise:** We seek a function $F(P, R)$ that satisfies three properties:

1. $F$ is symmetric in $P$ and $R$.
2. $F$ lies between $\min(P, R)$ and $\max(P, R)$.
3. $F = 0$ if either $P = 0$ or $R = 0$.

The **arithmetic mean** violates property (3) — it can give 0.5 when one variable is 0. The **geometric mean** $\sqrt{PR}$ satisfies all three but is hard to interpret additively. The **harmonic mean** emerges naturally from a **weighted reciprocal formulation**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{F_\beta} &= \frac{1}{1+\beta^2}\left(\frac{1}{P} + \frac{\beta^2}{R}\right) \\[6pt]
F_\beta &= \frac{(1+\beta^2) \cdot P \cdot R}{R + \beta^2 P}
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting $\beta = 1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{(1+1) \cdot P \cdot R}{R + 1 \cdot P} \\[4pt]
F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example — Document Retrieval

Suppose an IR system returns **8 documents** for a query, of which **5 are relevant**. The total relevant documents in the collection are **10**.

**Step 1 — Identify the Confusion Matrix entries:**
- TP = documents retrieved AND relevant = **5**
- FP = documents retrieved BUT not relevant = $8 - 5$ = **3**
- FN = documents relevant BUT not retrieved = $10 - 5$ = **5**
- TN = documents neither relevant nor retrieved (large, often ignored in IR)

**Step 2 — Compute Precision:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{5}{5 + 3} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{5}{8} \\[4pt]
  &= 0.625
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Recall:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{5}{5 + 5} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{5}{10} \\[4pt]
  &= 0.500
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute F1-Score:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{2 \cdot 0.625 \cdot 0.500}{0.625 + 0.500} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{0.625}{1.125} \\[4pt]
    &= 0.5556
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret:** Precision of 0.625 means 62.5% of returned docs are relevant; Recall of 0.500 means only half of the relevant docs were retrieved. The F1 of 0.556 reflects an under-performing system.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Systems

Given four candidate NLP classifiers on the same test set, evaluate which is "best" under different criteria:

| **System** | **TP** | **FP** | **FN** | **Precision** | **Recall** | **F1** | **Best For** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sys A (Conservative) | 40 | 5 | 60 | 0.889 | 0.400 | 0.552 | High-precision tasks |
| Sys B (Aggressive) | 90 | 80 | 10 | 0.529 | 0.900 | 0.667 | High-recall tasks |
| Sys C (Balanced) | 70 | 30 | 30 | 0.700 | 0.700 | 0.700 | General-purpose (F1-optimal) |
| Sys D (Random) | 50 | 50 | 50 | 0.500 | 0.500 | 0.500 | Baseline |

**Derivation for Sys C (the textbook example):**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_C &= \frac{70}{70 + 30} = \frac{70}{100} = 0.700 \\[4pt]
R_C &= \frac{70}{70 + 30} = \frac{70}{100} = 0.700 \\[4pt]
F_{1,C} &= \frac{2 \cdot 0.700 \cdot 0.700}{0.700 + 0.700} = \frac{0.980}{1.400} = 0.700
\end{aligned}$$

**Derivation for Sys A's F1:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{1,A} &= \frac{2 \cdot 0.889 \cdot 0.400}{0.889 + 0.400} = \frac{0.7112}{1.289} = 0.552
\end{aligned}$$

This tabular comparison is the **exact format KTU examiners expect** in 14-mark questions.

## 3.4 F1 Derivation from Confusion Matrix (Direct Form)

Sometimes students must compute F1 directly from raw counts:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{2 \cdot \frac{TP}{TP+FP} \cdot \frac{TP}{TP+FN}}{\frac{TP}{TP+FP} + \frac{TP}{TP+FN}} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{\frac{2 \cdot TP^2}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}}{\frac{TP(TP+FN) + TP(TP+FP)}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{2 \cdot TP^2}{TP \cdot \left[(TP+FN) + (TP+FP)\right]} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final elegant form:**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This direct-count formula is the **fastest way** to compute F1 in a KTU exam when only raw TP/FP/FN values are given. Memorize it.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow Diagram — Evaluation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Raw NLP Model Output] --> nodeB[Compare with Gold Standard]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Each Token or Document}
    nodeC -->|Relevant and Retrieved| nodeD[True Positive TP]
    nodeC -->|Not Relevant but Retrieved| nodeE[False Positive FP]
    nodeC -->|Relevant but Missed| nodeF[False Negative FN]
    nodeC -->|Truly Negative| nodeG[True Negative TN]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Compute Counts]
    nodeE --> nodeH
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI[Calculate Precision]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Calculate Recall]
    nodeI --> nodeK[Combine into F1 Score]
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL[Compare Systems / Report Benchmark]
```

## 4.2 Trade-off Block Diagram — Precision vs. Recall

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP[High Precision System]
    nodeR[High Recall System]
    nodeF[F1 Balanced System]
    nodeP -->|Few False Positives| nodePdesc[Conservative Predictions\nReturns only the most confident items]
    nodeR -->|Few False Negatives| nodeRdesc[Aggressive Predictions\nCatches almost every relevant item]
    nodeF -->|Harmonic Balance| nodeFdesc[Optimal Trade-off\nMaximizes 2PR / P+R]
    nodePdesc --> nodeCompare{Evaluation Choice}
    nodeRdesc --> nodeCompare
    nodeFdesc --> nodeCompare
    nodeCompare -->|Select by Task Requirements| nodeFinal[Deploy to Production NLP Pipeline]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Multi-Class F1 Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeInput[Test Set with N Classes] --> nodeSplit[Split by Class Label]
    nodeSplit --> nodeC1[Class 1 Predictions]
    nodeSplit --> nodeC2[Class 2 Predictions]
    nodeSplit --> nodeC3[Class N Predictions]
    nodeC1 --> nodeCM1[Per-Class Confusion Matrix]
    nodeC2 --> nodeCM2[Per-Class Confusion Matrix]
    nodeC3 --> nodeCM3[Per-Class Confusion Matrix]
    nodeCM1 --> nodeF1c1[Compute F1 per Class]
    nodeCM2 --> nodeF1c2[Compute F1 per Class]
    nodeCM3 --> nodeF1c3[Compute F1 per Class]
    nodeF1c1 --> nodeMacro[Macro Averaging\nSimple Mean]
    nodeF1c2 --> nodeMacro
    nodeF1c3 --> nodeMacro
    nodeCM1 --> nodeMicro[Micro Averaging\nPool TP FP FN Globally]
    nodeCM2 --> nodeMicro
    nodeCM3 --> nodeMicro
    nodeMacro --> nodeReport[Final F1 Report]
    nodeMicro --> nodeReport
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This functional architecture is exactly what KTU examiners expect when asking *"Explain how F-measure is computed for a multi-class NLP task."* The two averaging strategies produce different rankings when class distribution is skewed.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]
**Define Precision and Recall in the context of an Information Retrieval system. State one scenario where Precision is more important than Recall.**

**Model Answer:**
Precision is the fraction of retrieved documents that are relevant, given by $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$. Recall is the fraction of relevant documents that are retrieved, given by $R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$.

**Scenario:** In a **legal e-discovery search** where a lawyer needs to find *only* the smoking-gun documents, returning irrelevant documents (low precision) wastes review time. Hence, **Precision** is prioritized over Recall.

*(3 marks: 1 for definitions, 1 for formulas, 1 for scenario justification)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]
**Why is the F-measure computed as a harmonic mean instead of an arithmetic mean?**

**Model Answer:**
The harmonic mean is dominated by the smaller of the two values. If a system has P = 1.0 and R = 0.1, the arithmetic mean yields 0.55 (misleading), while the harmonic mean (F1) yields 0.181 — correctly reflecting that the system is severely failing on one metric. The F1 ensures that both Precision and Recall must be simultaneously high for a high score, preventing systems from "hiding" weakness behind strength.

*(3 marks: 1 for stating the issue, 1 for numerical counter-example, 1 for the conclusion)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Computational & Analytical

**(a)** Consider the output of a binary text classifier on 200 test documents. The confusion matrix is as follows:

| | Actually Positive (Spam) | Actually Negative (Ham) |
|---|---|---|
| **Predicted Spam** | TP = 50 | FP = 10 |
| **Predicted Ham** | FN = 20 | TN = 120 |

Calculate **Precision, Recall, Accuracy, and F1-Score**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A second classifier is evaluated on the same test set and yields P = 0.90, R = 0.30. Compute its F1-Score. Compare it with the first classifier's F1 using a **detailed analytical table** and justify which classifier should be deployed for a spam filter. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step Computation:**

**Step 1 — Extract counts from confusion matrix:**
- TP = 50, FP = 10, FN = 20, TN = 120
- Total = TP + FP + FN + TN = 50 + 10 + 20 + 120 = **200**

**Step 2 — Precision:**

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{50}{50 + 10} = \frac{50}{60} = 0.833$$

**Step 3 — Recall:**

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{50}{50 + 20} = \frac{50}{70} = 0.714$$

**Step 4 — Accuracy:**

$$\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{\text{Total}} = \frac{50 + 120}{200} = \frac{170}{200} = 0.850$$

**Step 5 — F1-Score (using direct formula):**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} = \frac{2 \cdot 50}{100 + 10 + 20} = \frac{100}{130} = 0.769$$

**Part (b) — Comparison with Classifier 2:**

Given P₂ = 0.90, R₂ = 0.30:

$$F_{1,2} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.90 \cdot 0.30}{0.90 + 0.30} = \frac{0.540}{1.20} = 0.450$$

**Analytical Comparison Table:**

| Metric | Classifier 1 | Classifier 2 | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Precision | 0.833 | 0.900 | Classifier 2 |
| Recall | 0.714 | 0.300 | Classifier 1 |
| F1-Score | 0.769 | 0.450 | **Classifier 1** |

**Deployment Justification for Spam Filter:**
A spam filter must avoid two failure modes — letting spam into the inbox (FP) and blocking legitimate emails (FN). For typical email users, **Classifier 1 is preferred** because:
1. Its F1-Score (0.769) is significantly higher, indicating balanced performance.
2. Classifier 2's catastrophic Recall of 0.30 means 70% of spam emails will reach the inbox — defeating the purpose of the filter.
3. While Classifier 2 has marginally better precision (0.90 vs 0.833), the F1-penalty for low recall is decisive.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Students commonly lose **2 to 3 marks** in this 14-mark question by:
> 1. **Mixing up FP and FN** in the confusion matrix — *FP = predicted positive but actually negative*; *FN = predicted negative but actually positive*.
> 2. **Forgetting to show the substitution step** in formulas — KTU valuation requires showing both the generic formula AND the numerical substitution.
> 3. **Giving a vague justification** in part (b) — you must explicitly cite the F1 values and explain the trade-off in the context of the spam-filter domain.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Theoretical & Concept-Focused (Internal Choice)

**(a)** Explain in detail the **F-measure** as a generalization of Precision and Recall. Derive the **Fβ formula** and discuss how the choice of β affects the metric's emphasis. Illustrate with **two real-world NLP scenarios** where different β values are appropriate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **Macro-F1** and **Micro-F1** averaging strategies. Using a hypothetical 3-class Named Entity Recognition task with the following per-class counts, compute both averages and explain which is more appropriate for reporting in the CoNLL-2003 NER benchmark. **(7 marks)**

| Class | TP | FP | FN |
|---|---|---|---|
| PER | 40 | 10 | 5 |
| LOC | 25 | 8 | 12 |
| ORG | 10 | 5 | 25 |

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — F-measure Derivation and Discussion:**

**Derivation:** The F-measure is derived as a weighted harmonic mean. Starting from the requirement that the metric be symmetric, bounded, and zero-penalizing:

$$\frac{1}{F_\beta} = \frac{1}{1+\beta^2}\left(\frac{1}{P} + \frac{\beta^2}{R}\right)$$

Solving for $F_\beta$:

$$F_\beta = \frac{(1+\beta^2) \cdot P \cdot R}{R + \beta^2 \cdot P}$$

**Effect of β:**

- **β → 0:** Fβ → P (precision-dominant)
- **β → ∞:** Fβ → R (recall-dominant)
- **β = 1:** F1 = $\frac{2PR}{P+R}$ (balanced)

**Scenario 1 — β = 0.5 (Precision-Heavy): Spam Detection**
Users are highly intolerant of legitimate emails being marked as spam. A spam classifier should err on the side of caution. Using β = 0.5 punishes false positives more than false negatives.

**Scenario 2 — β = 2 (Recall-Heavy): Medical NLP / Cancer Mention Detection**
Missing a malignant term in clinical notes (FN) is catastrophic; an extra flag (FP) prompts a second review. β = 2 weights Recall twice as much as Precision.

**Part (b) — Macro-F1 vs Micro-F1 Computation:**

**Step 1 — Per-class F1 for each entity:**

**PER class:**
$$F_{1,PER} = \frac{2 \cdot 40}{2 \cdot 40 + 10 + 5} = \frac{80}{95} = 0.842$$

**LOC class:**
$$F_{1,LOC} = \frac{2 \cdot 25}{2 \cdot 25 + 8 + 12} = \frac{50}{70} = 0.714$$

**ORG class:**
$$F_{1,ORG} = \frac{2 \cdot 10}{2 \cdot 10 + 5 + 25} = \frac{20}{50} = 0.400$$

**Step 2 — Macro-F1 (simple mean):**

$$\text{Macro-}F_1 = \frac{0.842 + 0.714 + 0.400}{3} = \frac{1.956}{3} = 0.652$$

**Step 3 — Micro-F1 (global pooling):**

Sum totals: $\Sigma TP = 40+25+10 = 75$; $\Sigma FP = 10+8+5 = 23$; $\Sigma FN = 5+12+25 = 42$.

$$\text{Micro-}F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 75}{2 \cdot 75 + 23 + 42} = \frac{150}{215} = 0.698$$

**Step 4 — Justification for CoNLL-2003 NER:**

The **CoNLL-2003 benchmark** officially reports **Macro-F1** (averaged over 4 entity classes) because:
1. The NER task values **balanced recognition** across all entity types, including rare ones like ORG.
2. Micro-F1 (0.698) is inflated by the dominant PER class; Macro-F1 (0.652) reveals that ORG recognition is poor (F1 = 0.40), which researchers must address.
3. Macro-F1 prevents a system from "winning" by only learning to detect frequent classes.

**Comparison Table:**

| Strategy | Value | Bias / Use |
|---|---|---|
| Macro-F1 | 0.652 | Equal weight per class; preferred for CoNLL NER |
| Micro-F1 | 0.698 | Frequency-weighted; biased toward common classes |

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Precision** answers *"How clean are my predictions?"* — formula: $P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$.
- **Recall** answers *"How complete are my predictions?"* — formula: $R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$.
- **F1-Score** is the harmonic mean — formula: $F_1 = \frac{2PR}{P+R}$ or equivalently $\frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$.
- **Fβ-Score** generalizes F1 with a β parameter: β > 1 favors Recall, β < 1 favors Precision.
- **Harmonic mean is punishing** — it forces both P and R to be high for a high F1; never use arithmetic mean.
- **Macro-F1** = simple mean of per-class F1; treats all classes equally → used in CoNLL NER.
- **Micro-F1** = global pooled F1; dominated by frequent classes → used in large-scale classification.
- **Confusion Matrix** is the prerequisite — always derive TP, FP, FN, TN *first* before computing any metric.
- **Accuracy is misleading** for imbalanced datasets — always report P, R, F1 for NLP.
- **KTU exam tip:** Memorize the direct-count F1 formula $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$ to save calculation time.
- **Domain rule of thumb:** Use **high Recall** for safety-critical NLP (medical, legal), use **high Precision** for user-facing filters (spam, recommendations).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Test Sets and Cross-Validation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT741 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Test Sets and Cross-Validation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In statistical learning and Natural Language Processing (NLP), a **Test Set** is a rigorously held-out, independent sample of labeled data that the learning algorithm has **never seen** during training or hyperparameter tuning. It serves as a *single-source-of-truth* estimator of the **Generalization Error** (also called the **True Risk** or **Out-of-Sample Error**) of the final, fully-trained model.

Mathematically, given a model $\hat{f}$ trained on data $\mathcal{D}_{train}$, the expected prediction error (generalization error) on an unseen test set $\mathcal{D}_{test} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N_{test}}$ drawn from the true underlying data distribution $\mathcal{P}(X, Y)$ is defined as:

$$\mathbb{E}_{(x, y) \sim \mathcal{P}} \big[ L(\hat{f}(x),\, y) \big] \;\approx\; \frac{1}{N_{test}} \sum_{i=1}^{N_{test}} L\big(\hat{f}(x_i),\, y_i\big)$$

where $L(\cdot, \cdot)$ is a chosen loss function (e.g., **0–1 loss**, **cross-entropy loss**, or **squared error**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Validation (CV)** is a *resampling* statistical procedure used to obtain a **low-variance, low-bias estimate** of model generalization performance when the available labeled data is **scarce, expensive to obtain, or class-imbalanced** — a near-universal reality in NLP corpora.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are a B.Tech student preparing for the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE) in NLP.

- **Training Set** → The textbook chapters and solved examples your professor taught you in class. You study these *intensively* and build your understanding from them.
- **Validation Set** → The *tutorial sheets and previous year university questions* (but not the exact ESE paper). You use these to *tune your strategy* — how much time to spend on each question type, which formulas to memorize, which diagrams to practice.
- **Test Set** → The *actual ESE question paper* that you walk into on exam day. You have **never seen these exact questions** before. Your grade on this paper is the *true measure* of how well you have *generalized* the concepts.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Cardinal Rule:** If you ever "peek" at the test set — even once — to make any decision (feature selection, threshold tuning, model selection), it **ceases to be a test set** and becomes contaminated. In KTU practical examinations and real NLP research, this contamination is the #1 cause of *over-optimistic, non-reproducible* results.

### 1.3 The Three-Way Data Partition

A canonical supervised NLP pipeline demands a **three-way split**:

| Partition | Purpose | Typical Size | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Training Set** ($\mathcal{D}_{train}$) | Fit model parameters (weights, embeddings) | **60–80 %** | Gradient descent updates |
| **Validation Set** ($\mathcal{D}_{val}$) | Tune hyperparameters, model selection, early stopping | **10–20 %** | Comparing candidate models |
| **Test Set** ($\mathcal{D}_{test}$) | Final, unbiased generalization estimate | **10–20 %** | One-time final reporting |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU-style NLP project reports, examiners specifically look for an explicit declaration of these splits with **non-overlapping indices** to ensure **reproducibility**.

### 1.4 Why Cross-Validation Exists

When total labeled data $N$ is small (e.g., $N < 10{,}000$ sentences for a niche NLP task like *legal clause classification* or *Malayalam sentiment analysis*), a single 80/20 split is **unstable** — the test score can swing by $\pm 5\%$ just by changing the random seed. Cross-validation solves this by:

1. **Rotating** which fold acts as the test set,
2. **Averaging** performance across all rotations,
3. **Quantifying variance** via the standard deviation across folds.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Statistical Foundation: Why a Single Test Set Fails

A single test score $\hat{R}_{test}$ is itself a **random variable** — it depends on the random sample drawn for testing. For a classifier with true error rate $p$, the test error follows approximately a **Binomial distribution**:

$$\hat{R}_{test} \;\sim\; \text{Binomial}(N_{test}, p) \;\approx\; \mathcal{N}\!\left(p,\; \frac{p(1-p)}{N_{test}}\right)$$

The **standard error** of the test estimate is therefore:

$$SE(\hat{R}_{test}) \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{N_{test}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** To halve the standard error, you must **quadruple** $N_{test}$. This is why, in low-resource NLP, a single held-out test set is statistically inefficient — and why cross-validation's *averaging* across $K$ folds effectively reduces the variance by a factor of $\sqrt{K}$.

### 2.2 K-Fold Cross-Validation — Operational Logic

In **K-Fold CV**, the dataset $\mathcal{D}$ of size $N$ is partitioned into $K$ approximately equal, mutually exclusive folds $\mathcal{F}_1, \mathcal{F}_2, \ldots, \mathcal{F}_K$. For each fold $k \in \{1, \ldots, K\}$:

1. **Train** the model on $\mathcal{D} \setminus \mathcal{F}_k$ (i.e., on the union of the other $K-1$ folds).
2. **Evaluate** the trained model on the held-out fold $\mathcal{F}_k$, producing a score $S_k$.
3. **Repeat** for all $K$ folds.

The **K-Fold Cross-Validation Score** is the arithmetic mean:

$$CV_{(K)} \;=\; \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} S_k$$

The **Variance of the CV estimate** captures fold-to-fold instability:

$$\sigma^2_{CV} \;=\; \frac{1}{K-1} \sum_{k=1}^{K} (S_k - CV_{(K)})^2$$

### 2.3 Common Cross-Validation Variants

| Variant | $K$ Value | Use Case | Bias / Variance Profile |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hold-Out** | $K = 1$ (single split) | Very large datasets ($N > 10^6$) | High variance, low bias |
| **K-Fold** | $K = 5$ or $K = 10$ | General-purpose default | Balanced |
| **Stratified K-Fold** | $K = 5$ or $K = 10$ | **Imbalanced** NLP classification | Preserves class proportions |
| **Leave-One-Out (LOOCV)** | $K = N$ | Tiny datasets ($N < 50$) | Low variance, **high bias** |
| **Leave-P-Out** | $K = \binom{N}{P}$ | Theoretical / asymptotic analysis | Computationally infeasible for large $N$ |
| **Repeated K-Fold** | $K \times R$ | When maximum stability is needed | Lowest variance estimate |
| **Time-Series Split** | Forward-chaining | NLP on temporal corpora (news, tweets) | Respects temporal causality |
| **Nested CV** | Outer + Inner loops | **Hyperparameter selection + unbiased test** | Gold standard for research |

### 2.4 Stratification — Critical for NLP

In NLP tasks like **toxic comment detection**, **rare disease NER**, or **low-resource language identification**, class distributions are often severely skewed (e.g., 1 % positive, 99 % negative). **Stratified K-Fold** ensures each fold preserves the original class ratio $p_c$ for every class $c \in \{1, \ldots, C\}$:

$$\frac{\#\{y_i = c \mid x_i \in \mathcal{F}_k\}}{N/K} \;\approx\; p_c \quad \forall\, k, c$$

### 2.5 The Bias-Variance Tradeoff in CV Choice

The **Bias** of a CV estimator (systematic deviation from the true generalization error on a hypothetical infinite test set) and its **Variance** depend on $K$:

- **Small $K$ (e.g., 2 or 3):** Each training set uses a *small fraction* of total data → the model trained is *slightly worse* than one trained on all data → **optimistic bias** (CV underestimates the true error). However, fewer folds ⇒ less averaging ⇒ **higher variance**.
- **Large $K$ (e.g., LOOCV, $K = N$):** Training sets are *almost* the full data → models are similar to the full-data model → **near-unbiased**. But the folds are *highly correlated* (each pair shares $N-2$ samples) → **high variance** in the estimate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Empirical rule** (supported by multiple asymptotic studies and adopted as default in scikit-learn, KTU lab standard): **$K = 5$ or $K = 10$** offers the best bias-variance compromise for most NLP tasks.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Definition |
|---|---|---|
| $N$ | Total number of labeled examples | $\vert \mathcal{D} \vert$ |
| $K$ | Number of folds | User-defined, typically $5$ or $10$ |
| $N_{fold}$ | Examples per fold | $\lfloor N / K \rfloor$ |
| $S_k$ | Score on the $k$-th held-out fold | e.g., accuracy, F1, BLEU |
| $CV_{(K)}$ | Mean CV score | $\frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} S_k$ |
| $\sigma_{CV}$ | Std-dev of fold scores | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{K-1}\sum_{k}(S_k - CV_{(K)})^2}$ |
| $SE_{CV}$ | Standard error of the mean | $\sigma_{CV} / \sqrt{K}$ |
| 95% CI | Confidence interval for true error | $CV_{(K)} \pm 1.96 \cdot SE_{CV}$ |
| $\mathbb{E}[L]$ | Expected loss (generalization error) | $\mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim\mathcal{P}}[L(\hat{f}(x), y)]$ |
| $L_{0/1}$ | 0-1 classification loss | $\mathbb{1}[\hat{y} \neq y]$ |

> [!TIP]
> Always report the **95 % confidence interval** $CV_{(K)} \pm 1.96 \cdot \sigma_{CV}/\sqrt{K}$ in your KTU NLP project reports. Examiners reward statistical rigor.

### 2.7 Real-World Utility in NLP Engineering

| NLP Application | CV Strategy Used |
|---|---|
| **Sentiment Analysis** (movie reviews) | Stratified 10-Fold |
| **Machine Translation** (English↔Malayalam) | Time-Series Split (if year-stamped) |
| **Spam Detection** (SMS / email) | Stratified 5-Fold |
| **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** | K-Fold with token-level stratification |
| **Question Answering (SQuAD-like)** | Group K-Fold (to avoid answer leakage across paragraphs) |
| **Language Model Pre-training** | Hold-out (datasets are too large for CV) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Variance Reduction via K-Fold Averaging

**Goal:** Show mathematically that the *variance* of the K-Fold CV estimate is smaller than that of a single hold-out split.

**Setup.** Let each fold score $S_k$ be an estimator of the true generalization error $R$, with each $S_k$ having:
- **Bias** $b = \mathbb{E}[S_k] - R$
- **Variance** $\sigma^2 = \text{Var}(S_k)$
- **Pairwise correlation** $\rho$ between any two fold scores (they share training data).

**Step 1.** The K-Fold CV estimator is the sample mean:

$$\hat{R}_{CV} \;=\; \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} S_k$$

**Step 2.** Compute its expectation (to confirm bias preservation):

$$\mathbb{E}[\hat{R}_{CV}] \;=\; \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbb{E}[S_k] \;=\; \frac{1}{K} \cdot K \cdot (R + b) \;=\; R + b$$

The bias is identical to the bias of a single fold — CV does *not* reduce bias.

**Step 3.** Compute the variance using the identity $\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{K}\big(1 + (K-1)\rho\big)$:

$$\text{Var}(\hat{R}_{CV}) \;=\; \frac{\sigma^2}{K}\big(1 + (K-1)\rho\big)$$

**Step 4.** Interpret: if folds were independent ($\rho = 0$), variance would be $\sigma^2/K$ — a $K$-fold reduction. But because folds share $\sim (K-2)/K$ of training data, $\rho > 0$ and the reduction is *less* than $K$-fold but still *substantial* for typical $K = 5$ or $K = 10$.

**Step 5.** Special case — **LOOCV** ($K = N$): Each training set shares $N-2$ samples with every other ⇒ $\rho$ is *very high* ⇒ LOOCV variance can be *higher* than 10-Fold CV. This is a classic counterintuitive result.

$$\text{Var}(\hat{R}_{LOOCV}) \;=\; \sigma^2 \big(1 + (N-1)\rho_{LOO}\big) \;\gg\; \frac{\sigma^2}{10}\big(1 + 9\rho_{10}\big)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion for KTU exams:** Always use $K = 5$ or $K = 10$ unless you have a *very specific* reason (e.g., $N < 50$) to use LOOCV.

### 3.2 Derivation: Bias of Hold-Out vs. Full-Data Training

**Setup.** A model trained on $m$ samples has true error $R(m)$ that *decreases* with $m$ (more data ⇒ better model). Suppose $R(m) = R_\infty + c/m$ for some constant $c > 0$, where $R_\infty$ is the asymptotic error on infinite data.

**Step 1.** In **Hold-Out** with fraction $\alpha$ for training, $m = \alpha N$, so the model has true error:

$$R_{hold\text{-}out} \;=\; R_\infty + \frac{c}{\alpha N}$$

**Step 2.** The CV estimate uses models trained on $(K-1)N/K$, with effective $\alpha_{CV} = (K-1)/K$. So:

$$R_{CV} \;=\; R_\infty + \frac{cK}{(K-1)N}$$

**Step 3.** The **optimistic bias** of Hold-Out compared to a hypothetical full-data model:

$$\text{Bias}_{hold\text{-}out} \;=\; R_{hold\text{-}out} - R_\infty \;=\; \frac{c}{\alpha N}$$

**Step 4.** The **bias of CV** (since CV scores are measured on held-out folds, *not* on the training data):

$$\text{Bias}_{CV} \;\approx\; \frac{cK}{(K-1)N} \;-\; \text{optimism correction}$$

**Step 5.** A common **bias-correction** (Webb 2000) estimates the *full-data* performance from K-Fold:

$$\hat{R}_{full} \;\approx\; \hat{R}_{CV} \cdot \frac{1}{\alpha_{CV}} \;\approx\; \hat{R}_{CV} \cdot \frac{K}{K-1}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right CV Strategy

| Scenario | Recommended Strategy | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| $N > 100{,}000$, IID data | Single 80/10/10 split + Hold-Out | CV is computationally wasteful |
| $N \in [1{,}000,\; 100{,}000]$, balanced classes | 10-Fold CV | Best bias-variance tradeoff |
| $N \in [1{,}000,\; 100{,}000]$, imbalanced classes | **Stratified** 10-Fold | Preserves rare-class prevalence |
| $N < 1{,}000$ | 5-Fold or 10-Fold + Repeated (3×) | Maximizes information per fold |
| $N < 50$ | LOOCV or Leave-P-Out | Cannot afford to lose 10–20 % for testing |
| Time-series / streaming text | Forward-Chaining CV | Prevents temporal leakage |
| Hyperparameter selection **and** unbiased test | **Nested CV** | Outer loop = test; inner loop = validation |
| Grouped data (e.g., one document = many sentences) | **Group K-Fold** | Prevents group leakage |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** An NLP classifier for *Malayalam fake-news detection* is evaluated using **5-Fold Stratified Cross-Validation**. The per-fold F1-scores are:

$$S_1 = 0.86,\; S_2 = 0.84,\; S_3 = 0.88,\; S_4 = 0.85,\; S_5 = 0.87$$

**Compute the CV mean, standard deviation, standard error, and 95 % CI.**

**Step 1 — Mean:**

$$CV_{(5)} \;=\; \frac{0.86 + 0.84 + 0.88 + 0.85 + 0.87}{5} \;=\; \frac{4.30}{5} \;=\; 0.860$$

**Step 2 — Variance (sample, denominator $K-1 = 4$):**

$$\sigma^2_{CV} \;=\; \frac{1}{4}\left[(0.86-0.86)^2 + (0.84-0.86)^2 + (0.88-0.86)^2 + (0.85-0.86)^2 + (0.87-0.86)^2\right]$$

**Step 3 — Expand each term:**

$$\sigma^2_{CV} \;=\; \frac{1}{4}\big[0.0000 + 0.0004 + 0.0004 + 0.0001 + 0.0001\big] \;=\; \frac{0.0010}{4} \;=\; 0.00025$$

**Step 4 — Standard deviation:**

$$\sigma_{CV} \;=\; \sqrt{0.00025} \;\approx\; 0.01581$$

**Step 5 — Standard error of the mean:**

$$SE_{CV} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{CV}}{\sqrt{K}} \;=\; \frac{0.01581}{\sqrt{5}} \;\approx\; 0.00707$$

**Step 6 — 95 % Confidence Interval:**

$$CI_{95\%} \;=\; CV_{(5)} \pm 1.96 \cdot SE_{CV} \;=\; 0.860 \pm 1.96 \times 0.00707 \;\approx\; 0.860 \pm 0.0139$$

$$\boxed{CI_{95\%} \;=\; [0.846,\; 0.874]}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Final Report Statement (KTU-ready):** *"The proposed model achieves a 5-Fold Stratified CV F1-score of **0.860 (95 % CI: 0.846 – 0.874)**, indicating stable and reliable performance across folds."*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Data Partitioning — Train / Validation / Test Split

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["FULL LABELED CORPUS D of size N"] --> B["RANDOM SHUFFLE seed=42"]
    B --> C["TRAINING SET 70 percent N=7000"]
    B --> D["VALIDATION SET 15 percent N=1500"]
    B --> E["TEST SET 15 percent N=1500 HELD OUT LOCKED"]

    C --> C1["Model Parameter Learning Weights Embeddings"]
    D --> D1["Hyperparameter Tuning Model Selection Early Stopping"]
    E --> E1["FINAL UNBIASED GENERALIZATION ESTIMATE one-time use"]

    E1 --> F["REPORTED METRIC e.g. F1 = 0.87"]
    F --> G{"Test Set Reused?"}
    G -- "NO" --> H["Valid Result Published"]
    G -- "YES" --> I["CONTAMINATION Result Invalid"]
```

### 4.2 K-Fold Cross-Validation Architecture (K = 5)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DATASET["FULL DATASET D size N"]
        direction TB
        F1["FOLD 1 N1 samples"]
        F2["FOLD 2 N2 samples"]
        F3["FOLD 3 N3 samples"]
        F4["FOLD 4 N4 samples"]
        F5["FOLD 5 N5 samples"]
    end

    subgraph ITER1["ITERATION k=1"]
        T1["TRAIN F2+F3+F4+F5"]
        V1["TEST FOLD 1"]
        S1["Score S1"]
    end

    subgraph ITER2["ITERATION k=2"]
        T2["TRAIN F1+F3+F4+F5"]
        V2["TEST FOLD 2"]
        S2["Score S2"]
    end

    subgraph ITER3["ITERATION k=3"]
        T3["TRAIN F1+F2+F4+F5"]
        V3["TEST FOLD 3"]
        S3["Score S3"]
    end

    subgraph ITER4["ITERATION k=4"]
        T4["TRAIN F1+F2+F3+F5"]
        V4["TEST FOLD 4"]
        S4["Score S4"]
    end

    subgraph ITER5["ITERATION k=5"]
        T5["TRAIN F1+F2+F3+F4"]
        V5["TEST FOLD 5"]
        S5["Score S5"]
    end

    F1 --> V1
    F2 --> V2
    F3 --> V3
    F4 --> V4
    F5 --> V5

    S1 --> AGG["MEAN CV = S1+S2+S3+S4+S5 over 5"]
    S2 --> AGG
    S3 --> AGG
    S4 --> AGG
    S5 --> AGG
    AGG --> OUT["FINAL CV SCORE plus minus 95 percent CI"]
```

### 4.3 Bias-Variance Tradeoff in Choice of K

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q["CHOOSE NUMBER OF FOLDS K"] --> A["K = 2 Small"]
    Q --> B["K = 5 or 10 SWEET SPOT"]
    Q --> C["K = N LOOCV Large"]

    A --> A1["LOW BIAS? NO optimistic bias"]
    A --> A2["HIGH VARIANCE few folds to average"]
    A --> A3["USE WHEN N is very large"]

    B --> B1["BALANCED BIAS AND VARIANCE"]
    B --> B2["STABLE ESTIMATE"]
    B --> B3["DEFAULT RECOMMENDATION"]

    C --> C1["NEAR UNBIASED"]
    C --> C2["HIGH VARIANCE correlated folds"]
    C --> C3["USE ONLY WHEN N is small under 50"]
```

### 4.4 Nested Cross-Validation — Hyperparameter Selection + Unbiased Test

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OUTER["OUTER LOOP 5 Folds for UNBIASED TESTING"]
    OUTER --> O1["Outer Fold 1"]
    OUTER --> O2["Outer Fold 2"]
    OUTER --> O3["Outer Fold 3"]
    OUTER --> O4["Outer Fold 4"]
    OUTER --> O5["Outer Fold 5"]

    O1 --> I1["INNER LOOP 5 Folds for HYPERPARAMETER TUNING"]
    I1 --> I1A["Inner Train Inner Validate pick best LR reg dropout"]
    I1A --> I1B["Retrain on Outer-Train with best HP"]
    I1B --> I1C["Evaluate on Outer-Test-Fold-1 yields T1"]

    O2 --> I2["Inner Loop for H2"]
    I2 --> I2A["Evaluate on Outer-Test-Fold-2 yields T2"]

    O3 --> I3["Inner Loop for H3"]
    I3 --> I3A["Evaluate on Outer-Test-Fold-3 yields T3"]

    O4 --> I4["Inner Loop for H4"]
    I4 --> I4A["Evaluate on Outer-Test-Fold-4 yields T4"]

    O5 --> I5["Inner Loop for H5"]
    I5 --> I5A["Evaluate on Outer-Test-Fold-5 yields T5"]

    I1C --> FINAL["FINAL UNBIASED SCORE mean of T1 to T5"]
    I2A --> FINAL
    I3A --> FINAL
    I4A --> FINAL
    I5A --> FINAL
```

### 4.5 Test Set Contamination — The "Data Leak" Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D["Original Dataset D"] --> S["Split D into Train Val Test"]
    S --> TR["Train Set"]
    S --> VA["Validation Set"]
    S --> TE["Test Set LOCKED"]

    TR --> M1["Train Model M1"]
    VA --> M1
    M1 --> P1["Report Validation Score 0.92"]

    P1 --> DEC{"Tune again?"}
    DEC -- "NO" --> TE2["Test on Locked Test Set ONCE"]
    TE2 --> R1["Report Test Score 0.89 VALID"]

    DEC -- "YES leak" --> TE3["Peek at Test Set to Tune"]
    TE3 --> M2["Retrain Model M2 with Test info"]
    M2 --> TE4["Re-test on Test Set"]
    TE4 --> R2["Report Test Score 0.97 CONTAMINATED UNRELIABLE"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
*Define a "test set" in the context of supervised NLP. Why must it be strictly held out from the training and validation phases?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **test set** is a labeled subset of the corpus, mutually exclusive from the training and validation sets, used **once** at the end of model development to obtain an **unbiased estimate of the generalization error** on unseen data.
It must be held out because:
1. **Statistical independence** ensures the test score reflects true out-of-sample performance, not memorization (1 mark).
2. **Decision contamination**: any peek at the test set for model selection, feature engineering, or threshold tuning causes the test score to become optimistically biased (1 mark).
3. **Reproducibility**: a locked test set (preferably with a public hash or held by a third party) is the only mechanism that allows independent verification of NLP research claims (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Style]**
*Differentiate between a **validation set** and a **test set**. Can the same data serve both purposes? Justify your answer.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Validation Set | Test Set |
|---|---|---|
| **Used during** | Hyperparameter tuning, model selection | Final evaluation only |
| **Times accessed** | Multiple times | **Exactly once** |
| **Purpose** | Compare candidate models | Estimate true generalization |
| **Risk if reused** | Mild overfitting to val | Severe optimism / leakage |

**Justification (1 mark):** No, the same data **cannot** serve both purposes. If used for both tuning and final reporting, the reported score is **biased upward** because the model was implicitly selected to maximize performance on that very data — violating the principle of independence between model selection and evaluation.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, both questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.**

---

#### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Theory + Bias-Variance*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain **K-Fold Cross-Validation** in detail. Describe the algorithm, state the formula for the CV score, and discuss how the choice of $K$ affects the **bias and variance** of the estimator. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A research team trains a sentiment classifier on $N = 1{,}200$ Malayalam product reviews. They perform **5-Fold CV** and obtain fold-wise accuracies: $S = [0.81,\; 0.78,\; 0.85,\; 0.80,\; 0.83]$. Compute the **mean CV accuracy, standard deviation, standard error, and the 95 % confidence interval**. Interpret whether the result is statistically reliable. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer for Q.A(a) — 7 marks:**

**Algorithm (3 marks):**
1. Shuffle the dataset $\mathcal{D}$ randomly.
2. Partition $\mathcal{D}$ into $K$ equal, non-overlapping folds $\mathcal{F}_1, \ldots, \mathcal{F}_K$.
3. For $k = 1, 2, \ldots, K$:
   - Train model on $\mathcal{D} \setminus \mathcal{F}_k$.
   - Evaluate on $\mathcal{F}_k$, record score $S_k$ (accuracy / F1 / BLEU).
4. Compute $CV_{(K)} = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K} S_k$.

**CV Score Formula (1 mark):**

$$CV_{(K)} \;=\; \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} S_k$$

**Bias-Variance Analysis (3 marks):**
- **Bias:** Each fold trains on $(K-1)/K$ fraction of data, so the model is *slightly worse* than one trained on full data ⇒ $CV$ is mildly **optimistic**. As $K$ increases, $(K-1)/K \to 1$, so **bias decreases**.
- **Variance:** $Var(\hat{R}_{CV}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{K}(1 + (K-1)\rho)$. As $K$ increases, the $\frac{1}{K}$ factor shrinks but $\rho$ (inter-fold correlation) grows. **Variance has a U-shape** with minimum near $K = 5$ or $K = 10$.
- **Conclusion:** $K = 5$ or $K = 10$ is the *empirical sweet spot* that minimizes mean squared error of the estimator.

---

**Model Answer for Q.A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Mean (2 marks):**

$$\bar{S} \;=\; \frac{0.81 + 0.78 + 0.85 + 0.80 + 0.83}{5} \;=\; \frac{4.07}{5} \;=\; 0.814$$

**Step 2 — Variance (2 marks):**

$$\sigma^2 \;=\; \frac{1}{K-1}\sum_{k=1}^{K}(S_k - \bar{S})^2$$

| Fold $k$ | $S_k$ | $S_k - \bar{S}$ | $(S_k - \bar{S})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.81 | $-0.004$ | $0.000016$ |
| 2 | 0.78 | $-0.034$ | $0.001156$ |
| 3 | 0.85 | $+0.036$ | $0.001296$ |
| 4 | 0.80 | $-0.014$ | $0.000196$ |
| 5 | 0.83 | $+0.016$ | $0.000256$ |
| **Sum** | — | — | $0.002920$ |

$$\sigma^2 \;=\; \frac{0.002920}{4} \;=\; 0.00073$$

**Step 3 — Std-Dev & SE (1 mark):**

$$\sigma \;=\; \sqrt{0.00073} \;\approx\; 0.0270$$

$$SE \;=\; \frac{0.0270}{\sqrt{5}} \;\approx\; 0.0121$$

**Step 4 — 95 % CI & Interpretation (2 marks):**

$$CI_{95\%} \;=\; 0.814 \pm 1.96 \times 0.0121 \;\approx\; 0.814 \pm 0.0237$$

$$\boxed{CI_{95\%} \;\approx\; [0.790,\; 0.838]}$$

**Interpretation:** The narrow CI ($\pm 2.4\%$) and the low fold variance indicate the model is **stable and statistically reliable**. If the team had compared against a baseline that achieves 0.78 accuracy, the difference (0.034) is **larger than the CI half-width (0.024)**, suggesting a *likely* significant improvement — but a paired t-test or McNemar's test should be performed for formal significance.

---

#### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice: Stratified & Time-Series*

**(a)** What is **Stratified K-Fold Cross-Validation**? Why is it essential for **imbalanced NLP classification tasks** (e.g., hate-speech detection with 2 % positive class)? Explain with reference to the class-distribution preservation constraint. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** You are building a **news article topic classifier** on a dataset of articles published between 2015 and 2024. Why is standard K-Fold CV *unsuitable* here? Propose a suitable CV strategy, sketch its data-splitting scheme, and justify your choice. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer for Q.B(a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Stratified K-Fold CV is a variant in which each fold $\mathcal{F}_k$ is constructed to preserve the **class proportion** $p_c$ of the original dataset for every class $c \in \{1, \ldots, C\}$.

**Class-Preservation Constraint (2 marks):**

$$\frac{\vert\{x_i \in \mathcal{F}_k : y_i = c\}\vert}{N/K} \;\approx\; p_c \quad \forall\, k \in \{1,\ldots,K\},\; \forall\, c$$

**Why essential for imbalanced NLP (3 marks):**
1. In hate-speech / fraud / rare-disease detection, the positive class may be $\leq 5\%$. With random K-Fold, some folds may contain **zero positive examples** ⇒ F1, recall become **undefined (0/0)**.
2. Stratification **guarantees every fold has at least $\lfloor N \cdot p_c / K \rfloor$ positive examples** ⇒ all metrics are computable in every fold.
3. It **reduces variance** of the F1 estimate across folds, giving a more reliable model comparison.
4. In KTU project evaluations, stratified CV is the **expected default** for any classification report.

---

**Model Answer for Q.B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Why standard K-Fold is unsuitable (3 marks):**
Random shuffling **destroys the temporal order** of articles. This causes **temporal data leakage**: the model is trained on 2023 articles and tested on 2018 articles (or vice-versa). A model that "cheats" by learning *future* vocabulary or topic trends will appear artificially good but will fail catastrophically in real deployment where the goal is to predict the *future from the past*.

**Proposed strategy: Forward-Chaining Time-Series CV (3 marks):**

| Split | Train On | Validate On | Test On |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2015–2017 | 2018 | 2019 |
| 2 | 2015–2018 | 2019 | 2020 |
| 3 | 2015–2019 | 2020 | 2021 |
| 4 | 2015–2020 | 2021 | 2022 |
| 5 | 2015–2021 | 2022 | 2023 |

Each successive training window **expands** (cumulative past) and the test window always **lies in the future** — exactly simulating real production deployment.

**Justification (1 mark):** This strategy respects causality, provides $K = 5$ reliable estimates, and yields a deployment-realistic estimate of the model's forward-looking generalization error.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing validation with test set** (–2 marks): Many students *report* validation accuracy as "test accuracy". Always label clearly.
> 2. **Forgetting the 95 % CI** (–1 mark): KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly rewards statistical reporting. Reporting only a point estimate is incomplete.
> 3. **Using $K = N$ (LOOCV) blindly** (–2 marks): For $N > 100$, LOOCV is *worse* than 10-Fold in variance. Examiners will deduct marks for unjustified LOOCV.
> 4. **Ignoring class imbalance** (–2 marks): Using plain K-Fold on a 98/2 split without stratification is a *direct violation* of best practice.
> 5. **Re-using the test set for model selection** (–3 marks): The most severe error; examiners may treat this as academic misconduct in lab reports.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Test Set = One-time, locked, unbiased estimator of generalization error.** Never use it for any decision.
- **Validation Set = Repeatedly used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection.** It is "spent" and *not* a substitute for the test set.
- **K-Fold CV** rotates the test fold $K$ times and averages; **always report $CV_{(K)} \pm 1.96 \cdot \sigma_{CV}/\sqrt{K}$** as the 95 % CI.
- **Choice of $K$:** $K = 5$ or $K = 10$ is the default. LOOCV ($K = N$) is *low-bias but high-variance* — use only for very small datasets.
- **Stratified K-Fold** is **mandatory** for imbalanced classification — preserves $p_c$ in every fold.
- **Time-Series / Temporal data** requires **Forward-Chaining CV** — never shuffle temporally ordered text.
- **Nested CV** is the gold standard when you need both *hyperparameter tuning* and an *unbiased test estimate* — outer loop tests, inner loop tunes.
- **Group K-Fold** prevents *group leakage* when one document/user/conversation contributes multiple samples.
- **Bias-Variance Tradeoff:** Small $K$ ⇒ low-bias? No, high-bias (small training set). Large $K$ ⇒ low-bias, high-variance (correlated folds). The sweet spot is $K \approx 5{-}10$.
- **Reproducibility mantra for KTU NLP reports:** Fix random seeds, declare splits explicitly, lock the test set with a hash, report confidence intervals.
- **Anti-contamination pledge:** *"The test set was accessed exactly once, after all model selection was complete."* — write this verbatim in your KTU project reports.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: POS Tagging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT741 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** POS Tagging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging** (also called *grammatical tagging* or *word-category disambiguation*) is the process of assigning a categorical lexical class label — known as a **POS tag** — to every token in a natural language corpus. Formally, given a sentence consisting of a sequence of words $W = (w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n)$, POS tagging seeks the most probable tag sequence $T^* = (t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n)$ such that:

$$T^* = \arg\max_{T \in \mathcal{T}^n} \; P(T \mid W)$$

where $\mathcal{T}$ is the closed tag vocabulary drawn from a predefined **tagset** (e.g., the **Penn Treebank** contains **45 tags**; the **Brown Corpus** uses **87 fine-grained tags**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** POS Tagging is listed as a core sub-topic under Module 2 (Statistical and Machine Learning Approaches to NLP) of PEITT741. Students are expected to demonstrate working knowledge of rule-based, stochastic (HMM/MEMM), and neural taggers along with the evaluation metric **tagging accuracy ≈ 95–97%** on benchmark corpora.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tagset Snapshot — Penn Treebank (Selected Tags):**
> - `NN` — Singular Noun
> - `NNS` — Plural Noun
> - `VBZ` — 3rd Person Singular Present Verb
> - `VBG` — Gerund / Present Participle
> - `JJ` — Adjective
> - `RB` — Adverb
> - `DT` — Determiner
> - `IN` — Preposition / Subordinating Conjunction
> - `PRP` — Personal Pronoun
> - `.` — Punctuation

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a school teacher correcting essays. As she reads each word, she mentally whispers: *"This is a noun… this is a verb… this is an adjective."* She is not rewriting the essay — she is merely **painting a colored label** over every word so that the grammatical structure becomes visible.

POS tagging is precisely this: the computer reads a raw sentence and, for every token, decides which grammatical "color" best fits, using both:
1. **The word itself** (its dictionary / lexicon likelihood), and
2. **The context around it** (what tags the neighboring words want).

### Why does context matter?
The word *"book"* can be:
- A **noun** → "I read a **book**."
- A **verb** → "Please **book** my ticket."

A naive dictionary lookup cannot disambiguate. Therefore, modern POS taggers are **sequence models** that look at the whole sentence jointly.

## 1.3 Why POS Tagging Matters in Real-World NLP

| Downstream Application | Role of POS Tags |
|---|---|
| **Machine Translation** | Reordering rules depend on grammatical roles |
| **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** | Proper Nouns (`NNP`) seed entity detectors |
| **Speech Synthesis (TTS)** | POS governs pronunciation & stress (e.g., `record` noun vs verb) |
| **Information Extraction** | Verbs anchor predicate–argument structures |
| **Parsing** | POS tags are the input features to constituency/dependency parsers |
| **Sentiment Analysis** | Adjectives (`JJ`) are primary polarity carriers |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of POS Tagging Approaches

POS taggers are conventionally classified into four generations:

### (a) **Rule-Based Tagger**
- Uses handcrafted linguistic rules operating on morphological or lexical patterns.
- **Example:** *ENGTWOL* (English Constraint Grammar) by Voutilainen; *EngCG*.
- Pipeline: Morphological lexicon lookup → Constraint rules (e.g., *"If the previous word is a determiner, this word is unlikely to be a determiner"*).
- **Accuracy range:** ≈ **90–93%** on Penn Treebank.

### (b) **Stochastic / Probabilistic Tagger**
- Learns probabilities from annotated corpora.
- Two main families:
  1. **Generative** — Hidden Markov Model (HMM).
  2. **Discriminative** — Maximum Entropy Markov Model (MEMM), Conditional Random Field (CRF).
- **Accuracy range:** ≈ **93–97%**.

### (c) **Transformation-Based (TBL) Tagger**
- Introduced by **Eric Brill (1995)**.
- Starts with the most-frequent tag for each word; iteratively learns **transformation rules** that correct errors.
- Combines the strengths of rule-based (interpretability) and stochastic (data-driven) approaches.

### (d) **Neural / Deep Learning Tagger**
- Bi-LSTM, Bi-LSTM-CRF, Transformers (BERT-based).
- Current state-of-the-art: ≈ **97–98%** on Penn Treebank.

## 2.2 Hidden Markov Model (HMM) Formulation — The Workhorse of Statistical POS Tagging

HMM is a **generative probabilistic model** in which:
- The **observed sequence** = the words $W = (w_1, \ldots, w_n)$.
- The **hidden states** = the POS tags $T = (t_1, \ldots, t_n)$.
- Two fundamental independence assumptions:
  1. **Markov Assumption:** $P(t_i \mid t_1,\ldots,t_{i-1}) = P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$.
  2. **Output Independence:** $P(w_i \mid t_1,\ldots,t_i,\ldots,t_n,w_1,\ldots,w_n) = P(w_i \mid t_i)$.

### Core Probabilistic Components

| Symbol | Name | Empirical Estimation |
|---|---|---|
| $P(t_i)$ | **Prior / Initial Probability** | $\dfrac{C(t_i)}{\sum_j C(t_j)}$ |
| $P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$ | **Transition Probability** | $\dfrac{C(t_{i-1}, t_i)}{C(t_{i-1})}$ |
| $P(w_i \mid t_i)$ | **Emission / Likelihood Probability** | $\dfrac{C(t_i, w_i)}{C(t_i)}$ |
| $v_t(j)$ | **Viterbi Score** at time $t$, state $j$ | Recursive — see below |

### Bayesian Reformulation

Using **Bayes' Rule**:

$$P(T \mid W) = \frac{P(W \mid T) \cdot P(T)}{P(W)}$$

Since $P(W)$ is constant w.r.t. $T$, maximizing $P(T \mid W)$ is equivalent to maximizing $P(W \mid T) \cdot P(T)$. Under the HMM independence assumptions:

$$\hat{T} = \arg\max_{T} \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid t_i) \cdot P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$$

with the convention that $P(t_1 \mid t_0) \equiv P(t_1)$.

### Log-Space Trick (Numerical Stability)

To prevent **underflow**, the product is converted to a sum in log space:

$$\hat{T} = \arg\max_{T} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ \log P(w_i \mid t_i) + \log P(t_i \mid t_{i-1}) \right]$$

## 2.3 The Viterbi Algorithm — Dynamic Programming Decoder

The Viterbi algorithm computes the **single best tag sequence** in $O(n \cdot \vert \mathcal{T} \vert^2)$ time, avoiding the exponential blowup of brute-force search.

### Recurrence Relation

$$v_t(j) = \max_{i \in \mathcal{T}} \; v_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{i,j} \cdot b_j(w_t)$$

where:
- $a_{i,j} = P(t_j \mid t_i)$ — transition probability from state $i$ to state $j$.
- $b_j(w_t) = P(w_t \mid t_j)$ — emission probability of word $w_t$ given state $j$.

### Backpointer

$$\text{bp}_t(j) = \arg\max_{i \in \mathcal{T}} \; v_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{i,j}$$

The best final state is:

$$\hat{t}_n = \arg\max_{j \in \mathcal{T}} v_n(j)$$

Tags are then recovered by **backtracking** through $\text{bp}_t$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Equation | Meaning | Common Use |
|---|---|---|
| $\hat{T} = \arg\max_T P(T \mid W)$ | Fundamental tagging objective | Definition |
| $\hat{T} = \arg\max_T \prod_i P(w_i \mid t_i) P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$ | HMM joint likelihood | Stochastic tagging |
| $P(t_i \mid t_{i-1}) = \dfrac{C(t_{i-1},t_i)}{C(t_{i-1})}$ | Transition probability estimation | MLE from corpus |
| $P(w_i \mid t_i) = \dfrac{C(t_i, w_i)}{C(t_i)}$ | Emission probability estimation | MLE from corpus |
| $v_t(j) = \max_i \, v_{t-1}(i) a_{i,j} b_j(w_t)$ | Viterbi recurrence | Decoding best sequence |
| $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{\text{Correctly tagged tokens}}{\text{Total tokens}}$ | Tagging accuracy metric | Evaluation |
| **Laplace Smoothing** | $P(w \mid t) = \dfrac{C(t,w)+1}{C(t)+\vert V \vert}$ | Handle unseen words |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** HMM-based POS taggers form the foundation of **bio-informatics gene finders** (e.g., GENSCAN), **speech recognition acoustic decoders**, and **handwriting recognition pipelines**, where the same forward–Viterbi mathematical machinery is reused.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Bayes' Theorem to the Viterbi Recurrence

We start from the maximum a posteriori (MAP) objective and rewrite it using HMM assumptions.

**Step 1 — Write the joint probability of $(W, T)$.**

By the chain rule of probability:

$$P(W, T) = P(w_1, \ldots, w_n, t_1, \ldots, t_n)$$

**Step 2 — Expand using the chain rule ordered by tags then words:**

$$P(W, T) = P(t_1) \cdot P(w_1 \mid t_1) \cdot \prod_{i=2}^{n} P(t_i \mid t_{i-1}) \cdot P(w_i \mid t_i)$$

**Step 3 — Apply Bayes' theorem (since $P(W)$ is constant):**

$$\hat{T} = \arg\max_T P(T \mid W) = \arg\max_T \frac{P(W \mid T) P(T)}{P(W)} = \arg\max_T P(W \mid T) P(T)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 3:**

$$\hat{T} = \arg\max_T \left[ P(t_1) P(w_1 \mid t_1) \prod_{i=2}^{n} P(t_i \mid t_{i-1}) P(w_i \mid t_i) \right]$$

**Step 5 — Convert the product to a log-sum for numerical safety:**

$$\hat{T} = \arg\max_T \left[ \log P(t_1) + \log P(w_1 \mid t_1) + \sum_{i=2}^{n} \left(\log P(t_i \mid t_{i-1}) + \log P(w_i \mid t_i)\right) \right]$$

**Step 6 — Define the Viterbi score $v_i(j)$ as the maximum log-probability of all paths ending at state $j$ at position $i$:**

$$v_i(j) = \max_{t_1,\ldots,t_{i-1}} \log P(t_1, \ldots, t_{i-1}, t_i = j, w_1, \ldots, w_i)$$

**Step 7 — Derive the recurrence by separating the last transition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_i(j) &= \max_{k} \Big[ v_{i-1}(k) + \log P(t_i = j \mid t_{i-1} = k) + \log P(w_i \mid t_i = j) \Big] \\
&= \max_{k} \Big[ v_{i-1}(k) + \log a_{k,j} + \log b_j(w_i) \Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Initialization and termination:**

$$v_1(j) = \log P(t_1 = j) + \log P(w_1 \mid t_1 = j), \qquad \hat{t}_n = \arg\max_j v_n(j)$$

Backtracking recovers $\hat{T}$ in $O(n)$ time using stored backpointers.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example (Mini-Corpus HMM)

Suppose a tiny tagset $\mathcal{T} = \{\text{N}, \text{V}\}$ for Noun / Verb, and the training counts yield the following tables:

| Transition | $P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$ | Value |
|---|---|---|
| N → N | $\dfrac{3}{5}$ | **0.60** |
| N → V | $\dfrac{2}{5}$ | **0.40** |
| V → N | $\dfrac{1}{3}$ | **0.33** |
| V → V | $\dfrac{2}{3}$ | **0.67** |
| `<start>` → N | initial | **0.50** |
| `<start>` → V | initial | **0.50** |

| Emission | $P(w \mid t)$ | Value |
|---|---|---|
| $P(\text{time} \mid \text{N})$ | — | **0.80** |
| $P(\text{flies} \mid \text{N})$ | — | **0.10** |
| $P(\text{flies} \mid \text{V})$ | — | **0.50** |
| $P(\text{like} \mid \text{V})$ | — | **0.40** |
| $P(\text{like} \mid \text{N})$ | — | **0.20** |
| $P(\text{an} \mid \text{N})$ | — | **0.60** |
| $P(\text{arrow} \mid \text{N})$ | — | **0.70** |

**Sentence to tag:** *"time flies like an arrow"*

We want to compute $v_i(j)$ for each position.

**Position 1 — `time`:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
v_1(\text{N}) &= \log P(\text{<start>} \to \text{N}) + \log P(\text{time} \mid \text{N}) \\
&= \log(0.50) + \log(0.80) = -0.301 - 0.097 = -0.398 \\
v_1(\text{V}) &= \log(0.50) + \log(\text{no emission of "time" as V, } \approx 0)
\end{aligned}
$$

For brevity, assume $v_1(\text{V}) \to -\infty$ (Laplace-smoothed).

**Position 2 — `flies`:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
v_2(\text{N}) &= \max[v_1(\text{N}) + \log P(\text{N} \to \text{N}) + \log P(\text{flies} \mid \text{N})] \\
&= -0.398 + \log(0.60) + \log(0.10) \\
&= -0.398 - 0.222 - 1.000 = -1.620 \\
v_2(\text{V}) &= v_1(\text{N}) + \log P(\text{N} \to \text{V}) + \log P(\text{flies} \mid \text{V}) \\
&= -0.398 + \log(0.40) + \log(0.50) \\
&= -0.398 - 0.398 - 0.301 = -1.097 \;\; \text{(best so far)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Position 3 — `like`:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
v_3(\text{V}) &= v_2(\text{V}) + \log P(\text{V} \to \text{V}) + \log P(\text{like} \mid \text{V}) \\
&= -1.097 + \log(0.67) + \log(0.40) \\
&= -1.097 - 0.174 - 0.398 = -1.669 \\
v_3(\text{N}) &= v_2(\text{V}) + \log P(\text{V} \to \text{N}) + \log P(\text{like} \mid \text{N}) \\
&= -1.097 + \log(0.33) + \log(0.20) \\
&= -1.097 - 0.481 - 0.699 = -2.277
\end{aligned}
$$

**Position 4 — `an`:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
v_4(\text{N}) &= v_3(\text{V}) + \log P(\text{V} \to \text{N}) + \log P(\text{an} \mid \text{N}) \\
&= -1.669 + \log(0.33) + \log(0.60) \\
&= -1.669 - 0.481 - 0.222 = -2.372 \;\; \text{(best)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Position 5 — `arrow`:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
v_5(\text{N}) &= v_4(\text{N}) + \log P(\text{N} \to \text{N}) + \log P(\text{arrow} \mid \text{N}) \\
&= -2.372 + \log(0.60) + \log(0.70) \\
&= -2.372 - 0.222 - 0.155 = -2.749 \;\; \text{(FINAL BEST)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final best tag sequence (by backtracking):** `N V V N N` → *"time/N flies/V like/V an/N arrow/N"* ✓ grammatically coherent.

## 3.3 Handling Unknown / Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV) Words

The naïve emission probability $P(w \mid t) = 0$ for unseen words destroys the Viterbi product. KTU-level remedies:

| Technique | Formula / Idea |
|---|---|
| **Laplace (Add-One) Smoothing** | $P(w \mid t) = \dfrac{C(t,w) + 1}{C(t) + \vert V \vert}$ |
| **Replace with `<UNK>` token** | All OOVs mapped to one symbol, prob estimated globally |
| **Suffix-based heuristics** | `-ly` → RB, `-ing` → VBG, `-tion` → NN, `-ous` → JJ |
| **Capitalisation heuristic** | Capitalised word → `NNP` with high prior |
| **Char-level RNN** (modern) | Embed sub-word n-grams to predict tag distribution |

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Taggers

| Tagger Family | Approach | Trainable? | Accuracy (PTB) | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Rule-based (EngCG)** | Linguistic constraints | No | ≈ 93% | Interpretable, language-portable | Labor-intensive |
| **Statistical HMM** | Generative MLE | Yes | ≈ 95–96% | Simple, fast, classic | Strong independence assumptions |
| **MEMM** | Discriminative log-linear | Yes | ≈ 96.5% | Avoids label bias partially | Local normalisation |
| **CRF** | Global log-linear | Yes | ≈ 97% | Best classical accuracy | Slow training |
| **Bi-LSTM-CRF** | Neural + CRF | Yes | ≈ 97.3% | Context-rich | Needs GPU |
| **BERT-based** | Transformer fine-tune | Yes | ≈ 97.8% | SOTA | Resource-heavy, black-box |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 POS Tagging — End-to-End Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawText[Raw Text Input] --> tokenize[Tokenizer Module]
    tokenize --> words[Word Sequence w1 w2 w3 ... wn]
    words --> lexicon[Lexicon Lookup Stage]
    lexicon --> knownWords[Known Words With Tag Probabilities]
    lexicon --> oovHandler[OOV Handling Stage]
    oovHandler --> suffixRule[Suffix and Capitalisation Heuristics]
    oovHandler --> unkToken[UNK Default Probabilities]
    knownWords --> contextStage[Context Modeling Stage]
    suffixRule --> contextStage
    unkToken --> contextStage
    contextStage --> hmmCore[HMM or CRF or Bi LSTM CRF Engine]
    hmmCore --> viterbi[Viterbi Decoder or Beam Search]
    viterbi --> tagSeq[Best Tag Sequence t1 t2 t3 ... tn]
    tagSeq --> eval[Accuracy Evaluation Against Gold Standard]
```

## 4.2 HMM Trellis — Visual Schematic of Viterbi Path Search

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start0[START] --> n1[N State]
    start0 --> v1[V State]
    n1 --> n2[N State]
    n1 --> v2[V State]
    v1 --> n2
    v1 --> v2
    n2 --> n3[N State]
    n2 --> v3[V State]
    v2 --> n3
    v2 --> v3
    n3 --> n4[N State]
    n3 --> v4[V State]
    v3 --> n4
    v3 --> v4
    n4 --> n5[N State]
    n4 --> v5[V State]
    v4 --> n5
    v4 --> v5
    n5 --> end1[END]
    v5 --> end1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Trellis:** Each **column** corresponds to a word position $i = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$. Each **row** corresponds to a candidate tag (N or V in this toy example). Every edge carries the joint cost $\log a_{i,j} + \log b_j(w_i)$. The Viterbi algorithm traces a single left-to-right path with the **maximum cumulative log-probability**, terminating at the state with the highest score at position $n$.

## 4.3 Brill Tagger Transformation Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    init[Initial Tagging - Most Frequent Tag per Word] --> errDetect[Error Detection Phase]
    errDetect --> ruleLearn[Rule Learning Phase]
    ruleLearn --> applyRule[Apply Best Transformation Rule]
    applyRule --> tagUpdate[Updated Tag Sequence]
    tagUpdate --> errDetect
    ruleLearn --> stopCheck{Error Reduction Below Threshold}
    stopCheck -->|No| ruleLearn
    stopCheck -->|Yes| finalTags[Final Tagged Corpus]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Brill Tagger Rule Template (typical form):**
> *"Change tag from X to Y if the previous word is tagged Z / if the word ends with suffix S / if the word has been seen in context C."* Each rule has an associated score equal to the number of errors it fixes minus the number of new errors it creates.
