# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Part-of-Speech tagging. List any four commonly used POS tags from the Penn Treebank tagset with one example word each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** POS Tagging is the NLP task of assigning a grammatical category label (such as noun, verb, adjective, adverb, pronoun, preposition, etc.) to each word in a sentence, based on both its definition and its context. Formally, it computes $\hat{T} = \arg\max_T P(T \mid W)$.

**Examples (1 mark — ¼ × 4):**
- `NN` (Singular Noun) → `dog`, `book`
- `VBZ` (3rd-person singular present verb) → `runs`, `eats`
- `JJ` (Adjective) → `beautiful`, `tall`
- `RB` (Adverb) → `quickly`, `silently`

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by the *ambiguity problem* in POS tagging? Give two examples of words that exhibit POS ambiguity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Ambiguity (2 marks):** Many English words belong to more than one grammatical category depending on the surrounding context. A dictionary-only tagger cannot resolve this; hence statistical and contextual models are required.

**Two examples (1 mark — ½ × 2):**
1. **`book`** — Noun: "I read a book." / Verb: "Book my ticket."
2. **`flies`** — Noun (plural): "Time flies." / Verb: "The bird flies high."

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question B — Option A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of a Hidden Markov Model (HMM) based POS tagger**. Clearly state the two key independence assumptions of HMMs and derive the formula for the best tag sequence. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the sentence **"Time flies like an arrow."** Given the small transition and emission probability tables below, compute the best tag sequence using the **Viterbi algorithm**. Assume the tag set is $\mathcal{T} = \{\text{N, V}\}$ and `<s>` is the start state. **(7 marks)**

| Transition | Probability |
|---|---|
| `<s>` → N | 0.5 |
| `<s>` → V | 0.5 |
| N → N | 0.6 |
| N → V | 0.4 |
| V → N | 0.33 |
| V → V | 0.67 |

| Emission | Probability |
|---|---|
| P(time ∣ N) | 0.80 |
| P(flies ∣ N) | 0.10 |
| P(flies ∣ V) | 0.50 |
| P(like ∣ V) | 0.40 |
| P(like ∣ N) | 0.20 |
| P(an ∣ N) | 0.60 |
| P(arrow ∣ N) | 0.70 |

---

#### Model Solution for B(a) — HMM Tagger Architecture (7 marks)

**Architecture Diagram (3 marks):**

```
                ┌─────────────────────────────────┐
                │      Training Corpus            │
                │ (Tagged Sentences — Penn TB)    │
                └──────────────┬──────────────────┘
                               │ counts
                ┌──────────────▼──────────────────┐
                │   Probability Estimation        │
                │  • Transition  P(t_i | t_{i-1}) │
                │  • Emission    P(w_i | t_i)     │
                │  • Prior       P(t_1)            │
                └──────────────┬──────────────────┘
                               │ param tables
                ┌──────────────▼──────────────────┐
                │   Viterbi Decoder (DP)          │
                │   v_t(j) = max_i v_{t-1}(i) *   │
                │             a_{i,j} * b_j(w_t)  │
                └──────────────┬──────────────────┘
                               │ best path
                ┌──────────────▼──────────────────┐
                │   Tagged Output Sequence        │
                └─────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Two Independence Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. **Markov (First-Order) Assumption:** The current tag depends only on the immediately preceding tag.
$$P(t_i \mid t_1, \ldots, t_{i-1}) = P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$$
2. **Output Independence Assumption:** A word's emission depends only on its own tag, not on neighbouring tags or words.
$$P(w_i \mid t_1, \ldots, t_n, w_1, \ldots, w_n) = P(w_i \mid t_i)$$

**Derivation of Best Tag Sequence (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{T} &= \arg\max_T P(T \mid W) \\
&= \arg\max_T \frac{P(W, T)}{P(W)} \\
&= \arg\max_T P(W, T) \quad \text{[since } P(W) \text{ is constant]} \\
&= \arg\max_T \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid t_i) \cdot P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Model Solution for B(b) — Viterbi Computation (7 marks)

Using the worked derivation of Section 3.2 above as the model solution. The student must present the Viterbi trellis table:

| Word | $v_i(\text{N})$ | $v_i(\text{V})$ | Best tag at $i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| time | $-0.398$ | $-\infty$ | N |
| flies | $-1.620$ | $\mathbf{-1.097}$ | V |
| like | $-2.277$ | $\mathbf{-1.669}$ | V |
| an | $\mathbf{-2.372}$ | $-2.557$ | N |
| arrow | $\mathbf{-2.749}$ | $-3.066$ | N |

**Final decoded sequence:** `time/N  flies/V  like/V  an/N  arrow/N` ✓ **(7 marks — 1.4 marks per position; backtracking 0 marks extra but state it explicitly).**

---

### Question B — Option B (Alternative 14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare **rule-based, statistical (HMM), and transformation-based (Brill)** POS taggers in terms of methodology, accuracy, advantages, and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with an example how **unknown / out-of-vocabulary (OOV) words** are handled in an HMM-based POS tagger. Discuss **Laplace smoothing** and **suffix-based heuristics** as remedies. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for B(a) — Comparative Study (7 marks)

| Dimension | Rule-Based (EngCG) | Statistical (HMM) | Transformation (Brill) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Methodology (2 m)** | Handcrafted morphological & syntactic constraints operating on a lexicon | Learns transition & emission probabilities from tagged corpus via MLE | Iterative rule learning — starts from most-frequent tag, corrects errors |
| **Accuracy (1 m)** | ≈ 90–93% | ≈ 95–96% | ≈ 95–96% |
| **Advantages (2 m)** | Interpretable, language-portable, no training data needed | Data-driven, principled probabilistic foundation, fast decoding | Combines interpretability of rules with statistical data; rules are human-readable |
| **Limitations (2 m)** | Labour-intensive rule writing; brittle to new phenomena | Strong independence assumptions; struggles with rare words | Sequential rule application can be slow; greedier than global HMM |

---

#### Model Solution for B(b) — OOV Handling (7 marks)

**Problem Statement (2 marks):** If an unseen word $w^*$ appears in the test set, the maximum-likelihood estimate $P(w^* \mid t) = C(t, w^*)/C(t) = 0$ since $C(t, w^*) = 0$. This zero collapses the entire Viterbi product to zero, killing the path.

**Solution 1 — Laplace (Add-One) Smoothing (2.5 marks):**

$$P(w \mid t) = \frac{C(t, w) + 1}{C(t) + \vert V \vert}$$

where $\vert V \vert$ is the vocabulary size. This redistributes a small probability mass to all unseen word–tag pairs, preventing exact zero. **Example:** If $C(\text{NN}) = 100{,}000$ and $\vert V \vert = 50{,}000$, then any unseen noun gets $P(w \mid \text{NN}) = 1/150{,}000 \approx 6.7 \times 10^{-6}$.

**Solution 2 — Suffix-Based Heuristics (2.5 marks):** Common morphological patterns are pre-tagged:

| Suffix | Likely Tag | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `-ing` | VBG | `running`, `eating` |
| `-tion`, `-sion` | NN | `action`, `decision` |
| `-ly` | RB | `quickly`, `softly` |
| `-ous`, `-ful`, `-able` | JJ | `famous`, `careful` |
| `-ed` | VBD | `walked`, `talked` |

The tagger falls back to these rules when $P(w \mid t) = 0$ for all tags, often yielding a coarse but useful initial estimate that the Viterbi decoder can refine using contextual transition probabilities.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the start state** $\langle s \rangle$ — many students begin Viterbi without initializing $v_1(j) = P(t_1 = j) \cdot P(w_1 \mid t_1 = j)$. **(-1 mark)**
> 2. **Mixing $\arg\max$ with $\max$** — backpointer stores $\arg\max$, not the maximum value itself. **(-1 mark)**
> 3. **Not showing backtracking** — the Viterbi forward pass alone is incomplete; examiners expect explicit mention of backpointers. **(-1 mark)**
> 4. **Using product instead of log-product** — naive product causes numerical underflow; examiners mark down for not addressing this. **(-½ mark)**
> 5. **Confusing MEMM and HMM** — MEMM is *discriminative* and *locally normalised*; HMM is *generative* and *globally normalised*. **(-1 mark)**
> 6. **Ignoring OOV handling** — for any HMM question on a test sentence, you **must** address unseen words. **(-½ mark)**

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **POS Tagging** = assigning grammatical category labels to every word in a sentence.
- **Tagset choice** matters: Penn Treebank (45 tags) is the de-facto academic standard.
- **Four generations of taggers:** Rule-based → Statistical → Transformation → Neural.
- **HMM is the canonical statistical model** — uses two assumptions (Markov + Output Independence).
- **Core HMM quantities:** Prior $P(t_1)$, Transition $P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$, Emission $P(w_i \mid t_i)$.
- **Objective:** $\hat{T} = \arg\max_T \prod_i P(w_i \mid t_i) P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$.
- **Viterbi Algorithm** decodes the best path in $O(n \cdot \vert \mathcal{T} \vert^2)$ using dynamic programming.
- **Always initialise** with $\langle s \rangle$ → first tag transition.
- **Always use log-probabilities** for numerical stability.
- **Always backtrack** through stored pointers to retrieve the tag sequence.
- **OOV problem** is resolved by Laplace smoothing, `<UNK>` token, and morphological (suffix) rules.
- **Accuracy benchmark:** ≈ **97%** is the practical ceiling for classical taggers on Penn Treebank; neural taggers push to **97.8%**.
- **Downstream uses:** Parsing, NER, MT, TTS, Information Extraction — all rely on POS tags.
- **KTU weightage tip:** HMM + Viterbi is a *guaranteed 14-mark question* in any NLP ESE; master the recurrence and the backtracking step.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HMM POS Tagging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT741 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** HMM POS Tagging # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)** > A **Hidden Markov Model (HMM) for Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging** is a statistical sequence labeling model that assigns the most probable sequence of grammatical tags $\mathbf{T} = (t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n)$ to an observed sequence of words $\mathbf{W} = (w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n)$. The model treats POS tags as **hidden states** (latent variables) and the words as **visible observations** (emissions), governed by two stochastic processes: a **state transition process** (tag-to-tag) and an **observation emission process** (tag-to-word). ## Intuitive Real-World Analogy: The "Weather & Mood" System Imagine you are locked inside a room and **cannot see the weather outside** (Sunny / Rainy / Cloudy). However, your friend walks in wearing either a **T-shirt, Umbrella, or Sweater** — and you can see the clothes. From the **sequence of clothes** (observations), you want to **guess the sequence of weather** (hidden states). | Element in Analogy | Mapping to HMM POS Tagging | |---|---| | Hidden weather (Sunny/Rainy) | **Hidden POS tags** (NN, VB, DT, JJ…) | | Visible clothes (T-shirt/Umbrella) | **Observed words** ("cat", "runs", "the") | | Probability of weather change today given yesterday | **Transition probability** $P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$ | | Probability of wearing a T-shirt given it is Sunny | **Emission probability** $P(w_i \mid t_i)$ | | Probability of first-day weather | **Initial probability** $\pi_i$ | Just as you would compute "what weather sequence **most likely** explains this clothing sequence?", HMM POS taggers ask: "what **tag sequence** most likely explains this word sequence?" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "Hidden"?** The tags are *latent* (never directly observed in text). We only see surface words. The Markov property assumes the next state depends **only on the current state** — not the entire history. This is the **First-Order Markov Assumption** critical to the KTU module. ## Core Terminology (Must Memorize for KTU) - **State Sequence** $\mathbf{Q} = \{q_1, q_2, \dots, q_N\}$ — the finite set of POS tags. - **Observation Sequence** $\mathbf{O} = (o_1, o_2, \dots, o_T)$ — the words in the sentence. - **Transition Probability** $A = \{a_{ij}\}$ where $a_{ij} = P(t_j \mid t_i)$. - **Emission Probability** $B = \{b_j(o_t)\}$ where $b_j(o_t) = P(o_t \mid t_j)$. - **Initial State Distribution** $\Pi = \{\pi_i\}$ where $\pi_i = P(t_1 = q_i)$. - **Compact Notation** — An HMM is fully specified by the **triple $\lambda = (A, B, \Pi)$**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Board Focus:** Every Part-A and Part-B question on this topic begins with the formal triple $\lambda = (A, B, \Pi)$ definition. Marks are awarded for stating all **five** parameters precisely. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Foundational Assumptions of HMM For tractable computation, HMM POS tagging rests on three simplifying assumptions. KTU examiners frequently test these: 1. **Markov Assumption (First-Order):** The current tag depends only on the *previous* tag. $$P(t_i \mid t_1, t_2, \dots, t_{i-1}) = P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$$ 2. **Output Independence Assumption (Bag-of-Outputs):** A word's probability depends only on its *own* tag, not on neighboring words or tags. $$P(w_i \mid t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n, w_1, \dots, w_n) = P(w_i \mid t_i)$$ 3. **Stationary Transition Assumption:** Transition probabilities do not change with position $i$. $$P(t_i \mid t_{i-1}) = P(t_{i+1} \mid t_i) \quad \forall i$$ ## 2.2 The Three Fundamental HMM Problems (Foundational for KTU) Daniel Jurafsky & James H. Martin (the standard NLP textbook) defines three canonical problems solved by HMMs. KTU Module 2 directly maps to Problem 2 (Decoding). | Problem # | Name | Goal | Algorithm | |---|---|---|---| | **Problem 1** | **Likelihood Evaluation** | Compute $P(\mathbf{O} \mid \lambda)$ | **Forward / Backward Algorithm** | | **Problem 2** | **Decoding** ★ *Most important for POS* | Find best tag sequence $\arg\max_{\mathbf{T}} P(\mathbf{T} \mid \mathbf{O})$ | **Viterbi Algorithm** | | **Problem 3** | **Learning** | Adjust $\lambda$ to maximize $P(\mathbf{O} \mid \lambda)$ | **Baum-Welch (EM) Algorithm** | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Save this table — these equations appear in 90% of KTU Module 2 numerical questions.** | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $P(\mathbf{T}, \mathbf{W}) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid t_i) \cdot P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$ | Joint probability of tag-word sequence | | 2 | $P(\mathbf{T} \mid \mathbf{W}) = \dfrac{P(\mathbf{W} \mid \mathbf{T}) \cdot P(\mathbf{T})}{P(\mathbf{W})}$ | Bayesian reformulation for tagging | | 3 | $P(\mathbf{T}) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$ | Tag-sequence probability (bigram) | | 4 | $P(\mathbf{W} \mid \mathbf{T}) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid t_i)$ | Word-sequence probability given tags | | 5 | $t^*_{1..n} = \arg\max_{\mathbf{T}} \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid t_i) \cdot P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$ | POS decoding objective | | 6 | $v_1(j) = \pi_j \cdot b_j(o_1)$ | Viterbi initialization | | 7 | $v_t(j) = \max_{i} \left[ v_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \right] \cdot b_j(o_t)$ | Viterbi recursion ★ | | 8 | $bt_t(j) = \arg\max_{i} \left[ v_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \right]$ | Backpointer for path reconstruction | | 9 | $\alpha_t(j) = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \right] \cdot b_j(o_t)$ | Forward variable (summing) | | 10 | $\beta_t(i) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij} \cdot b_j(o_{t+1}) \cdot \beta_{t+1}(j)$ | Backward variable | > **Critical Reminder:** The **only** difference between Viterbi (Formula 7) and Forward (Formula 9) is `max` vs `sum`. This is a classic KTU trap question. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Machine Translation (Google Translate):** HMMs were the backbone of early statistical MT before neural seq2seq models. - **Speech Recognition:** Acoustic states are hidden, phonemes/sounds are observed. - **Bioinformatics:** Gene-finding (coding vs. non-coding regions) uses HMM profiles (e.g., Pfam database). - **Spell Checkers & Handwriting Recognition:** Sequence correction models. - **Information Extraction:** Named entity recognition, slot filling in chatbots. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example ## 3.1 Derivation of the Decoding Rule We want the **best tag sequence** $t^*_{1..n}$ that maximizes the posterior probability: $$ t^*_{1..n} = \arg\max_{t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n} P(t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n \mid w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n) $$ By **Bayes' Rule**: $$ P(\mathbf{T} \mid \mathbf{W}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{W} \mid \mathbf{T}) \cdot P(\mathbf{T})}{P(\mathbf{W})} $$ Since $P(\mathbf{W})$ is independent of $\mathbf{T}$, we drop the denominator (the "naive Bayes trick"): $$ t^*_{1..n} = \arg\max_{\mathbf{T}} P(\mathbf{W} \mid \mathbf{T}) \cdot P(\mathbf{T}) $$ Applying the **Markov assumption** and **output independence**: $$ P(\mathbf{T}) = P(t_1) \cdot \prod_{i=2}^{n} P(t_i \mid t_{i-1}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(t_i \mid t_{i-1}) $$ $$ P(\mathbf{W} \mid \mathbf{T}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid t_i) $$ Multiplying both and introducing the **start sentinel** $t_0 = \langle s \rangle$ (a fictitious "beginning of sentence" tag) so that $P(t_1 \mid t_0) = \pi_{t_1}$: $$ t^*_{1..n} = \arg\max_{\mathbf{T}} \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid t_i) \cdot P(t_i \mid t_{i-1}) $$ In practice, products of many small probabilities cause **underflow** (numbers become $10^{-300}$). We convert to **log-space**: $$ t^*_{1..n} = \arg\max_{\mathbf{T}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ \log P(w_i \mid t_i) + \log P(t_i \mid t_{i-1}) \right] $$ > [!NOTE] > **This log-transformation step is frequently asked in KTU 14-mark questions as a 2-mark sub-part.** ## 3.2 The Viterbi Algorithm: Complete Pseudocode & Numerical Walkthrough The Viterbi algorithm is **dynamic programming**: it fills a **trellis** of size $(N \times T)$, where $N$ = number of tags, $T$ = sentence length. ### Setup — Mini Training Corpus for Worked Example **Tags:** N (Noun), V (Verb), D (Determiner) **Initial probabilities $\Pi$:** | Tag | $\pi$ | |---|---| | N | **0.5** | | V | **0.2** | | D | **0.3** | **Transition matrix $A$** — $a_{ij} = P(t_j \mid t_i)$: | From \ To | **N** | **V** | **D** | |---|---|---|---| | **N** | 0.3 | 0.5 | 0.2 | | **V** | 0.6 | 0.2 | 0.2 | | **D** | 0.7 | 0.1 | 0.2 | **Emission matrix $B$** — $b_j(w) = P(w \mid t_j)$: | Tag | $P(\text{time} \mid t)$ | $P(\text{flies} \mid t)$ | $P(\text{an} \mid t)$ | $P(\text{arrow} \mid t)$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | **N** | 0.6 | 0.1 | 0.0 | 0.6 | | **V** | 0.2 | 0.6 | 0.0 | 0.0 | | **D** | 0.0 | 0.0 | 0.7 | 0.0 | **Target sentence:** "time flies an arrow" (4 words, $T=4$) ### Step 1 — Viterbi Initialization ($t=1$, word = "time") Using $v_1(j) = \pi_j \cdot b_j(o_1)$: $$ \begin{aligned} v_1(\text{N}) &= \pi_{\text{N}} \cdot b_{\text{N}}(\text{time}) = 0.5 \times 0.6 = 0.30 \\ v_1(\text{V}) &= \pi_{\text{V}} \cdot b_{\text{V}}(\text{time}) = 0.2 \times 0.2 = 0.04 \\ v_1(\text{D}) &= \pi_{\text{D}} \cdot b_{\text{D}}(\text{time}) = 0.3 \times 0.0 = 0.00 \end{aligned} $$ | Tag | $v_1$ | $bt_1$ | |---|---|---| | N | **0.30** | — | | V | **0.04** | — | | D | **0.00** | — | ### Step 2 — Recursion ($t=2$, word = "flies") Using $v_2(j) = \max_i \left[ v_1(i) \cdot a_{ij} \right] \cdot b_j(\text{flies})$: **For tag N at $t=2$:** $$ \begin{aligned} \max( v_1(\text{N}) \cdot a_{\text{N,N}}, v_1(\text{V}) \cdot a_{\text{V,N}}, v_1(\text{D}) \cdot a_{\text{D,N}} ) \cdot b_{\text{N}}(\text{flies}) \\ = \max(0.30 \cdot 0.3,\ 0.04 \cdot 0.6,\ 0.00 \cdot 0.7) \cdot 0.1 \\ = \max(0.090,\ 0.024,\ 0.000) \cdot 0.1 = 0.090 \cdot 0.1 = 0.009 \end{aligned} $$ Winner: $i = \text{N}$, so $bt_2(\text{N}) = \text{N}$. **For tag V at $t=2$:** $$ \begin{aligned} \max(0.30 \cdot 0.5,\ 0.04 \cdot 0.2,\ 0.00 \cdot 0.1) \cdot 0.6 \\ = \max(0.150,\ 0.008,\ 0.000) \cdot 0.6 = 0.150 \cdot 0.6 = 0.090 \end{aligned} $$ Winner: $bt_2(\text{V}) = \text{N}$. **For tag D at $t=2$:** $$ \max(0.30 \cdot 0.2,\ 0.04 \cdot 0.2,\ 0.00 \cdot 0.2) \cdot 0.0 = 0.0 $$ ### Step 3 — Recursion ($t=3$, word = "an") **For tag N:** $$ \max(0.009 \cdot 0.3,\ 0.090 \cdot 0.6,\ 0.0 \cdot 0.7) \cdot 0.0 = 0.0 $$ **For tag V:** $$ \max(0.009 \cdot 0.5,\ 0.090 \cdot 0.2,\ 0.0 \cdot 0.1) \cdot 0.0 = 0.0 $$ **For tag D:** $$ \max(0.009 \cdot 0.2,\ 0.090 \cdot 0.2,\ 0.0 \cdot 0.2) \cdot 0.7 = \max(0.0018,\ 0.018,\ 0.0) \cdot 0.7 = 0.018 \cdot 0.7 = 0.0126 $$ Winner: $bt_3(\text{D}) = \text{V}$. ### Step 4 — Recursion ($t=4$, word = "arrow") **For tag N:** $$ \max(0.0 \cdot 0.3,\ 0.0 \cdot 0.6,\ 0.0126 \cdot 0.7) \cdot 0.6 = \max(0,\ 0,\ 0.00882) \cdot 0.6 = 0.00882 \cdot 0.6 = 0.005292 $$ Winner: $bt_4(\text{N}) = \text{D}$. (V and D paths collapse to 0 since "arrow" cannot be emitted by V or D.) ### Step 5 — Termination & Backtrace Best final score: $v_4(\text{N}) = 0.005292$ (only non-zero). Backtrace using $bt$: - $t=4$: best tag = **N** (came from D) - $t=3$: tag = **D** (came from V) - $t=2$: tag = **V** (came from N) - $t=1$: tag = **N** (start) **Final best tag sequence: N → V → D → N** **Output:** `time/N flies/V an/D arrow/N` ✓ **Best probability:** $0.005292$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Many students forget to **multiply the final emission** $b_{\text{N}}(\text{arrow})$ in Step 4. Examiners award 2 marks separately for the termination step. ## 3.3 Forward Algorithm Derivation (Problem 1 — Likelihood) The forward variable $\alpha_t(j)$ = probability of observing the partial sequence $o_1 \dots o_t$ AND being in state $j$ at time $t$: $$ \alpha_t(j) = P(o_1, o_2, \dots, o_t,\ t_t = q_j \mid \lambda) $$ **Initialization:** $$ \alpha_1(j) = \pi_j \cdot b_j(o_1) $$ **Recursion:** $$ \alpha_t(j) = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij} \right] \cdot b_j(o_t) $$ **Termination:** $$ P(\mathbf{O} \mid \lambda) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_T(j) $$ --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 HMM Trellis Architecture for POS Tagging The figure below shows the **trellis structure** for our worked example. Each column represents a time-step (word position); each row represents a hidden tag state. Solid arrows are transitions $a_{ij}$, dashed arrows are emissions $b_j(o_t)$. ```mermaid graph LR start((START)) --> initN start((START)) --> initV start((START)) --> initD initN["v1 N = 0.30"] initV["v1 V = 0.04"] initD["v1 D = 0.00"] initN --> n2["v2 N = 0.009"] initN --> v2["v2 V = 0.090"] initN --> d2["v2 D = 0.000"] initV --> n2 initV --> v2 initV --> d2 initD --> n2 initD --> v2 initD --> d2 n2 --> n3["v3 N = 0.0"] n2 --> v3["v3 V = 0.0"] n2 --> d3["v3 D = 0.0126"] v2 --> n3 v2 --> v3 v2 --> d3 d2 --> n3 d2 --> v3 d2 --> d3 n3 --> n4["v4 N = 0.005292"] n3 --> v4["v4 V = 0.0"] n3 --> d4["v4 D = 0.0"] v3 --> n4 v3 --> v4 v3 --> d4 d3 --> n4 d3 --> v4 d3 --> d4 n4 --> final((END)) v4 --> final d4 --> final obs1([O1 time]) -.-> initN obs2([O2 flies]) -.-> v2 obs3([O3 an]) -.-> d3 obs4([O4 arrow]) -.-> n4 ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an HMM POS Tagger ```mermaid graph TB input["INPUT Sentence
time flies an arrow"] tokenizer["Tokenizer
Splits into tokens"] lexicon["Lexicon / Unknown Word Handler
Suffix features capitalisation digits"] hmmCore["HMM Core lambda = A B Pi"] piBlock["Initial Probabilities Pi
P t1 q j"] aBlock["Transition Matrix A
a ij = P t j given t i"] bBlock["Emission Matrix B
b j o t = P o t given t j"] viterbi["Viterbi Decoder
Dynamic Programming"] backptr["Backpointer Table bt t j"] output["OUTPUT Tag Sequence
N V D N"] input --> tokenizer tokenizer --> lexicon lexicon --> hmmCore hmmCore --> viterbi piBlock --> hmmCore aBlock --> hmmCore bBlock --> hmmCore hmmCore --> viterbi viterbi --> backptr backptr --> output style hmmCore fill:#ffe699,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style viterbi fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style output fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 HMM Three-Algorithm Flow ```mermaid graph TB problem1["Problem 1
Likelihood P O given lambda"] problem2["Problem 2
Decoding argmax T P T given O"] problem3["Problem 3
Learning argmax lambda P O given lambda"] forward["Forward Algorithm
Alpha recursion"] viterbi["Viterbi Algorithm
Max recursion"] baum["Baum Welch EM
Forward + Backward"] fwdUse["P O given lambda
for sentence scoring"] vitUse["Best tag sequence
T1 T2 ... Tn"] baumUse["Updated lambda A B Pi"] problem1 --> forward --> fwdUse problem2 --> viterbi --> vitUse problem3 --> baum --> baumUse style forward fill:#fff2cc style viterbi fill:#c5e0b4 style baum fill:#fce4d6 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The **Forward algorithm** and **Viterbi algorithm** have *identical* structure — the only change is replacing `sum` with `max`. This duality is exploited in the Forward-Backward and Baum-Welch algorithms used in **speech recognition** (HMMs power assistants like Siri's pre-neural acoustic model). --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls** > 1. Forgetting to include the **emission probability $b_j(o_t)$** in the Viterbi recursion — costs **2 marks**. > 2. Confusing `max` (Viterbi) with `sum` (Forward) — examiners deliberately set comparison questions. > 3. Not specifying the **start sentinel $\langle s \rangle$** when computing $P(t_1 \mid t_0)$ — costs **1 mark**. > 4. Using the **same base-10 log product** without converting probabilities to log-space — full step ignored. > 5. Failing to **backtrace** the path using $bt_t(j)$ — only forward scores are computed, no final answer. --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Define Hidden Markov Model. List and explain the five elements that fully specify an HMM.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **Hidden Markov Model (HMM)** is a probabilistic sequence model in which a system is assumed to be a Markov process with unobserved (hidden) states, generating an observable output at each time step. The **five elements** specifying an HMM $\lambda = (A, B, \Pi, Q, O)$: 1. **$Q = \{q_1, q_2, \dots, q_N\}$** — finite set of $N$ hidden states (e.g., POS tags). 2. **$A = \{a_{ij}\}_{N \times N}$** — transition probability matrix where $a_{ij} = P(t_j \mid t_i)$. 3. **$B = \{b_j(o_t)\}$** — observation/emission probabilities $b_j(o_t) = P(o_t \mid t_j)$. 4. **$\Pi = \{\pi_i\}$** — initial state distribution $\pi_i = P(t_1 = q_i)$. 5. **$O = (o_1, o_2, \dots, o_T)$** — observed sequence (the words). > **Valuation Tip:** Award full 3 marks only if all five elements are listed with correct mathematical notation. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]` **State and explain the two key assumptions of HMM-based POS tagging.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. **First-Order Markov Assumption (1.5 marks):** The probability of a tag depends only on the *immediately preceding* tag, not on the full history: $$ P(t_i \mid t_1, t_2, \dots, t_{i-1}) = P(t_i \mid t_{i-1}) $$ 2. **Output Independence Assumption (1.5 marks):** The probability of an observed word depends only on its own tag, not on neighbouring words or tags: $$ P(w_i \mid t_1, \dots, t_n, w_1, \dots, w_n) = P(w_i \mid t_i) $$ These assumptions make HMM computation **tractable** ($O(N^2 T)$ instead of exponential). --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > *Note: As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 2 contains a choice. Below are **two independent alternative 14-mark questions**. Solve any one.* --- ### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]` > Consider the following corpus: "Time flies." Assume two tags: **N** (Noun) and **V** (Verb). The model parameters are: > > | | $P(\text{Time} \mid t)$ | $P(\text{flies} \mid t)$ | $P(\text{end} \mid t)$ | > |---|---|---|---| > | N | 0.7 | 0.2 | 0.1 | > | V | 0.3 | 0.6 | 0.1 | > > Initial: $\pi_{\text{N}}=0.6,\ \pi_{\text{V}}=0.4$. > Transition: $a_{\text{N,N}}=0.3,\ a_{\text{N,V}}=0.7,\ a_{\text{V,N}}=0.4,\ a_{\text{V,V}}=0.6$. > > **(a)** [7 Marks] Run the **Viterbi algorithm** to find the best tag sequence for the sentence "Time flies" and write the full DP tables. > > **(b)** [7 Marks] Compare **Viterbi** and **Forward algorithm** with a focus on equations, purpose, output, and time complexity. Why does HMM POS tagging prefer Viterbi? #### Solution to (a) — Viterbi Step-by-Step **Step 1 — Initialization** ($t=1$, word = "Time"): $$ \begin{aligned} v_1(\text{N}) &= \pi_{\text{N}} \cdot b_{\text{N}}(\text{Time}) = 0.6 \times 0.7 = 0.42 \\ v_1(\text{V}) &= \pi_{\text{V}} \cdot b_{\text{V}}(\text{Time}) = 0.4 \times 0.3 = 0.12 \end{aligned} $$ | Tag | $v_1$ | $bt_1$ | |---|---|---| | N | **0.42** | — | | V | **0.12** | — | **Step 2 — Recursion** ($t=2$, word = "flies"): For N at $t=2$: $$ v_2(\text{N}) = \max(0.42 \cdot 0.3,\ 0.12 \cdot 0.4) \cdot 0.2 = \max(0.126,\ 0.048) \cdot 0.2 = 0.126 \cdot 0.2 = 0.0252 $$ $bt_2(\text{N}) = \text{N}$ For V at $t=2$: $$ v_2(\text{V}) = \max(0.42 \cdot 0.7,\ 0.12 \cdot 0.6) \cdot 0.6 = \max(0.294,\ 0.072) \cdot 0.6 = 0.294 \cdot 0.6 = 0.1764 $$ $bt_2(\text{V}) = \text{N}$ **Step 3 — Termination & Backtrace:** Best path ends at $\arg\max(0.0252, 0.1764) = \text{V}$ with score **0.1764**. Backtrace: V at $t=2$ came from N at $t=1$. **Best tag sequence: `Time/N flies/V`**, with probability **0.1764**. ✓ | Step | Marks Awarded | |---|---| | Initialization equations correct | 2 | | Recursion formulas correct | 2 | | Numerical substitution shown | 1.5 | | Backpointer table + final tag | 1.5 | #### Solution to (b) — Viterbi vs Forward Comparison | Feature | Viterbi Algorithm | Forward Algorithm | |---|---|---| | **Objective** | Find best tag sequence (decoding) | Compute $P(\mathbf{O} \mid \lambda)$ (likelihood) | | **Recursion Operator** | `max` | `sum` | | **Equation** | $v_t(j) = \max_i [v_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij}] \cdot b_j(o_t)$ | $\alpha_t(j) = \left[\sum_i \alpha_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij}\right] \cdot b_j(o_t)$ | | **Output** | Single best path + its score | Total probability of all paths | | **Uses Backpointers** | Yes ($bt$) for reconstruction | No | | **Time Complexity** | $O(N^2 T)$ | $O(N^2 T)$ | | **Used in POS Tagging?** | **Yes** — we want a tag *sequence* | No — we need a *sequence*, not a sum | **Why HMM POS Tagging prefers Viterbi:** The goal is to assign a *single best tag* to *each word*. We are not interested in the sum over all paths; we need the **single most probable path** (MAP estimate). The Forward algorithm gives the *total* probability of the observations summed across all tag sequences, which is useless for labeling individual words. Hence Viterbi is the **decoding algorithm** of choice. (7 marks: 1 mark for Viterbi equation; 1 for Forward equation; 1 for operator distinction `max` vs `sum`; 2 for comparison rows; 2 for clear justification of Viterbi's role in POS tagging.) --- ### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]` > **(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the **joint probability** $P(\mathbf{W}, \mathbf{T})$ for an HMM tagger using the chain rule and the two fundamental assumptions. State the final form. > > **(b)** [7 Marks] Using the corpus of 3 sentences below, compute the **Maximum Likelihood Estimates (MLE)** of $P(\text{VB} \mid \text{NN})$ and $P(\text{``dog''} \mid \text{NN})$: > > 1. "The **dog** runs." → (DT, NN, VB) > 2. "A **dog** barks." → (DT, NN, VB) > 3. "The **cat** sleeps." → (DT, NN, VB) #### Solution to (a) — Joint Probability Derivation By the **chain rule of probability**, the joint probability of word sequence $\mathbf{W}$ and tag sequence $\mathbf{T}$ is: $$ P(\mathbf{W}, \mathbf{T}) = P(w_1, \dots, w_n, t_1, \dots, t_n) $$ We factorize over time-steps: $$ P(\mathbf{W}, \mathbf{T}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i, t_i \mid w_1^{i-1}, t_1^{i-1}) $$ where $w_1^{i-1} = (w_1, \dots, w_{i-1})$. **Applying the First-Order Markov Assumption** (state depends only on previous state): $$ P(t_i \mid w_1^{i-1}, t_1^{i-1}) = P(t_i \mid t_{i-1}) $$ **Applying the Output Independence Assumption** (word depends only on its own tag): $$ P(w_i \mid w_1^{i-1}, t_1^{i}) = P(w_i \mid t_i) $$ Substituting both into the product: $$ P(\mathbf{W}, \mathbf{T}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid t_i) \cdot P(t_i \mid t_{i-1}) $$ With the start sentinel $t_0 = \langle s \rangle$ so that $P(t_1 \mid t_0) = \pi_{t_1}$: $$ \boxed{P(\mathbf{W}, \mathbf{T}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid t_i) \cdot P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})} $$ > **Valuation Note (7 marks):** 1 mark for chain rule; 1 mark for identifying the product form; 2 marks for stating both assumptions explicitly; 2 marks for substituting them; 1 mark for the final boxed equation. #### Solution to (b) — MLE Computation **Method:** Count occurrences in the corpus. **Counting $P(\text{VB} \mid \text{NN})$:** Total number of times a tag has previous tag NN = number of (NN, X) transitions in the corpus. Sentences: 1) (DT,NN,VB), 2) (DT,NN,VB), 3) (DT,NN,VB) In each sentence, the transition **NN → VB** occurs once. So: - Count(NN, VB) = 3 (one per sentence) - Count(NN, any) = 3 (each sentence has exactly one tag following NN) $$ P(\text{VB} \mid \text{NN}) = \frac{\text{Count}(\text{NN}, \text{VB})}{\text{Count}(\text{NN}, \text{anything})} = \frac{3}{3} = 1.0 $$ **Counting $P(\text{``dog''} \mid \text{NN})$:** - Count(NN, "dog") = 2 (in sentences 1 and 2) - Count(NN, anything) = 3 (in all 3 sentences: dog, dog, cat) $$ P(\text{``dog''} \mid \text{NN}) = \frac{\text{Count}(\text{NN}, \text{``dog''})}{\text{Count}(\text{NN}, \text{any word})} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667 $$ > **Valuation Note (7 marks):** 2 marks for the correct MLE formula; 1 mark for explaining the counting process; 2 marks for computing Count(NN,VB) and Count(NN,*); 1 mark for the final ratio; 1 mark for the second computation. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist — Module 2: HMM POS Tagging** - ✅ An HMM is fully defined by the **triple $\lambda = (A, B, \Pi)$** (plus state set $Q$ and observation set $O$). - ✅ **Two key assumptions** (memorize the equations, not just the names): Markov + Output Independence. - ✅ **Three HMM problems** — Likelihood (Forward), Decoding (Viterbi), Learning (Baum-Welch). - ✅ Viterbi uses `max` and backpointers; Forward uses `sum` and no backpointers. - ✅ **Log-space transformation** is mandatory to avoid numerical underflow. - ✅ Joint probability formula: $P(\mathbf{W}, \mathbf{T}) = \prod_i P(w_i \mid t_i) \cdot P(t_i \mid t_{i-1})$. - ✅ **MLE for transition**: $a_{ij} = \dfrac{\text{Count}(t_i, t_j)}{\text{Count}(t_i, *)}$. - ✅ **MLE for emission**: $b_j(w) = \dfrac{\text{Count}(t_j, w)}{\text{Count}(t_j, *)}$. - ✅ **Limitations of HMM POS Tagging:** 1. Cannot handle **long-distance dependencies** (only 1-gram Markov). 2. Cannot use **rich features** (suffixes, capitalization) — hand-engineered features must be baked into emission probabilities. 3. Suffers from **data sparsity** — unseen word/tag pairs give 0 probability (mitigated by **smoothing**: Laplace/add-$\delta$). 4. Tag independence assumption ignores **contextual tag interaction**. - ✅ **Modern alternative:** **CRFs**, **BiLSTM-CRF**, and **Transformer-based** taggers (BERT) outperform classical HMMs but are *not* part of KTU Module 2 syllabus. - ✅ KTU Module 2 is **fully covered** if you can: (i) Define HMM, (ii) State assumptions, (iii) Run Viterbi on a 2–3 word toy problem, (iv) Compute MLEs from a small corpus, (v) Explain why Viterbi is used for decoding. > **Suggested KTU Reading:** *Speech and Language Processing* (Jurafsky & Martin) — Chapter on HMMs and POS Tagging (available freely online as draft notes).

topics.title: Viterbi Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT741 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Viterbi Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

The **Viterbi Algorithm** is a dynamic programming (DP) algorithm introduced by Andrew Viterbi in 1967, designed to compute the **most probable sequence of hidden states** — known as the **Viterbi path** — in a Hidden Markov Model (HMM) given an observed sequence of events. Formally, it solves the decoding problem of an HMM by finding the single best state sequence $Q^* = (q_1^*, q_2^*, \dots, q_T^*)$ that maximizes $P(Q \mid O, \lambda)$, where $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ denotes the HMM parameters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> The Viterbi Algorithm is a **Dynamic Programming based decoding technique** used in Hidden Markov Models to find the **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) estimate** of the hidden state sequence for a given observation sequence. It avoids brute-force exponential search by storing and reusing optimal sub-solutions in a **trellis structure**.

### 1.2 Intuition: Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **🧠 Think of it like navigating a road trip with multiple route options!**

Imagine you are driving from **Kochi to Delhi** over 3 days, and each day you can either take a *Highway (H)* or a *Backroad (B)*. Each road segment has two properties:
1. **Transition cost**: how convenient it is to switch from yesterday's road to today's road.
2. **Emission reward**: how much sightseeing you get at that day's stop (this is the *observed* thing you actually see).

The catch? **You only see the sightseeing spots (observations), not which road you took yesterday (hidden state).** You want the **road sequence** that gives you the maximum total sightseeing.

**Brute force** would try all $2^3 = 8$ combinations. But Viterbi is smarter — at each day, for each possible road, it stores only the **best cumulative score so far** and **which previous road led to it**. This is the essence of dynamic programming: *re-use yesterday's best answer, do not recompute it.*

### 1.3 Position in the KTU NLP Pipeline

The Viterbi Algorithm is the **decoding engine** of statistical NLP. In KTU's Module 2 sequence-labeling roadmap, it follows N-grams and HMMs and directly powers:

| Application | Hidden States | Observations |
|---|---|---|
| Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging | Tags (NN, VB, JJ) | Words in a sentence |
| Named Entity Recognition (NER) | BIO tags (B-PER, I-LOC) | Tokens in text |
| Word Sense Disambiguation | Word senses | Context words |
| Speech Recognition | Phonemes | Audio signal frames |

### 1.4 Key Terminology Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HMM Parameters (λ) — Recall from Module 2.1:**
> - **$N$** = Number of hidden states in the model.
> - **$A$** = State transition probability matrix, $A = [a_{ij}]$.
> - **$B$** = Observation emission probability matrix, $B = [b_j(o_t)]$.
> - **$\pi$** = Initial state distribution, $\pi = [\pi_i]$.
> - **$T$** = Length of the observation sequence.
> - **$O = o_1, o_2, \dots, o_T$** = Observed sequence.
> - **$Q = q_1, q_2, \dots, q_T$** = Hidden state sequence.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" — Why Do We Need Viterbi?

For an HMM with **$N$ states** and an observation sequence of length **$T$**, the number of possible state sequences is **$N^T$** (exponential). For $N = 12$ POS tags and a sentence of $T = 10$ words, there are $12^{10} \approx 61.9$ billion paths — clearly infeasible by brute force.

Viterbi reduces this to **$O(N^2 \cdot T)$** by exploiting the **principle of optimality**: if the optimal path passes through state $j$ at time $t$, then the sub-path from time 1 to $t$ ending at $j$ must itself be optimal. We never need to reconsider it.

### 2.2 The Three Operational Phases

The Viterbi Algorithm operates in three logically distinct phases (the KTU board specifically asks about these):

**Phase 1 — Initialization (t = 1):**
- Set the base case using initial probabilities and the first observation.
- Initialize a **backpointer matrix** to record the best predecessor.

**Phase 2 — Recursion / Forward Pass (t = 2 to T):**
- For every state $j$ at time $t$, compute the maximum probability of any path that ends at state $j$, by extending the best path from time $t-1$.
- Store the **backpointer** indicating *which* previous state $i$ gave the maximum.

**Phase 3 — Termination & Backtracking (t = T to 1):**
- Pick the state with the highest final probability.
- Walk **backwards** through the backpointers to reconstruct the full optimal path.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all** formulas the KTU board can ask. Master every row.

| # | Phase | Formula | Variable Definitions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Initialization (Viterbi)** | $V_1(j) = \pi_j \cdot b_j(o_1)$ | $V_t(j)$ = best score for being in state $j$ after seeing first $t$ observations; $\pi_j$ = prior for state $j$; $b_j(o_1)$ = emission of $o_1$ from $j$ |
| 2 | **Backpointer Initialization** | $B_1(j) = 0$ | Sentinel / start marker |
| 3 | **Recursion** | $V_t(j) = \displaystyle\max_{1 \leq i \leq N} \big[V_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij}\big] \cdot b_j(o_t)$ | $a_{ij}$ = transition from $i$ to $j$; $b_j(o_t)$ = emission |
| 4 | **Backpointer Update** | $B_t(j) = \displaystyle\arg\max_{1 \leq i \leq N} \big[V_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij}\big]$ | State index that gave the max |
| 5 | **Termination** | $P^* = \displaystyle\max_{1 \leq j \leq N} V_T(j)$ | Best final score |
| 6 | **Final State** | $q_T^* = \displaystyle\arg\max_{1 \leq j \leq N} V_T(j)$ | Best last state |
| 7 | **Backtracking (Path Recovery)** | $q_t^* = B_{t+1}(q_{t+1}^*)$ | Recursive, runs for $t = T-1, T-2, \dots, 1$ |

### 2.4 Numerical Stability — Log-Space Variant

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Raw Viterbi multiplies many probabilities, leading to **underflow** (numbers smaller than $10^{-308}$, the IEEE 754 double limit). The KTU board *loves* this as a 2-mark conceptual sub-part.

| # | Log-Space Formula | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|
| L1 | $v_1(j) = \log \pi_j + \log b_j(o_1)$ | Converts multiplication to addition |
| L2 | $v_t(j) = \displaystyle\max_{i} \big[v_{t-1}(i) + \log a_{ij}\big] + \log b_j(o_t)$ | Avoids underflow for long sequences |
| L3 | $b_t(j) = \displaystyle\arg\max_{i} \big[v_{t-1}(i) + \log a_{ij}\big]$ | Backpointer unchanged in structure |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Viterbi Algorithm is the silent workhorse behind:
- **Google Speech-to-Text** (decoding phoneme sequences from audio).
- **Apple Siri** and **Amazon Alexa** (command interpretation).
- **GPS navigation** (map matching, the Viterbi-style algorithm finds the most likely road segment for noisy GPS signals).
- **Bioinformatics** (gene prediction in DNA sequences using profile HMMs).
- **Telecommunications** (Viterbi's original 1967 use — convolutional code decoding in noisy channels; this is why it has the "Viterbi Decoder" name in 4G/5G/CDMA).

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example (POS Tagging)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup (mimics KTU textbook style):**
> Consider an HMM for Part-of-Speech tagging with **two states**:
> - $q_1 = N$ (Noun), $q_2 = V$ (Verb)
>
> **Observation sequence:** $O = \text{``time flies''}$  →  $o_1 = \text{time}$, $o_2 = \text{flies}$
>
> **Given parameters:**
> - Initial: $\pi_N = 0.6$, $\pi_V = 0.4$
> - Transition matrix $A$:
> $$A = \begin{bmatrix} a_{NN} & a_{NV} \\ a_{VN} & a_{VV} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 & 0.7 \\ 0.8 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$
> - Emission probabilities (the only two words in our toy vocabulary):
> $$b_N(\text{time}) = 0.9, \quad b_N(\text{flies}) = 0.1, \quad b_V(\text{time}) = 0.2, \quad b_V(\text{flies}) = 0.8$$
>
> **Task:** Use the Viterbi Algorithm to find the most likely tag sequence for the observation *"time flies"*.

### 3.2 Phase 1 — Initialization (t = 1)

**For state N (Noun):**

$$V_1(N) = \pi_N \cdot b_N(o_1) = 0.6 \cdot 0.9 = 0.54$$

**For state V (Verb):**

$$V_1(V) = \pi_V \cdot b_V(o_1) = 0.4 \cdot 0.2 = 0.08$$

**Backpointers at t = 1** (sentinel value, no predecessor):

$$B_1(N) = 0, \quad B_1(V) = 0$$

> **Interpretation:** After seeing *"time"*, the path ending at Noun has score **0.54**, much higher than Verb's **0.08**. This is because *"time"* is a far more typical noun than verb.

### 3.3 Phase 2 — Recursion (t = 2)

**For state N at t = 2:**

We must consider coming from either N or V at $t=1$, multiply by the corresponding transition, then by the emission of *"flies"* from N.

$$V_2(N) = \max_{i \in \{N, V\}} \big[V_1(i) \cdot a_{iN}\big] \cdot b_N(o_2)$$

**Path via N → N:**

$$V_1(N) \cdot a_{NN} = 0.54 \cdot 0.3 = 0.162$$

**Path via V → N:**

$$V_1(V) \cdot a_{VN} = 0.08 \cdot 0.8 = 0.064$$

**Take maximum and multiply by emission:**

$$V_2(N) = \max(0.162,\ 0.064) \cdot b_N(\text{flies}) = 0.162 \cdot 0.1 = 0.0162$$

**Backpointer:**

$$B_2(N) = \arg\max(0.162,\ 0.064) = N$$

**For state V at t = 2:**

**Path via N → V:**

$$V_1(N) \cdot a_{NV} = 0.54 \cdot 0.7 = 0.378$$

**Path via V → V:**

$$V_1(V) \cdot a_{VV} = 0.08 \cdot 0.2 = 0.016$$

**Take maximum and multiply by emission:**

$$V_2(V) = \max(0.378,\ 0.016) \cdot b_V(\text{flies}) = 0.378 \cdot 0.8 = 0.3024$$

**Backpointer:**

$$B_2(V) = \arg\max(0.378,\ 0.016) = N$$

### 3.4 Phase 3 — Termination

**Best final score:**

$$P^* = \max\big(V_2(N),\ V_2(V)\big) = \max(0.0162,\ 0.3024) = 0.3024$$

**Best final state:**

$$q_2^* = \arg\max\big(V_2(N),\ V_2(V)\big) = V$$

### 3.5 Phase 3 (cont.) — Backtracking (Path Reconstruction)

**Step 1 (recover q₁ from q₂\* = V):**

$$q_1^* = B_2(q_2^*) = B_2(V) = N$$

**Step 2 (recover q₀, which is the start symbol — terminates backtracking).**

### 3.6 Final Result & Verification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Viterbi-decoded tag sequence is: $Q^* = (N,\ V)$**
> i.e., *"time"* is a **Noun** and *"flies"* is a **Verb** — the linguistically correct reading: *"Time flies (like an arrow)."* ✔

**Verification by brute force over the 4 possible paths:**

| Path | Computation | Score | Max? |
|---|---|---|---|
| N → N | $0.6 \cdot 0.9 \cdot 0.3 \cdot 0.1$ | 0.0162 | No |
| N → V | $0.6 \cdot 0.9 \cdot 0.7 \cdot 0.8$ | **0.3024** | **✔ YES** |
| V → N | $0.4 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 0.1$ | 0.0064 | No |
| V → V | $0.4 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 0.8$ | 0.0128 | No |

The Viterbi result $0.3024$ matches the brute-force maximum — **algorithm verified**.

### 3.7 Trellis Summary Table (Analytical Matrix)

| Time $t$ | State $j$ | $V_t(j)$ | Best predecessor $B_t(j)$ | Winning predecessor value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | N | 0.54 | 0 (start) | — |
| 1 | V | 0.08 | 0 (start) | — |
| 2 | N | 0.0162 | N | 0.162 |
| 2 | V | **0.3024** | N | 0.378 |

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Viterbi Engine

The diagram below maps the **complete functional architecture** of a Viterbi decoder as deployed in real NLP systems (e.g., a POS tagger or speech recognizer). It shows how the algorithm interacts with upstream and downstream components.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputLayer["INPUT LAYER"]
        obsSeq["Observation Sequence O = o1, o2, ... oT\ne.g., words of a sentence"]:::inputNode
        hmmParams["HMM Parameters lambda = A, B, pi\n(pre-trained via Baum-Welch or MLE)"]:::inputNode
    end

    subgraph InitBlock["PHASE 1: INITIALIZATION MODULE"]
        initCalc["Compute V1(j) = pi_j * bj o1\nfor all j in 1..N\nSet B1(j) = 0"]:::initNode
    end

    subgraph RecurBlock["PHASE 2: RECURSION MODULE"]
        recurCalc["For t = 2 to T:\nVt(j) = max over i of Vt-1 i * aij * bj ot\nBt(j) = argmax over i of Vt-1 i * aij"]:::recurNode
        trellisStore["Trellis Matrix Storage\nsize N x T"]:::storeNode
    end

    subgraph TermBlock["PHASE 3: TERMINATION AND BACKTRACKING"]
        termCalc["P-star = max over j of VT j\nq-star-T = argmax over j of VT j"]:::termNode
        backtrack["Path Reconstruction Loop\nfor t = T-1 down to 1:\nq-star-t = Bt+1 q-star-t+1"]:::backNode
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        bestPath["Optimal Hidden State Sequence Q-star = q1, q2, ... qT\ne.g., NN VB DT NN"]:::outNode
    end

    obsSeq --> initCalc
    hmmParams --> initCalc
    initCalc --> recurCalc
    recurCalc --> trellisStore
    trellisStore --> recurCalc
    recurCalc --> termCalc
    termCalc --> backtrack
    trellisStore --> backtrack
    backtrack --> bestPath

    classDef inputNode fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef initNode fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef recurNode fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef storeNode fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef termNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef backNode fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**
*Define the Viterbi Algorithm. Why is dynamic programming essential for its implementation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Viterbi Algorithm** is a dynamic programming method used to decode the most probable sequence of hidden states in a **Hidden Markov Model (HMM)** given a sequence of observations. It computes the **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** estimate by recursively storing the best score for each state at each time step in a trellis structure, thereby finding the optimal state sequence $Q^* = (q_1^*, q_2^*, \dots, q_T^*)$.

Dynamic programming is essential because **(2 marks)**:
1. **Exponential brute-force infeasibility:** Without DP, one must enumerate all $N^T$ possible state sequences, which is computationally intractable for any realistic $N$ and $T$.
2. **Optimal substructure:** Viterbi exploits the principle that the best path to a state $j$ at time $t$ consists of the best path to some state $i$ at time $t-1$ followed by transition $i \to j$. This allows reusing previously computed scores, reducing complexity from $O(N^T)$ to $O(N^2 \cdot T)$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is the role of the backpointer matrix in the Viterbi Algorithm? What happens if we omit it?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **backpointer matrix $B$** stores, for each state $j$ at time $t$, the identity of the *previous* state $i$ that maximized the recurrence $V_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij}$. Formally:
$$B_t(j) = \arg\max_{1 \leq i \leq N}\big[V_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij}\big]$$

Its role is to enable **path reconstruction via backtracking**: once termination picks the best final state $q_T^*$, the backpointers are followed in reverse from $t = T-1$ down to $t = 1$ to recover the full optimal state sequence $Q^*$.

**If omitted (1 mark):** We would only know the *score* of the best path, but not the *path itself*. The algorithm would degenerate into the **Forward Algorithm** (which computes total probability), and the decoding objective would fail entirely. The backpointer is what distinguishes Viterbi from a simple scoring algorithm.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**
*Consider the following HMM used for Part-of-Speech Tagging:*
- *States: $S_1$ = Noun (N), $S_2$ = Verb (V)*
- *Observation sequence: $O = (\text{Dogs}, \text{run})$*
- *Initial distribution: $\pi_N = 0.7$, $\pi_V = 0.3$*
- *Transition probabilities:*
  * $a_{N \to N} = 0.4$, $a_{N \to V} = 0.6$, $a_{V \to N} = 0.5$, $a_{V \to V} = 0.5$
- *Emission probabilities:*
  * $b_N(\text{Dogs}) = 0.8$, $b_N(\text{run}) = 0.1$, $b_V(\text{Dogs}) = 0.2$, $b_V(\text{run}) = 0.9$

**(a) Apply the Viterbi Algorithm to find the most likely tag sequence for the sentence "Dogs run". Show all initialization, recursion, termination, and backtracking steps. (7 marks)**

**(b) Verify your answer by computing the probability of all four possible state sequences using the brute-force method. Explain why Viterbi is preferred over brute force for longer sentences. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q3(a) — Viterbi Algorithm Steps (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Initialization at t = 1 for observation "Dogs":**

$$V_1(N) = \pi_N \cdot b_N(\text{Dogs}) = 0.7 \cdot 0.8 = 0.56$$

$$V_1(V) = \pi_V \cdot b_V(\text{Dogs}) = 0.3 \cdot 0.2 = 0.06$$

**Backpointers at t = 1:**

$$B_1(N) = 0, \quad B_1(V) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Recursion at t = 2 for observation "run":**

**For state N at t = 2:**

$$V_2(N) = \max\big[V_1(N) \cdot a_{N \to N},\ V_1(V) \cdot a_{V \to N}\big] \cdot b_N(\text{run})$$

$$= \max\big[0.56 \cdot 0.4,\ 0.06 \cdot 0.5\big] \cdot 0.1$$

$$= \max\big[0.224,\ 0.030\big] \cdot 0.1 = 0.224 \cdot 0.1 = 0.0224$$

$$B_2(N) = \arg\max(0.224,\ 0.030) = N$$

**For state V at t = 2:**

$$V_2(V) = \max\big[V_1(N) \cdot a_{N \to V},\ V_1(V) \cdot a_{V \to V}\big] \cdot b_V(\text{run})$$

$$= \max\big[0.56 \cdot 0.6,\ 0.06 \cdot 0.5\big] \cdot 0.9$$

$$= \max\big[0.336,\ 0.030\big] \cdot 0.9 = 0.336 \cdot 0.9 = 0.3024$$

$$B_2(V) = \arg\max(0.336,\ 0.030) = N$$

**Step 3 — Termination:**

$$P^* = \max\big[V_2(N),\ V_2(V)\big] = \max(0.0224,\ 0.3024) = 0.3024$$

$$q_2^* = \arg\max\big[V_2(N),\ V_2(V)\big] = V$$

**Step 4 — Backtracking:**

Recover $q_1^*$ using the backpointer of the winning final state:

$$q_1^* = B_2(q_2^*) = B_2(V) = N$$

**Final Viterbi Output:**

$$\boxed{Q^* = (N,\ V) = \text{(Noun, Verb)}}$$

The most likely tag sequence for *"Dogs run"* is **Noun → Verb**, which matches the linguistically correct grammatical reading.

---

#### Model Solution for Q3(b) — Brute-Force Verification and Complexity Analysis (7 marks)

**Brute-force enumeration of all 4 paths:**

$$P(\text{path} \mid \text{obs}) = \pi_{q_1} \cdot b_{q_1}(o_1) \cdot a_{q_1 \to q_2} \cdot b_{q_2}(o_2)$$

| # | Path | Calculation | Probability | Max? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | N → N | $0.7 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 0.1$ | 0.0224 | No |
| 2 | N → V | $0.7 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 0.9$ | **0.3024** | **✔ YES** |
| 3 | V → N | $0.3 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.1$ | 0.0030 | No |
| 4 | V → V | $0.3 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.9$ | 0.0270 | No |

**Verification:** The brute-force maximum of **0.3024** for path **N → V** exactly matches the Viterbi output. ✔

**Why Viterbi is preferred for longer sentences (3 marks):**
- For a sentence of length $T$ with $N$ possible tags, brute force requires evaluating $N^T$ paths.
  - For $N = 10$ and $T = 10$: $10^{10} = 10$ billion evaluations.
  - For $N = 45$ (Penn Treebank tags) and $T = 30$: $45^{30} \approx 10^{48}$ — physically impossible.
- Viterbi reduces this to $O(N^2 \cdot T) = O(10^2 \cdot 10) = 1000$ operations for the first case and $O(45^2 \cdot 30) \approx 60{,}750$ for the second — both trivially fast.
- Viterbi uses $O(N \cdot T)$ memory for the trellis, which is the *minimum* needed to retain enough information for backtracking.
- Conclusion: Viterbi achieves **exponential speedup with no loss of optimality**, making it the standard decoder in production NLP systems.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest Pattern]**
*(a) With a neat trellis diagram, explain the three phases of the Viterbi Algorithm: Initialization, Recursion, and Backtracking. State the recurrence relation used in the Recursion step. (7 marks)*

*(b) Given the observation sequence $O = (o_1, o_2, o_3) = (\text{The}, \text{cat}, \text{sleeps})$ and the following HMM with 2 states N (Noun) and V (Verb), use Viterbi to find the best tag sequence. (7 marks)*

$$\pi_N = 0.5,\ \pi_V = 0.5$$

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.4 \\ 0.3 & 0.7 \end{bmatrix}$$

| | "The" | "cat" | "sleeps" |
|---|---|---|---|
| $b_N$ | 0.4 | 0.8 | 0.1 |
| $b_V$ | 0.6 | 0.2 | 0.9 |

#### Model Solution for Q4(a) — Three Phases of Viterbi (7 marks)

The Viterbi Algorithm operates on a **trellis** — a 2-D grid of $N$ states by $T$ time steps, where each cell $(t, j)$ stores the score of the best path ending at state $j$ at time $t$.

**Phase 1 — Initialization (1 mark):**
At $t = 1$, for each state $j$:
$$V_1(j) = \pi_j \cdot b_j(o_1), \quad B_1(j) = 0$$

**Phase 2 — Recursion (3 marks):**
For $t = 2, 3, \dots, T$ and each state $j$:
$$V_t(j) = \max_{1 \leq i \leq N}\big[V_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij}\big] \cdot b_j(o_t)$$
$$B_t(j) = \arg\max_{1 \leq i \leq N}\big[V_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij}\big]$$

The **backpointer** $B_t(j)$ is the *identity* of the previous state that yielded the maximum; this is what makes path recovery possible.

**Phase 3 — Termination and Backtracking (3 marks):**
$$P^* = \max_{1 \leq j \leq N} V_T(j), \quad q_T^* = \arg\max V_T(j)$$
Then walk backwards:
$$q_t^* = B_{t+1}(q_{t+1}^*), \quad t = T-1, T-2, \dots, 1$$

The trellis has $N$ cells per time step; total complexity is $O(N^2 \cdot T)$.

---

#### Model Solution for Q4(b) — Numerical Viterbi for 3-word Sentence (7 marks)

**t = 1 (Initialization for "The"):**

$$V_1(N) = 0.5 \cdot 0.4 = 0.20, \quad B_1(N) = 0$$

$$V_1(V) = 0.5 \cdot 0.6 = 0.30, \quad B_1(V) = 0$$

**t = 2 (Recursion for "cat"):**

**State N:**
$$V_2(N) = \max\big[V_1(N) \cdot a_{NN},\ V_1(V) \cdot a_{VN}\big] \cdot b_N(\text{cat})$$
$$= \max\big[0.20 \cdot 0.6,\ 0.30 \cdot 0.3\big] \cdot 0.8 = \max(0.12,\ 0.09) \cdot 0.8 = 0.12 \cdot 0.8 = 0.096$$
$$B_2(N) = N$$

**State V:**
$$V_2(V) = \max\big[V_1(N) \cdot a_{NV},\ V_1(V) \cdot a_{VV}\big] \cdot b_V(\text{cat})$$
$$= \max\big[0.20 \cdot 0.4,\ 0.30 \cdot 0.7\big] \cdot 0.2 = \max(0.08,\ 0.21) \cdot 0.2 = 0.21 \cdot 0.2 = 0.042$$
$$B_2(V) = V$$

**t = 3 (Recursion for "sleeps"):**

**State N:**
$$V_3(N) = \max\big[V_2(N) \cdot a_{NN},\ V_2(V) \cdot a_{VN}\big] \cdot b_N(\text{sleeps})$$
$$= \max\big[0.096 \cdot 0.6,\ 0.042 \cdot 0.3\big] \cdot 0.1 = \max(0.0576,\ 0.0126) \cdot 0.1 = 0.0576 \cdot 0.1 = 0.00576$$
$$B_3(N) = N$$

**State V:**
$$V_3(V) = \max\big[V_2(N) \cdot a_{NV},\ V_2(V) \cdot a_{VV}\big] \cdot b_V(\text{sleeps})$$
$$= \max\big[0.096 \cdot 0.4,\ 0.042 \cdot 0.7\big] \cdot 0.9 = \max(0.0384,\ 0.0294) \cdot 0.9 = 0.0384 \cdot 0.9 = 0.03456$$
$$B_3(V) = N$$

**Termination:**

$$P^* = \max(V_3(N),\ V_3(V)) = \max(0.00576,\ 0.03456) = 0.03456$$
$$q_3^* = V$$

**Backtracking:**

$$q_2^* = B_3(q_3^*) = B_3(V) = N$$

$$q_1^* = B_2(q_2^*) = B_2(N) = N$$

**Final Viterbi Output:**

$$\boxed{Q^* = (N,\ N,\ V) = \text{(Noun, Noun, Verb)}}$$

The correct linguistic parse: *"The cat sleeps"* → Determinant-Noun-Verb. ✔

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Common Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU Board Valued Answers)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the emission probability in recursion (–1 to –2 marks):**
>    Students often compute only $\max_i [V_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij}]$ and *forget* to multiply by $b_j(o_t)$. Both the transition and emission must be included.
>
> 2. **Confusing $V_t(j)$ with $b_j(o_t)$ (–1 mark):**
>    $V_t(j)$ is the cumulative best score; $b_j(o_t)$ is the local emission. They are fundamentally different.
>
> 3. **Omitting the backpointer from the answer (–1 to –2 marks):**
>    The backpointer $B_t(j)$ is mandatory for the path-reconstruction phase. Students who compute $V$ correctly but never write $B$ lose marks on the "Termination and Backtracking" step.
>
> 4. **Wrong final state selection (–1 mark):**
>    Some students terminate with $q_T^* = N$ without first verifying that $V_T(N) > V_T(V)$. The argmax must be **explicitly computed**, not assumed.
>
> 5. **Confusing Viterbi with Forward Algorithm (–1 mark):**
>    Forward computes $\sum_i$ (total probability); Viterbi computes $\max_i$ (best path). The summation vs. maximization is the *key distinguishing feature*.
>
> 6. **Ignoring numerical underflow (–1 mark for conceptual Qs):**
>    For long sequences, raw Viterbi underflows. Mentioning the log-space variant $v_t(j) = \max_i [v_{t-1}(i) + \log a_{ij}] + \log b_j(o_t)$ earns full marks on stability questions.
>
> 7. **Brute-force verification must be a separate table (–0.5 mark):**
>    KTU expects a clearly tabulated brute-force check; merging it into narrative text loses legibility marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist for Viterbi Algorithm**
>
> - [x] **Viterbi Algorithm** = Dynamic programming decoder for HMMs that finds the MAP hidden-state sequence.
> - [x] **Three phases:** Initialization → Recursion (forward pass with backpointers) → Termination + Backtracking.
> - [x] **Core recurrence:** $V_t(j) = \max_i [V_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij}] \cdot b_j(o_t)$.
> - [x] **Backpointer:** $B_t(j) = \arg\max_i [V_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij}]$ — stores the *identity* of the winning previous state, not the value.
> - [x] **Termination:** $q_T^* = \arg\max_j V_T(j)$ and $P^* = \max_j V_T(j)$.
> - [x] **Backtracking:** $q_t^* = B_{t+1}(q_{t+1}^*)$, runs for $t = T-1, \dots, 1$.
> - [x] **Time complexity:** $O(N^2 \cdot T)$; **Space complexity:** $O(N \cdot T)$ for the trellis.
> - [x] **Brute-force complexity:** $O(N^T)$ — infeasible for $N, T \geq 5$.
> - [x] **Log-space variant** is essential for long sequences to avoid underflow.
> - [x] **Viterbi vs. Forward:** Viterbi uses $\max$ (single best path); Forward uses $\sum$ (total probability over all paths).
> - [x] **Viterbi vs. Baum-Welch:** Viterbi is the *decoding* step (assumes known HMM parameters); Baum-Welch is the *learning* step that estimates those parameters.
> - [x] **Applications:** POS tagging, NER, speech recognition, bioinformatics gene finding, telecommunications (convolutional codes), GPS map matching.
> - [x] **Trellis diagram** is the standard visual — $N$ states vertically, $T$ time steps horizontally, with arrows for transitions.
> - [x] **Backpointer matrix is mandatory** — without it, you only have the score, not the path.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Morphology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT741 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Morphology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Morphology** is the branch of linguistics (and a foundational sub-field of Natural Language Processing) that studies the **internal structure of words** and the **smallest meaning-bearing units** of a language. These minimal units are called **morphemes**. Morphology investigates how morphemes combine to form words, how they interact with each other, and how their surface forms are realized from abstract underlying representations.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – PEITT741 (NLP)**, Module 2 frames morphology as the computational study of word formation, focusing on:
- Identifying the morpheme inventory of a language.
- Modeling morphological rules (both concatenative and non-concatenative).
- Building computational tools such as **morphological parsers** and **stemmers**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEITT741 – Module 2):** Students must be able to explain morphemes, word-formation processes, finite-state morphology, the Porter stemmer, and morphological parsing techniques (left-to-right and right-to-left).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Words are like LEGO Towers"

Imagine a **LEGO tower**. Each LEGO brick is too small to be a full "house," but combining bricks makes a stable structure. Likewise:

- A **word** (e.g., "unhappinesses") = the completed tower.
- A **morpheme** = an individual LEGO brick (e.g., *un-, happy, -ness, -es*).
- **Morphology** = the rulebook that tells you *which* bricks can be stacked and *in which order*.

A native speaker intuitively knows that "unhappinesses" is legal but "happinessun" is not. Morphology is precisely the formalization of this intuitive knowledge for a computer.

## 1.3 Key Building Blocks — Morphemes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Morpheme:** The minimal meaningful unit of a language that cannot be further divided into smaller meaningful parts.

| Term | Definition | Example (English) |
|---|---|---|
| **Root** | The core morpheme carrying the principal lexical meaning. | *act* in *actor, acting, acted* |
| **Stem** | The form to which an affix is attached (may equal root). | *act, acted* (after -ed is removed) |
| **Affix** | A bound morpheme attached to a root/stem. | *-ed, -ing, un-, re-* |
| **Free morpheme** | Can stand alone as a word. | *cat, run, book* |
| **Bound morpheme** | Cannot stand alone; must attach to others. | *-s, -ed, -ly, un-* |

## 1.4 Why Morphology Matters in NLP

> [!NOTE]
> Search engines, IR systems, chatbots, and machine translators **all** depend on morphological analysis. Without it, "running," "ran," and "runs" would be three unrelated tokens, exploding the vocabulary size and degrading recall. Morphological normalization (stemming/lemmatization) collapses these to a single index term *run*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Morphemes

### 2.1.1 By Combinatorial Freedom
- **Free morphemes** — function independently: *go, fast, child, in, on*
- **Bound morphemes** — must attach: *-able, -ly, -ity, re-, -s (plural)*

### 2.1.2 By Position (for Affixes)
- **Prefix** — before the root: *un-happy, re-write*
- **Suffix** — after the root: *play-er, quick-ly*
- **Infix** — inserted inside the root: *f*-*ing*-*able* (Tagalog: *basa* → *b-um-asa*)
- **Circumfix** — wraps around the root: German *ge-…-t* (e.g., *ge-sag-t* "said")

## 2.2 Inflectional vs. Derivational Morphology

| Property | Inflectional | Derivational |
|---|---|---|
| **POS change?** | Never | Often |
| **Meaning change?** | Grammatical (tense, number, case) | Lexical (new concept) |
| **Productivity** | Highly productive | Limited & selective |
| **Order wrt root** | Outside derivational affixes | Closer to the root |
| **English examples** | *cats, walked, bigger (comparative)* | *happiness (n), unhappy (adj), modernize (v)* |
| **POS category** | Same syntactic category | Often different category |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners **love** asking students to produce 5+ word families and tag each affix as inflectional or derivational. Master the "INFLECTION is OUTSIDE DERIVATION" rule.

## 2.3 Word-Formation Processes

1. **Affixation** — adding prefixes/suffixes/infixes/circumfixes.
2. **Compounding** — joining two free morphemes: *blackboard, software, bedroom.*
3. **Cliticization** — attachment of clitics (*n't, 's, 'm, 've*).
4. **Reduplication** — full or partial repetition (Malayalam: *waLam waLam* "again and again").
5. **Suppletion** — irregular replacement: *go → went, good → better.*
6. **Conversion / Zero-derivation** — POS change without affix: *to run (n→v), an empty (n→adj).*
7. **Back-formation** — affix removal mistakenly interpreted as derivation: *edit* (from *editor*).
8. **Blending** — merging parts of two words: *smoke + fog = smog, motor + hotel = motel.*
9. **Acronyming / Clipping** — *FBI, gym, flu.*

## 2.4 Concatenative vs. Non-Concatenative Morphology

| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Concatenative** | Morphemes are linearly concatenated. | *un + kind + ness* |
| **Non-concatenative** | Uses templatic patterns, infixes, or vowel changes. | Arabic root *k-t-b* → *kataba, kātib, maktūb* |

## 2.5 Finite-State Morphology — Theoretical Foundation

A **finite-state automaton (FSA)** or **finite-state transducer (FST)** encodes morphological rules as states and transitions. Each transition consumes a morpheme (FSA) or maps an input morpheme to an output feature (FST).

For FSTs used in morphology:

$$ \delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q \times \Delta $$

where:
- $Q$ = finite set of states
- $\Sigma$ = input alphabet (surface forms)
- $\Delta$ = output alphabet (lexical features: `+Noun`, `+Pl`, `+Past` …)
- $\delta$ = transition function

A two-level morphology (Koskenniemi) uses **parallel FSTs** to map between:
- **Lexical level** (lemma + features)
- **Surface level** (realized word)

## 2.6 Porter Stemmer — Theoretical Core

The Porter stemmer removes common morphological and inflectional endings from English words. It works in **5 steps** applied sequentially, each containing a set of **rewrite rules** of the form:

$$ (condition) \;\; L1 \rightarrow R1 $$

where $L1$ is the longest suffix matching a condition and $R1$ is its replacement.

### 2.6.1 The "Measure" Function m

For any word $w$ partitioned as $[C](VC)^m[V]$, the **measure** $m$ is the number of VC pairs. Porter uses this to gauge morphological complexity.

$$ m(w) = \text{count of } (VC) \text{ sequences in } w $$

Examples:
- $m(\text{trouble}) = 1$  → `tr ouble`
- $m(\text{generalizations}) = 4$  → `gen er al iz at ions`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $V = \{a, e, i, o, u, y\}$ (treats initial *y* as consonant). $C$ = all other letters.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Function | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| $m(w)$ | Porter measure of word $w$ | Decides which rule to apply |
| $\ast m$ | condition "m > 0" | Restrict rule to multisyllabic words |
| $\ast v\ast$ | contains a vowel | Avoid stemming vowel-only strings |
| $\ast d$ | ends in double consonant | Step 5b double-letter removal |
| $\ast o$ | ends in CVVC (CVC w/ 2nd v ≠ W,X,Y) | Step 5b |
| $L1$ | longest matching suffix | Rule LHS |
| $R1$ | replacement suffix | Rule RHS |
| $\Sigma$ | surface alphabet | FSA input |
| $\Delta$ | feature alphabet | FST output |
| $Q$ | FSA state set | FSA definition |
| $q_0$ | start state | FSA definition |
| $F$ | final/accepting states | FSA definition |

## 2.8 Real-World Utility of Morphological Systems

- **Information Retrieval** — indexing by stem boosts recall.
- **Machine Translation** — handling morphologically rich languages (Malayalam, Arabic, Finnish).
- **Spell-checkers & Grammar-correctors** — generate candidate surface forms.
- **Speech Synthesis** — produce correct phoneme sequences for inflected forms.
- **Named-Entity Recognition** — match *corporation, corporate, corp.* by stem.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Morphological Analysis of "unhappinesses"

Let us trace the morpheme-by-morpheme decomposition, which is a routine 3- to 7-mark derivation the KTU examiner expects.

**Input word:** `unhappinesses`

### Step 1 — Identify the Free Morpheme
- `happy` is a free morpheme (an adjective in English).
- All other units are bound morphemes.

### Step 2 — Peel off Suffixes (right-to-left)

$$
\text{unhappinesses} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{unhappi} + \text{ness} + \text{es}
$$

- `es` → plural suffix on nouns ending in -s, -x, -z, -ch, -sh.
- `ness` → derivational suffix converting adjective → abstract noun.

### Step 3 — Peel off Remaining Suffix

$$
\text{unhappi} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{un} + \text{happi}
$$

- `i` here is not a separate morpheme but a result of **y → i** orthographic rule (since the next suffix begins with a vowel: *-ness*).

### Step 4 — Peel off Prefix

$$
\text{unhappi} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{un} + \text{happy}
$$

- `un` → derivational prefix, antonym-forming.

### Step 5 — Final Root

$$
\boxed{\text{Root} = \text{happy}}
$$

### Summary Table

| Surface | Morpheme | Category | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| `un-` | Prefix | Bound, derivational | Negation |
| `happy` | Root (free) | Free | "feeling pleasure" |
| `-i` | orthographic | n/a | y→i before vowel-initial suffix |
| `-ness` | Suffix | Bound, derivational | Adj → Noun |
| `-es` | Suffix | Bound, inflectional | Singular → Plural |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Porter Stemmer on "generalizations"

We will exhaustively derive the stem of the word `generalizations`. This is a high-yield KTU derivation.

### Pre-processing
Initial word: $w_0 = $ `generalizations`

Length: 16 characters. Vowel set $V = \{a, e, i, o, u, y\}$.

### Step 1a — Plural and -ed/-ing

Rules applied (only if measure $m>0$):
- SSES → SS (*caresses → caress*)
- IES → I (*ponies → poni*)
- SS → SS (*caress → caress*)
- S → ∅ (*cats → cat*)

`generalizations` ends in `-izations`; **none** of the Step 1a patterns match the suffix form, so no change.

$$ w_1 = w_0 = \text{generalizations} $$

### Step 1b — -ed, -ing cleanup

Patterns look for `(*v*) ED` or `(*v*) ING`. No match.

$$ w_1 = \text{generalizations} $$

### Step 1c — y → i

Pattern `(m>0) Y → I`. Does `generalizations` end in `Y`? **No** (ends in `s`).

$$ w_1 = \text{generalizations} $$

### Step 2 — Derivational Suffix Removal

Pattern: `(m>0) ATIONAL → ATE` (e.g., *relational → relate*).

`generalizations` ends in `-izations`, not `-ational`. The closest match is `(m>0) IZATION → IZE` (e.g., *formalization → formalize*).

- Compute $m(\text{generalization})$: split as `[C](VC)^m[V]` → `g-en-er-al-iz-at-ion` → measure = **4**.
- Rule `(m>0) IZATIONS → IZE` removes the final plural **AND** the `-ation` and adds `-ize`. Standard Porter list maps `-izations → -ize`.

Apply:
$$ w_2 : \text{generalizations} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{generalize} $$

### Step 3 — Further Suffix Removal

Patterns: `(m>0) ATIVE →`, `(m>0) ICAL → IC`, etc. No match in `generalize`.

$$ w_3 = \text{generalize} $$

### Step 4 — Heavy Suffix Removal (requires $m > 1$)

Patterns: `ALIZE → AL`, `ICITI → IC`, `ICAL → IC`, `FUL → ∅`, `NESS → ∅`.

`generalize` ends in `-alize`. Rule `(m>1) ALIZE → AL` → since $m(\text{generalize}) = 3 > 1$:

$$ w_4 : \text{generalize} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{general} $$

### Step 5a — Final 'e' Removal

Rule: `(m>1) E → ∅` OR `(m=1 and not *o) E → ∅`.

For `general`: $m = 3 > 1$, so:

$$ w_5 : \text{general} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{general} \;\;(\text{ends in non-e, no change}) $$

### Step 5b — Double-Consonant Cleanup

Rule: `(m>1 and *d and *o) → remove last letter`.

`general` does not end in a double consonant, so no change.

$$ w_5 = \text{general} $$

### Final Stem

$$
\boxed{\text{stem}(\text{generalizations}) = \text{general}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Porter Stemmer Step-wise Decision Flow

| Step | Trigger Pattern | Required Condition | Example (Before → After) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1a | SSES / IES / SS / S | none / (m>0) | `caresses` → `caress` |
| 1b | (EED|ED|ING) + cleanup | (*v*) present | `plastered` → `plaster` |
| 1c | Y → I | (*v* present in stem) | `happy` → `happi` |
| 2 | Various ization, ational, etc. | m > 0 | `formalization` → `formalize` |
| 3 | ative, ical, alize, icate, ful, ness | m > 0 | `triplicate` → `triplic` |
| 4 | al, ance, ence, er, ic, … | m > 1 | `revival` → `reviv` |
| 5a | E → ∅ | m > 1 or (m=1 & not *o) | `probate` → `probat` |
| 5b | double-consonant end | m > 1 & *d & *o | `controll` → `control` |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Finite-State Transducer for English Regular Plurals

Build an FST that maps:
- `cat +N +Pl` ↔ `cats`
- `dog +N +Pl` ↔ `dogs`
- `fox +N +Pl` ↔ `foxes`
- `church +N +Pl` ↔ `churches`

### Step 1 — State Design

States: $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_s, q_x, q_z, q_ch, q_sh, q\_es, q_f\}$

### Step 2 — Transitions

A partial FST transition table:

| State | Input | Output | Next State |
|---|---|---|---|
| $q_0$ | `cat` | `cat` | $q_1$ |
| $q_1$ | `+N+Pl` | `+s` | $q_s$ |
| $q_0$ | `fox` | `fox` | $q_x$ |
| $q_x$ | `+N+Pl` | `+es` | $q\_es$ |
| $q_0$ | `church` | `church` | $q_{ch}$ |
| $q_{ch}$ | `+N+Pl` | `+es` | $q\_es$ |

This FST accepts `[stem]+N+Pl` and emits the surface form. Reverse direction maps surface → feature bundle (analysis).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Word-Internal Morphological Hierarchy

The following diagram depicts how a complex English word (`unhappinesses`) decomposes hierarchically from surface form to root morpheme.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootNode["Word: unhappinesses"] --> morph1["Prefix: un"]
    rootNode --> morph2["Root: happy"]
    rootNode --> morph3["Suffix: ness"]
    rootNode --> morph4["Inflectional: es"]
    morph1 --> desc1["Derivational, Antonymy"]
    morph2 --> desc2["Free Morpheme, Adjective"]
    morph3 --> desc3["Derivational, Noun-Former"]
    morph4 --> desc4["Plural Marker"]
```

## 4.2 Classification of Morphological Processes (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Morphology"] --> B["Concatenative"]
    A --> C["Non-Concatenative"]
    B --> B1["Affixation"]
    B --> B2["Compounding"]
    B --> B3["Cliticization"]
    B --> B4["Reduplication"]
    C --> C1["Infixation"]
    C --> C2["Templatic"]
    C --> C3["Suppletion"]
    C --> C4["Vowel Ablaut"]
```

## 4.3 Finite-State Transducer for English Plural Generation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s0["q0 start"] -->|cat : cat| s1["q1 stem cat"]
    s0 -->|dog : dog| s2["q2 stem dog"]
    s0 -->|fox : fox| s3["q3 stem fox"]
    s0 -->|church : church| s4["q4 stem church"]
    s0 -->|box : box| s5["q5 stem box"]
    s1 -->|plus N plus Pl : plus s| fs["fs accept s plural"]
    s2 -->|plus N plus Pl : plus s| fs
    s3 -->|plus N plus Pl : plus es| fes["fes accept es plural"]
    s4 -->|plus N plus Pl : plus es| fes
    s5 -->|plus N plus Pl : plus es| fes
```

## 4.4 Porter Stemmer Pipeline (5-Step Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inp["Input Word"] --> s1a["Step 1a: Plurals and -ed -ing"]
    s1a --> s1b["Step 1b: -ed -ing cleanup"]
    s1b --> s1c["Step 1c: y to i"]
    s1c --> s2["Step 2: Derivational Suffixes"]
    s2 --> s3["Step 3: More Suffixes"]
    s3 --> s4["Step 4: Heavy Suffixes m greater 1"]
    s4 --> s5a["Step 5a: Final e removal"]
    s5a --> s5b["Step 5b: Double Letter Cleanup"]
    s5b --> out["Stem Output"]
```

## 4.5 Morphological Analyzer vs. Generator (Functional Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    surface["Surface Word"] --> ana["Morphological Analyzer"]
    ana --> feat["Feature Bundle plus Root"]
    feat --> gen["Morphological Generator"]
    gen --> surf2["Surface Forms"]
```

## 4.6 Cliticization and Affixation in English Negation (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    w["can not"] --> cl["Cliticization: cannot / cant"]
    w2["will not"] --> cl2["Cliticization: wont"]
    w3["is not"] --> cl3["Cliticization: isnt"]
    w4["un kind"] --> p["Prefixation: unkind"]
    w5["kind ly"] --> suf["Suffixation: kindly"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define morphology. Distinguish between inflectional and derivational morphemes with two examples each. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*Definition (1 mark):* Morphology is the study of the internal structure of words and the minimal meaningful units (morphemes) from which words are constructed.

*Inflectional morphemes (1 mark):* These do **not** change the word-class; they signal grammatical information such as tense, number, or case.
- `cat → cats` (-s marks plural)
- `walk → walked` (-ed marks past tense)

*Derivational morphemes (1 mark):* These usually change the word-class and the lexical meaning; they form new words.
- `happy (adj) → happiness (noun)` (suffix -ness)
- `kind (adj) → unkind (adj)` (prefix un-)

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Explain the following terms with examples: (i) Free morpheme, (ii) Bound morpheme, (iii) Allomorph. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

- **Free morpheme (1 mark):** A morpheme that can stand alone as an independent word. *Example:* `cat`, `run`, `in`, `book`.
