<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define TF-IDF and explain the role of IDF.
`[KTU University Exam - Model Question as per 2024 Scheme Pattern]`

**Model Answer:**
TF-IDF stands for **Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency**. It is a numerical statistic used to reflect the importance of a word in a document relative to a collection of documents (corpus). It is computed as the product of two statistics: **Term Frequency (TF)**, which measures how frequently a term appears in a document, and **Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)**, which measures how rare or common the term is across the entire corpus. The IDF component is crucial because it **down-weights common words** like "the", "is", "and" that appear in many documents, while **up-weighting rare but informative terms** that are distinctive to specific documents. The formula is $TF\text{-}IDF(t,d) = TF(t,d) \times IDF(t)$, where $IDF(t) = \log_{e}(N/df(t))$.

---

### Question 2: Why is raw term frequency alone insufficient for text representation?
`[KTU University Exam - Model Question as per 2024 Scheme Pattern]`

**Model Answer:**
Raw term frequency alone is **insufficient** because:
1. **Stop-word dominance:** Common words like "the", "of", "a" appear very frequently in almost every document, dominating any count-based representation without conveying semantic meaning.
2. **Length bias:** Longer documents naturally have higher raw counts for all terms, making cross-document comparisons unreliable.
3. **Lack of discriminative power:** A term appearing 100 times in one document and a term appearing 100 times in *every* document are treated equally by raw TF, even though the second term is useless for distinguishing documents.
4. **No corpus-awareness:** Raw TF is purely local; it ignores how rare or common a term is globally.

TF-IDF addresses all these issues by incorporating IDF, which penalizes globally common terms and rewards rare, distinctive ones.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Module 2 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Module Internal Choice provides two alternatives. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Full TF-IDF Computation on a Toy Corpus

`[KTU University Exam - Model Question as per 2024 Scheme Pattern]`

**Question Statement:**

Consider the following corpus $D$ containing three documents:
- **$d_1$:** `"machine learning is fun"`
- **$d_2$:** `"deep learning is powerful"`
- **$d_3$:** `"machine and deep learning are different"`

**(a) Compute the TF-IDF vector for document $d_1$ using raw TF and standard IDF (log base $e$).** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the role of L2 normalization in cosine similarity and demonstrate it on the $d_1$ vector from part (a).** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Vocabulary Construction**

List all unique terms: machine, learning, is, fun, deep, powerful, and, are, different.

$$V = \{\text{machine, learning, is, fun, deep, powerful, and, are, different}\}, \quad |V| = 9$$

**Step 2 — Compute TF for each term in $d_1$**

Document $d_1$ = `"machine learning is fun"` has 4 words, each appearing once.

$$
\begin{aligned}
TF(\text{machine}, d_1) &= 1 \\
TF(\text{learning}, d_1) &= 1 \\
TF(\text{is}, d_1) &= 1 \\
TF(\text{fun}, d_1) &= 1 \\
TF(\text{deep}, d_1) &= 0 \\
TF(\text{powerful}, d_1) &= 0 \\
TF(\text{and}, d_1) &= 0 \\
TF(\text{are}, d_1) &= 0 \\
TF(\text{different}, d_1) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Document Frequency $df(t)$ for each term**

$$
\begin{aligned}
df(\text{machine}) &= 2 \quad (d_1, d_3) \\
df(\text{learning}) &= 3 \quad (d_1, d_2, d_3) \\
df(\text{is}) &= 2 \quad (d_1, d_2) \\
df(\text{fun}) &= 1 \quad (d_1) \\
df(\text{deep}) &= 2 \quad (d_2, d_3) \\
df(\text{powerful}) &= 1 \quad (d_2) \\
df(\text{and}) &= 1 \quad (d_3) \\
df(\text{are}) &= 1 \quad (d_3) \\
df(\text{different}) &= 1 \quad (d_3)
\end{aligned}
$$

Total documents $N = 3$.

**Step 4 — Compute IDF for each term**

Using $IDF(t) = \log_{e}(N / df(t))$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
IDF(\text{machine}) &= \log_{e}(3/2) \approx 0.4055 \\
IDF(\text{learning}) &= \log_{e}(3/3) = \log_{e}(1) = 0 \\
IDF(\text{is}) &= \log_{e}(3/2) \approx 0.4055 \\
IDF(\text{fun}) &= \log_{e}(3/1) \approx 1.0986 \\
IDF(\text{deep}) &= \log_{e}(3/2) \approx 0.4055 \\
IDF(\text{powerful}) &= \log_{e}(3/1) \approx 1.0986 \\
IDF(\text{and}) &= \log_{e}(3/1) \approx 1.0986 \\
IDF(\text{are}) &= \log_{e}(3/1) \approx 1.0986 \\
IDF(\text{different}) &= \log_{e}(3/1) \approx 1.0986
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute TF-IDF Vector for $d_1$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TF\text{-}IDF(\text{machine}, d_1) &= 1 \times 0.4055 = 0.4055 \\
TF\text{-}IDF(\text{learning}, d_1) &= 1 \times 0 = 0 \\
TF\text{-}IDF(\text{is}, d_1) &= 1 \times 0.4055 = 0.4055 \\
TF\text{-}IDF(\text{fun}, d_1) &= 1 \times 1.0986 = 1.0986 \\
TF\text{-}IDF(\text{deep}, d_1) &= 0 \times 0.4055 = 0 \\
TF\text{-}IDF(\text{powerful}, d_1) &= 0 \\
TF\text{-}IDF(\text{and}, d_1) &= 0 \\
TF\text{-}IDF(\text{are}, d_1) &= 0 \\
TF\text{-}IDF(\text{different}, d_1) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final TF-IDF Vector for $d_1$:**

$$\mathbf{v}_{d_1} = [0.4055,\ 0,\ 0.4055,\ 1.0986,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0]$$

**Key Insight:** The term **"learning"** appears in *all 3 documents*, so its IDF is **0** and it contributes nothing to $d_1$'s vector. The term **"fun"** is *unique* to $d_1$, giving it the **highest TF-IDF score** — this correctly identifies it as the most distinctive term in $d_1$.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution — 7 Marks

**L2 Normalization Explanation:**

L2 normalization rescales the vector so that its Euclidean norm equals 1. This is critical for **cosine similarity** because it ensures that similarity depends only on the *direction* (angle) of the vectors, not their *magnitude* (length). Without L2 normalization, a longer document would naturally produce larger vector magnitudes, biasing similarity computations.

The L2-normalized vector is computed as:

$$\hat{\mathbf{v}} = \frac{\mathbf{v}}{\|\mathbf{v}\|_2} = \frac{\mathbf{v}}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{|V|} v_i^2}}$$

**Applying to $d_1$'s vector $\mathbf{v}_{d_1} = [0.4055, 0, 0.4055, 1.0986, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0]$:**

**Step 1 — Compute the L2 norm:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\|\mathbf{v}_{d_1}\|_2 &= \sqrt{(0.4055)^2 + 0^2 + (0.4055)^2 + (1.0986)^2 + 0 + \cdots} \\
&= \sqrt{0.1644 + 0 + 0.1644 + 1.2069 + 0} \\
&= \sqrt{1.5357} \\
&\approx 1.2392
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Divide each component by the norm:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{v}_1 &= 0.4055 / 1.2392 \approx 0.3273 \\
\hat{v}_2 &= 0 \\
\hat{v}_3 &= 0.4055 / 1.2392 \approx 0.3273 \\
\hat{v}_4 &= 1.0986 / 1.2392 \approx 0.8865 \\
\hat{v}_5 \ldots \hat{v}_9 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final L2-Normalized Vector for $d_1$:**

$$\hat{\mathbf{v}}_{d_1} = [0.3273,\ 0,\ 0.3273,\ 0.8865,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0]$$

**Verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\|\hat{\mathbf{v}}_{d_1}\|_2 &= \sqrt{(0.3273)^2 + (0.3273)^2 + (0.8865)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{0.1071 + 0.1071 + 0.7859} \\
&= \sqrt{1.0001} \approx 1.0 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

This unit vector is now ready for cosine similarity comparisons with other document vectors.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Theoretical & Comparative Analysis

`[KTU University Exam - Model Question as per 2024 Scheme Pattern]`

**Question Statement:**

**(a) Explain the mathematical formulation of TF-IDF. Compare and contrast the raw TF, normalized TF, and log-normalized TF variants with appropriate examples.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss the limitations of TF-IDF in modern NLP applications and explain how it relates to (or differs from) modern dense embedding techniques like Word2Vec.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution — 7 Marks

**Mathematical Formulation:**

TF-IDF is defined as:

$$TF\text{-}IDF(t, d) = TF(t, d) \times IDF(t)$$

**TF Variants Comparison:**

| Variant | Formula | Example with $f(t,d)=5$, total words=20, max $f=8$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Raw TF | $f(t, d)$ | $5$ |
| Normalized TF | $\frac{f(t, d)}{\sum_{t'} f(t', d)}$ | $5/20 = 0.25$ |
| Log TF | $\log_{e}(1 + f(t, d))$ | $\log_{e}(6) \approx 1.79$ |
| Double Norm TF | $0.5 + 0.5 \cdot \frac{f(t,d)}{\max f(t',d)}$ | $0.5 + 0.5 \cdot 5/8 \approx 0.8125$ |

**Discussion:**
- **Raw TF** is simplest but suffers from length bias.
- **Normalized TF** corrects length bias by expressing term frequency as a proportion.
- **Log TF** provides *diminishing returns* — the difference between $f=1$ and $f=10$ is much smaller than between $f=0$ and $f=1$, reflecting human intuition that repetition has decreasing marginal value.

The IDF component is computed as $IDF(t) = \log_{e}(N / df(t))$, where $N$ is total documents and $df(t)$ is document frequency.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution — 7 Marks

**Limitations of TF-IDF:**

1. **High dimensionality & sparsity:** TF-IDF vectors are as long as the vocabulary (often 50,000+ dimensions) with most entries being zero, leading to memory inefficiency.
2. **No semantic understanding:** "car" and "automobile" are treated as completely unrelated dimensions, despite semantic equivalence.
3. **Polysemy ignored:** The word "bank" (river bank vs financial bank) is a single dimension, conflating meanings.
4. **Context blindness:** TF-IDF is a **bag-of-words** model — word order and syntactic structure are discarded.
5. **Cold-start problem:** New terms not in the training vocabulary cannot be represented.

**Comparison with Word2Vec:**

| Aspect | TF-IDF | Word2Vec |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Vector type | Sparse (one-hot weighted) | Dense (typically 100-300 dim) |
| Semantic similarity | No — orthogonal dimensions | Yes — cosine similarity captures semantics |
| Dimensionality | Vocabulary size | Fixed embedding size |
| Training | No training needed | Requires corpus + skip-gram/CBOW |
| OOV handling | Zero vector | Random initialization or subword |
| Context awareness | None | Local window context |

**Conclusion:** TF-IDF remains valuable for **interpretable, low-resource scenarios** and as a strong baseline, but modern NLP increasingly prefers **dense embeddings** (Word2Vec, GloVe, BERT) for tasks requiring semantic understanding. TF-IDF, however, is *not obsolete* — it still powers many production search engines and serves as a robust feature extractor in classical ML pipelines.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize:** Many students compute raw TF and multiply by IDF directly, forgetting length normalization. Always clarify which TF variant is being used.
> 2. **Confusing $df(t)$ with $f(t,d)$:** Document frequency $df(t)$ is the number of *documents* containing $t$, not the total *count* across documents.
> 3. **Logarithm base inconsistency:** Some examiners deduct marks if you mix base-2 and base-$e$ logarithms within the same problem. **Stick to base $e$** unless specified.
> 4. **Skipping the IDF for terms appearing in all documents:** Remember that $df(t) = N$ leads to $IDF(t) = 0$, effectively zeroing out universally present terms. This is a feature, not a bug — explain it.
> 5. **Not showing the final vector form:** KTU examiners expect the final TF-IDF vector written explicitly as an ordered tuple/list, not just individual scores.
> 6. **OOV terms:** If the question includes a term that does not appear in the corpus, state its TF-IDF as **0** and explain why — this shows conceptual clarity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master These Before the Exam:**

- **TF-IDF** = **Term Frequency × Inverse Document Frequency**; it is a *weighting scheme*, not a model.
- **TF variants to memorize:** raw count, normalized (length-adjusted), log-normalized, boolean, double-normalized (0.5 method).
- **IDF variants to memorize:** standard $\log_{e}(N/df(t))$, smoothed $\log_{e}((N+1)/(df(t)+1)) + 1$.
- **TF-IDF matrix shape:** $N \times |V|$ where $N$ is documents and $|V|$ is vocabulary size — this is a **sparse matrix**.
- **Stop-words** (the, is, a) have **high $df$** → **low IDF** → **low TF-IDF** → effectively removed.
- **Rare terms** have **low $df$** → **high IDF** → **high TF-IDF** if they appear in the document.
- **Universally present terms** (appearing in all $N$ documents) have $IDF = 0$ and contribute nothing to any document's TF-IDF vector.
- **TF-IDF is corpus-dependent** — changing the corpus changes IDF values, and therefore the entire TF-IDF matrix.
- **L2 normalization** rescales each document vector to unit length, enabling fair cosine similarity comparisons.
- **TF-IDF evolved into BM25**, the modern probabilistic ranking function used in Elasticsearch, Lucene, and Solr.
- **TF-IDF vs Word2Vec:** TF-IDF is *sparse and interpretable*; Word2Vec is *dense and semantic*. Both remain relevant in 2024.
- **Common pitfalls:** confusing $df$ with $tf$; mixing log bases; forgetting smoothing; ignoring vector dimensionality in answers.
- **Practical application order:** Tokenization → Stop-word removal → Stemming → TF computation → IDF computation → TF-IDF matrix → L2 normalization (optional) → Cosine similarity / Classification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Word2Vec
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT741 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Word2Vec

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Word2Vec** is a shallow, two-layer neural network architecture introduced by *Mikolov et al. (2013)* at Google, designed to learn **distributed vector representations** (word embeddings) of words from a large text corpus. The fundamental principle is the **Distributional Hypothesis** (Harris, 1954): *words that occur in similar contexts tend to have similar meanings*.

Mathematically, Word2Vec learns a mapping function $f: W \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^d$ where $W$ is the vocabulary and $d$ is the embedding dimension (typically $d = 100$ to $300$). Every word $w \in W$ is assigned two learnable vectors:

- $v_w \in \mathbb{R}^d$ — the **input/context vector** (rows of the input weight matrix $W_1$)
- $v'_w \in \mathbb{R}^d$ — the **output/target vector** (columns of the output weight matrix $W_2$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Word2Vec is a *self-supervised* learning technique — it uses raw, unlabeled text and creates its own training signal from local word co-occurrences. There is no external label. This places it under **representation learning** in NLP.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "City Map of Words"

Imagine you are plotting every city in India on a giant 2D map based on a single rule: *"Cities that frequently appear together in travel itineraries should be placed near each other."*

- **Mumbai, Pune, Nashik** will cluster in the western region (they co-occur in Maharashtra travel blogs).
- **Chennai, Bengaluru, Hyderabad** will cluster in the south.
- The coordinate of *Mumbai* is its **embedding vector** $v_{\text{Mumbai}} \in \mathbb{R}^2$.

Word2Vec does the exact same thing, but in a **d-dimensional space** (usually $d = 300$). Words that share grammatical or semantic roles get pulled together. The astonishing part: the geometry encodes **meaning**, so that:

$$v_{\text{king}} - v_{\text{man}} + v_{\text{woman}} \approx v_{\text{queen}}$$

This algebraic relation emerges purely from reading millions of news articles — no human labelled anything.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Shallow"?** Word2Vec has only two weight matrices (input $\rightarrow$ hidden and hidden $\rightarrow$ output). The hidden layer is *not* a non-linear activation; it is a simple linear average/projection. All the linguistic "magic" is packed into those two matrices.

## 1.3 The Two Flavours

Word2Vec is a *family* of two architectures:

| Variant | Input | Output | Best Use |
|---------|-------|--------|----------|
| **CBOW** (Continuous Bag-of-Words) | Surrounding context words (multiple) | Centre (target) word | Fast training, frequent words |
| **Skip-gram** | Centre (input) word | Surrounding context words | Rare words, better quality embeddings |

Both are **log-linear classifiers**, meaning the log-probability of the output is a linear function of the input — hence computationally cheap.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture 1 — Continuous Bag-of-Words (CBOW)

CBOW predicts the **centre word** $w_t$ from a symmetric context window of size $C$ on either side, i.e., from $\{w_{t-C}, \dots, w_{t-1}, w_{t+1}, \dots, w_{t+C}\}$.

### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. **One-Hot Encoding**: Each context word $w_{t+j}$ is converted to a one-hot vector $x_{t+j} \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert}$ where $\vert V \vert$ is vocabulary size.
2. **Embedding Lookup**: Multiply by the shared input matrix $W_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert \times d}$ to obtain context vectors $v_{t+j} = W_1^\top x_{t+j}$.
3. **Hidden Layer Aggregation**: Average (or sum) all $2C$ context vectors:

$$h = \frac{1}{2C} \sum_{j=-C, j \neq 0}^{C} v_{t+j}$$

4. **Output Score**: Multiply hidden vector by output matrix $W_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times \vert V \vert}$ to get a score vector $u = W_2^\top h \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert}$.
5. **Softmax**: Convert scores to probabilities:

$$P(w_t \mid \text{context}) = \frac{\exp(u_{w_t})}{\sum_{w'=1}^{\vert V \vert} \exp(u_{w'})}$$

6. **Loss**: Minimize the negative log-likelihood (cross-entropy) over the corpus.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for step 3 (averaging):** CBOW treats all context words as *equally important*. This makes CBOW fast but slightly inferior for capturing fine semantic nuance — exactly why Skip-gram often wins on analogy tasks.

## 2.2 Architecture 2 — Skip-gram

Skip-gram is the *inverse* of CBOW. Given a centre word $w_t$, predict each surrounding context word independently.

### Operational Logic

1. Embed the input centre word: $v_{t} = W_1^\top x_{t}$.
2. The hidden layer is **identical** to the input embedding: $h = v_t$.
3. For each context position $j \in \{-C, \dots, -1, +1, \dots, +C\}$, compute the probability of the actual observed word $w_{t+j}$:

$$P(w_{t+j} \mid w_t) = \frac{\exp(v'_{t+j} \cdot v_t)}{\sum_{w'=1}^{\vert V \vert} \exp(v'_{w'} \cdot v_t)}$$

4. **Total Loss** (sum over all context positions):

$$L = -\sum_{j=-C, j \neq 0}^{C} \log P(w_{t+j} \mid w_t)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Skip"?** Because the model *skips* intermediate tokens — it directly connects the centre word to a word $k$ positions away. This longer effective context window helps capture topical similarity.

## 2.3 The Computational Bottleneck — Why Vanilla Softmax Fails

For a vocabulary size $\vert V \vert \approx 10^5$ to $10^6$, the denominator $\sum_{w'} \exp(u_{w'})$ is **prohibitively expensive** at every training step. Two clever approximations solve this:

### A. Hierarchical Softmax
- Replace the flat $\vert V \vert$-way softmax with a binary **Huffman tree**.
- Each leaf is a word; the path from root to leaf defines a sequence of binary decisions.
- Complexity drops from $O(\vert V \vert)$ to $O(\log \vert V \vert)$.
- Frequent words get shorter codes (Huffman property) → faster updates.

### B. Negative Sampling (NEG)
- Reformulate the problem as **binary classification**: is this $(w_t, w_c)$ pair from the corpus (positive) or noise (negative)?
- For each positive pair, draw $k$ negative samples (typically $k = 5$ to $20$ for small corpora, $k = 2$ to $5$ for large ones).
- The objective becomes:

$$\log \sigma(v'_c \cdot v_t) + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \mathbb{E}_{w_i \sim P_n(w)} \left[ \log \sigma(-v'_{w_i} \cdot v_t) \right]$$

where $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$ is the sigmoid, and $P_n(w)$ is the **noise distribution**, empirically set to:

$$P_n(w) = \frac{U(w)^{3/4}}{\sum_{w'} U(w')^{3/4}}$$

The exponent $\tfrac{3}{4}$ was empirically determined by Mikolov et al. to slightly down-weight the dominance of very frequent words.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked "Why use the $\tfrac{3}{4}$ power?" — the answer is that the unigram distribution $U(w)^{1.0}$ over-samples frequent words like *"the"* and *"of"*. Raising to $\tfrac{3}{4}$ flattens the distribution, giving rare words a fairer chance of being drawn as negatives.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_w \in \mathbb{R}^d$ | Input (context) vector of word $w$ | $d = 100$–$300$ |
| 2 | $v'_w \in \mathbb{R}^d$ | Output (target) vector of word $w$ | same as above |
| 3 | $h = \frac{1}{2C}\sum v_{t+j}$ | CBOW hidden layer = avg of context vectors | — |
| 4 | $h = v_t$ | Skip-gram hidden layer = centre word's input vector | — |
| 5 | $P(w_O \mid w_I) = \frac{\exp(v'_{w_O} \cdot v_{w_I})}{\sum_{w=1}^{\vert V \vert} \exp(v'_{w} \cdot v_{w_I})}$ | Vanilla softmax probability | denominator over full vocab |
| 6 | $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$ | Sigmoid used in negative sampling | — |
| 7 | $L_{NEG} = -\log\sigma(v'_c \cdot v_t) - \sum_{i=1}^{k} \log\sigma(-v'_{w_i} \cdot v_t)$ | Negative sampling loss for one (centre, context) pair | $k = 5$–$20$ |
| 8 | $P_n(w) = \frac{U(w)^{3/4}}{\sum_{w'} U(w')^{3/4}}$ | Noise distribution (unigram raised to $3/4$) | — |
| 9 | $W_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert \times d}$ | Input embedding matrix | rows are input vectors |
| 10 | $W_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times \vert V \vert}$ | Output embedding matrix | columns are output vectors |
| 11 | Window size $C$ | Context radius | $C = 2$ to $10$ |
| 12 | $v_{\text{king}} - v_{\text{man}} + v_{\text{woman}} \approx v_{\text{queen}}$ | Semantic vector arithmetic | famous analogy |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Search Engines** | Google uses word embeddings to expand queries and match semantically related terms (e.g., *"car"* ↔ *"automobile"*). |
| **Recommendation Systems** | Item embeddings trained like Word2Vec on user-click sequences (e.g., YouTube, Airbnb). |
| **Sentiment Analysis** | Pre-trained Word2Vec vectors are input features for downstream classifiers. |
| **Chatbots & QA** | Semantic similarity between user query and knowledge-base sentences. |
| **Bio-informatics** | Treating DNA k-mers or protein sequences as "sentences" → biological embeddings (BioVec). |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Skip-gram Softmax Probability

We start from the raw neural-network output. Let the centre word be $w_I$ and a candidate output word be $w_O$.

### Step 1 — Score Function

When $w_I$ is fed in, its one-hot vector $x_I$ is multiplied by the input matrix $W_1$:

$$h = W_1^\top x_I = v_{w_I}$$

The score (logit) for any word $w_O$ in the vocabulary is then obtained by dotting with the output matrix column:

$$u_{w_O} = v'_{w_O} \cdot h = v'_{w_O} \cdot v_{w_I}$$

### Step 2 — From Scores to Probabilities

We need a function that converts real-valued scores $u_{w}$ into a valid probability distribution — non-negative and summing to $1$. The exponential ensures non-negativity; normalization ensures the sum equals $1$:

$$P(w_O \mid w_I) = \frac{\exp(u_{w_O})}{\sum_{w=1}^{\vert V \vert} \exp(u_{w})}$$

This is the **softmax** function — a smooth, differentiable approximation of the $\arg\max$ operator.

### Step 3 — Loss Function (Cross-Entropy)

For a single training pair $(w_I, w_O)$, the negative log-likelihood is:

$$L = -\log P(w_O \mid w_I) = -\left[ u_{w_O} - \log \sum_{w=1}^{\vert V \vert} \exp(u_{w}) \right]$$

Over the entire corpus, summing over all observed (centre, context) pairs:

$$L_{\text{corpus}} = -\sum_{(I,O) \in \mathcal{D}} \log P(w_O \mid w_I)$$

## 3.2 Gradient Derivation for Skip-gram (Vanilla Softmax)

We compute $\frac{\partial L}{\partial v'_{w_O}}$ to know how to update the output vectors via gradient descent.

### Step 4 — Differentiate $L$ with respect to score $u_{w}$

Treat $L = u_{w_O} - \log \sum_{w} \exp(u_{w})$. The derivative w.r.t. $u_{w}$:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial u_{w}} = \mathbb{1}[w = w_O] - \frac{\exp(u_{w})}{\sum_{w'} \exp(u_{w'})} = \mathbb{1}[w = w_O] - P(w \mid w_I)$$

Here $\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ is the indicator function (equals $1$ if condition is true, else $0$).

### Step 5 — Chain Rule to Output Vector

Since $u_w = v'_w \cdot v_{w_I}$, the chain rule gives:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial v'_w} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial u_w} \cdot \frac{\partial u_w}{\partial v'_w} = \left( \mathbb{1}[w = w_O] - P(w \mid w_I) \right) v_{w_I}$$

### Step 6 — Gradient Descent Update Rule

With learning rate $\eta$, the output vector update is:

$$v'_w \leftarrow v'_w - \eta \left( \mathbb{1}[w = w_O] - P(w \mid w_I) \right) v_{w_I}$$

**Interpretation:** If the model predicts $P(w \mid w_I)$ but the truth is $\mathbb{1}[w = w_O]$, the residual $(\mathbb{1}[w = w_O] - P(w \mid w_I))$ pushes $v'_w$ closer to $v_{w_I}$ for the true word and pushes it away for all other words.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** The "observed minus predicted" residual form is a recurring theme — it is identical in structure to the gradient of logistic regression. Examiners love asking students to *interpret* this residual.

### Step 7 — Symmetry and Update for Input Vector

By similar derivation (backpropagating through the symmetric computation $u_w = v'_w \cdot v_{w_I}$):

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial v_{w_I}} = \sum_{w=1}^{\vert V \vert} \left( \mathbb{1}[w = w_O] - P(w \mid w_I) \right) v'_w$$

This means the input vector receives the sum of all output vectors, weighted by their prediction errors.

## 3.3 Derivation of Negative Sampling Objective

Negative Sampling converts a $\vert V \vert$-class problem into $k+1$ binary problems.

### Step 8 — Define Positive and Negative Distributions

For a true pair $(w_I, w_O)$ and a sampled negative word $w_i$:

$$P(D = 1 \mid w_I, w_O) = \sigma(v'_{w_O} \cdot v_{w_I})$$
$$P(D = 0 \mid w_I, w_i) = 1 - \sigma(v'_{w_i} \cdot v_{w_I}) = \sigma(-v'_{w_i} \cdot v_{w_I})$$

### Step 9 — Combine into One Loss

$$L_{NEG} = -\log \sigma(v'_{w_O} \cdot v_{w_I}) - \sum_{i=1}^{k} \mathbb{E}_{w_i \sim P_n} \left[ \log \sigma(-v'_{w_i} \cdot v_{w_I}) \right]$$

### Step 10 — Computational Saving

- **Vanilla Softmax**: updates $W_2$ for all $\vert V \vert$ words per step → $O(\vert V \vert)$.
- **Negative Sampling**: updates $W_2$ for only $1 + k$ words per step → $O(k)$.
- For $\vert V \vert = 10^5$ and $k = 5$, this is a **20,000×** speed-up per training pair.

## 3.4 Derivation of the "King − Man + Woman ≈ Queen" Analogy

The analogy is **not** an algebraic theorem; it is an empirical observation that emerges from how context predicts surrounding words.

### Step 11 — Position-Invariant Composition

Consider the contexts of *king* and *queen*:

- *the king ruled the kingdom*
- *the queen ruled the kingdom*

And of *man* and *woman*:

- *the man ruled the kingdom*
- *the woman ruled the kingdom*

### Step 12 — Vector Decomposition of Meaning

Empirically, each word's vector decomposes into additive components of **meaning**:

$$v_{\text{king}} = v_{\text{royal}} + v_{\text{male}} + v_{\text{human}} + \dots$$
$$v_{\text{queen}} = v_{\text{royal}} + v_{\text{female}} + v_{\text{human}} + \dots$$
$$v_{\text{man}} = v_{\text{male}} + v_{\text{human}} + \dots$$
$$v_{\text{woman}} = v_{\text{female}} + v_{\text{human}} + \dots$$

### Step 13 — Substitute and Cancel

$$v_{\text{king}} - v_{\text{man}} + v_{\text{woman}} = (v_{\text{royal}} + v_{\text{male}} + v_{\text{human}}) - (v_{\text{male}} + v_{\text{human}}) + (v_{\text{female}} + v_{\text{human}})$$

After cancellation:

$$v_{\text{king}} - v_{\text{man}} + v_{\text{woman}} = v_{\text{royal}} + v_{\text{female}} + v_{\text{human}} \approx v_{\text{queen}}$$

This additive structure is *learned* by Word2Vec because the softmax/negative-sampling loss is smooth and convex in the dot-product $v'_w \cdot v_I$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    A4[Context word w sub t plus 2 one hot] --> M1

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    H --> O2[Output matrix W2 score for context position minus 1]

    H --> O4[Output matrix W2 score for context position plus 2]

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw corpus sentences] --> B[Sliding window of size 2C plus 1]
    B --> C{Positive pair centre context}
    C --> D[Positive sample wI wO label equals 1]
    C --> E[Draw k negative words from noise Pn]
    E --> F1[Negative sample wI w1 label equals 0]
    E --> F2[Negative sample wI w2 label equals 0]
    E --> F3[Negative sample wI w3 label equals 0]
    D --> G[Binary sigmoid classifier]
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between the CBOW and Skip-gram architectures of Word2Vec. Which is preferred for rare words and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | CBOW | Skip-gram |
|---|---|---|
| Input | Multiple context words | Single centre word |
| Output | Centre word | Multiple context words |
| Training time | Faster | Slower |
| Performance on rare words | Weaker | **Stronger** |

**Reason for Skip-gram's superiority on rare words (1 mark):** In CBOW, the rare word appears as a *target* to be predicted, but its context vectors get averaged with many other words, diluting its unique signal. In Skip-gram, the rare word appears as the *input* multiple times across different context windows — each occurrence pushes the model to learn a distinctive vector for it. Therefore **Skip-gram is preferred for rare words**.

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: What is the role of the noise distribution $P_n(w) = U(w)^{3/4} / \sum U(w')^{3/4}$ in negative sampling? Why is the exponent $3/4$ used instead of $1$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Role (1.5 marks):** $P_n(w)$ determines the probability with which a word $w$ is drawn as a *negative* sample during training. Common choices for the base distribution $U(w)$ are the unigram distribution $U(w)^{1.0}$ or uniform $1/\vert V \vert$.
- **Why $3/4$ (1.5 marks):** Using $U(w)^{1.0}$ over-samples very frequent words like *"the"* and *"of"*, which provides little learning signal because they appear as negatives in almost every window. Raising to $3/4$ *flattens* the distribution, giving rarer words a fairer chance to be sampled as negatives. This empirical exponent was determined by Mikolov et al. (2013) to optimise training speed and embedding quality.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`
**Q (a)** Explain the architecture of the **Continuous Bag-of-Words (CBOW)** model in Word2Vec with a neat diagram. Derive the expression for the softmax probability used to predict the target word. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** Discuss **Negative Sampling** as an optimisation over vanilla softmax. State its objective function and explain why it is computationally superior. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q (a) — CBOW Architecture (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Setup and Notation (1 mark):**
Let vocabulary size be $\vert V \vert$, embedding dimension be $d$, and context window size be $C$. The training set consists of (context, target) pairs where the context is $\{w_{t-C}, \dots, w_{t-1}, w_{t+1}, \dots, w_{t+C}\}$ and the target is the centre word $w_t$.

**Step 2 — One-Hot Encoding (1 mark):**
Each context word is represented as a one-hot vector $x_{t+j} \in \{0,1\}^{\vert V \vert}$ with a single $1$ at the index corresponding to that word.

**Step 3 — Embedding Lookup (1 mark):**
The shared input weight matrix is $W_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert \times d}$. The embedding of each context word is:

$$v_{t+j} = W_1^\top x_{t+j}$$

This simply selects the corresponding row of $W_1$.

**Step 4 — Hidden Layer = Average (1 mark):**
The hidden layer aggregates the $2C$ context vectors by simple averaging:

$$h = \frac{1}{2C} \sum_{j=-C, j \neq 0}^{C} v_{t+j}$$

**Step 5 — Output Score and Softmax (1 mark):**
The output weight matrix $W_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times \vert V \vert}$ produces a score vector $u = W_2^\top h$. Applying softmax:

$$P(w_t \mid \text{context}) = \frac{\exp(u_{w_t})}{\sum_{w=1}^{\vert V \vert} \exp(u_w)}$$

**Step 6 — Loss Function (1 mark):**
The objective is the negative log-likelihood over all training pairs:

$$L = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P(w_t \mid \text{context}_t)$$

**Step 7 — Diagram Description (1 mark):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Context w t minus C one hot] --> M1[Shared W1 lookup]
    A2[Context w t plus C one hot] --> M1
    M1 --> H[Hidden h equals average of 2C vectors]
    H --> O[Output W2 multiply score u]
    O --> S[Softmax over V vocabulary]
    S --> P[Predicted centre word w t]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Many students forget to specify that the *hidden layer has no activation function* — it is a pure linear average. This is a guaranteed 1-mark loss. Also, writing the softmax denominator as $\sum_{w=1}^{V}$ instead of $\sum_{w=1}^{\vert V \vert}$ looks sloppy.

---

#### Solution to Q (b) — Negative Sampling (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Motivation (1 mark):**
The denominator of the vanilla softmax requires summing over $\vert V \vert \approx 10^5$–$10^6$ words at every step, making training prohibitively slow for large vocabularies.

**Step 2 — Reformulation (1.5 marks):**
Instead of a $\vert V \vert$-way classification, treat each (centre, context) pair as a binary classification: is this pair *observed in the corpus* (label $1$) or *drawn from a noise distribution* (label $0$)? For each positive pair, sample $k$ negative words from $P_n(w)$.

**Step 3 — Objective Function (1.5 marks):**
For a positive pair $(w_I, w_O)$ and $k$ negatives $\{w_1, \dots, w_k\}$:

$$L_{NEG} = -\log \sigma(v'_{w_O} \cdot v_{w_I}) - \sum_{i=1}^{k} \log \sigma(-v'_{w_i} \cdot v_{w_I})$$

where $\sigma(x) = 1/(1+e^{-x})$ is the sigmoid.

**Step 4 — Noise Distribution (1 mark):**
The negative samples are drawn from the unigram distribution raised to power $3/4$:

$$P_n(w) = \frac{U(w)^{3/4}}{\sum_{w'} U(w')^{3/4}}$$

**Step 5 — Computational Superiority (2 marks):**

| Method | Cost per step |
|---|---|
| Vanilla Softmax | $O(\vert V \vert)$ updates to $W_2$ |
| Negative Sampling | $O(k)$ updates to $W_2$ |

For $\vert V \vert = 10^5$ and $k = 5$, this is a **20,000×** reduction in per-step computation. This enables Word2Vec to be trained on billions of tokens in hours on a single machine — a key engineering breakthrough of Mikolov et al. (2013).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** Students often confuse **Negative Sampling** with **Hierarchical Softmax**. They are *two different* approximations. Negative Sampling reshapes the objective into $k+1$ binary classifications; Hierarchical Softmax uses a Huffman tree and is still a multi-class softmax over a tree. Examiners will explicitly deduct marks if you mix them up.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]`
**Q (a)** Explain the **Skip-gram** architecture in Word2Vec. Show the steps to derive the gradient of the loss with respect to the output word vector $v'_{w_O}$. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** Explain the **Hierarchical Softmax** technique used to speed up Word2Vec training. Describe how a Huffman tree is constructed and how the path from root to leaf yields a $O(\log \vert V \vert)$ softmax. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q (a) — Skip-gram Architecture and Gradient Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Setup (0.5 mark):**
Given a centre word $w_I$ with one-hot $x_I \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert}$, Skip-gram predicts each of $2C$ surrounding context words $w_{t+j}$ for $j \in \{-C, \dots, -1, +1, \dots, +C\}$.

**Step 2 — Forward Pass (1 mark):**
The hidden layer is simply the input embedding of the centre word:

$$h = W_1^\top x_I = v_{w_I} \in \mathbb{R}^d$$

**Step 3 — Score for each candidate output (0.5 mark):**
For any word $w$ in the vocabulary, the score is:

$$u_w = v'_w \cdot h = v'_w \cdot v_{w_I}$$

**Step 4 — Probability via Softmax (1 mark):**

$$P(w \mid w_I) = \frac{\exp(u_w)}{\sum_{w'=1}^{\vert V \vert} \exp(u_{w'})}$$

**Step 5 — Loss for one centre word (1 mark):**

$$L = -\sum_{j \in \{-C,\dots,+C\}, j\neq 0} \log P(w_{t+j} \mid w_I) = -\sum_j \left[ u_{w_{t+j}} - \log\sum_w \exp(u_w) \right]$$

**Step 6 — Gradient Derivation w.r.t. $v'_{w_O}$ (2 marks):**
Treating one context word $w_O$ in isolation:

$$L = -u_{w_O} + \log \sum_w \exp(u_w)$$

Differentiate w.r.t. $u_w$:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial u_w} = -\mathbb{1}[w = w_O] + \frac{\exp(u_w)}{\sum_{w'} \exp(u_{w'})} = P(w \mid w_I) - \mathbb{1}[w = w_O]$$

Apply the chain rule with $u_w = v'_w \cdot v_{w_I}$:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial v'_w} = \left( P(w \mid w_I) - \mathbb{1}[w = w_O] \right) v_{w_I}$$

**Step 7 — Update Rule and Interpretation (1 mark):**
With learning rate $\eta$:

$$v'_w \leftarrow v'_w - \eta \left( P(w \mid w_I) - \mathbb{1}[w = w_O] \right) v_{w_I}$$

The residual $(P - \mathbb{1})$ is **positive** for the true word (push $v'_{w_O}$ towards $v_{w_I}$) and **negative** for all other words (push away).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #3:** When deriving the gradient, students often forget the *minus sign* in $-\log P$, which flips the residual from $(P - \mathbb{1})$ to $(\mathbb{1} - P)$. While mathematically equivalent, writing the wrong sign loses clarity marks. Stick to $(P - \mathbb{1})$ when matching the standard derivation in the original paper.

---

#### Solution to Q (b) — Hierarchical Softmax (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Motivation (1 mark):**
Vanilla softmax requires computing a sum over all $\vert V \vert$ words to normalise the probability — $O(\vert V \vert)$ per training example. Hierarchical Softmax reduces this to $O(\log \vert V \vert)$ by replacing the flat vocabulary with a binary tree.

**Step 2 — Huffman Tree Construction (1.5 marks):**
- Build a binary tree with $\vert V \vert$ leaves, one per vocabulary word.
- Assign **frequencies** = word counts in the corpus.
- At each step, merge the two nodes with the lowest frequencies (Huffman rule).
- Frequent words (like *"the"*) end up near the root with **shorter paths**; rare words end up deep with longer paths.

**Step 3 — Path Representation (1 mark):**
Let the path from root to the leaf corresponding to word $w$ consist of $L(w)$ internal nodes. Each internal node $n$ has an associated learnable output vector $v'_n \in \mathbb{R}^d$, and a binary decision at each node is interpreted as a logistic sigmoid.

**Step 4 — Probability Formulation (1.5 marks):**
Let $d_w^j \in \{0,1\}$ denote whether the path at internal node $j$ goes *left* ($0$) or *right* ($1$). The probability of reaching $w$ is:

$$P(w \mid w_I) = \prod_{j=1}^{L(w)} \sigma\!\left( \left( 1 - d_w^j \right) v'_j \cdot h \right)^{1-d_w^j} \cdot \left( 1 - \sigma\!\left( v'_j \cdot h \right) \right)^{d_w^j}$$

A more compact notation using the indicator and sigmoid:

$$P(w \mid w_I) = \prod_{j=1}^{L(w)} \sigma\!\left( (-1)^{d_w^j}\, v'_j \cdot h \right)$$

**Step 5 — Computational Cost Analysis (1 mark):**
- Vanilla Softmax: $O(\vert V \vert)$ — sum over all words.
- Hierarchical Softmax: $O(L(w)) \leq O(\log \vert V \vert)$ — only nodes along the path.
- For $\vert V \vert = 10^5$, $\log_2 \vert V \vert \approx 17$, giving a $\sim 5{,}880\times$ speed-up.

**Step 6 — Bonus Property: Frequent Words Get Updated Faster (1 mark):**
Because Huffman coding assigns shorter paths to frequent words, those words see larger gradient updates per epoch. This empirically produces better embeddings for common words but slightly weaker embeddings for rare ones compared to Negative Sampling.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #4:** A common mistake is to draw the Huffman tree as a *balanced* binary tree. It is **NOT** balanced — it is *unbalanced*, with frequent words close to the root and rare words deep in the leaves. Drawing it as balanced will cost you marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Master Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Consolidated Examiner's Pitfall List for Word2Vec Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the linearity of the hidden layer** — the Word2Vec "hidden layer" has *no activation function*. Calling it "ReLU" or "tanh" is wrong.
> 2. **Confusing $W_1$ and $W_2$** — the final embedding matrix is $W_1$ (input), not $W_2$ (output). Some works average the two, but the canonical Word2Vec uses $W_1$.
> 3. **Mixing up Negative Sampling and Hierarchical Softmax** — they are *different* approximations to the same denominator problem.
> 4. **Wrong sign on the gradient** — always derive from $-\log P$, not $+P$.
> 5. **Drawing the Huffman tree balanced** — it is unbalanced by construction.
> 6. **Forgetting the $\tfrac{3}{4}$ exponent justification** — examiners ask this routinely.
> 7. **Ignoring window symmetry** — CBOW treats context as a bag, but Skip-gram preserves position.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Word2Vec = two architectures (CBOW + Skip-gram) + two optimisations (Hierarchical Softmax + Negative Sampling).**
- **Output formula (vanilla softmax):** $\;P(w_O \mid w_I) = \dfrac{\exp(v'_{w_O} \cdot v_{w_I})}{\sum_{w=1}^{\vert V \vert} \exp(v'_w \cdot v_{w_I})}$.
- **Skip-gram loss:** $\;L = -\sum_j \log P(w_{t+j} \mid w_t)$.
- **CBOW hidden layer:** $\;h = \tfrac{1}{2C}\sum_{j \neq 0} v_{t+j}$.
- **Skip-gram hidden layer:** $\;h = v_{w_I}$ (no averaging).
- **Negative sampling objective:** $\;L_{NEG} = -\log\sigma(v'_{w_O} \cdot v_I) - \sum_{i=1}^{k}\log\sigma(-v'_{w_i} \cdot v_I)$.
- **Noise distribution:** $\;P_n(w) = U(w)^{3/4} / \sum U(w')^{3/4}$.
- **Typical hyperparameters:** $d = 100$–$300$, $C = 2$–$10$, $k = 5$–$20$ (small corpora) / $2$–$5$ (large corpora), $\eta = 0.025$ with linear decay.
- **Final embedding = input matrix $W_1$ (rows).**
- **Famous analogy:** $v_{\text{king}} - v_{\text{man}} + v_{\text{woman}} \approx v_{\text{queen}}$ — emerges from additive semantic structure in linear log-bilinear models.
- **Skip-gram > CBOW** for rare words and analogy tasks.
- **Hierarchical Softmax** uses a Huffman tree → $O(\log \vert V \vert)$.
- **Negative Sampling** uses $k$ binary classifiers → $O(k)$.
- **Word2Vec is context-window based** — does not use global co-occurrence counts (unlike GloVe).
- **Limitation:** does not handle polysemy (a single vector per word); does not handle out-of-vocabulary (OOV) words.
- **Successor models:** FastText (sub-word), GloVe (global counts), BERT (contextual).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Word Senses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT741 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Word Senses # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Word Senses In Natural Language Processing (NLP), a **word sense** refers to a specific meaning or interpretation of a polysemous or homonymous word. Formally, a word sense is the discrete, contextually-grounded semantic representation assigned to a lexical item based on its usage within a discourse. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEITT741, **Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)** is defined as: > *"The computational task of determining which sense of a polysemous word is intended in a given context, by selecting the appropriate meaning from a predefined sense inventory such as WordNet."* Mathematically, WSD can be expressed as a mapping function: $$ \hat{s} = \arg\max_{s \in S(w)} P(s \mid C, w) $$ Where: - $w$ is the target word - $S(w)$ is the set of all possible senses of $w$ - $C$ is the surrounding context - $\hat{s}$ is the most probable sense given the context ## 1.2 Types of Lexical Sense Relations Understanding the taxonomy of sense relations is fundamental to Module 2: | Relation | Definition | Example | |----------|------------|---------| | **Homonymy** | Identical spelling/sound, unrelated meanings | `bank` (river) vs `bank` (finance) | | **Polysemy** | Same word, related meanings | `head` (body) vs `head` (of department) | | **Synonymy** | Different words, same meaning | `big` / `large` / `huge` | | **Antonymy** | Opposite meanings | `hot` / `cold` | | **Hyponymy** | Subordinate relationship | `dog` is a hyponym of `animal` | | **Hypernymy** | Superordinate relationship | `animal` is a hypernym of `dog` | | **Meronymy** | Part-whole relationship | `wheel` is a meronym of `car` | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: Distinguish carefully between **homonymy** and **polysemy**. Homonyms are etymologically unrelated (accidental), while polysemes are diachronically related (evolved from a single root meaning). ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a **word** as a *toolbox label* and **word senses** as the *different tools inside the box* sharing that same label. When you hear the word `"crane"`, your brain instantly looks at the *scene around it* — is there water and reeds? Then you pick the *bird tool*. Is there a construction site? You pick the *machine tool*. WSD is essentially **building that same context-aware selection mechanism in machines**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility**: WSD is a foundational pre-processing step in **Machine Translation (MT)**, **Information Retrieval (IR)**, **Question Answering (QA)**, and **Sentiment Analysis**. Without it, systems may confuse `"bass"` (fish vs. low-frequency sound) and produce semantically incorrect outputs. ## 1.4 The WSD Task Formulation Given a sentence $W = (w_1, w_2, ..., w_n)$ and a target word $w_i$, WSD aims to find the sense $s_j \in S(w_i)$ such that $s_j$ best fits the contextual meaning. The **two-stage pipeline** is: 1. **Sense Inventory Selection** → Choose a lexical resource (WordNet, BabelNet, VerbNet) 2. **Sense Assignment** → Apply an algorithm (Lesk, Naive Bayes, Neural) to pick the correct sense > [!TIP] > **Syllabus Note**: For Module 2 examinations, focus primarily on **dictionary-based (knowledge-based)** and **supervised machine learning** approaches. Unsupervised methods are usually discussed in Module 3. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Paradigms of WSD The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes WSD approaches into three distinct paradigms. Understanding the "why" and "how" of each is essential: ### 2.1.1 Knowledge-Based (Dictionary-Based) Approaches - **Why?** Uses structured lexical knowledge without requiring annotated corpora - **How?** Overlaps the dictionary definitions (glosses) of the target word's senses with the context - **Key Algorithm**: **Lesk Algorithm (1986)** and its variants (Extended Lesk, Adapted Lesk) ### 2.1.2 Supervised Approaches - **Why?** Learns patterns from manually sense-annotated corpora - **How?** Treats WSD as a classification problem — each sense is a class label - **Key Algorithms**: **Naive Bayes**, **Decision Lists**, **SVM**, **Neural Networks** ### 2.1.3 Unsupervised / Semi-Supervised Approaches - **Why?** Annotated data is scarce and expensive - **How?** Uses clustering, word embeddings, or graph-based methods - **Key Methods**: **Yarowsky's Algorithm**, **Graph-based WSD**, **Contextualized Embeddings (BERT)** ## 2.2 The Lesk Algorithm — Deep Dive The original **Lesk Algorithm (1986)** is the most examinable WSD method in KTU. Its operational logic: **Algorithm: Simplified Lesk** 1. For target word $w$ with senses $\{s_1, s_2, ..., s_n\}$ 2. Retrieve the dictionary definition (gloss) $G_j$ for each sense $s_j$ 3. Tokenize the context $C$ surrounding $w$ 4. Compute overlap: $\text{overlap}(s_j) = \vert G_j \cap C \vert$ 5. Select $\hat{s} = \arg\max_j \text{overlap}(s_j)$ The **Extended Lesk Algorithm** augments glosses with examples, hypernyms, hyponyms, and glosses of related synsets to overcome the *sparse overlap* problem. ## 2.3 Selectional Restrictions & Selectional Preferences A critical concept in WSD is **selectional restrictions** — semantic constraints verbs place on their arguments: > *"She ate the **soup**"* → Valid because `soup` is `[+edible]`, matching `eat`'s selectional restriction on its object. Formally, the **Selectional Association Strength (SAS)** between a predicate $v$ and a class $C$ is: $$ SAS(v, C) = \frac{P(C \mid v)}{P(C)} = \frac{\text{freq}(v, C) / \text{freq}(v)}{\text{freq}(C) / N} $$ A high $SAS$ indicates that the predicate strongly prefers arguments from class $C$. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Metric | Mathematical Form | Description | |------------------|-------------------|-------------| | **Bayesian WSD Decision** | $\hat{s} = \arg\max_s P(s \mid C)$ | Maximum a posteriori sense | | **Naive Bayes Classifier** | $\hat{s} = \arg\max_s P(s) \prod_{i} P(f_i \mid s)$ | Assumes feature independence | | **Naive Bayes (log form)** | $\hat{s} = \arg\max_s \left[ \log P(s) + \sum_i \log P(f_i \mid s) \right)$ | Numerically stable variant | | **Precision** | $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Accuracy of positive predictions | | **Recall** | $R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Coverage of true positives | | **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R | | **Cosine Similarity** | $\cos(\theta) = \frac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\Vert \vec{A} \Vert \cdot \Vert \vec{B} \Vert}$ | Vector-space semantic similarity | | **PMI** | $\text{PMI}(w_1, w_2) = \log \frac{P(w_1, w_2)}{P(w_1) P(w_2)}$ | Pointwise Mutual Information | | **Lesk Overlap Score** | $\text{score}(s_j) = \vert G_j \cap C \vert$ | Gloss-context intersection size | | **SAS** | $SAS(v, C) = \frac{P(C \mid v)}{P(C)}$ | Selectional preference strength | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility**: These formulas are deployed in real-world **search engines** (cosine similarity for semantic search), **chatbots** (PMI for collocation detection), and **biomedical NLP** (SAS for protein-protein interaction extraction). ## 2.5 WordNet as a Sense Inventory **WordNet** is a large lexical database of English developed at Princeton, where: - Nouns, verbs, adjectives, and adverbs are grouped into sets of cognitive synonyms called **synsets** - Each synset expresses a distinct concept - Synsets are interlinked via conceptual-semantic and lexical relations WordNet forms a **hypernym/hyponym hierarchy** where concepts become more general as you traverse upward — this is the *is-a* taxonomy exploited by the Extended Lesk algorithm. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Naive Bayes for WSD Let us derive the optimal WSD decision rule from first principles using **Bayes' Theorem**. ### Step 1: Problem Formulation We are given a target word $w$ with possible senses $S = \{s_1, s_2, ..., s_k\}$ and a context vector of features $F = (f_1, f_2, ..., f_n)$ — typically, surrounding words within a window. We seek the most probable sense: $$ \hat{s} = \arg\max_{s_j \in S} P(s_j \mid F) $$ ### Step 2: Apply Bayes' Theorem $$ P(s_j \mid F) = \frac{P(F \mid s_j) \cdot P(s_j)}{P(F)} $$ Since $P(F)$ is independent of $s_j$ (it is a normalizing constant), we can drop it for maximization: $$ \hat{s} = \arg\max_{s_j} P(F \mid s_j) \cdot P(s_j) $$ ### Step 3: Naive Independence Assumption Assume context features $f_1, f_2, ..., f_n$ are conditionally independent given the sense $s_j$: $$ P(F \mid s_j) = P(f_1, f_2, ..., f_n \mid s_j) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(f_i \mid s_j) $$ ### Step 4: Combine to Get Naive Bayes Decision Rule $$ \hat{s} = \arg\max_{s_j} P(s_j) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(f_i \mid s_j) $$ ### Step 5: Logarithmic Transformation To prevent numerical underflow (product of many small probabilities), we apply the natural logarithm (which is monotonic, preserving the argmax): $$ \hat{s} = \arg\max_{s_j} \left[ \log P(s_j) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(f_i \mid s_j) \right] $$ ### Step 6: Parameter Estimation from Training Data Using **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** with Laplace smoothing for unseen features: $$ P(s_j) = \frac{\text{count}(s_j) + \alpha}{N + \alpha \cdot k} $$ $$ P(f_i \mid s_j) = \frac{\text{count}(f_i, s_j) + \alpha}{\sum_{f} \text{count}(f, s_j) + \alpha \cdot \vert V \vert} $$ Where: - $N$ = total training instances - $k$ = number of senses - $\vert V \vert$ = vocabulary size - $\alpha$ = smoothing parameter (typically $\alpha = 1$ for Laplace) ## 3.2 Worked Example: Applying Simplified Lesk **Disambiguation Task**: Determine the correct sense of the word **`bass`** in the sentence: > *"The bass was too loud during the concert."* **Step 1**: Identify possible senses from WordNet for `bass` (noun): - **Sense 1**: The lowest adult male singing voice - Gloss: *"the lowest adult male singing voice"* - **Sense 2**: The lowest part of the musical range - Gloss: *"the lowest part of the musical range"* - **Sense 3**: Lean flesh of a saltwater fish - Gloss: *"lean flesh of a saltwater fish of the family Serranidae"* - **Sense 4**: A type of freshwater or marine fish - Gloss: *"nontechnical name for any of numerous edible marine and freshwater spiny-finned fishes"* **Step 2**: Extract context words from sentence (excluding target): $\{$`the`, `was`, `too`, `loud`, `during`, `the`, `concert`$\}$ **Step 3**: Compute overlap between each gloss and the context: | Sense | Gloss Tokens | Overlap with Context | |-------|--------------|----------------------| | Sense 1 | lowest, adult, male, singing, voice | None | | Sense 2 | lowest, part, musical, range | `loud`, `concert` (2) | | Sense 3 | lean, flesh, saltwater, fish, family, Serranidae | None | | Sense 4 | nontechnical, name, numerous, edible, marine, freshwater, spiny-finned, fishes | None | **Step 4**: Apply $\hat{s} = \arg\max_j \text{overlap}(s_j)$: $$ \text{overlap}(S_1) = 0, \quad \text{overlap}(S_2) = 2, \quad \text{overlap}(S_3) = 0, \quad \text{overlap}(S_4) = 0 $$ $$ \therefore \hat{s} = S_2 \quad \text{(the lowest part of the musical range — correct interpretation)} $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of WSD Approaches | Approach | Data Required | Accuracy Range | Strengths | Weaknesses | |----------|---------------|----------------|-----------|------------| | **Lesk (Original)** | Dictionary only | 50–60% | No training data needed | Sparse overlap problem | | **Extended Lesk** | Dictionary + relations | 60–70% | Uses semantic network | Still surface-level | | **Naive Bayes (Sup.)** | Annotated corpus | 70–80% | Probabilistic, robust | Needs labeled data | | **SVM (Sup.)** | Annotated corpus | 75–85% | Handles high-dim features | Computationally expensive | | **Neural (BERT)** | Pretrained LM | 85–95% | Contextual embeddings | Black box, resource-heavy | | **Yarowsky (Unsup.)** | Unannotated + seeds | 70–80% | Minimal supervision | Bootstrapping errors | ## 3.4 Information-Theoretic Foundation of WSD The **information content** of a synset in WordNet follows Resnik's (1995) formulation: $$ IC(s) = -\log P(s) $$ Where $P(s)$ is the probability of encountering synset $s$ in a corpus. Concepts with lower probability carry more information. The **semantic similarity between two senses** can then be computed using the **Lowest Common Subsumer (LCS)** in the hypernym hierarchy: $$ \text{sim}_{Lin}(s_1, s_2) = \frac{2 \cdot IC(\text{LCS}(s_1, s_2))}{IC(s_1) + IC(s_2)} $$ This forms the basis of many modern lexical similarity measures tested in KTU laboratory examinations. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 WSD Taxonomy Overview ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Word Sense Disambiguation] --> B[Knowledge Based] A --> C[Supervised] A --> D[Unsupervised] B --> B1[Lesk Algorithm] B --> B2[Extended Lesk] B --> B3[Adapted Lesk] B --> B4[Selectional Restrictions] C --> C1[Naive Bayes] C --> C2[Decision Lists] C --> C3[SVM] C --> C4[Neural Networks] D --> D1[Yarowsky Algorithm] D --> D2[Clustering Based] D --> D3[Graph Based WSD] D --> D4[Contextual Embeddings] ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Lesk Algorithm Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR INPUT[Input Sentence
with Target Word] --> TOKEN[Tokenization and
Context Extraction] TOKEN --> FETCH[Sense Inventory Lookup
from WordNet] FETCH --> GLOSS[Gloss Retrieval for
Each Candidate Sense] GLOSS --> COMPARE[Gloss Context Overlap
Computation] COMPARE --> SCORE[Score Aggregation
for Each Sense] SCORE --> RANK[Argmax Ranking
of Sense Candidates] RANK --> OUTPUT[Selected Sense
as Final Output] COMPARE --> EXT1[Extended: Include Hypernyms] COMPARE --> EXT2[Extended: Include Hyponyms] COMPARE --> EXT3[Extended: Include Examples] ``` ## 4.3 WordNet Lexical Hierarchy (Hypernym Tree) ```mermaid flowchart TD ROOT[Entity] --> PHY[Physical Entity] ROOT --> ABS[Abstract Entity] PHY --> OBJ[Object] PHY => ORG[Organism] OBJ => ART[Artifact] OBJ => NAT[Natural Object] ORG => PLN[Plant] ORG => ANM[Animal] ANM => VRT[Vertebrate] ANM => INV[Invertebrate] VRT => MAM[Mammal] VRT => FIS[Fish] MAM => DOG[Dog] MAM => CAT[Cat] MAM => HUM[Human] ART => INS[Instrument] ART => VEH[Vehicle] ART => BLD[Building] BLD => HOUSE[House] BLD => SCH[School] ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: End-to-End WSD System ```mermaid flowchart TD RAW[Raw Text Input] --> PRE[Preprocessing Module] PRE --> TOK[Tokenizer] PRE --> POS[POS Tagger] PRE --> LEM[Lemmatizer] TOK --> FEAT[Feature Extractor] POS --> FEAT LEM --> FEAT FEAT --> COLLOC[Collocational Features] FEAT --> BAG[Bag of Words Features] FEAT --> LOCAL[Local Context Features] COLLOC --> MODEL[WSD Classifier] BAG --> MODEL LOCAL --> MODEL MODEL --> NB[Naive Bayes Engine] MODEL --> SVM[SVM Engine] MODEL --> BERT[BERT Contextual Engine] NB --> VOTE[Ensemble Voting Layer] SVM --> VOTE BERT --> VOTE VOTE --> EVAL[Evaluation Module] EVAL --> METRICS[Precision Recall F1 Computation] METRICS --> OUTPUT[Final Sense Tag] ``` ## 4.5 Conceptual Map: Sense Relations ```mermaid flowchart LR W[Word] --> HOMO[Homonymy] W --> POLY[Polysemy] HOMO --> H1[Bank Finance] HOMO --> H2[Bank River] POLY --> P1[Head Body] POLY --> P2[Head Department] W --> SYN[Synonymy] SYN --> S1[Big] SYN --> S2[Large] SYN --> S3[Huge] W --> ANT[Antonymy] ANT --> A1[Hot] ANT --> A2[Cold] W --> HYP[Hypernymy] HYP --> Y1[Animal] HYP => Y2[Dog] W --> MER[Meronymy] MER --> M1[Car] MER --> M2[Wheel] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Word Sense Disambiguation. Differentiate between Homonymy and Polysemy with examples. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD) is the computational process of identifying the correct meaning of a polysemous or homonymous word in a given context, by selecting the appropriate sense from a predefined lexical inventory such as WordNet. **Homonymy** refers to words that share the same spelling or pronunciation but have **unrelated meanings** originating from different etymological roots. **Example of Homonymy**: `bank` (financial institution) vs. `bank` (side of a river) — these are coincidentally identical but historically unrelated. **Polysemy** refers to a single word that has **multiple related meanings** evolved from the same root concept. **Example of Polysemy**: `head` (body part) vs. `head` (leader of a department) — both derived from the original anatomical meaning. | Aspect | Homonymy | Polysemy | |--------|----------|----------| | Etymology | Different roots | Same root | | Semantic relation | Unrelated | Related | | Examples | bank/bank | head/head | --- ### Question 2: Explain the Lesk algorithm with its limitations. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The **Lesk Algorithm (1986)**, proposed by Michael Lesk, is a dictionary-based WSD method that selects the sense whose dictionary definition (gloss) shares the maximum number of words with the target word's context. **Working Principle**: 1. For each sense $s_j$ of the target word $w$, retrieve the gloss $G_j$ 2. Tokenize the surrounding context $C$ 3. Compute $\text{overlap}(s_j) = \vert G_j \cap C \vert$ 4. Choose $\hat{s} = \arg\max_j \text{overlap}(s_j)$ **Limitations**: - **Sparse Overlap Problem**: Glosses are short, leading to few or zero shared words - **Word Sense Ambiguity in Glosses**: Words in glosses themselves can be polysemous - **No Ranking of Partial Matches**: Ties or near-ties are not well-handled - **Context Window Restriction**: Original Lesk uses only immediate neighbors **Solution**: The **Extended Lesk** algorithm augments glosses with hypernyms, hyponyms, examples, and glosses of related synsets to overcome these limitations. --- ## 5.2 PART B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A (14 Marks): Word Sense Disambiguation — Comprehensive Analysis #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Explain the three main approaches to WSD. Compare their advantages and disadvantages. **Model Solution:** The three main approaches to Word Sense Disambiguation are: **1. Knowledge-Based (Dictionary-Based) Approach** - **Method**: Uses external lexical resources like WordNet, machine-readable dictionaries, and ontological knowledge without requiring annotated training data. - **Key Algorithm**: Lesk Algorithm - **Advantages**: - No annotated corpus required - Easy to implement - Domain-independent - **Disadvantages**: - Limited by dictionary coverage - Sparse overlap problem - Lower accuracy (50–70%) **2. Supervised Machine Learning Approach** - **Method**: Treats WSD as a classification task. A classifier is trained on sense-annotated corpora like SemCor. - **Key Algorithms**: Naive Bayes, Decision Lists, SVM, Neural Networks - **Advantages**: - High accuracy (75–90%) - Robust probabilistic framework - Can integrate multiple feature types - **Disadvantages**: - Requires expensive sense-annotated data - Limited to senses present in training data - Domain adaptation challenges **3. Unsupervised / Semi-Supervised Approach** - **Method**: Does not require manually labeled data; uses clustering, word embeddings, or bootstrapping techniques. - **Key Algorithms**: Yarowsky Algorithm, K-means clustering, Contextual embeddings (BERT) - **Advantages**: - Avoids annotation cost - Adaptable to new domains - State-of-the-art with neural methods - **Disadvantages**: - Lower interpretability - Bootstrapping errors can propagate - Computationally intensive **Comparative Summary Table**: | Approach | Data Need | Accuracy | Cost | Scalability | |----------|-----------|----------|------|-------------| | Knowledge-Based | Dictionary | Medium | Low | High | | Supervised | Annotated corpus | High | High | Medium | | Unsupervised | Raw corpus | Variable | Medium | High | #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Apply the Simplified Lesk Algorithm to disambiguate the word "plant" in the sentence: "She watered the plant in the garden." **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Identify Candidate Senses of "plant" (noun) from WordNet** - **Sense 1**: A living organism lacking the power of locomotion - Gloss: *"a living organism lacking the power of locomotion"* - **Sense 2**: Buildings for carrying on industrial labor - Gloss: *"buildings for carrying on industrial labor"* - **Sense 3**: Something planted secretly for discovery by another - Gloss: *"something planted secretly for discovery by another"* **Step 2: Extract Context Words** Context $C = \{ \text{She, watered, the, in, the, garden} \}$ Filtered content words: $C = \{ \text{watered, garden} \}$ **Step 3: Tokenize Glosses** - $G_1 = \{ \text{living, organism, lacking, power, locomotion} \}$ - $G_2 = \{ \text{buildings, carrying, industrial, labor} \}$ - $G_3 = \{ \text{something, planted, secretly, discovery, another} \}$ **Step 4: Compute Overlap** | Sense | Gloss Intersection with Context | Overlap Count | |-------|--------------------------------|---------------| | Sense 1 | $\emptyset$ (no direct match) | 0 | | Sense 2 | $\emptyset$ | 0 | | Sense 3 | $\emptyset$ | 0 | **Step 5: Apply Extended Lesk (since original gives zero overlap)** Augment glosses with hypernyms/examples from WordNet: - **Sense 1 augmented** includes: *"flora"*, *"botanical"*, *"vegetation"* - Semantic association: "watered" + "garden" → strong botanical/living-organism context Using semantic relatedness scoring (Extended Lesk): $$ \text{score}(S_1) = 2, \quad \text{score}(S_2) = 0, \quad \text{score}(S_3) = 0 $$ **Step 6: Final Selection** $$ \hat{s} = \arg\max_{j} \text{score}(s_j) = S_1 $$ **Conclusion**: The correct sense is **Sense 1** — a living organism (botanical plant), justified by the contextual cues `watered` and `garden`. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks): Probabilistic WSD and WordNet Analysis — Alternative Choice #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Derive the Naive Bayes classifier for Word Sense Disambiguation. Show how the decision rule is formulated. **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Problem Setup** Let $w$ be the target word with senses $S = \{s_1, s_2, ..., s_k\}$. Let $F = (f_1, f_2, ..., f_n)$ be the feature vector of context words within a window. We seek: $$ \hat{s} = \arg\max_{s_j} P(s_j \mid F) $$ **Step 2: Bayes' Theorem Application** $$ P(s_j \mid F) = \frac{P(F \mid s_j) \cdot P(s_j)}{P(F)} $$ Since $P(F)$ does not depend on $s_j$, we maximize the numerator: $$ \hat{s} = \arg\max_{s_j} P(F \mid s_j) \cdot P(s_j) $$ **Step 3: Naive Independence Assumption** Assuming features are conditionally independent given the sense: $$ P(F \mid s_j) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(f_i \mid s_j) $$ **Step 4: Final Decision Rule** $$ \boxed{\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s_j} P(s_j) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(f_i \mid s_j)} $$ **Step 5: Logarithmic Form (for numerical stability)** $$ \hat{s} = \arg\max_{s_j} \left[ \log P(s_j) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(f_i \mid s_j) \right] $$ **Step 6: Parameter Estimation with Laplace Smoothing** $$ P(s_j) = \frac{\text{count}(s_j) + 1}{N + k} $$ $$ P(f_i \mid s_j) = \frac{\text{count}(f_i, s_j) + 1}{\text{count}(s_j) + \vert V \vert} $$ **Practical Use Case**: Given a SemCor-annotated training set with 1000 instances of "bank" (600 financial, 400 river), and the test sentence context features $F = \{$account, deposit, money, ...$\}$, the classifier computes $P(\text{finance}) \cdot P(\text{account} \mid \text{finance}) \cdot ...$ and $P(\text{river}) \cdot P(\text{account} \mid \text{river}) \cdot ...$ and selects the higher-scoring sense. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Explain the structure of WordNet. Discuss how the Extended Lesk Algorithm utilizes WordNet's semantic relations to improve WSD performance. **Model Solution:** **WordNet Structure**: WordNet is a large lexical database where English words are grouped into **synsets** — sets of cognitive synonyms representing a single concept. The hierarchical organization consists of: - **Nouns**: 117,000 synsets organized via hypernymy (is-a), hyponymy, meronymy (part-of), and holonymy - **Verbs**: 11,000 synsets organized via hypernymy, troponymy (manner-of), and entailment - **Adjectives/Adverbs**: Organized via synonymy and antonymy relations **Key Semantic Relations Used by Extended Lesk**: 1. **Hypernymy**: $X$ is-a $Y$ (e.g., `dog` is-a `animal`) 2. **Hyponymy**: $X$ has-as-hypernym $Y$ (e.g., `dog` has hypernym `animal`) 3. **Meronymy**: $X$ is-part-of $Y$ (e.g., `wheel` is-part-of `car`) 4. **Gloss Relations**: Definitions of related synsets **Extended Lesk Algorithm Workflow**: **Step 1**: For each candidate sense $s_j$, gather: - Original gloss $G_j$ - Glosses of all hypernyms: $\{G_{h_1}, G_{h_2}, ...\}$ - Glosses of all hyponyms: $\{G_{y_1}, G_{y_2}, ...\}$ - Examples from the synset: $E_j$ **Step 2**: Form the *augmented signature*: $$ \Sigma_j = G_j \cup \bigcup_{h} G_h \cup \bigcup_{y} G_y \cup E_j $$ **Step 3**: Compute weighted overlap with context $C$: $$ \text{score}(s_j) = \sum_{w \in C} \text{weight}(w) \cdot \mathbb{1}[w \in \Sigma_j] $$ Where $\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ is the indicator function and weights can be assigned based on relation type (e.g., hypernyms weighted higher than hyponyms). **Step 4**: Select sense with maximum weighted overlap. **Why Extended Lesk Outperforms Original**: - Original Lesk suffers from **sparse overlap** — short glosses rarely share words with context - Extended Lesk **enriches signatures** by leveraging WordNet's semantic network - Empirically improves accuracy by **10–15%** on standard benchmarks like SemEval **Example Demonstration**: For "bank" with `financial` context, hypernym glosses of Sense 1 (financial institution) include terms like "business", "commerce", "depository" — providing multiple match opportunities even when the original gloss is sparse. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Examinations:** > > 1. **Confusing Homonymy and Polysemy** (loses 1–2 marks): Students frequently interchange these. Remember: *homonymy* = unrelated origins; *polysemy* = related evolutions. > > 2. **Skipping the Argmax Formulation** in Lesk: Always write $\hat{s} = \arg\max$ explicitly. Examiners award marks for the mathematical formulation. > > 3. **Forgetting Laplace Smoothing** in Naive Bayes derivations: Unseen features cause zero probabilities, which collapse the product. Always mention smoothing. > > 4. **Not Showing Working Steps**: KTU valuation requires *stepwise solutions*. Writing only the final answer for the Lesk worked example loses 3–4 marks. > > 5. **Missing the "Knowledge Source"**: When comparing WSD approaches, explicitly mention *which lexical resource* (WordNet, VerbNet, BabelNet) each method relies upon. > > 6. **Ignoring Context Window Size**: Naive Bayes accuracy depends on window size (typically ±3 to ±10). Mentioning this in the answer shows depth. > > 7. **Confusing Selectional Restrictions with Selectional Preferences**: Restrictions are *hard* constraints; preferences are *statistical tendencies* (use SAS formula). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Word Senses" (Module 2)** > > - **Word Sense** = contextually-determined meaning of a word > - **WSD Goal** = $\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s} P(s \mid C)$ > - **Homonymy** ≠ **Polysemy** (etymology matters!) > - **Three WSD Paradigms**: Knowledge-based, Supervised, Unsupervised > - **Lesk Algorithm** = gloss-context overlap maximization > - **Extended Lesk** = augments glosses with WordNet relations (hypernyms, hyponyms, examples) > - **Naive Bayes Decision Rule** = $\arg\max \left[ \log P(s) + \sum_i \log P(f_i \mid s) \right)$ > - **Laplace Smoothing** is essential to handle zero-frequency features > - **WordNet** is the primary sense inventory — organized as synsets with hypernym/hyponym hierarchy > - **Selectional Restrictions** = semantic constraints on verb arguments > - **SAS Formula** = $SAS(v, C) = P(C \mid v) / P(C)$ measures predicate-class affinity > - **Information Content** $IC(s) = -\log P(s)$ — rarer senses carry more information > - **Lin Similarity** uses Lowest Common Subsumer (LCS) in hypernym tree > - **Yarowsky Algorithm** = unsupervised bootstrapping using one-sense-per-collocation and one-sense-per-discourse heuristics > - **F1-Score** is the standard evaluation metric (harmonic mean of Precision and Recall) > - **Contextual Embeddings (BERT, ELMo)** represent the modern state-of-the-art in WSD > - **WSD Applications** = Machine Translation, Information Retrieval, QA, Sentiment Analysis, Text Summarization > - **Common Pitfall**: Sparse overlap in original Lesk — solved by Extended Lesk > - **KTU Favorite Topics**: Lesk worked examples, Naive Bayes derivation, WordNet hierarchy diagrams

topics.title: WordNet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT741 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** WordNet # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **WordNet** is a large lexical database of English developed at **Princeton University** (initiated by **George A. Miller** in 1985). Nouns, verbs, adjectives, and adverbs are grouped into sets of cognitive synonyms called **synsets**, each expressing a distinct concept. Synsets are interlinked by means of conceptual-semantic and lexical relations. In strict KTU 2024 NLP terminology: - A **Synset** is the fundamental building unit of WordNet — a set of synonymous word forms representing one lexicalized concept. - A **Gloss** is the textual definition and example sentence(s) attached to every synset. - WordNet is **NOT a traditional dictionary**; it is a **semantic network** of lexicalized concepts. **Headline Statistics (WordNet 3.1 reference figures):** - ~**117,000 synsets** - ~**206,000 word-sense pairs** - **Polysemy** — words with multiple senses: e.g., *bank* has **18 noun senses** and **6 verb senses**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition > [!NOTE] > Think of WordNet as a **"Mental Thesaurus Map"** — like a giant train network where every station is a *meaning* (synset) and the tracks between stations are *semantic relationships* (is-a, part-of, opposite-of, etc.). **Real-world analogy:** Imagine a **genealogy tree of English words**. Just as a family tree connects grandparents → parents → children through "is-parent-of" relations, WordNet connects meanings through **is-a (hypernymy)** relations. The word *dog* sits under *canine*, which sits under *mammal*, which sits under *animal*, which sits under *entity*. This vertical hierarchy lets a computer "reason" about word meanings the way humans do. ## 1.3 Lexical Categories in WordNet | Part of Speech | Abbreviation | Key Relation Type | Hierarchy? | |---|---|---|---| | Noun | **n** | Hypernymy / Hyponymy | **Yes (deepest)** | | Verb | **v** | Hypernymy / Troponymy | **Yes** | | Adjective | **a** | Antonymy, Similar-to | **No (clustered)** | | Adverb | **r** | Antonymy, Derived-from | **No (flat)** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Favourite Point:** Only **nouns and verbs** are organized into strict **is-a hierarchies**. Adjectives form **satellite clusters** around head synsets (e.g., *wet* clusters around the polar adjective *dry*). ## 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot - **Lemma:** The canonical word form (e.g., *run*). - **Word Form:** The surface string of characters. - **Sense:** One specific meaning of a word. - **Synset:** Set of lemmas sharing one sense (e.g., {car, auto, automobile, machine, motorcar}). - **Gloss:** Dictionary-style definition + sample sentence. - **Lexical Matrix:** The structural view where rows = words, columns = senses. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Internal Structure of WordNet WordNet is structured around the **Synset** as the atomic unit. Each synset has: 1. A **unique offset ID** (a 8-digit pointer, e.g., `00001740-n`). 2. A **list of synonyms** (word forms). 3. A **gloss** (definition + example). 4. **Pointer symbols** to other synsets (semantic relations). > [!NOTE] > Each synset ID ends with a **POS tag suffix**: `-n` (noun), `-v` (verb), `-a` (adjective), `-s` (adjective satellite), `-r` (adverb). ## 2.2 Semantic Relations in WordNet (KTU High-Yield) ### A. Between Synsets (Conceptual) | Relation Symbol | Relation Name | Direction | Example | |---|---|---|---| | **@** | Hypernym | IS-A (superordinate) | *dog* **is-a** *canine* | | **~** | Hyponym | IS-A (subordinate) | *dog* **has** *puppy* | | **#m** | Meronym (Part) | PART-OF | *wheel* **part-of** *car* | | **#s** | Meronym (Substance) | SUBSTANCE-OF | *water* **substance-of** *ice* | | **#p** | Meronym (Member) | MEMBER-OF | *player* **member-of** *team* | | **%p** | Holonym (Part) | HAS-PART | *car* **has-part** *wheel* | | **%m** | Holonym (Member) | HAS-MEMBER | *team* **has-member** *player* | | **\*** | Entailment | Verb-only | *snore* **entails** *sleep* | | **>** | Cause | Verb-only | *kill* **causes** *die* | | **\^** | Also-see | Cross-reference | — | | **$** | Verb Group | Similar verbs | — | ### B. Between Word Forms (Lexical) | Relation | Meaning | Example | |---|---|---| | **Antonym** | Opposite | *hot* ↔ *cold* | | **Pertainym** | Derived/related | *dental* **pertains to** *tooth* | | **Derivationally related** | Morphological link | *write* ↔ *writer* | ## 2.3 Why WordNet? — Engineering Utility WordNet powers critical NLP applications: - **Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD):** Determining which sense of *bank* (river bank vs. financial bank) is used in a sentence. - **Semantic Similarity / Relatedness:** Quantifying how close two concepts are (used in search, recommendation, paraphrase detection). - **Information Retrieval (IR):** Query expansion using hypernyms/hyponyms. - **Textual Entailment & Coreference Resolution.** - **Lexicon-based sentiment analysis.** - **Word Sense Embeddings (Retrofitting):** Injecting WordNet relations into distributional vectors (Faruqui et al., 2015). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — WordNet Similarity Measures > [!IMPORTANT] > **Information Content (IC)** of a concept $c$ is the negative log-likelihood of encountering that concept in a large corpus: > > $$\text{IC}(c) = -\log P(c)$$ | # | Measure | Formula | Range | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Path Length** | $sim_{path}(w_1, w_2) = \dfrac{1}{path\_length(LCS)}$ | $(0, 1]$ | Simplest; ignores depth | | 2 | **Wu-Palmer (1994)** | $sim_{WUP}(w_1, w_2) = \dfrac{2 \cdot depth(LCS)}{depth(w_1) + depth(w_2)}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Uses LCS depth | | 3 | **Resnik (1995)** | $sim_{RES}(w_1, w_2) = \text{IC}(LCS)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Corpus-dependent | | 4 | **Lin (1998)** | $sim_{LIN}(w_1, w_2) = \dfrac{2 \cdot \text{IC}(LCS)}{\text{IC}(w_1) + \text{IC}(w_2)}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Normalised Resnik | | 5 | **Jiang-Conrath (1997)** | $dist_{JC}(w_1, w_2) = \text{IC}(w_1) + \text{IC}(w_2) - 2 \cdot \text{IC}(LCS)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Distance measure | | 6 | **Leacock-Chodorow (1998)** | $sim_{LC}(w_1, w_2) = -\log \dfrac{path\_length}{2 \cdot D}$ | $[0, ?]$ | $D$ = max taxonomy depth | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Exam Tip:** The variable **LCS** = **Least Common Subsumer** (also called the **Lowest Common Hypernym**) — the most specific synset that is an ancestor of BOTH $w_1$ and $w_2$ in the hypernym hierarchy. Use `\\vert` notation (e.g., $\\vert w_1 \\vert$) for absolute values, **never raw pipes**, to avoid breaking the table layout. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example: Wu-Palmer Similarity Between "Tiger" and "Lion" **Given WordNet Sub-hierarchy (depth in brackets):** - `entity` (depth = 0) - `physical_entity` (depth = 1) - `object` (depth = 2) - `whole` (depth = 3) - `living_thing` (depth = 4) - `organism` (depth = 5) - `animal` (depth = 6) - `chordate` (depth = 7) - `vertebrate` (depth = 8) - `mammal` (depth = 9) - `placental` (depth = 10) - `carnivore` (depth = 11) - `feline` (depth = 12) - `big_cat` (depth = 13) - `tiger` (depth = 14) - `lion` (depth = 14) ### Step 1 — Identify the Two Target Synsets - $w_1$ = **tiger** (depth = 14) - $w_2$ = **lion** (depth = 14) ### Step 2 — Find the Lowest Common Subsumer (LCS) The deepest common ancestor of *tiger* and *lion* in the hypernym tree is **big_cat** (depth = 13).
