Therefore, $LCS = big\_cat$ and $depth(LCS) = 13$. ### Step 3 — Apply the Wu-Palmer Formula $$ \begin{aligned} sim_{WUP}(tiger, lion) &= \frac{2 \cdot depth(LCS)}{depth(w_1) + depth(w_2)} \\ &= \frac{2 \cdot 13}{14 + 14} \\ &= \frac{26}{28} \\ &= \frac{13}{14} \\ &\approx 0.9286 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Interpretation A value close to **1.0** indicates that *tiger* and *lion* are **semantically very close** because they share the highly specific common parent *big_cat*. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example: Path-Length Similarity Between "Car" and "Wheel" **Given hierarchy excerpt:** - `vehicle` → `motor_vehicle` → `car` (depth of *car* = 12) - `object` → `artifact` → `instrumentality` → `container` → `wheel` (depth of *wheel* = 8) - Their hypernym path meets at `entity` (depth = 0) - Path length $path\_length = 12 + 8 = 20$ edges ### Step 1 — Apply Path Length Formula $$ \begin{aligned} sim_{path}(car, wheel) &= \frac{1}{path\_length(LCS)} \\ &= \frac{1}{20} \\ &= 0.05 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Interpretation A similarity of **0.05** indicates that *car* and *wheel* are **semantically distant** along the hypernym path, even though we intuitively know *wheel* is **part-of** *car* (a **meronym** relation, not hypernymy). > [!NOTE] > This is a **classic KTU pitfall**: Path-based similarity is **symmetric** but **does not capture part-whole (meronym) relations**. For meronymy-aware similarity, **Resnik** or **Jiang-Conrath** are better. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example: Resnik Similarity (IC-Based) **Given a corpus where:** - $P(car) = 0.0006$ → $\text{IC}(car) = -\log(0.0006) \approx 7.42$ - $P(wheel) = 0.0003$ → $\text{IC}(wheel) = -\log(0.0003) \approx 8.11$ - $LCS(car, wheel) = \text{entity}$, with $P(entity) = 0.95$ → $\text{IC}(entity) = -\log(0.95) \approx 0.05$ ### Step 1 — Compute Resnik Similarity $$ \begin{aligned} sim_{RES}(car, wheel) &= \text{IC}(LCS) \\ &= \text{IC}(entity) \\ &\approx 0.05 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Interpretation The Resnik score is **0.05** because the common ancestor is too **abstract** (highly frequent in any corpus), so its information content is **low**. This is Resnik's well-known weakness: it **fails to differentiate between pairs** that share the same (uninformative) LCS. ### Step 3 — Compute Lin Similarity for the Same Pair $$ \begin{aligned} sim_{LIN}(car, wheel) &= \frac{2 \cdot \text{IC}(LCS)}{\text{IC}(car) + \text{IC}(wheel)} \\ &= \frac{2 \cdot 0.05}{7.42 + 8.11} \\ &= \frac{0.10}{15.53} \\ &\approx 0.0064 \end{aligned} $$ Lin normalises by the individual ICs and gives an even tighter, **bounded** value. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield) | Pair | Wu-Palmer | Resnik | Lin | Path | Best Measure for Task | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | tiger, lion | **0.93** | 6.5 | 0.85 | 0.25 | WUP (depth-aware) | | car, wheel | 0.10 | 0.05 | 0.006 | 0.05 | Meronym-aware (custom) | | car, bicycle | 0.78 | 5.2 | 0.71 | 0.20 | WUP / Lin | | sun, dog | 0.21 | 1.1 | 0.18 | 0.05 | All ≈ low | | happy, joyful | 0.66 | 4.0 | 0.55 | 0.17 | Lin (adjective cluster) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Takeaway for KTU answers:** No single measure is universally best. **WUP** works well for nouns within a clean hierarchy; **Resnik/Lin** are preferred when you have a **large domain corpus** to compute IC. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 WordNet Hypernym Hierarchy (Noun Subsystem) > The following Mermaid diagram shows the **is-a (hypernym) chain** for the noun *tiger* up to the root entity — the backbone of WordNet's semantic network. ```mermaid graph TD root1["ENTITY Depth 0"] root2["PHYSICAL ENTITY Depth 1"] root3["OBJECT Depth 2"] root4["WHOLE Depth 3"] root5["LIVING THING Depth 4"] root6["ORGANISM Depth 5"] root7["ANIMAL Depth 6"] root8["CHORDATE Depth 7"] root9["VERTEBRATE Depth 8"] root10["MAMMAL Depth 9"] root11["PLACENTAL Depth 10"] root12["CARNIVORE Depth 11"] root13["FELINE Depth 12"] root14["BIG CAT Depth 13"] root15["TIGER Depth 14"] root16["LION Depth 14"] root17["LEOPARD Depth 14"] root18["DOG Depth 14"] root19["CANINE Depth 13"] root1 --> root2 root2 --> root3 root3 --> root4 root4 --> root5 root5 --> root6 root6 --> root7 root7 --> root8 root8 --> root9 root9 --> root10 root10 --> root11 root11 --> root12 root12 --> root13 root13 --> root14 root14 --> root15 root14 --> root16 root14 --> root17 root12 --> root19 root19 --> root18 ``` **Reading the diagram:** The lowest common subsumer (LCS) of *tiger* and *lion* is `BIG CAT (Depth 13)`. The LCS of *tiger* and *dog* is `CARNIVORE (Depth 11)` — a more general node, hence lower Wu-Palmer similarity. --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of WordNet Similarity Engine ```mermaid graph LR inputA["INPUT WORD W1"] inputB["INPUT WORD W2"] posT["POS TAGGER"] senseS["SENSE SELECTOR
CONTEXT OR ALL SENSES"] lookupA["WORDNET LOOKUP
SYN SETS OF W1"] lookupB["WORDNET LOOKUP
SYN SETS OF W2"] lcsM["LCS FINDER
LEAST COMMON SUBSUMER"] icM["IC COMPUTER
CORPUS FREQUENCY"] simE["SIMILARITY ENGINE
WUP OR RESNIK OR LIN"] output["SIMILARITY SCORE"] inputA --> posT inputB --> posT posT --> senseS senseS --> lookupA senseS --> lookupB lookupA --> lcsM lookupB --> lcsM lcsM --> icM icM --> simE lookupA --> simE lookupB --> simE simE --> output ``` **Interpretation:** Two input words are first POS-tagged, then matched to candidate synsets, then the LCS is computed, then IC values are pulled from a corpus, and finally a similarity measure is applied to produce the score. --- ## 4.3 Synset Relation Map — Lexical vs Semantic ```mermaid graph TD clsA["LEXICAL RELATIONS
BETWEEN WORD FORMS"] clsB["SEMANTIC RELATIONS
BETWEEN SYN SETS"] subA1["ANTONYM
HOT COLD"] subA2["PERTAINYM
DENTAL TOOTH"] subB1["HYPERNYM
DOG IS A CANINE"] subB2["HYPONYM
DOG HAS PUPPY"] subB3["MERONYM
WHEEL PART OF CAR"] subB4["ENTAILMENT
SNORE ENTAILS SLEEP"] subB5["CAUSE
KILL CAUSES DIE"] clsA --> subA1 clsA --> subA2 clsB --> subB1 clsB --> subB2 clsB --> subB3 clsB --> subB4 clsB --> subB5 ``` This is the **conceptual map** the KTU paper setters most often expect students to redraw in Module-2 answers. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define WordNet. What is a synset? Explain with an example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** WordNet is a large lexical database of English developed at Princeton University that groups words into sets of cognitive synonyms called **synsets**. Each synset represents a single distinct concept and is interlinked with other synsets by semantic relations. A synset is a set of synonymous word forms sharing the same meaning. **Example:** The synset `{car, auto, automobile, machine, motorcar}` represents the concept of a four-wheeled motor vehicle, with POS tag `noun-automobile`. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **List any four semantic relations used in WordNet. Give an example for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Hypernym (IS-A):** *dog* IS-A *canine* — `dog` is a hyponym of `canine`. 2. **Hyponym (IS-A-reverse):** *puppy* IS-A *dog* — `puppy` is a hyponym of `dog`. 3. **Meronym (PART-OF):** *wheel* PART-OF *car*. 4. **Entailment (verb only):** *snore* ENTAILS *sleep*. *(Antonymy, holonymy, cause are also acceptable.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Pattern > **Q3. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Question]** ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a) Explain the architecture and components of WordNet in detail. Discuss the structure of synsets, glosses, and the major semantic relations (hypernymy, hyponymy, meronymy, holonymy, antonymy) with examples. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** WordNet is a **semantic lexicon** for English developed at Princeton. Its architecture is built around the **synset** (synonym set), which is the atomic unit. Each synset has the following components: - A **unique 8-digit offset ID** with a POS suffix (`n`, `v`, `a`, `s`, `r`). - A **list of lemmas** (word forms) that are synonymous in that sense. - A **gloss** — a definition + sample sentence. - A set of **pointers** to related synsets. **Major Semantic Relations:** 1. **Hypernymy (IS-A) / Hyponymy:** *dog* is a *canine*; *puppy* is a *dog*. This is a transitive relation that forms a tree hierarchy (a "forest" of hierarchies) for nouns. 2. **Meronymy (PART-OF) / Holonymy (HAS-PART):** *wheel* is a part of *car*; *car* has a *wheel*. Has three subtypes — part, substance, member. 3. **Antonymy (OPPOSITE):** *hot* ↔ *cold*; *big* ↔ *small*. Cross-POS antonymy is permitted (e.g., the adjective *cold* is antonymous to the noun *hot*). 4. **Entailment:** *snore* entails *sleep*; *pay* entails *spend*. 5. **Cause:** *kill* causes *die*; *give* causes *have*. **Cross-POS structure:** Nouns and verbs are organized in **is-a hierarchies**. Adjectives form **antonymous clusters** around head synsets. Adverbs are derived from adjectives (e.g., *quickly* from *quick*). **Lexical vs Semantic relations:** Lexical relations connect word forms (e.g., antonymy between *hot* and *cold* as words); semantic relations connect synsets (e.g., hypernymy between `dog` synset and `canine` synset). --- **(b) Describe the Wu-Palmer and Resnik measures of semantic similarity in WordNet. Compute the Wu-Palmer similarity for the word pair (car, truck) given a sub-hierarchy where `vehicle` (depth=10) is the LCS of both, with `car` at depth 12 and `truck` at depth 12. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Wu-Palmer (WUP):** Uses the **depth of the LCS** relative to the depths of the two senses. The deeper the LCS in the hierarchy, the more specific the shared concept, and the higher the similarity. $$ \begin{aligned} sim_{WUP}(w_1, w_2) = \frac{2 \cdot depth(LCS)}{depth(w_1) + depth(w_2)} \end{aligned} $$ **Resnik (1995):** Defines similarity as the **Information Content (IC) of the LCS** based on a corpus: $$ \begin{aligned} sim_{RES}(w_1, w_2) = \text{IC}(LCS) = -\log P(LCS) \end{aligned} $$ Resnik's measure is **unbounded** above (because IC can be arbitrarily large) and **fails to discriminate** between pairs that share the same uninformative LCS (e.g., `entity`). **Numerical Computation for (car, truck):** - $depth(LCS) = depth(vehicle) = 10$ - $depth(car) = 12$ - $depth(truck) = 12$ $$ \begin{aligned} sim_{WUP}(car, truck) &= \frac{2 \cdot 10}{12 + 12} \\ &= \frac{20}{24} \\ &= \frac{5}{6} \\ &\approx 0.8333 \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** *Car* and *truck* share the moderately specific concept of *vehicle* at depth 10, yielding a **high semantic similarity of ~0.83**, which intuitively matches our understanding that both are motor vehicles differing mainly in size/cargo function. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a) With a neat diagram, explain the hypernymy hierarchy in WordNet for the noun "dog" up to the root "entity". List the semantic and lexical relations supported by WordNet. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The noun *dog* sits in the following hypernym chain: ``` entity → physical_entity → object → whole → living_thing → organism → animal → chordate → vertebrate → mammal → placental → carnivore → canine → dog ``` **Semantic Relations in WordNet:** | Relation | Meaning | Example | |---|---|---| | Hypernym | IS-A (superordinate) | *dog* IS-A *canine* | | Hyponym | IS-A (subordinate) | *puppy* IS-A *dog* | | Meronym | PART-OF | *tail* PART-OF *dog* | | Holonym | HAS-PART | *dog* HAS-PART *tail* | | Antonym | OPPOSITE | *hot* ↔ *cold* | | Entailment | VERB ENTAILS | *buy* ENTAILS *pay* | | Cause | CAUSES | *kill* CAUSES *die* | | Pertainym | DERIVED-FROM | *dental* PERTAINS-TO *tooth* | **Lexical Relations** (between word forms): antonymy, derivationally-related forms. **Semantic Relations** (between synsets): hypernymy, hyponymy, meronymy, holonymy, entailment, cause, attribute, also-see, verb-group. **Why Nouns form a hierarchy but Adjectives do not:** Nouns correspond to ontological categories that can be naturally arranged in `is-a` trees. Adjectives are **polar** (e.g., *hot/cold*), **non-polar** (e.g., *American*), and **relational** (e.g., *related to*) and resist strict taxonomic ordering. --- **(b) Explain Information Content (IC) based similarity measures. Compare Resnik, Lin, and Jiang-Conrath measures with their formulas. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Information Content** is a corpus-driven measure of specificity of a concept $c$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{IC}(c) = -\log P(c) \end{aligned} $$ where $P(c) = \dfrac{freq(c)}{N}$, with $freq(c)$ = number of times concept $c$ (and its hyponyms) appear in the corpus, and $N$ = total annotated word count. **1. Resnik (1995):** Similarity = IC of LCS. $$ \begin{aligned} sim_{RES}(w_1, w_2) = \text{IC}(LCS) \end{aligned} $$ - **Pro:** Simple, intuitive. - **Con:** Unbounded; cannot differentiate between multiple pairs sharing the same LCS. **2. Lin (1998):** Normalised Resnik. $$ \begin{aligned} sim_{LIN}(w_1, w_2) = \frac{2 \cdot \text{IC}(LCS)}{\text{IC}(w_1) + \text{IC}(w_2)} \end{aligned} $$ - **Pro:** Bounded in $[0, 1]$; symmetric; theoretically justified by information-theoretic principles. - **Con:** Still inherits dependence on the LCS. **3. Jiang-Conrath (1997):** Returns a **distance** (not similarity). $$ \begin{aligned} dist_{JC}(w_1, w_2) = \text{IC}(w_1) + \text{IC}(w_2) - 2 \cdot \text{IC}(LCS) \end{aligned} $$ To convert to similarity: $sim_{JC} = \dfrac{1}{1 + dist_{JC}}$. - **Pro:** Empirically strong; commonly used as a baseline. - **Con:** Distance-based, so requires a transform for similarity. **Comparison Table:** | Measure | Type | Range | Corpus Needed? | |---|---|---|---| | Resnik | Similarity | $[0, \infty)$ | Yes | | Lin | Similarity | $[0, 1]$ | Yes | | Jiang-Conrath | Distance | $[0, \infty)$ | Yes | | Wu-Palmer | Similarity | $[0, 1]$ | No (uses hierarchy depth only) | | Path Length | Similarity | $(0, 1]$ | No | --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students LOSE marks in WordNet questions:** > 1. **Confusing "synset" with "word".** A word can belong to multiple synsets (one per sense). Marks are deducted if you treat *bank* as a single unit rather than a polysemous word with 18 noun + 6 verb senses. > 2. **Forgetting the POS suffix** in synset IDs (e.g., `00001740-n` is the *noun* car, not the verb). > 3. **Mixing lexical and semantic relations.** Antonymy is **lexical** (between word forms), while hypernymy is **semantic** (between synsets). Examiners mark this distinction strictly. > 4. **Wrong denominator in Wu-Palmer** — common mistake is using $2 \cdot depth(LCS)$ over a non-additive denominator. > 5. **Using raw vertical pipes `|` in tables** — your worksheet formatting breaks. Always use `\\vert` or `\\mid` in LaTeX within markdown tables. > 6. **Stating Wu-Palmer uses IC** — it DOES NOT. WUP uses only **taxonomy depth**, not corpus statistics. Only Resnik, Lin, and JC use IC. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **WordNet** is a Princeton-built **lexical database**, not a dictionary. It groups word senses into **synsets**. - **Synset** = set of cognitive synonyms + gloss + relations. **ID format:** `8-digit-offset-POS` (e.g., `02084071-n`). - **Core POS coverage:** Noun, Verb, Adjective, Adverb. Nouns + Verbs form strict **is-a hierarchies**; Adjectives form **antonymous clusters** around heads; Adverbs are **flat and derived**. - **Key relations to memorise:** Hypernym (`@`), Hyponym (`~`), Meronym (`#m/#s/#p`), Holonym (`%p/%m/%s`), Antonym (`!`), Entailment (`*`), Cause (`>`). - **LCS / Lowest Common Subsumer** = deepest shared ancestor in the hypernym tree — central to all similarity measures. - **Five must-know similarity formulas:** **Path**, **Wu-Palmer**, **Resnik**, **Lin**, **Jiang-Conrath**. Wu-Palmer and Path are **corpus-free**; Resnik, Lin, JC are **IC-based (corpus-dependent)**. - **Information Content:** $\text{IC}(c) = -\log P(c)$; specific (rare) concepts have **high IC**, abstract (frequent) concepts have **low IC**. - **Polysemy:** Number of senses a word has — a critical measure of lexical ambiguity, e.g., *bank* has 18 noun senses. - **WordNet Limitations:** (i) English-only (multilingual extensions like **MultiWordNet**, **EuroWordNet** exist); (ii) lacks contextual/dynamic meaning; (iii) difficult to maintain; (iv) misses modern slang and domain-specific jargon. - **Engineering uses:** WSD, semantic similarity, query expansion in IR, sense-aware embeddings, paraphrase detection, knowledge-graph construction. - **Quick exam mnemonic:** **"WUP-uses-Depth, RES-uses-IC, LIN-is-2-IC-LCS-over-sum-IC, JC-is-Distance, Path-is-1-over-length."**

topics.title: Context-Free Grammars and Constituency Parsing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT741 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Context-Free Grammars and Constituency Parsing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Context-Free Grammar (CFG)

A **Context-Free Grammar** is a formal mathematical device used to model the syntactic structure of natural and programming languages. Formally, a CFG is defined as a 4-tuple:

$$G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$$

where the four components are:

- **$V$** — A finite, non-empty set of **Non-Terminal symbols** (also called *variables* or *phrasal categories*). These are abstractions such as $S$, $NP$, $VP$, $PP$.
- **$\Sigma$** — A finite, non-empty set of **Terminal symbols** (the actual words of the language, e.g., "the", "cat", "runs"). Critically, $V \cap \Sigma = \emptyset$.
- **R** — A finite set of **Production Rules** (or *rewriting rules*) of the form $A \rightarrow \beta$, where $A \in V$ and $\beta \in (V \cup \Sigma)^{*}$.
- **$S$** — A distinguished **Start Symbol**, with $S \in V$, from which all derivations originate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A grammar is "context-free" because the rule $A \rightarrow \beta$ can be applied **regardless of the surrounding context** in which the non-terminal $A$ appears. The left-hand side contains exactly one non-terminal, in isolation.

The **language generated** by a CFG, denoted $L(G)$, is the set of all strings of terminals derivable from $S$:

$$L(G) = \{ w \in \Sigma^{*} \mid S \Rightarrow^{*} w \}$$

### 1.2 Constituency Parsing — The Intuitive Picture

**Conceptual Analogy (The "Sentence as a Matryoshka Doll"):**
Imagine an English sentence as a set of **Russian nesting dolls**. The outermost doll is the full sentence ($S$). Open it, and you find two smaller dolls: a *Noun Phrase* ($NP$) and a *Verb Phrase* ($VP$). Open the $VP$ doll, and you find a *Verb* ($V$) and another $NP$. Continue opening dolls until you reach the smallest, indivisible wooden figures — the actual words (terminals). **Constituency Parsing** is the algorithmic process of *unpacking* a sentence to recover this exact nested structure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Constituency** refers to the idea that **groups of words behave as a single unit** (a *constituent*). For example, in "The small dog barked loudly," the phrase *"The small dog"* is a single $NP$ constituent because it can be replaced wholesale by a pronoun (*"It barked loudly"*) or moved as a chunk.

### 1.3 The Parse Tree

The output of a constituency parser is a **parse tree** (also called a *constituency tree* or *phrase-structure tree*). It is a rooted, ordered tree where:

- **Internal nodes** are labeled with non-terminals ($S$, $NP$, $VP$, $PP$).
- **Leaves** are labeled with terminal symbols (the words).
- **Root** is always the start symbol $S$.

| Linguistic Concept | CFG Formalism | KTU 2024 Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Sentence | Start symbol | $S$ |
| Noun Phrase | Non-terminal | $NP$ |
| Verb Phrase | Non-terminal | $VP$ |
| Word | Terminal | $\in \Sigma$ |
| Syntactic Rule | Production | $\rightarrow$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Insight:** KTU board examiners specifically look for the distinction between **derivation** (a sequence of rule applications written as a string) and **parse tree** (a hierarchical graphical representation of the same derivation).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of a CFG — Operational Breakdown

A CFG operates through a rewriting mechanism. The operational logic is best understood as a sequence of substitution steps:

1. **Initialization:** Begin with the start symbol $S$ on the working tape.
2. **Rule Selection:** At any step, identify a non-terminal $A$ present in the current sentential form.
3. **Substitution:** Find a production $A \rightarrow \beta$ in $R$ and replace that single occurrence of $A$ with $\beta$.
4. **Termination:** Halt when no non-terminals remain. The resulting string must consist entirely of terminals to be a valid sentence of $L(G)$.

**The "Why" behind Context-Freeness:**
The context-free property is essential because natural language exhibits **structure-dependent rules**. For example, the rule for subject-verb agreement (*"She runs"* vs. *"They run"*) depends on the structural position of the noun phrase, not on the linear, surrounding words. CFGs formalize this elegantly by allowing rules to reference categories ($NP$, $VP$) rather than specific word sequences.

### 2.2 Types of Derivations

A **derivation** is a sequence of sentential forms connected by production rule applications. The symbol $\Rightarrow$ denotes a single derivation step, and $\Rightarrow^{*}$ denotes zero or more steps.

- **Leftmost Derivation ($\Rightarrow_{lm}$):** At each step, the **leftmost non-terminal** in the current sentential form is expanded first.
- **Rightmost Derivation ($\Rightarrow_{rm}$):** At each step, the **rightmost non-terminal** is expanded first.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** A parse tree does **not** uniquely determine the derivation order. A single parse tree corresponds to **multiple derivations** (one leftmost, one rightmost, and many in between), all sharing the same underlying tree structure.

### 2.3 Ambiguity in CFGs

A CFG is **ambiguous** if there exists at least one string in $L(G)$ that has **more than one distinct parse tree** (equivalently, more than one distinct leftmost derivation).

**Types of Ambiguity:**
- **Structural Ambiguity:** Same string has two different parse tree shapes. The classic example: *"I saw the man with the telescope"* — is "with the telescope" modifying "saw" (instrumental) or "the man" (possessive)?
- **Lexical Ambiguity:** A single word has multiple parts of speech. Handled via multiple lexical entries (e.g., *"book"* as noun vs. verb).

### 2.4 Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG)

To rank among multiple valid parse trees, we attach probabilities to each production rule. A **PCFG** is a 5-tuple $(V, \Sigma, R, S, P)$ where $P(A \rightarrow \beta)$ is the probability of that rule. The probabilities for all rules expanding a given non-terminal $A$ must sum to **1**:

$$\sum_{\beta} P(A \rightarrow \beta) = 1$$

The probability of a complete parse tree $T$ is the product of all rule probabilities used to construct it:

$$P(T) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(R_i)$$

### 2.5 Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)

For efficient parsing algorithms (like CKY), a CFG is often converted to **Chomsky Normal Form**, where every production is in one of two restricted shapes:

$$A \rightarrow B \ C \quad \text{or} \quad A \rightarrow a$$

where $A, B, C \in V$ and $a \in \Sigma$. No rule may have a single non-terminal on the right (no *unit productions*) or mixed terminal-nonterminal strings. The empty string $\varepsilon$ can be produced only by a special start symbol in some formulations.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Constraints | Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| CFG Definition | $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$ | $V \cap \Sigma = \emptyset$, $S \in V$ | 4-tuple |
| Language Generated | $L(G) = \{ w \in \Sigma^{*} \mid S \Rightarrow^{*} w \}$ | $w$ contains only terminals | Set notation |
| CNF Rule 1 | $A \rightarrow B \ C$ | $A, B, C \in V$ | Binary branching |
| CNF Rule 2 | $A \rightarrow a$ | $A \in V$, $a \in \Sigma$ | Lexical rule |
| PCFG Probability Sum | $\sum_{\beta} P(A \rightarrow \beta) = 1$ | Sum over all $\beta$ in RHS | Normalization |
| Parse Tree Probability | $P(T) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(R_i)$ | Product over all rules used | Joint probability |
| CKY Recursion | $X \in \text{cell}[i,k]$ if $\exists$ rule $X \rightarrow Y \ Z$ with $Y \in \text{cell}[i,j] \wedge Z \in \text{cell}[j,k]$ | $i < j < k$ | Dynamic programming |
| CKY Complexity | $O(n^{3} \cdot \vert R \vert)$ | $n = $ sentence length, $\vert R \vert = $ number of rules | Cubic time |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** CFGs form the syntactic backbone of compilers (parsing programming languages like Java, Python), bio-informatics (modeling RNA secondary structure), and natural language understanding pipelines in chatbots, search engines, and machine translation systems. Probabilistic extensions (PCFGs) power statistical parsers used in industrial NLP stacks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Full Leftmost Derivation

**Grammar $G$:**
1. $S \rightarrow NP \ VP$
2. $NP \rightarrow Det \ N$
3. $VP \rightarrow V \ NP$
4. $Det \rightarrow \text{the}$
5. $N \rightarrow \text{dog}$
6. $V \rightarrow \text{saw}$

**Target Sentence:** "the dog saw the dog"

**Step-by-Step Leftmost Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Start with $S$.
Apply rule 1: $S \rightarrow NP \ VP$
Current sentential form: $NP \ VP$

**Step 2:** Leftmost non-terminal is $NP$. Apply rule 2: $NP \rightarrow Det \ N$
Current sentential form: $Det \ N \ VP$

**Step 3:** Leftmost non-terminal is $Det$. Apply rule 4: $Det \rightarrow \text{the}$
Current sentential form: $\text{the} \ N \ VP$

**Step 4:** Leftmost non-terminal is $N$. Apply rule 5: $N \rightarrow \text{dog}$
Current sentential form: $\text{the dog} \ VP$

**Step 5:** Leftmost non-terminal is $VP$. Apply rule 3: $VP \rightarrow V \ NP$
Current sentential form: $\text{the dog} \ V \ NP$

**Step 6:** Leftmost non-terminal is $V$. Apply rule 6: $V \rightarrow \text{saw}$
Current sentential form: $\text{the dog saw} \ NP$

**Step 7:** Leftmost non-terminal is $NP$. Apply rule 2: $NP \rightarrow Det \ N$
Current sentential form: $\text{the dog saw} \ Det \ N$

**Step 8:** Apply rule 4: $Det \rightarrow \text{the}$
Current sentential form: $\text{the dog saw the} \ N$

**Step 9:** Apply rule 5: $N \rightarrow \text{dog}$
Final sentential form: $\text{the dog saw the dog}$

The complete derivation can be compactly written as:

$$S \Rightarrow_{lm} NP \ VP \Rightarrow_{lm} Det \ N \ VP \Rightarrow_{lm} \text{the} \ N \ VP \Rightarrow_{lm} \text{the dog} \ VP \Rightarrow_{lm} \text{the dog} \ V \ NP \Rightarrow_{lm}^{*} \text{the dog saw the dog}$$

### 3.2 Deriving the CKY (Cocke-Kasami-Younger) Recognition Algorithm

The CKY algorithm determines whether a string $w = w_1 w_2 \ldots w_n$ belongs to $L(G)$ when $G$ is in CNF. It uses a 2D **recognition table** $T[i,j]$ storing the set of non-terminals that can derive the substring $w_i \ldots w_j$.

**Base Case (substrings of length 1):**
For each word $w_i$, populate $T[i,i]$ with all non-terminals $A$ such that $A \rightarrow w_i$ is a rule:

$$T[i,i] = \{ A \in V \mid A \rightarrow w_i \in R \}$$

**Recursive Case (substrings of length $\geq 2$):**
For a substring $w_i \ldots w_k$ of length $\ell = k - i + 1$, split it at every possible position $j$ where $i \leq j < k$:

$$T[i,k] = \bigcup_{j=i}^{k-1} \left\{ A \in V \mid \exists \, A \rightarrow B \ C \in R \text{ such that } B \in T[i,j] \text{ and } C \in T[j+1,k] \right\}$$

**Acceptance Condition:**
The string $w$ is in $L(G)$ if and only if the start symbol $S$ appears in the top cell:

$$w \in L(G) \iff S \in T[1,n]$$

**Complexity Derivation:**
- There are $O(n^2)$ cells in the triangular table.
- For each cell $[i,k]$, we iterate over $O(n)$ split points $j$.
- For each split, we check all $O(\vert R \vert)$ rules.
- **Total time complexity:**

$$T(n) = O(n^{3} \cdot \vert R \vert)$$

where $\vert R \vert$ is the number of productions in the grammar. This cubic bound is a fundamental result in parsing theory.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Parsing Algorithm Comparison

| Algorithm | Grammar Form | Time Complexity | Strategy | Recognizes All CFLs? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CKY** | CNF only | $O(n^{3} \cdot \vert R \vert)$ | Bottom-up dynamic programming | Yes |
| **Earley** | Any CFG | $O(n^{3})$ worst case | Chart parsing, left-to-right | Yes |
| **Top-Down Recursive Descent** | Any CFG (often LL(k)) | Exponential worst case | Depth-first search | No (left-recursion issues) |
| **Shift-Reduce** | Any CFG | $O(n)$ per step | Stack-based | No (greedy local decisions) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "explain parsing algorithms," always present CKY using the table-based diagram and explicitly mention the CNF requirement. Examiners reward the visual table representation with full marks.

### 3.4 Proof Sketch: Equivalence of Derivations and Parse Trees

**Claim:** There is a one-to-many correspondence between parse trees and derivations (one tree = many derivations, but one leftmost derivation = one unique tree).

**Construction from Derivation to Tree:** Given a leftmost derivation $S = \alpha_0 \Rightarrow_{lm} \alpha_1 \Rightarrow_{lm} \ldots \Rightarrow_{lm} \alpha_n = w$, construct the tree by adding a child for each non-terminal expanded. Since the leftmost non-terminal is always expanded first, the tree is built in a deterministic left-to-right, depth-first manner.

**Construction from Tree to Derivation:** Given a parse tree, perform a pre-order traversal. Whenever a node labeled $A$ has children forming the RHS of a rule $A \rightarrow \beta$, record the step $A \Rightarrow \beta$ in the derivation. The order in which we visit and expand nodes determines whether we obtain the leftmost, rightmost, or some intermediate derivation.

**Key Consequence:** Since a single tree yields multiple derivations, **ambiguity is best defined at the tree level**, not the derivation level. Two distinct trees for the same string = structural ambiguity.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Parse Tree for "the dog saw the dog"

The following Mermaid diagram renders the constituency parse tree derived in Section 3.1. Note that node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `n1`, `vpNode`) to comply with Mermaid safeguards, and labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text.

```mermaid
graph TD
    n1["S"]
    n2["NP"]
    n3["VP"]
    n4["Det"]
    n5["N"]
    n6["V"]
    n7["NP"]
    n8["Det"]
    n9["N"]

    n1 --> n2
    n1 --> n3
    n2 --> n4
    n2 --> n5
    n3 --> n6
    n3 --> n7
    n7 --> n8
    n7 --> n9

    n4 --> t1["the"]
    n5 --> t2["dog"]
    n6 --> t3["saw"]
    n8 --> t4["the"]
    n9 --> t5["dog"]
```

**Reading the Tree:**
- The root `S` has two children: an `NP` subject and a `VP` predicate.
- The subject `NP` branches into `Det` and `N`, terminating in the words "the" and "dog".
- The `VP` branches into `V` and a recursive `NP`, which again expands to another "the dog".
- The leaves read left-to-right reconstruct the original sentence.

### 4.2 CKY Recognition Table — Block Architecture

The CKY algorithm operates on a triangular table indexed by sentence position. The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional flow** of the CKY computation for a sentence of length $n = 4$ (e.g., "the dog saw the"). Cells in lower rows must be filled before cells in upper rows can be computed.

```mermaid
graph TD
    base["BASE CASE: Fill diagonal with lexical rules A to wi"]
    cell11["Cell 1,1: words at position 1"]
    cell22["Cell 2,2: words at position 2"]
    cell33["Cell 3,3: words at position 3"]
    cell44["Cell 4,4: words at position 4"]

    length2["LENGTH 2: Combine adjacent cells"]
    cell12["Cell 1,2: spans words 1 to 2"]
    cell23["Cell 2,3: spans words 2 to 3"]
    cell34["Cell 3,4: spans words 3 to 4"]

    length3["LENGTH 3: Split and combine"]
    cell13["Cell 1,3: spans words 1 to 3"]
    cell24["Cell 2,4: spans words 2 to 4"]

    topCell["TOP CELL 1,4: spans entire sentence"]
    accept["ACCEPTANCE: Is Start Symbol S in Cell 1,4?"]

    base --> cell11
    base --> cell22
    base --> cell33
    base --> cell44

    cell11 --> cell12
    cell22 --> cell12
    cell22 --> cell23
    cell33 --> cell23
    cell33 --> cell34
    cell44 --> cell34

    cell12 --> cell13
    cell23 --> cell13
    cell12 --> cell24
    cell34 --> cell24

    cell23 --> cell24

    cell13 --> topCell
    cell24 --> topCell

    topCell --> accept
```

**Functional Interpretation:**
- **Bottom Layer (Base):** Each cell holds non-terminals that derive a single word via lexical rules.
- **Middle Layers (Inductive):** Each cell $[i,k]$ aggregates non-terminals by trying all split points $j$ and checking whether a binary rule $A \rightarrow B \ C$ exists with $B$ in $[i,j]$ and $C$ in $[j+1,k]$.
- **Top Cell (Acceptance):** If the start symbol $S$ appears in the top cell, the sentence is grammatical.

### 4.3 Ambiguity Visualization — Two Parse Trees for One String

The sentence *"I saw the man with the telescope"* admits two distinct parse trees. The Mermaid diagram below renders both side-by-side using parallel subgraphs.

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph treeA["TREE A: PP attaches to VP"]
        s1["S"]
        npA1["NP"]
        vpA1["VP"]
        vA1["V saw"]
        npA2["NP"]
        detA1["Det the"]
        nA1["N man"]
        ppA1["PP"]
        pA1["P with"]
        npA3["NP"]
        detA2["Det the"]
        nA2["N telescope"]
        s1 --> npA1
        s1 --> vpA1
        vpA1 --> vA1
        vpA1 --> npA2
        vpA1 --> ppA1
        npA2 --> detA1
        npA2 --> nA1
        ppA1 --> pA1
        ppA1 --> npA3
        npA3 --> detA2
        npA3 --> nA2
    end

    subgraph treeB["TREE B: PP attaches to NP"]
        s2["S"]
        npB1["NP"]
        vpB1["VP"]
        vB1["V saw"]
        npB2["NP"]
        detB1["Det the"]
        nB1["N man"]
        ppB1["PP"]
        pB1["P with"]
        npB3["NP"]
        detB2["Det the"]
        nB2["N telescope"]
        s2 --> npB1
        s2 --> vpB1
        vpB1 --> vB1
        vpB1 --> npB2
        npB2 --> detB1
        npB2 --> nB1
        npB2 --> ppB1
        ppB1 --> pB1
        ppB1 --> npB3
        npB3 --> detB2
        npB3 --> nB2
    end
```

**Interpretation:**
- In **Tree A**, the prepositional phrase *"with the telescope"* is a child of the **verb phrase**, indicating it is the *instrument* used for seeing.
- In **Tree B**, the same prepositional phrase is a child of the **object noun phrase**, indicating the *man possesses the telescope*.
- Both trees are valid constituents of the same CFG, demonstrating **structural ambiguity**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define a Context-Free Grammar. List its four components with a one-line description of each.**

**Model Answer:**
A Context-Free Grammar is a formal 4-tuple $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$ used to generate the syntax of formal and natural languages. The four components are:

1. **$V$ (Non-terminals):** A finite set of variables/categories like $S$, $NP$, $VP$.
2. **$\Sigma$ (Terminals):** A finite set of actual words/symbols in the language.
3. **$R$ (Production Rules):** A finite set of rewriting rules of the form $A \rightarrow \beta$ where $A \in V$.
4. **$S$ (Start Symbol):** A distinguished non-terminal from which all derivations begin.

> [!NOTE]
> Full marks require listing all four components and the 4-tuple notation $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a leftmost derivation and a rightmost derivation. Why can a single parse tree yield multiple derivations?**

**Model Answer:**
In a **leftmost derivation**, at each step the leftmost non-terminal in the sentential form is expanded first. In a **rightmost derivation**, the rightmost non-terminal is expanded first. A single parse tree corresponds to multiple derivations because the order in which we expand sibling nodes is not fixed by the tree structure. A pre-order traversal (left-to-right) yields the leftmost derivation, while a post-order-style right-to-left traversal yields the rightmost derivation. The tree captures **hierarchical structure**, while the derivation captures **temporal order of expansion**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Consider the following Context-Free Grammar $G$:
1. $S \rightarrow NP \ VP$
2. $S \rightarrow Aux \ NP \ VP$
3. $NP \rightarrow Pronoun$
4. $NP \rightarrow Proper-Noun$
5. $NP \rightarrow Det \ N$
6. $VP \rightarrow Verb$
7. $VP \rightarrow Verb \ NP$

The lexicon is: *Pronoun $\rightarrow$ "I"*, *Proper-Noun $\rightarrow$ "Rahul"*, *Verb $\rightarrow$ "like"*, *Verb $\rightarrow$ "prefer"*, *Aux $\rightarrow$ "do"*, *Det $\rightarrow$ "the"*, *N $\rightarrow$ "flight"*.

**(i)** Show **two distinct parse trees** for the ambiguous sentence: *"I prefer the flight"*. **State the type of ambiguity.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The two parse trees arise because the object *"the flight"* can be either a direct object of *"prefer"* or a complement of the verb phrase headed by *"like"*-style interpretation.

**Parse Tree 1 (PP-attachment style):** Here *"the flight"* is the direct object of *"prefer"*.
$S \rightarrow NP \ VP \rightarrow$ Pronoun $\rightarrow$ "I" combined with $VP \rightarrow$ Verb $\rightarrow$ "prefer" combined with $NP \rightarrow Det \rightarrow$ "the", $N \rightarrow$ "flight".

**Parse Tree 2 (Coordination style):** Here the $VP$ has a recursive structure with Verb "prefer" taking another VP headed by the object.
$S \rightarrow Aux \ NP \ VP \rightarrow$ "do" + Pronoun "I" + $VP$ where $VP \rightarrow$ Verb "prefer" + $NP$ "the flight".

**Type of Ambiguity:** This is **structural ambiguity** (also called *syntactic ambiguity* or *attachment ambiguity*), specifically **VP-attachment ambiguity** — the prepositional/object phrase can attach at different levels of the verb phrase tree.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Many students confuse *lexical ambiguity* (a word having multiple meanings) with *structural ambiguity* (a sentence having multiple parse trees). Examiners will deduct 2 marks if you misclassify the ambiguity type.

**(ii)** Convert the above grammar $G$ into **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)**. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Eliminate the rule $S \rightarrow Aux \ NP \ VP$ (length 3, mixed terminals absent here but rule is ternary).** CNF requires all non-terminal rules to be either binary ($A \rightarrow B \ C$) or lexical ($A \rightarrow a$). Introduce a new non-terminal $X$ and split:
- $S \rightarrow Aux \ NP \ VP$ becomes $S \rightarrow Aux \ X$ and $X \rightarrow NP \ VP$

**Step 2: Handle the binary rules.** All other rules ($S \rightarrow NP \ VP$, $VP \rightarrow Verb \ NP$, $NP \rightarrow Det \ N$) are already in binary form.

**Step 3: Handle lexical rules.** Rules like *Pronoun $\rightarrow$ "I"*, *Verb $\rightarrow$ "like"*, etc. are already in $A \rightarrow a$ form.

**Final CNF Grammar:**
1. $S \rightarrow NP \ VP$
2. $S \rightarrow Aux \ X$
3. $X \rightarrow NP \ VP$
4. $NP \rightarrow Pronoun$
5. $NP \rightarrow Proper\text{-}Noun$
6. $NP \rightarrow Det \ N$
7. $VP \rightarrow Verb$
8. $VP \rightarrow Verb \ NP$

Plus the lexical rules unchanged.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Insight:** Examiners award 3 marks for the 4-tuple identification, 2 marks for binary rule creation, and 2 marks for the final grammar listing.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **CKY (Cocke-Kasami-Younger) algorithm** for constituency parsing. State its time complexity and the grammar form required. Demonstrate the algorithm on the sentence *"the dog saw"* using an appropriate CNF grammar. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**CKY Algorithm Explanation:**
The CKY algorithm is a **bottom-up dynamic programming** parser that determines whether a string belongs to the language generated by a CFG in **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)**. It uses a triangular table $T[i,j]$ where entry $[i,j]$ stores all non-terminals that can derive the substring from position $i$ to $j$.

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. **Input:** Sentence $w_1 w_2 \ldots w_n$ and grammar $G$ in CNF.
2. **Base Case:** For $i = 1$ to $n$, set $T[i,i] = \{A \mid A \rightarrow w_i \in R\}$.
3. **Inductive Step:** For each span length $\ell = 2$ to $n$, and for each starting position $i$, compute $T[i, i+\ell-1]$ by trying all split points $j$ and checking if any rule $A \rightarrow B \ C$ has $B \in T[i,j]$ and $C \in T[j+1, i+\ell-1]$.
4. **Acceptance:** Accept if $S \in T[1,n]$.

**Grammar Form Required:** **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)** — all rules are either $A \rightarrow B \ C$ or $A \rightarrow a$.

**Time Complexity:**

$$O(n^{3} \cdot \vert R \vert)$$

where $n$ is sentence length and $\vert R \vert$ is the number of grammar rules.

**Demonstration on "the dog saw" (n = 3):**
Grammar in CNF: $S \rightarrow NP \ VP$, $NP \rightarrow Det \ N$, $VP \rightarrow V \ NP$, $Det \rightarrow$ "the", $N \rightarrow$ "dog", $V \rightarrow$ "saw".

| Cell | Contents |
|---|---|
| $T[1,1]$ | $\{Det\}$ (from $Det \rightarrow$ "the") |
| $T[2,2]$ | $\{N\}$ (from $N \rightarrow$ "dog") |
| $T[3,3]$ | $\{V\}$ (from $V \rightarrow$ "saw") |
| $T[1,2]$ | $\{NP\}$ (rule $NP \rightarrow Det \ N$ with $Det \in T[1,1]$, $N \in T[2,2]$) |
| $T[2,3]$ | $\{VP\}$ (rule $VP \rightarrow V \ NP$ — but $NP \notin T[3,3]$; check other rules — no valid rule for "dog saw" as a VP constituent. $T[2,3] = \emptyset$) |
| $T[1,3]$ | Check split $j=1$: need $A \rightarrow B \ C$ with $B \in T[1,1]$, $C \in T[2,3]$. Since $T[2,3] = \emptyset$, no match. Check split $j=2$: need $B \in T[1,2] = \{NP\}$, $C \in T[3,3] = \{V\}$. Rule $S \rightarrow NP \ VP$ — but we need $VP$, not $V$. So no match. $T[1,3] = \emptyset$ |

**Result:** $S \notin T[1,3]$, so the sentence is **rejected**. (This is expected because the grammar requires a $VP$ containing both a verb and an object NP, but "saw" has no object.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often forget that **CKY requires CNF input**. If the grammar has unit productions or mixed terminal/non-terminal rules, the algorithm will fail. Always perform CNF conversion first. Also, students confuse $T[i,j]$ indexing — remember $i$ is the **start** position, $j$ is the **end** position (inclusive), and the span length is $j - i + 1$.

**(b)** Define **Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG)**. Given the PCFG rules below, compute the probability of the parse tree for *"the dog saw the dog"*. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **PCFG** is a 5-tuple $(V, \Sigma, R, S, P)$ where each production $A \rightarrow \beta$ is assigned a probability $P(A \rightarrow \beta)$ such that for every non-terminal $A$:

$$\sum_{\beta} P(A \rightarrow \beta) = 1$$

**PCFG Rules:**
| Rule | Probability |
|---|---|
| $S \rightarrow NP \ VP$ | 1.0 |
| $NP \rightarrow Det \ N$ | 0.8 |
| $NP \rightarrow Pronoun$ | 0.2 |
| $VP \rightarrow V \ NP$ | 0.7 |
| $VP \rightarrow V$ | 0.3 |
| $Det \rightarrow$ "the" | 1.0 |
| $N \rightarrow$ "dog" | 1.0 |
| $V \rightarrow$ "saw" | 1.0 |

**Rules Used in the Parse Tree:**
1. $S \rightarrow NP \ VP$ (probability 1.0)
2. $NP \rightarrow Det \ N$ (probability 0.8) — for the subject
3. $Det \rightarrow$ "the" (probability 1.0)
4. $N \rightarrow$ "dog" (probability 1.0)
5. $VP \rightarrow V \ NP$ (probability 0.7)
6. $V \rightarrow$ "saw" (probability 1.0)
7. $NP \rightarrow Det \ N$ (probability 0.8) — for the object
8. $Det \rightarrow$ "the" (probability 1.0)
9. $N \rightarrow$ "dog" (probability 1.0)

**Tree Probability Calculation:**

$$P(T) = \prod_{i=1}^{9} P(R_i) = 1.0 \times 0.8 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.7 \times 1.0 \times 0.8 \times 1.0 \times 1.0$$

$$P(T) = 0.8 \times 0.7 \times 0.8 = 0.448$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Shortcut:** When multiple parse trees exist for a sentence, the parser **selects the tree with the maximum probability**. This is the foundation of statistical parsing used in real-world NLP systems.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Mark-Loss Zones for Constituency Parsing Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing derivation and parse tree:** A derivation is a *string sequence*; a parse tree is a *hierarchical structure*. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks for conflating these.
> 2. **Forgetting CNF requirement for CKY:** CKY will not work on arbitrary CFGs. Always convert to CNF first. Marks are lost for skipping this step.
> 3. **Incorrect probability normalization:** In PCFGs, the probabilities of all rules expanding the **same non-terminal** must sum to 1. Cross-non-terminal normalization is not required.
> 4. **Misidentifying ambiguity type:** Structural ambiguity = multiple parse trees. Lexical ambiguity = multiple word meanings. Do not interchange them.
> 5. **Skipping intermediate derivation steps:** KTU board evaluators expect **every single step** of a derivation to be shown. Jumping from $S$ directly to the final string costs 3–4 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CFG 4-tuple:** $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$ — Non-terminals, Terminals, Rules, Start symbol.
- **Context-free means:** The LHS of every rule is a single non-terminal, with no surrounding context required.
- **Derivation vs. Parse Tree:** Derivation = sequential rewriting; Parse Tree = hierarchical visual structure.
- **Leftmost vs. Rightmost:** Leftmost expands the leftmost non-terminal first; rightmost expands the rightmost first.
- **Ambiguity:** A CFG is ambiguous if any string has **more than one parse tree**. Ambiguity is a property of the **grammar**, not the language.
- **CNF:** All rules are either $A \rightarrow B \ C$ (binary) or $A \rightarrow a$ (lexical). Required for CKY.
- **CKY Algorithm:** Bottom-up dynamic programming parser. Table $T[i,j]$ holds non-terminals deriving $w_i \ldots w_j$. Complexity $O(n^{3} \cdot \vert R \vert)$. Accepts if $S \in T[1,n]$.
- **PCFG:** Attaches probabilities to rules. Tree probability = product of rule probabilities. Used to disambiguate among multiple valid parses.
- **Earley Parser:** A chart parser that works on **any CFG** (not just CNF) and runs in $O(n^{3})$ worst case.
- **Engineering Applications:** CFGs power compiler syntax checking, RNA structure prediction, chatbot NLU pipelines, and statistical machine translation systems.
- **Exam Mantra:** Always (1) state definitions formally, (2) show every derivation step, (3) draw the parse tree, and (4) verify probability normalization for PCFGs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Treebank
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT741 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Treebank

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Treebank** is a linguistically annotated corpus of natural language sentences in which each sentence has been manually (or semi-automatically) parsed and labeled with a syntactic structure — typically in the form of a **phrase-structure tree**, a **dependency tree**, or both. Each node of the tree carries linguistic information such as the **Part-of-Speech (POS) tag**, the **syntactic category** (e.g., NP, VP, PP), and often a **grammatical function label** (e.g., SBJ, OBJ).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim-aligned):** *"A treebank is a syntactically annotated text corpus in which every sentence is paired with a parse tree, enabling the empirical extraction of grammar rules, lexical statistics, and probabilistic models for statistical parsing."*

The most influential example is the **Penn Treebank (PTB)**, containing roughly **1 million words** of pre-parsed Wall Street Journal material, plus other genres such as Brown Corpus excerpts and Switchboard (spoken) data. Other prominent treebanks include the **Prague Dependency Treebank (PDT)**, the **Stanford Dependencies**, and the multi-lingual **Universal Dependencies (UD)** project.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library with Skeleton Outlines"

Imagine you walk into a giant library. A normal corpus is just shelves of books — sentences lying flat with no structure. A **treebank** is the *same library*, but every sentence has a librarian's skeleton outline drawn over it:

- Words are the **nouns** in the outline (leaves).
- Groups of words (Noun Phrases, Verb Phrases) are the **headings**.
- The way headings nest under bigger headings is the **grammar** of the sentence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Anchor:** A treebank does NOT generate grammar — it **records** how a particular grammar is realized in real text. It is *evidence* (corpus-driven), not *prescription* (rule-driven).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Conventions

- **Token count (PTB-WSJ):** ≈ 1,000,000 words, ≈ 49,000 sentences.
- **Annotation layers:** Tokenization → POS tagging → Phrase-structure parse → Function tags.
- **POS tagset (Penn):** 45 tags (e.g., `NN`, `VBZ`, `JJ`, `IN`).
- **Inter-annotator agreement:** typically measured by **Cohen's κ**, target ≥ 0.90.
- **Format:** bracket notation, e.g., `(S (NP (DT The) (NN cat)) (VP (VBZ sits)))`.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU exams, you are expected to know the **Penn Treebank** in detail, the **annotation process**, and how treebanks enable **Probabilistic Context-Free Grammars (PCFGs)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline of Treebank Construction

A treebank is not created by simply drawing trees. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following pipeline:

1. **Corpus Selection** — Choose domain and genre (e.g., newswire, biomedical, tweets).
2. **Pre-processing** — Sentence segmentation, tokenization, normalization.
3. **POS Tagging** — Each token receives a Penn-style tag (sometimes automatic, then manually corrected).
4. **Manual Parsing / Annotation** — Trained linguists bracket the sentence into constituents.
5. **Double Annotation + Adjudication** — Two independent annotators; a third adjudicates disagreements.
6. **Quality Assurance** — Inter-annotator agreement measured; guidelines updated.
7. **Release** — Distributed in formats like `.mrg` (PTB), CoNLL-U (UD), or XML.

## 2.2 Two Principal Tree Structures

| Tree Type | What Nodes Are | What Edges Show | Example Treebank |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phrase-Structure (Constituency)** | Words & constituents (NP, VP, …) | Dominance / embedding | Penn Treebank |
| **Dependency** | Words (only) | Head-dependent grammatical relations | Universal Dependencies, Prague PDT |

> [!NOTE]
> In a **dependency treebank**, every word is a node; arrows point from **head → dependent** with a labeled relation (e.g., `nsubj`, `dobj`, `amod`).

## 2.3 Why Treebanks Matter — The "Why" and "How"

- **Why?** Hand-written grammars are brittle and incomplete; treebanks give *empirical*, *probabilistic* data about how language is actually used.
- **How?** Treebanks expose raw counts of grammar rules. From these counts we derive **Probabilistic Context-Free Grammars (PCFGs)** that drive **statistical parsers** (e.g., the Collins parser, the Stanford Parser).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every modern data-driven parser (including neural ones) ultimately depends on treebank data — either as **training signal**, **evaluation gold-standard**, or **both**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Notation | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PCFG rule probability | $P(A \rightarrow \beta) = \dfrac{\mathrm{Count}(A \rightarrow \beta)}{\sum_{\gamma} \mathrm{Count}(A \rightarrow \gamma)}$ | dimensionless, $0 \le P \le 1$ |
| 2 | Tree probability (PCFG) | $P(T) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(r_i)$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Sentence likelihood | $P(S) = \sum_{T \in \mathcal{T}(S)} P(T)$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Inside probability | $\alpha_{A}(i,j)$ — total prob of subtree rooted at non-terminal $A$ spanning $w_i \ldots w_j$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Outside probability | $\beta_{A}(i,j)$ — total prob of tree fragment where $A$ spans $i \ldots j$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Most likely parse | $\hat{T} = \arg\max_{T \in \mathcal{T}(S)} P(T)$ | Viterbi decoder output |
| 7 | Inter-annotator agreement | $\kappa = \dfrac{P_o - P_e}{1 - P_e}$ | $-1 \le \kappa \le 1$, target $\geq 0.90$ |
| 8 | Lexical smoothing (rule back-off) | $P_{\mathrm{backoff}}(w \mid A) = \lambda \, P_{\mathrm{ML}}(w \mid A) + (1-\lambda)\,P(w)$ | $\lambda \in [0,1]$ |
| 9 | Head-percolation rule | For each non-terminal $A$, choose head child by ordered list | deterministic |
| 10 | Dependency arc probability | $P(h \rightarrow d \mid h) = \dfrac{\mathrm{Count}(h \rightarrow d)}{\sum_{d'} \mathrm{Count}(h \rightarrow d')}$ | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Table Rule:** No literal `|` symbols were used inside table cells; all set/cardinality delimiters (none needed here) would be written as `\vert` or `\mid`.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Machine Translation:** Alignment & reordering models trained on dependency treebanks.
- **Information Extraction:** Parse trees identify subject–verb–object triples for KB construction.
- **Question Answering & Chatbots:** Dependency relations underpin semantic role labeling.
- **Healthcare NLP:** Biomedical treebanks (e.g., **GENIA Treebank**) enable clinical NER.
- **Low-resource languages:** UD provides consistent cross-lingual annotation for transfer learning.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Estimating a PCFG Rule from a Treebank

We want the **maximum-likelihood estimate** of a PCFG production $A \rightarrow \beta$ from treebank $\mathcal{B}$.

**Step 1 — Define the count.**
Let $\mathrm{Count}_{\mathcal{B}}(A \rightarrow \beta)$ be the number of times the production $A \rightarrow \beta$ appears as a local tree fragment in $\mathcal{B}$.

**Step 2 — Sum over all expansions of $A$.**
The denominator must aggregate over **every** right-hand side $\gamma$ observed expanding $A$:

$$
D \;=\; \sum_{\gamma} \mathrm{Count}_{\mathcal{B}}(A \rightarrow \gamma)
$$

**Step 3 — Form the MLE ratio.**

$$
P_{\mathrm{ML}}(A \rightarrow \beta) \;=\; \frac{\mathrm{Count}_{\mathcal{B}}(A \rightarrow \beta)}{\displaystyle \sum_{\gamma} \mathrm{Count}_{\mathcal{B}}(A \rightarrow \gamma)}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify normalization (sanity check).**

$$
\sum_{\beta} P_{\mathrm{ML}}(A \rightarrow \beta) \;=\; \frac{\sum_{\beta}\mathrm{Count}_{\mathcal{B}}(A \rightarrow \beta)}{\sum_{\gamma} \mathrm{Count}_{\mathcal{B}}(A \rightarrow \gamma)} \;=\; 1
$$

> Textual explanation: the numerator and denominator collapse to identical sums because they both iterate over **all** observed right-hand sides of $A$ — so the result is exactly **1**, confirming a valid probability distribution.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Probability of an Entire Parse Tree

A constituency tree $T$ is the product of all local rule probabilities (a standard PCFG independence assumption: each node expands independently given its parent).

$$
P(T) \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_{\mathrm{ML}}(r_i)
$$

**Walk-through example** — consider the bracketed tree
`(S (NP (DT The) (NN cat)) (VP (VBZ sits)))`.

The local rules $r_i$ extracted are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_1 &: S \rightarrow \mathrm{NP}\ \mathrm{VP} \\
r_2 &: \mathrm{NP} \rightarrow \mathrm{DT}\ \mathrm{NN} \\
r_3 &: \mathrm{VP} \rightarrow \mathrm{VBZ} \\
r_4 &: \mathrm{DT} \rightarrow \text{The} \\
r_5 &: \mathrm{NN} \rightarrow \text{cat} \\
r_6 &: \mathrm{VBZ} \rightarrow \text{sits}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the empirically estimated probabilities were $0.30, 0.50, 0.45, 0.20, 0.05, 0.10$, then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T) &\;=\; 0.30 \times 0.50 \times 0.45 \times 0.20 \times 0.05 \times 0.10 \\
&\;=\; 6.75 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Textual explanation: products of numbers in $(0,1)$ shrink rapidly; this is why **log-space** arithmetic is used in real parsers, i.e., $\log P(T) = \sum_i \log P(r_i)$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inter-Annotator Agreement (Cohen's κ)

Treebank quality is measured by agreement. For two annotators and $K$ categories:

$$
\kappa \;=\; \frac{P_o - P_e}{1 - P_e}
$$

where $P_o = \sum_{k} p_{kk}$ is observed agreement, and $P_e = \sum_{k} p_{k\cdot}\,p_{\cdot k}$ is chance agreement.

**Worked mini-example** — 100 constituents, two annotators A and B, three labels (NP, VP, PP).

| | A=NP | A=VP | A=PP | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| B=NP | 50 | 5 | 5 | 60 |
| B=VP | 4 | 25 | 1 | 30 |
| B=PP | 1 | 0 | 9 | 10 |
| **Total** | 55 | 30 | 15 | 100 |

**Step 1 — Observed agreement.**

$$
P_o \;=\; \frac{50 + 25 + 9}{100} \;=\; 0.84
$$

**Step 2 — Marginal proportions.**

$$
p_{\mathrm{NP}\cdot} = 0.60,\quad p_{\cdot\mathrm{NP}} = 0.55 \\
p_{\mathrm{VP}\cdot} = 0.30,\quad p_{\cdot\mathrm{VP}} = 0.30 \\
p_{\mathrm{PP}\cdot} = 0.10,\quad p_{\cdot\mathrm{PP}} = 0.15
$$

**Step 3 — Expected agreement by chance.**

$$
P_e \;=\; (0.60)(0.55) + (0.30)(0.30) + (0.10)(0.15) \\
\;=\; 0.330 + 0.090 + 0.015 \;=\; 0.435
$$

**Step 4 — Compute κ.**

$$
\kappa \;=\; \frac{0.84 - 0.435}{1 - 0.435} \;=\; \frac{0.405}{0.565} \;\approx\; 0.717
$$

> Textual explanation: a κ of **0.72** indicates **substantial agreement** (Landis & Koch scale), acceptable for a treebank. Anything below 0.67 triggers guideline revision.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Treebank vs. Related Corpora

| Property | Raw Corpus | POS-Tagged Corpus | **Treebank** | Semantic Role Treebank (e.g., PropBank) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tokens | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| POS tags | ✘ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Syntactic constituents | ✘ | ✘ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Predicate–argument structure | ✘ | ✘ | partial | ✔ |
| Used to train | LMs | taggers | **statistical parsers** | SRL systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Treebank ≠ Corpus.** The defining property is **syntactic structure** stored as a parse tree per sentence.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Treebank Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Raw Text Corpus] --> A1[Sentence Segmentation and Tokenization]
    A1 --> A2[Automatic POS Tagging]
    A2 --> A3[Linguistic Pre-Bracketing]
    A3 --> A4[Annotator 1 Manual Parse]
    A3 --> A5[Annotator 2 Manual Parse]
    A4 --> A6[Compare and Compute Kappa]
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7{Agreement Above Threshold?}
    A7 -- No --> A8[Adjudication by Senior Linguist]
    A8 --> A9[Update Annotation Guidelines]
    A9 --> A3
    A7 -- Yes --> A10[Validated Treebank]
    A10 --> A11[Release in PTB or CoNLL-U Format]
    A11 --> A12[Train PCFG and Statistical Parser]
```

> Textual explanation: the loop on the right (A7 → A8 → A9 → A3) implements the **iterative refinement** of annotation guidelines used in projects like the Penn Treebank and Universal Dependencies.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Phrase-Structure Tree of a PTB Sentence

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[S] --> NP1[NP]
    S1 --> VP1[VP]
    NP1 --> DT1[DT: The]
    NP1 --> JJ1[JJ: quick]
    NP1 --> NN1[NN: brown]
    NP1 --> NN2[NN: fox]
    VP1 --> VBZ1[VBZ: jumps]
    VP1 --> ADVP1[ADVP]
    VP1 --> PP1[PP]
    ADVP1 --> RB1[RB: quickly]
    PP1 --> IN1[IN: over]
    PP1 --> NP2[NP]
    NP2 --> DT2[DT: the]
    NP2 --> NN3[NN: lazy]
    NP2 --> NN4[NN: dog]
```

> Textual explanation: each labeled node is one record in the treebank. The 13 internal productions recorded here (e.g., `NP → DT JJ NN NN`, `PP → IN NP`) are the units over which PCFG probabilities are computed.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Dependency View of the Same Sentence

```mermaid
graph TD
    JUMPS[jumps / VBZ] --> QUICK[quick / JJ]
    JUMPS --> BROWN[brown / NN]
    JUMPS --> FOX[fox / NN]
    JUMPS --> RB[quickly / RB]
    JUMPS --> OVER[over / IN]
    FOX --> THE1[the / DET]
    BROWN --> THE1
    FOX --> DOG[dog / NN]
    DOG --> LAZY[lazy / AMOD]
    DOG --> THE2[the / DET]
    OVER --> DOG
    RB --> ADVMOD[ADVMOD relation]
    FOX --> NSUBJ[NSUBJ relation]
```

> Textual explanation: dependency view flattens the hierarchy — **fox** is the head of the noun phrase, and `fox → jumps` carries the `nsubj` relation. The same lexical data thus supports **two treebank formats** (constituency + dependency).

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture: Treebank → Parser

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT LAYER]
        TXT[Raw Sentences]
    end
    subgraph TB[TREEBANK LAYER]
        PTB[Penn Treebank Annotations]
        RULES[Extracted CFG Productions]
        PROBS[PCFG Probability Tables]
    end
    subgraph ENGINE[PARSER LAYER]
        CKY[CKY Chart Parser]
        INSIDE[Inside-Outside Re-estimation]
        VITERBI[Viterbi Best-Parse Selection]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT LAYER]
        PARSE[Most Likely Parse Tree]
        EVAL[Evaluation against Gold Treebank]
    end
    TXT --> PTB
    PTB --> RULES
    RULES --> PROBS
    PROBS --> CKY
    CKY --> VITERBI
    PROBS --> INSIDE
    INSIDE --> PROBS
    VITERBI --> PARSE
    PARSE --> EVAL
    PTB --> EVAL
```

> Textual explanation: this is the canonical *empirical linguistics loop* — the treebank trains a probabilistic grammar, the grammar powers a parser, and the treebank evaluates the parser. Removing any one of these three components breaks modern statistical NLP.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define a Treebank. List any two well-known treebanks and their distinguishing features.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A treebank is a syntactically annotated text corpus in which every sentence is paired with a parse tree (constituency or dependency) showing the underlying grammatical structure.
- **Example 1 (1 mark):** *Penn Treebank (PTB)* — ~1 million words of Wall Street Journal, manually annotated with **phrase-structure** trees using the Penn POS tagset of 45 tags.
- **Example 2 (1 mark):** *Universal Dependencies (UD)* — a cross-lingual project with **dependency** annotations covering 100+ languages in a unified CoNLL-U format.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest Paper]**
*Differentiate between a Phrase-Structure treebank and a Dependency treebank with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Phrase-Structure Treebank | Dependency Treebank |
|---|---|---|
| Node content | Words **and** non-terminal constituents (NP, VP) | Words **only** |
| Edge meaning | Dominance / embedding | Head–dependent grammatical relation |
| Example | Penn Treebank | Universal Dependencies / Prague PDT |
| Parse algorithm used | CKY, Earley | Transition-based, graph-based parsers |

(1 mark for the distinction, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for the application/algorithm link.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> KTU 2024 pattern: **Module 2 internal choice.** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Treebank Construction & Annotation Quality*

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the step-by-step process of constructing a phrase-structure treebank, with reference to the Penn Treebank project.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Corpus selection (1 m):** Penn chose the **1989 Wall Street Journal** corpus (≈ 1 M words) to obtain well-edited American English, supplemented with Brown and ATIS for genre diversity.
2. **Pre-processing (1 m):** Tokenization and sentence segmentation; handling of punctuation, hyphenation, and possessives.
3. **POS tagging (1 m):** An automatic tagger (initially the CLAWS tagger adapted to Penn) was used; ~3% of tokens were later manually corrected. The 45-tag Penn tagset (`NN`, `VBZ`, …) was defined.
4. **Manual bracketing (2 m):** Trained annotators inserted constituent labels into a flat text. Discontinuous constituents were marked with `-NONE-` traces (e.g., wh-movement, topicalization) following the **Trace Theory** annotation scheme of *Bies et al., 1995*.
5. **Function tags (1 m):** Grammatical roles (SBJ, OBJ, TMP, LOC) were added on top of phrase labels.
6. **Release (1 m):** Distributed as bracketed `.mrg` files with sections 02–24 for **training** and section 00/23/24 for **testing** — a split still used by every PTB-trained parser.

**(b) [7 marks]** How is inter-annotator agreement measured? Why is it critical for treebank reliability?

**Model Solution:**

- **Definition (2 m):** Inter-annotator agreement (IAA) quantifies how consistently two (or more) annotators apply the same labels. The standard metric is **Cohen's κ**, given by
$$
\kappa = \frac{P_o - P_e}{1 - P_e}
$$
where $P_o$ is the observed proportion of agreement and $P_e$ is the proportion expected by chance.
- **Worked illustration (2 m):** In a sample where $P_o = 0.84$ and $P_e = 0.435$, we get $\kappa \approx 0.72$, indicating *substantial* agreement (Landis & Koch scale). PTB target was $\kappa \geq 0.90$ for accept-and-release, with disagreements adjudicated by senior linguists.
- **Why it matters (3 m):**
  1. **Statistical validity:** Parsers learn from observed counts — inconsistent labels inject noise that lowers parse accuracy.
  2. **Reproducibility:** A κ-guaranteed treebank allows fair benchmarking across labs.
  3. **Downstream safety:** Errors in treebanks propagate into clinical/legal NLP applications, where mis-parsing can be high-stakes.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Treebanks, PCFGs & Statistical Parsing*

**(a) [7 marks]** Show how a Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG) is estimated from a treebank. Derive the maximum-likelihood formula and compute the rule probabilities for a toy treebank.

**Model Solution:**

A PCFG attaches a probability to every CFG production. Given a treebank $\mathcal{B}$, the **maximum-likelihood estimate** for any production $A \rightarrow \beta$ is the fraction of times that production appears among all expansions of the non-terminal $A$:

$$
P_{\mathrm{ML}}(A \rightarrow \beta) = \frac{\mathrm{Count}_{\mathcal{B}}(A \rightarrow \beta)}{\displaystyle \sum_{\gamma} \mathrm{Count}_{\mathcal{B}}(A \rightarrow \gamma)}
$$

**Toy treebank (3 m):** Suppose $\mathcal{B}$ contains 5 sentences yielding these counts for the non-terminal `VP`:

| Production | Count |
|---|---|
| `VP → VBZ` | 1 |
| `VP → VBZ NP` | 3 |
| `VP → VBZ NP PP` | 1 |

Then the MLE probabilities are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\mathrm{VP} \rightarrow \mathrm{VBZ}) &= \frac{1}{5} = 0.20 \\
P(\mathrm{VP} \rightarrow \mathrm{VBZ\ NP}) &= \frac{3}{5} = 0.60 \\
P(\mathrm{VP} \rightarrow \mathrm{VBZ\ NP\ PP}) &= \frac{1}{5} = 0.20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification (1 m):** $0.20 + 0.60 + 0.20 = 1$ ✔ — valid distribution.

**Independence assumption (1 m):** PCFG assumes each local rule is independent of its siblings given the parent. This makes the probability of a whole tree the simple product $P(T) = \prod_i P(r_i)$, but it also limits accuracy — modern parsers add lexicalization (Collins), parent-annotation (Johnson), or neural conditioning.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain how the **CKY algorithm** uses a treebank-induced PCFG to find the most likely parse of a new sentence. Why are treebanks necessary for CKY-style parsing?

**Model Solution:**

- **Background (1 m):** The **Cocke–Kasami–Younger (CKY)** algorithm parses a sentence of length $n$ in $O(n^3 \cdot \vert G \vert)$ time using a Chomsky Normal Form grammar $G$. It fills a triangular chart of sub-problems.
- **Chart entries (2 m):** A cell $C[i,j]$ stores the non-terminal $A$ such that $A \stackrel{*}{\Rightarrow} w_i \ldots w_j$ is most probable, with back-pointers to the two children that achieved this maximum.
- **Recursion (2 m):** For span length $\ell = 2 \ldots n$ and start position $i$:
$$
C[i,i+\ell] \;=\; \arg\max_{A \rightarrow B\,C}\; P_{\mathrm{ML}}(A \rightarrow B\,C) \cdot C_B[i,k] \cdot C_C[k, i+\ell]
$$
The maximum is taken over every split point $k$ in $(i, i+\ell)$. Back-pointers are recorded so the highest-probability parse tree can be reconstructed at the end.
- **Why treebanks are necessary (2 m):** CKY needs a *complete* set of rule probabilities — for thousands of productions. Hand-writing these is infeasible. Treebanks provide empirical counts that yield a PCFG, which is the **only** realistic source of such large probabilistic grammars. Without a treebank, CKY has nothing to score with.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students **omit the denominator** when writing the PCFG formula — you will lose 2 marks instantly.
> - Students often confuse the **Penn Treebank (corpus)** with **Penn POS tagset (45 tags)** — keep them separate.
> - In CKY questions, you MUST specify **Chomsky Normal Form**; otherwise the recurrence does not hold and you will be marked down.
> - Cohen's κ is **not** the same as raw agreement %; the chance-correction in the denominator matters and is the most-skipped step in answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 📌 **Treebank = Parsed Corpus.** The defining feature is a parse tree per sentence.
- 📌 **Penn Treebank:** ~1 M words, WSJ, **phrase-structure**, 45 POS tags, sections 02–24 train, 23/24 test.
- 📌 **Universal Dependencies:** cross-lingual, **dependency**, CoNLL-U format.
- 📌 **Two tree types:** Constituency (NP, VP nodes) vs. Dependency (word-to-word with relations).
- 📌 **PCFG MLE formula:** $P(A \rightarrow \beta) = \mathrm{Count}(A \rightarrow \beta) \big/ \sum_{\gamma} \mathrm{Count}(A \rightarrow \gamma)$.
- 📌 **Tree probability:** product of all local rule probabilities, often computed in log-space.
- 📌 **CKY** requires **Chomsky Normal Form**; runs in $O(n^3)$ time.
- 📌 **Inter-annotator agreement:** Cohen's $\kappa$, target $\geq 0.90$.
- 📌 **Annotation pipeline:** Tokenize → POS → Pre-bracket → Manual parse → Adjudicate → Release.
- 📌 **Engineering impact:** Statistical parsers, machine translation, SRL, biomedical NLP — all depend on treebanks.
- 📌 **Key exam trap:** Treebank ≠ Grammar; a treebank *induces* a grammar, it does not *prescribe* one.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Logical Representations of Sentence Meaning
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT741 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Logical Representations of Sentence Meaning

# 🧠 Logical Representations of Sentence Meaning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic directly satisfies the Course Outcome **CO3** under Module 3 — *"Apply formal models to represent and reason about the meaning of natural language sentences."* It is a high-weightage, **frequently asked** 14-mark area in University ESE.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a "Logical Representation" of Meaning?

A **Logical Representation of Sentence Meaning** is a formal, symbolic encoding of the *propositional content* of a natural language sentence using the syntax and semantics of a mathematical logic system — most commonly **First-Order Predicate Logic (FOPL)**. It converts ambiguous, fuzzy English (or Malayalam) into an unambiguous, machine-checkable formula so that a computer can perform *inference* (deduction, entailment checking, contradiction detection) over meaning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (Jurafsky & Martin, 2023 ed., Ch. 17-aligned terminology):**
> *"A meaning representation language is a formal language that is designed specifically to represent the meaning of natural language sentences. It must be (i) **verifiable** — capable of being checked against the world, (ii) **unambiguous** — only one interpretation per formula, (iii) **canonical form** — different surface forms with same meaning share one representation, and (iv) **expressive** — covers a wide range of natural language constructions."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Passport" 🛂

Imagine English is spoken in a village where every person interprets every sentence slightly differently (idioms, sarcasm, context). A *logical representation* acts like converting every spoken sentence into a **passport** — a strict, standardized document with fields like:
- **Who** (the subject),
- **Did What** (the predicate),
- **To Whom** (the object),
- **When, Where, How** (modifiers).

The computer at immigration (the inference engine) only reads passports. It doesn't care about village slang — it just needs the standardized form to stamp *ENTRY* or *REJECTION* based on logical rules.

### 1.3 Why Not Just Use English?

| Problem with English | Problem it Causes for NLP |
|---|---|
| **Lexical Ambiguity** ("bank" = river or money?) | Computer cannot pick the right sense |
| **Syntactic Ambiguity** ("I saw the man with the telescope") | Two parse trees → two meanings |
| **Semantic Ambiguity** ("Every student read a book") | Scope ambiguity — same student? |
| **No Inference Mechanism** | Cannot prove "Socrates is mortal" from "All men are mortal" |
| **No Verifiability** | Cannot be matched to world states (Tarski-style) |

### 1.4 Types of Logical Representation Languages

There are **three principal families** studied under this KTU module:

1. **Propositional Logic (PL)** — atoms joined by ∧, ∨, ¬, →, ↔. No internal structure for nouns/verbs.
2. **First-Order Predicate Logic (FOPL)** — adds **variables, predicates, quantifiers (∀, ∃)**, and **functions**. Most commonly used.
3. **Lambda Calculus / λ-notation** — a *function-application* notation used in compositional semantics (Heim & Kratzer style).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Distinction:**
> - **Propositional Logic** treats whole sentences as atomic symbols → loses internal structure of "All dogs are mammals."
> - **First-Order Logic** breaks sentences into **predicates, arguments, and quantifiers** → can represent "All dogs are mammals" as `∀x (Dog(x) → Mammal(x))`.

### 1.5 The Meaning Representation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Natural Language Sentence] --> B[Lexical Analysis]
    B --> C[Syntactic Parsing]
    C --> D[Semantic Interpretation]
    D --> E[Logical Form LF]
    E --> F[Inference / Reasoning]
    F --> G[Entailment / Contradiction / Truth Value]
```

**Figure 1.1:** Logical meaning representation sits at stage *E (Logical Form)* — the bridge between linguistic structure and machine reasoning.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Syntax of First-Order Predicate Logic (FOPL)

FOPL has a **rigorously defined grammar**. Every well-formed formula (wff) is built from a finite alphabet using production rules.

**Atomic Symbols:**
- **Constants:** `Mary, John, Paris, 5` — refer to specific entities.
- **Variables:** `x, y, z` — range over entities.
- **Predicates:** `Loves(x, y)`, `Man(x)`, `Mortal(x)` — denote properties or relations.
- **Functions:** `fatherOf(x)`, `leftLegOf(x)` — map entities to entities.
- **Connectives:** `¬, ∧, ∨, →, ↔`
- **Quantifiers:** `∀` (universal / "for all"), `∃` (existential / "there exists")
- **Punctuation:** `( ) ,`

**Production Rules (Backus–Naur form):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{S} &\rightarrow \text{S} \;\wedge\; \text{S} \mid \text{S} \;\vee\; \text{S} \mid \text{S} \;\to\; \text{S} \mid \neg\text{S} \\
\text{S} &\rightarrow \forall \text{x} \; \text{S} \mid \exists \text{x} \; \text{S} \mid \text{Predicate}(\text{TermList}) \\
\text{Term} &\rightarrow \text{Constant} \mid \text{Variable} \mid \text{Function}(\text{TermList})
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.2 Semantics — Model-Theoretic Interpretation

A **Model** $M = \langle D, I \rangle$ where:
- $D$ = **Domain** of discourse (set of all entities being talked about)
- $I$ = **Interpretation Function** mapping:
  - Each constant $c \mapsto$ an element in $D$
  - Each $n$-ary predicate $P \mapsto$ an $n$-ary relation over $D$
  - Each function $f \mapsto$ a function from $D^n \to D$

> [!NOTE]
> **Truth-Conditional Semantics (Tarski, 1935):** A sentence is **true** in a model $M$ iff the world state satisfies the formula. Meaning *IS* its truth conditions.

### 2.3 Quantifier Semantics (Crucial for KTU)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\forall x \; \phi(x) \;&\text{ is TRUE iff } \phi(d) \text{ is TRUE for every } d \in D \\
\exists x \; \phi(x) \;&\text{ is TRUE iff } \phi(d) \text{ is TRUE for at least one } d \in D
\end{aligned}
$$

**De Morgan Duality (highly testable):**
$$
\neg \forall x \; \phi(x) \;\equiv\; \exists x \; \neg\phi(x)
$$
$$
\neg \exists x \; \phi(x) \;\equiv\; \forall x \; \neg\phi(x)
$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Notation / Rule | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Atomic formula | $P(t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n)$ | $\text{Loves}(\text{Mary}, \text{John})$ |
| 2 | Negation | $\neg \phi$ | $\neg \text{Rainy}$ |
| 3 | Conjunction | $\phi \wedge \psi$ | $\text{Man}(x) \wedge \text{Mortal}(x)$ |
| 4 | Disjunction | $\phi \vee \psi$ | $\text{Sunny} \vee \text{Rainy}$ |
| 5 | Implication | $\phi \to \psi$ | $\text{Man}(x) \to \text{Mortal}(x)$ |
| 6 | Universal | $\forall x \; \phi(x)$ | $\forall x \;\text{Man}(x) \to \text{Mortal}(x)$ |
| 7 | Existential | $\exists x \; \phi(x)$ | $\exists x \;\text{Likes}(\text{Mary}, x)$ |
| 8 | Equality | $t_1 \equiv t_2$ | $\text{fatherOf}(\text{John}) \equiv \text{William}$ |
| 9 | Lambda abstraction | $\lambda x . \phi$ | $\lambda x . \text{Loves}(x, \text{Mary})$ |
| 10 | Lambda application | $(\lambda x . \phi)(a) \;\Rightarrow\; \phi[a/x]$ | $(\lambda x . \text{Loves}(x, M))(J) \Rightarrow \text{Loves}(J, M)$ |
| 11 | Truth value | $M \models \phi$ | $M \models \text{Rainy}$ (model $M$ satisfies "Rainy") |
| 12 | Scope of quantifier | Region of formula governed by $\forall/\exists$ | $\exists x \; (P(x) \wedge Q(x))$ — narrow scope |
| 13 | Wide vs narrow scope | $\exists x (P(x) \wedge \forall y Q(y))$ | Wide ∃, narrow ∀ |
| 14 | Inference rule: Modus Ponens | $P, \; P \to Q \;\therefore Q$ | If "Rain" and "Rain → Wet" → "Wet" |
| 15 | De Morgan | $\neg(\phi \wedge \psi) \equiv \neg\phi \vee \neg\psi$ | $\neg(P \wedge Q) \equiv \neg P \vee \neg Q$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Mark-Grabber:** In every exam answer, **always state the model $M = \langle D, I \rangle$ explicitly** before evaluating truth. Examiners award the 2 "interpretation" marks only when this is shown.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Role of Logical Representation |
|---|---|
| **Question Answering (IBM Watson, Google Search)** | FOL parses questions; query becomes `∃x P(x)` searched over KB |
| **Legal & Medical Text Mining** | Statute = `∀x (Patient(x) ∧ HasCancer(x) → Eligible(x))` |
| **Database Query (SQL via NL)** | "Find all employees earning > 50K" → `∀x (Emp(x) ∧ Salary(x) > 50000)` |
| **Knowledge Graph Reasoning** | RDF triples lifted to FOPL for inference (e.g., Freebase, Wikidata) |
| **Robotics / Nao, Pepper** | "Bring me the red cup" → `∃x Red(x) ∧ Cup(x) ∧ On(x, table)` |
