> **Coreference Resolution (CR)** is the NLP task of identifying all expressions (called *mentions*) in a text that refer to the same real-world entity, and grouping them into equivalence classes called **coreference chains** (or *coreference clusters*).

In the KTU 2024 scheme terminology, a **mention** is any noun phrase (NP), named entity, pronoun, or nominal expression that potentially refers to an entity. When two or more mentions refer to the same entity, they are said to **co-refer**. The system that performs this task is called a **Coreference Resolution System**.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (Aligned with KTU 2024 Syllabus)

- **Antecedent**: The mention that is referred to (usually appears earlier in text).
- **Anaphor / Anaphoric Expression**: The referring mention (e.g., a pronoun).
- **Coreferring Mentions**: Mentions referring to the same entity (e.g., *Elon Musk ... he ... the CEO ... Musk*).
- **Coreference Chain / Cluster**: The full set of mentions referring to a single entity.
- **Singletons**: Mentions that have no other co-referring expression in the document.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Detective Linking Clues" Metaphor**
>
> Imagine reading a news article: *"Dr. Priya won the National Science Award. She thanked her team. The professor said the recognition was for collective effort."* As a human, you instantly understand that **Dr. Priya = She = The professor**. This mental "linking of identity clues" is exactly what a coreference resolution system does automatically. It is the NLP equivalent of **stitching scattered identity threads** into one unified entity profile.

### 1.4 Why It Matters in Engineering Applications

Coreference resolution is a critical **upstream preprocessing module** for:

- **Machine Translation** (gender/case agreement across languages)
- **Question Answering Systems** (resolving *Who did X?* references)
- **Text Summarization** (avoiding repetition of entity names)
- **Dialogue Systems / Chatbots** (maintaining conversational context)
- **Information Extraction** (building entity knowledge graphs)

### 1.5 Coreference vs. Anaphora — A Subtle Distinction

| Aspect | Anaphora | Coreference |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Linguistic relation between two items | Set-based grouping of all mentions |
| Order | Requires antecedent **before** anaphor | Order-independent |
| Linguistic focus | Pronoun/lexical reference | Entity identity across whole text |
| Example | *Ram... he* | *Ram ... he ... the boy ... his father* (all in one cluster) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All anaphoric relations are coreferential, but not all coreferential relations are anaphoric.** KTU questions often test this distinction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Coreference Models

Coreference resolution systems are broadly classified into four paradigms:

#### A. Rule-Based / Heuristic Approaches (Hobbs Algorithm Era)
- Use handcrafted linguistic rules (gender agreement, number agreement, syntactic binding).
- **Hobbs' Algorithm (1978)**: Traverses the syntactic tree of the previous sentence to find the antecedent using a breadth-first left-to-right search.
- **Limitations**: Brittle, low recall, not data-adaptive.

#### B. Mention-Pair Models
- Formulate CR as a binary classification problem: for every pair of mentions $(m_i, m_j)$, predict whether they corefer.
- **Independent classification assumption** is the major weakness — pairwise decisions can be globally inconsistent.

#### C. Entity-Mention (Clustering) Models
- Greedily build coreference clusters. For each new mention, decide whether it joins an existing cluster or starts a new one.
- Eliminates pairwise inconsistency through incremental clustering.

#### D. End-to-End Neural Models (State-of-the-Art)
- Span-based representations using deep learning (BERT, SpanBERT).
- Example: **Lee et al. (2017, 2018)** — first end-to-end neural CR achieving near-human performance on OntoNotes.
- Models consider **all spans of text** in one pass, scoring each span and pairwise links jointly.

### 2.2 The Two-Stage Neural Pipeline

Modern systems follow a **two-stage architecture**:

1. **Stage 1 — Span Enumeration**: Generate all possible spans up to a maximum length $L$ from the document.
2. **Stage 2 — Span Scoring**: For each span $s_i$, compute a unary score $f(s_i)$ indicating whether it is a valid mention.
3. **Stage 3 — Pairwise Linking**: For every pair $(s_i, s_j)$ with $s_i$ before $s_j$, compute a binary coreference score $g(s_i, s_j)$.
4. **Stage 4 — Cluster Inference**: Use the scores to find the highest-scoring coreference partition via a learned antecedent distribution.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Expression |
|---|---|---|
| $s_i, s_j$ | Spans $i$ and $j$ in document | $s_i = (start_i, end_i)$ |
| $f(s_i)$ | Unary mention score | $f(s_i) \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| $g(s_i, s_j)$ | Pairwise coreference score | $g(s_i, s_j) \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| $\alpha_{j}$ | Antecedent distribution for $s_j$ | $\alpha_j \in [0, 1]$ |
| Antecedent rule | Sum over all candidates | $\sum_{y_j \in Y(j)} \alpha_j(y_j) = 1$ |
| $\epsilon$ | Dummy antecedent (no link) | $\alpha_j(\epsilon) + \sum_{y_j \neq \epsilon} \alpha_j(y_j) = 1$ |
| Loss | Marginal log-likelihood | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{j} \log \sum_{y_j \in \text{GOLD}(j)} \alpha_j(y_j)$ |
| MUC $F_1$ | Evaluation metric | $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot R \cdot P}{R + P}$ |
| $B^3$ | Evaluation metric | $F_1 = \sum_{i} w_i \cdot F_1(i)$ |
| $\text{CEAF}_\phi$ | Entity-based metric | $\phi_4$ similarity-based |
| CoNLL Avg | Gold-standard CR benchmark | Average of MUC + $B^3$ + $\text{CEAF}_\phi$ |

> [!NOTE]
> For all table expressions involving sizes or sets, the **vertical bar** notation is written using $\vert$ to avoid markdown parsing issues, e.g., the set cardinality $\vert Y \vert$.

### 2.4 Evaluation Metrics — Deep Dive

Three primary metrics, all based on the **CoNLL shared task** standard:

1. **MUC (Mention-based)**: Counts minimum number of link operations to align system and gold clusters.
2. **$B^3$ (Bag-of-words per mention)**: Weighted average per-mention $F_1$.
3. **CEAF-$\phi_4$ (Entity-aware)**: Uses $\phi_4$ similarity between system and gold entities.

The **CoNLL $F_1$** (gold-standard) is the unweighted mean of all three.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare NLP**: Linking *"the patient ... he ... Mr. Sharma ... the diabetic"* across clinical notes.
- **Legal AI**: Resolving *"the accused ... he ... the appellant"* across judgments.
- **Search Engines**: Improving document understanding for entity-centric queries.
- **Robotics/Dialogue**: Maintaining consistent references in multi-turn conversations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Mathematical Foundation of the End-to-End Neural CR Model

We will derive the **Lee et al. (2017) end-to-end coreference resolution objective** step by step, as this is the most likely KTU 14-mark derivation.

#### Step 1 — Span Enumeration

Given a document $D$ with $T$ tokens, generate the set $\mathcal{S}$ of all valid spans:

$$
\mathcal{S} = \{ s_i \mid s_i = (start, end),\ 1 \leq start \leq end \leq T,\ \text{length}(s_i) \leq L_{\max} \}
$$

This yields $\mathcal{O}(T^2)$ candidate spans.

#### Step 2 — Span Representation (using BERT-style encoder)

Each span $s_i$ is represented by concatenating three embeddings:

$$
\mathbf{x}_i = [\mathbf{h}_{\text{start}};\ \mathbf{h}_{\text{end}};\ \hat{\mathbf{h}}_i]
$$

where $\hat{\mathbf{h}}_i$ is a self-attention weighted sum over tokens in $s_i$:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{h}}_i = \sum_{t = \text{start}}^{\text{end}} \alpha_t \cdot \mathbf{h}_t
$$

The attention weight $\alpha_t$ is computed via a feed-forward network $\text{FFNN}_{\alpha}$.

#### Step 3 — Mention Score (Unary)

A binary indicator $\mathbf{I}(s_i)$ marks whether span $s_i$ is a mention. The score is:

$$
f(s_i) = \mathbf{w}_m^\top \cdot \text{FFNN}_m(\mathbf{x}_i)
$$

Mention probability:

$$
P(\mathbf{I}(s_i) = 1) = \sigma(f(s_i))
$$

#### Step 4 — Pairwise Coreference Score

For $s_i$ and $s_j$ (with $i < j$), the coreference compatibility score is:

$$
g(s_i, s_j) = \mathbf{w}_c^\top \cdot \text{FFNN}_c([\mathbf{x}_i;\ \mathbf{x}_j;\ \mathbf{x}_i \circ \mathbf{x}_j;\ \boldsymbol{\phi}(i,j)])
$$

where:

- $\mathbf{x}_i \circ \mathbf{x}_j$ is element-wise product
- $\boldsymbol{\phi}(i,j)$ is a feature vector encoding speaker, distance, and genre metadata.

#### Step 5 — Antecedent Distribution (Softmax over antecedents)

For each span $s_j$, compute the probability over its possible antecedents $Y(j)$ (including the dummy $\epsilon$):

$$
\alpha_j(y_j) = \frac{\exp(g(y_j, s_j))}{\sum_{y' \in Y(j) \cup \{\epsilon\}} \exp(g(y', s_j))}
$$

Explicitly expanded:

$$
\alpha_j(y_j) =
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{\exp(g(y_j, s_j))}{\exp(f(s_j)) + \sum_{y' \in Y(j)} \exp(g(y', s_j))} & \text{if } y_j \in Y(j) \\[10pt]
\dfrac{\exp(f(s_j))}{\exp(f(s_j)) + \sum_{y' \in Y(j)} \exp(g(y', s_j))} & \text{if } y_j = \epsilon
\end{cases}
$$

> **Logic**: $f(s_j)$ plays the role of the dummy score, ensuring $\alpha_j$ is a valid probability distribution summing to **1**.

#### Step 6 — Marginal Log-Likelihood Objective

To handle multiple gold antecedents (a mention may legitimately co-refer with several mentions), the loss marginalizes over all valid gold assignments:

$$
\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{j=1}^{\vert \mathcal{S} \vert} \log \sum_{y_j \in \text{GOLD}(j)} \alpha_j(y_j)
$$

where $\text{GOLD}(j)$ is the set of correct antecedents for span $s_j$.

#### Step 7 — Inference via Highest-Scoring Antecedent

At test time, the predicted antecedent $\hat{y}_j$ is:

$$
\hat{y}_j = \underset{y_j \in Y(j) \cup \{\epsilon\}}{\arg\max}\ \alpha_j(y_j)
$$

Then clusters are built by transitive closure: if $\hat{y}_j = s_i$, then $s_j$ joins the cluster of $s_i$.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Mention-Pair Classifier

Suppose we have 4 mentions: $m_1 =$ *"Dr. Priya"*, $m_2 =$ *"She"*, $m_3 =$ *"the professor"*, $m_4 =$ *"the team"*.

Pairwise classifier scores $g(m_i, m_j) \in [0, 1]$ (assume threshold $\tau = 0.5$):

| Pair $(m_i, m_j)$ | Score | Corefer? |
|---|---|---|
| $(m_1, m_2)$ | 0.92 | ✓ |
| $(m_1, m_3)$ | 0.88 | ✓ |
| $(m_2, m_3)$ | 0.71 | ✓ |
| $(m_1, m_4)$ | 0.21 | ✗ |
| $(m_2, m_4)$ | 0.18 | ✗ |

**Cluster inference (transitive closure)**:

$$
\text{Cluster}_1 = \{m_1, m_2, m_3\}
$$

$$
\text{Cluster}_2 = \{m_4\}
$$

Singleton $m_4$ is correctly excluded.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Comparison

| Method | Recall | Precision | Complexity | Data Need |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hobbs Rule-Based | Low | High | $O(n)$ syntactic | None |
| Mention-Pair SVM | Medium | Medium | $O(n^2)$ | Moderate |
| Bell-tree Clustering | Medium-High | Medium | $O(n^2)$ | Moderate |
| Neural Span Model | High | High | $O(T^2)$ | Large |
| GPT-style LLM | High | High | Inference-time | Zero-shot capable |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an End-to-End Neural CR System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Document Input] --> B[Tokenization Layer]
    B --> C[BERT or SpanBERT Encoder]
    C --> D[Span Enumeration Module]
    D --> E[Span Representation Builder]
    E --> F[Mention Scoring Head]
    E --> G[Pairwise Coreference Scorer]
    F --> H[Cluster Inference Engine]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Coreference Chains Output]
    I --> J[Downstream NLP Applications]
```

**Description**: Each block performs a distinct transformation — encoder produces contextual token embeddings; span enumeration generates $\mathcal{O}(T^2)$ candidate spans; scoring heads compute unary and pairwise scores; cluster inference applies the highest-scoring antecedent rule.

### 4.2 Detailed Mention-Pair Classification Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Sentence] --> B[POS Tagging and NER]
    B --> C[Mention Detection]
    C --> D[Feature Extraction]
    D --> D1[Gender Agreement]
    D --> D2[Number Agreement]
    D --> D3[Syntactic Position]
    D --> D4[Semantic Compatibility]
    D1 --> E[Pairwise Classifier SVM or NN]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Coreference Decision Matrix]
    F --> G[Cluster Building]
```

### 4.3 Antecedent Resolution Flow (Per Span)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Span s_j Detected] --> Q1{Is s_j a valid mention?}
    Q1 -- No --> Skip[Discard]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Score[Compute g(s_i, s_j) for all earlier spans]
    Score --> Softmax[Apply Softmax over candidates]
    Softmax --> ArgMax[Select arg max antecedent]
    ArgMax --> Decision{Is antecedent = epsilon?}
    Decision -- Yes --> NewCluster[Start New Cluster]
    Decision -- No --> Merge[Merge with antecedent cluster]
    NewCluster --> End[Output Updated Chains]
    Merge --> End
```

### 4.4 Evaluation Pipeline (CoNLL Metrics)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G[Gold Clusters] --> M[MUC Computation]
    G --> B3[B cubed Metric]
    G --> CEAF[CEAF phi4]
    SYS[System Clusters] --> M
    SYS --> B3
    SYS --> CEAF
    M --> Avg[CoNLL Average F1]
    B3 --> Avg
    CEAF --> Avg
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define coreference resolution. Distinguish between anaphora and coreference with an example.**

**Model Answer**:
Coreference resolution is the NLP task of identifying all mentions in a text that refer to the same real-world entity and grouping them into coreference clusters.

**Anaphora** is a linguistic phenomenon where a subsequent expression (the *anaphor*) refers back to an earlier expression (*antecedent*). **Coreference** is a broader, order-independent notion of identity equivalence.

**Example**: In *"Rahul bought a car. He drove it home."*, the anaphoric relation is *He → Rahul*. However, the coreference cluster may also include *"the driver"* if mentioned later — even if it appears before *Rahul* — since all refer to the same entity.

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**List and briefly explain any three evaluation metrics used for coreference resolution.**

**Model Answer**:
1. **MUC $F_1$**: Counts the minimum number of mention-link insertions/deletions required to align system clusters with gold clusters.
2. **$B^3$ $F_1$**: Computes per-mention precision and recall of the cluster containing each mention, then averages weighted by frequency.
3. **CEAF-$\phi_4$**: Uses a $\phi_4$ similarity function to find the optimal one-to-one alignment between system and gold entities.

The **CoNLL $F_1$** is the unweighted average of these three.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]** — *Coreference Resolution, Model Architectures, and Evaluation*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of the **end-to-end neural coreference resolution model** proposed by Lee et al. (2017). Include details of span representation, mention scoring, and pairwise coreference scoring.

**Model Solution**:

The Lee et al. (2017) end-to-end neural CR model operates in four stages:

**Stage 1 — Span Enumeration**:
Generate all spans up to a maximum length $L_{\max}$. Total candidate spans $\mathcal{O}(T^2)$.

**Stage 2 — Span Representation**:
Each span $s_i$ is encoded as:

$$
\mathbf{x}_i = [\mathbf{h}_{\text{start}_i};\ \mathbf{h}_{\text{end}_i};\ \hat{\mathbf{h}}_i]
$$

where $\hat{\mathbf{h}}_i$ is the attention-weighted sum over tokens inside the span.

**Stage 3 — Mention Scoring (Unary)**:
A feed-forward network outputs the mention score $f(s_i)$. Mentions with $f(s_i)$ above threshold are kept.

**Stage 4 — Pairwise Scoring**:
For each pair $(s_i, s_j)$ with $i < j$:

$$
g(s_i, s_j) = \mathbf{w}_c^\top \cdot \text{FFNN}_c([\mathbf{x}_i;\ \mathbf{x}_j;\ \mathbf{x}_i \circ \mathbf{x}_j;\ \boldsymbol{\phi}(i,j)])
$$

The score combines contextual embeddings with metadata features (speaker, distance).

The final cluster inference uses a softmax over all antecedents plus a dummy $\epsilon$ option, allowing the model to decide *not* to link a mention to any earlier span (i.e., starting a new entity).

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **three CoNLL evaluation metrics** (MUC, $B^3$, CEAF-$\phi_4$) with their formulas. Why is the average of the three preferred?

**Model Solution**:

**MUC $F_1$**:
MUC counts the minimum number of link insertions or deletions to make system clusters equivalent to gold clusters.

$$
R_{\text{MUC}} = \frac{\sum_i (\vert m_i \vert - \vert p_i \vert)}{\sum_i (\vert m_i \vert - 1)}
$$

$$
P_{\text{MUC}} = \frac{\sum_i (\vert m_i \vert - \vert r_i \vert)}{\sum_i (\vert m_i \vert - 1)}
$$

where $p_i$ is the partition of gold cluster $i$ induced by system, $r_i$ is the partition of system cluster by gold.

**$B^3$ $F_1$**:
For each mention $m_i$ in gold:

$$
P(B^3) = \frac{\sum_i \vert S_i \cap G_i \vert}{\sum_i \vert S_i \vert},\quad R(B^3) = \frac{\sum_i \vert S_i \cap G_i \vert}{\sum_i \vert G_i \vert}
$$

**CEAF-$\phi_4$**:
Uses a similarity function $\phi_4$ between system entity $E$ and gold entity $E'$:

$$
\phi_4(E, E') = \frac{2 \cdot \vert E \cap E' \vert}{\vert E \vert + \vert E' \vert}
$$

Best alignment via Hungarian algorithm:

$$
\text{CEAF}_\phi = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \phi_4(E_i, E_{\pi(i)})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \phi_4(E_i, E_i)}
$$

**Why average of three?** Each metric emphasizes different aspects:
- MUC rewards linking correct mentions.
- $B^3$ rewards correct cluster membership per mention.
- CEAF rewards correct one-to-one entity alignment.

A single metric can be gamed; the CoNLL average provides a robust, holistic measure.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** — *Classical and Pairwise Models*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **mention-pair model** for coreference resolution. Explain its features and limitations.

**Model Solution**:

The mention-pair model formulates CR as a binary classification problem over all pairs of mentions in a document.

**Pipeline**:

1. **Mention Detection**: Use a POS tagger + NER + NP chunker to extract candidate mentions.
2. **Feature Extraction** for pair $(m_i, m_j)$:
   - **Lexical features**: String match, alias, head-word overlap.
   - **Grammatical features**: Gender, number, animacy agreement.
   - **Positional features**: Sentence distance, syntactic position (subject/object).
   - **Semantic features**: WordNet similarity, entity type compatibility.
3. **Classifier**: SVM, logistic regression, or neural network trained on annotated data.
4. **Clustering**: Transitive closure over predicted pairs.

**Limitations**:

1. **Pairwise Inconsistency**: Independent classification may produce contradictions (e.g., A↔B and B↔C predicted corefer, but A↔C predicted non-corefer).
2. **No Global View**: Each decision ignores cluster-level coherence.
3. **Class Imbalance**: Most pairs do NOT corefer; classifiers skew toward majority.
4. **Oversimplification**: Ignores structural relationships within clusters.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **rule-based, mention-pair, and entity-mention (clustering)** approaches for coreference resolution. Highlight how entity-mention models address the limitations of mention-pair models.

**Model Solution**:

| Aspect | Rule-Based | Mention-Pair | Entity-Mention (Clustering) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Core idea | Handcrafted rules | Pairwise binary classification | Greedy incremental clustering |
| Features | Linguistic heuristics | Engineered features | Joint cluster features |
| Consistency | High (deterministic) | Low (independent decisions) | High (incremental) |
| Data Need | None | Annotated pairs | Annotated clusters |
| Scalability | Brittle | Medium | Better global coherence |

**How entity-mention solves mention-pair limitations**:

- **Cluster-level features**: Decisions consider the existing cluster's aggregate properties (e.g., dominant gender).
- **Incremental consistency**: Each new mention is compared to cluster prototypes, ensuring internal coherence.
- **Avoids contradictions**: Once $m_i$ joins cluster $C$, all subsequent links use the same cluster context.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **Common Mistake #1**: Confusing *anaphora resolution* with *coreference resolution*. Anaphora is a linguistic subtype; coreference is broader. Always clarify the distinction in definitions.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2**: Writing MUC, $B^3$, CEAF formulas without explaining what each variable denotes. Examiners award marks for **defined variables**.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3**: In mention-pair models, students forget to mention **transitive closure** during cluster inference — this is a frequent 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4**: For end-to-end neural CR, students often skip the **dummy antecedent $\epsilon$** in the softmax — without it, the model cannot decide *not* to link a mention.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5**: Mixing up the **CoNLL $F_1$ formula** with simple accuracy. The CoNLL average is the unweighted mean of three specific metrics.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Coreference Resolution**

- **Definition**: Grouping all mentions in a text referring to the same entity.
- **Mention types**: Pronouns, proper nouns, common nouns, named entities.
- **Anaphora vs Coreference**: Anaphora is order-dependent; coreference is order-independent.
- **Mention-Pair Model**: Binary classification; suffers from inconsistency.
- **Entity-Mention Model**: Greedy clustering; maintains cluster coherence.
- **End-to-End Neural Model** (Lee et al.): Span enumeration → span representation → unary + pairwise scoring → softmax antecedent distribution.
- **Dummy antecedent $\epsilon$**: Allows the model to start a new cluster.
- **Marginal log-likelihood loss**: Marginalizes over all valid gold antecedents.
- **Evaluation**: MUC, $B^3$, CEAF-$\phi_4$; average = **CoNLL $F_1$**.
- **Benchmark Dataset**: OntoNotes 5.0 (English), used in CoNLL-2012 shared task.
- **State-of-the-art performance**: ~80+ CoNLL $F_1$ on OntoNotes.
- **Real-world impact**: Translation, QA, Summarization, Dialogue, Information Extraction.
- **KTU Focus Areas**: (1) Definitions, (2) Algorithm derivations, (3) Metric formulas, (4) Comparison tables.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discourse Coherence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT741 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Discourse Coherence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Discourse Coherence

In Natural Language Processing (NLP), **Discourse Coherence** refers to the property of a sequence of sentences (a *discourse*) being logically and semantically connected in such a way that they collectively convey a unified, interpretable meaning to the reader. A discourse is *coherent* if the propositions expressed by its sentences stand in meaningful logical, rhetorical, and informational relationships that allow a hearer or reader to construct a consistent mental model (also called a *situation model* or *text world*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition**: Discourse Coherence is the linguistic and cognitive property that binds a multi-sentence text into a unified whole by means of semantic relations (rhetorical, causal, temporal, contrastive, etc.) operating between text spans (clauses, sentences, paragraphs).

A formal NLP definition (following the conventions used in Mann & Thompson's Rhetorical Structure Theory and Grosz & Sidner's discourse model) treats discourse coherence as:

$$C(D) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} R(S_i, S_{i+1})$$

where $C(D)$ denotes the **overall coherence score** of discourse $D$, $S_i$ is the $i^{th}$ sentence (or *discourse unit*), and $R(\cdot,\cdot)$ is a **coherence relation function** that returns a relation label and weight between consecutive (or related) units.

## 1.2 Coherence vs. Cohesion — A Critical Distinction

| Aspect | Coherence | Cohesion |
|---|---|---|
| **Level** | Semantic / Conceptual | Lexical / Syntactic (surface) |
| **Nature** | Logical sense of "sticking together" in meaning | Linguistic markers binding sentences |
| **Devices** | Implicit relations (cause, contrast, elaboration) | Pronouns, conjunctions, lexical repetition |
| **Failure Example** | "The cat sat on the mat. Banks play a key role in monetary policy." | "The cat sat on the mat. It sat on it." (cohesive but odd context) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Architect's Blueprint"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Building a House**:
> Imagine constructing a house. *Cohesion* is like the nails, screws, and glue that physically join two planks. *Coherence* is the **architectural blueprint** that dictates *why* a wall must come before the roof, why the kitchen must adjoin the dining hall, and why load-bearing columns cannot be placed randomly. A house with plenty of nails but no blueprint is a pile of wood; a house built from a blueprint, even with fewer nails, stands strong.
>
> Likewise, sentences joined by pronouns and conjunctions (cohesive) may still lack coherence if there is no underlying logical plan. Coherence provides the **plan**; cohesion provides the **joints**.

## 1.4 Why Discourse Coherence Matters in NLP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Machine Translation**: Translating sentence-by-sentence loses discourse-level meaning (e.g., pronoun resolution across sentences).
> - **Text Summarization**: Coherent summaries must preserve the rhetorical flow, not just keyword frequency.
> - **Question Answering**: Many answers require multi-sentence reasoning grounded in coherence relations.
> - **Dialogue Systems**: A chatbot's response is judged as coherent only if it relates to the prior turn via a valid discourse relation.
> - **Essay Scoring & Detection of Disfluent Text**: Automated scoring systems explicitly evaluate coherence.

## 1.5 Canonical Examples

**Coherent Text**:
> "John went to the bakery **because** he wanted bread. **However**, the bakery was closed, so he returned home empty-handed."

Here, `because` (CAUSE), `however` (CONTRAST), and `so` (RESULT) explicitly signal coherence relations.

**Incoherent Text (Cohesive but not Coherent)**:
> "John bought bread yesterday. Bread was bought yesterday. Yesterday bread John."

Although lexical cohesion (repetition of *bread*, *yesterday*) is high, the text is incoherent because no clear logical progression exists between the propositions.

## 1.6 Coherence Relations — The Atomic Units

A **coherence relation** is a labeled semantic relationship between two (or more) abstract discourse units, often abbreviated as **EDU** (Elementary Discourse Unit). Standard relation inventories (e.g., the Penn Discourse Treebank 2.0 / PDTB-3) contain roughly **30–100 relations**, but the four universal cognitive primitives are:

- **CAUSE** — one event/idea brings about another.
- **CONTRAST** — ideas oppose or differ.
- **ELABORATION** — additional detail about the same idea.
- **NARRATION** — sequential events in time.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Discourse Coherence

Coherence is operationalized in NLP through two complementary machinery:

### 2.1.1 Cohesion (Surface-Level Binding)
Cohesion refers to the explicit linguistic devices that link text spans:

- **Reference (Anaphora)**: Pronouns and noun phrases referring back to prior entities.
  - *"Riya bought a book. **She** read it at night."*
- **Substitution and Ellipsis**: Replacement or omission of repeated material.
  - *"He tried, but couldn't **do so**."*
- **Conjunction**: Explicit connectives (*because*, *although*, *then*).
- **Lexical Cohesion**: Word repetition, synonymy, hypernymy, and collocation.

### 2.1.2 Coherence (Deep Logical Structure)
Coherence involves the implicit logical and intentional relations:

- **Rhetorical Structure Theory (RST)** — Mann & Thompson (1988).
- **Penn Discourse Treebank (PDTB)** relations.
- **Centering Theory** — Grosz, Joshi & Weinstein (1995).
- **Hobbs' Coherence Relations** — first-order logic representations.

## 2.2 Rhetorical Structure Theory (RST) — Detailed Mechanics

RST posits that any coherent text can be recursively decomposed into a **rhetorical tree** of *nucleus–satellite* pairs.

- **Nucleus (N)**: The more essential span (carries the central meaning).
- **Satellite (S)**: The less essential span, interpreted *in light of* the nucleus.
- **Relation**: A labeled rhetorical link (e.g., `EVIDENCE`, `JUSTIFY`, `CONCESSION`, `BACKGROUND`).

### RST Example Tree

> "**[John couldn't sleep last night]**<sub>S1</sub> **because** **[he drank three cups of coffee after dinner]**<sub>S2</sub>."

Structure:
```
                [CAUSE]
              /         \
        Satellite     Nucleus
           S1            S2
       (effect)      (reason)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight**: RST has **~30 canonical relations**; the most-frequently tested are `ELABORATION`, `BACKGROUND`, `CAUSE`, `CONCESSION`, `CONDITION`, `EVIDENCE`, `JUSTIFY`, `CONTRAST`, `SEQUENCE`, `TEMPORAL`.

## 2.3 The Penn Discourse Treebank (PDTB) Relation Hierarchy

PDTB classifies coherence relations into a **two-level hierarchy**:

**Level 1 (Coarse — 4 classes)**:
1. **Temporal** — events in time (`when`, `after`, `before`).
2. **Comparison** — `but`, `however`, `contrast`.
3. **Contingency** — `because`, `so`, `if…then`.
4. **Expansion** — `and`, `also`, elaboration, instantiation.

**Level 2 (Fine-grained subtypes)** — e.g., `Comparison.Contrast`, `Contingency.Cause.Reason`.

The probability of a relation between two clauses can be modeled as:

$$P(R \mid S_i, S_j) = \frac{\exp(\mathbf{w}_R^\top \cdot \mathbf{f}(S_i, S_j))}{\sum_{R' \in \mathcal{R}} \exp(\mathbf{w}_{R'}^\top \cdot \mathbf{f}(S_i, S_j))}$$

where $\mathbf{f}(S_i, S_j)$ is a feature vector of the candidate clause pair and $\mathbf{w}_R$ are relation-specific weights.

## 2.4 Centering Theory — Local Coherence via Attention

Centering Theory (Grosz et al., 1995) formalizes local coherence through the shifting focus of attention across discourse.

### 2.4.1 Key Entities

- **Utterance $U_i$** — the $i^{th}$ sentence.
- **Center of $U_i$** — the set of entities realized (mentioned) in $U_i$.
- **$\text{Cb}(U_i)$** (Backward-looking Center) — the most salient entity in $U_i$ that is also in $U_{i-1}$.
- **$\text{Cf}(U_i, n)$** (Forward-looking Centers) — the ranked list of entities in $U_i$, ordered by prominence.

### 2.4.2 Transition States

| Transition | Condition | Coherence Quality |
|---|---|---|
| **CONTINUE** | $\text{Cb}(U_i) = \text{Cb}(U_{i-1})$ AND same $\text{Cf}_1$ | Most coherent |
| **RETAIN** | $\text{Cb}(U_i) = \text{Cb}(U_{i-1})$ BUT $\text{Cf}_1$ changed | Coherent |
| **SMOOTH-SHIFT** | $\text{Cb}(U_i) \neq \text{Cb}(U_{i-1})$ AND $\text{Cb}(U_i) = \text{Cf}_1(U_{i-1})$ | Acceptable |
| **ROUGH-SHIFT** | $\text{Cb}(U_i) \neq \text{Cb}(U_{i-1})$ AND $\text{Cb}(U_i) \neq \text{Cf}_1(U_{i-1})$ | Least coherent |

### 2.4.3 Coherence Score via Centering

The local coherence score of a discourse segment of $n$ utterances can be computed as:

$$\text{Coherence}_{\text{Centering}}(D) = \frac{\#\text{CONTINUE} + \#\text{RETAIN} + \#\text{SMOOTH-SHIFT}}{n - 1}$$

## 2.5 Entity-Based Coherence Model (Barzilay & Lapata, 2008)

The **Entity Grid** model represents a discourse as an $N \times E$ matrix, where:
- $N$ = number of sentences.
- $E$ = number of distinct entities (nouns / noun phrases).
- Cell value $\text{Grid}[i][j] \in \{+, -, [\,], \emptyset\}$ denotes the grammatical role of entity $j$ in sentence $i$:
  - `+` = Subject
  - `-` = Object / other non-subject
  - `[+]` or `[-]` = appears in a coordinated structure
  - `∅` = does not appear

### Coherence Probability

A discourse's coherence is computed by examining entity transition patterns across consecutive sentences. Let $\mathcal{P}$ be the set of $k$-gram transition patterns (e.g., `(+ , + , ∅)`, `(∅ , + , -)`). The probability is:

$$P(D) = \prod_{i=1}^{n - k + 1} P(\text{pattern}_i \mid \text{entity}_i)$$

with smoothing:

$$P(\text{pattern} \mid \text{entity}) = \frac{\text{count}(\text{pattern}, \text{entity}) + \epsilon}{\sum_{\text{pattern}'} \text{count}(\text{pattern}', \text{entity}) + k \cdot \epsilon}$$

where $\epsilon$ is a small smoothing constant (typically $\epsilon = 0.01$).

## 2.6 Lexical Cohesion & Lexical Chains

A **lexical chain** is a sequence of semantically related words spanning multiple sentences, computed using WordNet or distributional embeddings.

$$\text{Chain Coherence} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n} \text{Sim}(w_k, w_{k+1})$$

where $\text{Sim}$ may be **Wu-Palmer similarity** from WordNet:

$$\text{WuPalmer}(c_1, c_2) = \frac{2 \cdot \text{depth}(\text{LCS}(c_1, c_2))}{\text{depth}(c_1) + \text{depth}(c_2)}$$

where $\text{LCS}$ is the *Least Common Subsumer* in the WordNet hierarchy and $\text{depth}(c)$ is the distance from the root to concept $c$.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Formula | Range / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Discourse coherence score | $C(D) = \sum R(S_i, S_{i+1})$ | Normalized to $[0, 1]$ in practice |
| Centering coherence score | $(\text{CONT} + \text{RET} + \text{SMOOTH}) / (n-1)$ | $0 \le \text{score} \le 1$ |
| Entity Grid probability | $P(D) = \prod P(\text{pattern}_i)$ | Smoothing constant $\epsilon \in [0.001, 0.1]$ |
| Wu-Palmer similarity | $2 \cdot \text{LCS}/(\text{d}_1 + \text{d}_2)$ | $0 \le \text{score} \le 1$ |
| RST nucleus-satellite | $(N, S, \text{relation})$ | 30+ relations in canonical inventory |
| PDTB top-level classes | $\{\text{Temporal}, \text{Comparison}, \text{Contingency}, \text{Expansion}\}$ | 4 top-level, $\sim$ 30 sub-types |
| Centering transition | $\{\text{CONTINUE}, \text{RETAIN}, \text{SMOOTH-SHIFT}, \text{ROUGH-SHIFT}\}$ | Ordered by coherence preference |

## 2.8 Engineering Utility

| Field | Use of Coherence Analysis |
|---|---|
| **Machine Translation** | Resolves discourse-level pronoun translation (gender/agreement). |
| **Summarization** | RST-based summarizers select nucleus spans only. |
| **Essay Grading (AEG)** | Coherence scores are weighted heavily in scoring rubrics. |
| **Dialogue Systems** | Response selection uses coherence relations to choose context-fit replies. |
| **Authorship Verification** | Author-specific coherence patterns are stylistic fingerprints. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Building the Entity Grid

Consider the following 4-sentence discourse:

> $S_1$: *John* bought a *book* from the *store*.
> $S_2$: The *book* was expensive.
> $S_3$: *John* read the *book* at night.
> $S_4$: He enjoyed *it* very much.

### Step 1 — Identify Entities

Entities $= \{$ `John`, `book`, `store`, `He/it` $\}$. Note that `He/it` is coreferential with `John`, so we collapse them: Entities $= \{$ `John`, `book`, `store` $\}$.

### Step 2 — Assign Grammatical Roles per Sentence

| Entity | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $S_3$ | $S_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `John` | + | ∅ | + | + (as *He*) |
| `book` | - | + | - | - (as *it*) |
| `store` | - | ∅ | ∅ | ∅ |

### Step 3 — Construct Entity Grid

$$G = \begin{bmatrix} + & - & - \\ \emptyset & + & \emptyset \\ + & - & \emptyset \\ + & - & \emptyset \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4 — Extract $k$-gram Transition Patterns

For $k = 3$ and entity = `book`, the column is $\{-,\ +,\ -\}$. The transitions across rows for `book` are:
- $(S_1, S_2)$: $(- \to +)$
- $(S_2, S_3)$: $(+ \to -)$

### Step 5 — Compute Probability (Illustrative)

Suppose the empirical counts on a training corpus are:

| Pattern (bigram) | Count |
|---|---|
| $(- \to +)$ | 12 |
| $(- \to -)$ | 25 |
| $(- \to \emptyset)$ | 8 |
| $(+ \to +)$ | 30 |
| $(+ \to -)$ | 18 |
| $(+ \to \emptyset)$ | 5 |
| $(\emptyset \to +)$ | 15 |
| $(\emptyset \to -)$ | 22 |
| $(\emptyset \to \emptyset)$ | 50 |
| **Total** | **185** |

With smoothing constant $\epsilon = 0.5$ and $k = 9$ patterns:

$$P(- \to + \mid \text{book}) = \frac{12 + 0.5}{185 + 9 \times 0.5} = \frac{12.5}{189.5} \approx 0.0660$$

$$P(+ \to - \mid \text{book}) = \frac{18 + 0.5}{189.5} \approx 0.0975$$

Therefore for entity `book` in this 4-sentence text:

$$P_{\text{book}}(D) = P(- \to +) \cdot P(+ \to -) \approx 0.0660 \times 0.0975 \approx 0.00644$$

### Step 6 — Aggregate Across Entities

Assuming independence across entities (a simplifying assumption made by Barzilay & Lapata):

$$P(D) = \prod_{e \in \{John, book, store\}} P_e(D)$$

The final coherence log-likelihood used for ranking alternatives:

$$\text{Log-Coherence}(D) = \sum_{e} \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \log P(\text{pattern}_i \mid e)$$

Higher (less negative) values indicate greater coherence.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Centering Transitions

Discourse:
> $S_1$: *Anjali* prepared coffee.
> $S_2$: *She* poured it into a cup.
> $S_3$: *The cup* was beautiful.
> $S_4$: *Anjali* drank slowly.

### Step 1 — Identify Centers per Utterance

| $U_i$ | $\text{Cb}(U_i)$ | $\text{Cf}_1(U_i)$ | Other Cfs |
|---|---|---|---|
| $U_1$ | $\emptyset$ (no prior) | *Anjali* | coffee |
| $U_2$ | *Anjali* (= Cf₁ of $U_1$) | *Anjali* | coffee, cup |
| $U_3$ | *coffee* (≠ Cf₁ of $U_2$) | *cup* | coffee |
| $U_4$ | *cup* (≠ Cf₁ of $U_3$) | *Anjali* | coffee |

### Step 2 — Determine Transitions

$$U_1 \to U_2: \quad \text{Cb}(U_2) = \text{Cf}_1(U_1) \Rightarrow \textbf{CONTINUE}$$

$$U_2 \to U_3: \quad \text{Cb}(U_3) = \text{coffee} \neq \text{Cf}_1(U_2) = \text{Anjali} \Rightarrow \textbf{ROUGH-SHIFT}$$

$$U_3 \to U_4: \quad \text{Cb}(U_4) = \text{cup} \neq \text{Cf}_1(U_3) = \text{cup}; \text{Cb} \neq \text{Cf}_1 \Rightarrow \textbf{ROUGH-SHIFT}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Centering Coherence Score

$$\text{Coherence}_{\text{Centering}} = \frac{0_{\text{CONT}} + 0_{\text{RET}} + 0_{\text{SMOOTH}} + 2_{\text{ROUGH}}}{3} = 0.000$$

A perfectly coherent alternative would have score $= 1.0$.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Computing Wu-Palmer Similarity

Suppose we wish to compare `puppy` and `dog` in WordNet.
- Hypernym path of `puppy` (depth $= 11$).
- Hypernym path of `dog` (depth $= 10$).
- Lowest Common Subsumer (LCS) = `dog` (depth $= 10$).

$$\text{WuPalmer}(\text{puppy}, \text{dog}) = \frac{2 \times 10}{11 + 10} = \frac{20}{21} \approx 0.952$$

For `puppy` and `kitten`:
- LCS = `young_mammal` (depth $= 9$).
- Depth(puppy) $= 11$, Depth(kitten) $= 11$.

$$\text{WuPalmer}(\text{puppy}, \text{kitten}) = \frac{2 \times 9}{11 + 11} = \frac{18}{22} \approx 0.818$$

Lexical-chain coherence can use these scores as similarity values.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing a Coherence Model

| Model | Strengths | Weaknesses | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **RST** | Hierarchical, captures long-range structure | Parser error propagation | Summarization, discourse parsing |
| **PDTB** | Fine-grained, annotated corpora available | Local (pairwise) only | Implicit discourse relation classification |
| **Centering Theory** | Models attention / focus | Limited to entity-based focus | Dialogue, narrative analysis |
| **Entity Grid** | Unsupervised, simple | Bag-of-features, ignores semantics | Essay scoring, document ordering |
| **Lexical Chains** | Captures topic progression | Requires WordNet/embeddings | Topical segmentation |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Discourse Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Document] --> B[Sentence Segmentation]
    B --> C[Tokenization POS Tagging]
    C --> D[Entity Recognition and Coreference]
    D --> E[Discourse Unit Segmentation]
    E --> F[Relation Classifier RST or PDTB]
    F --> G[Coherence Model Entity Grid or Centering]
    G --> H[Coherence Score]
    H --> I[Downstream Task Summarization QA MT]
```

## 4.2 RST Rhetorical Tree Example

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[ROOT: Justify]
    Root --> Sat[S: Result]
    Root --> Nuc[N: Evidence]
    Sat --> R1[Sentence A The experiment failed]
    Nuc --> R2[Sentence B Because the sensors were faulty]
    Nuc --> R3[Sentence C And the readings were inconsistent]
```

## 4.3 Centering Transition State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Continue
    Continue --> Retain
    Retain --> SmoothShift
    SmoothShift --> RoughShift
    RoughShift --> Continue
    note right of Continue: Highest coherence
    note left of RoughShift: Lowest coherence
```

## 4.4 Entity Grid Construction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Input Sentences] --> T2[NER and Coreference Module]
    T2 --> T3[Entity List Builder]
    T3 --> T4[Grammatical Role Tagger Subject or Object]
    T4 --> T5[Grid Matrix NxE]
    T5 --> T6[Transition Pattern Extractor]
    T6 --> T7[Probability Estimation]
    T7 --> T8[Final Coherence Probability]
```

## 4.5 Discourse Coherence — Conceptual Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Coherence[Discourse Coherence]
    Coherence --> Cohesion[Cohesion Surface]
    Coherence --> Logical[Logical Coherence Deep]
    Cohesion --> Ref[Reference Resolution]
    Cohesion --> Conj[Conjunction Use]
    Cohesion --> Lex[Lexical Repetition]
    Logical --> RST[Rhetorical Structure Theory]
    Logical --> PDTB[PDTB Relations]
    Logical --> Centering[Centering Theory]
    Logical --> EntGrid[Entity Grid Model]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define discourse coherence. Differentiate it from cohesion with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Discourse coherence is the property by which a sequence of sentences (discourse) forms a logically unified and interpretable whole through semantic, rhetorical, and intentional relations between its parts. It is a *deep* semantic property.

Cohesion, in contrast, refers to the *surface-level* linguistic devices — pronouns, conjunctions, lexical repetition — that bind sentences grammatically.

*Example*: *John bought a book. He read it at night.* — **Cohesive** (pronoun *he/it* links sentences) **and Coherent** (logical narrative). Whereas *John bought a book. The sky is blue.* — Cohesion is low; coherence is also low because no logical relation exists.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**List and briefly explain the four transition states defined in Centering Theory.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **CONTINUE** — $\text{Cb}(U_i) = \text{Cb}(U_{i-1})$ and $\text{Cb}(U_i) = \text{Cf}_1(U_i)$: same backward center is also the most prominent forward center. (Most coherent.)
2. **RETAIN** — $\text{Cb}(U_i) = \text{Cb}(U_{i-1})$ but $\text{Cb}(U_i) \neq \text{Cf}_1(U_i)$: focus is retained but a new prominent entity has been introduced.
3. **SMOOTH-SHIFT** — $\text{Cb}(U_i) \neq \text{Cb}(U_{i-1})$ but $\text{Cb}(U_i) = \text{Cf}_1(U_{i-1})$: smooth focus shift to a previously anticipated entity.
4. **ROUGH-SHIFT** — $\text{Cb}(U_i) \neq \text{Cb}(U_{i-1})$ AND $\text{Cb}(U_i) \neq \text{Cf}_1(U_{i-1})$: abrupt, less coherent shift. (Least coherent.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **Entity Grid model** of coherence proposed by Barzilay and Lapata. Construct the entity grid for the following text and compute its coherence probability using the (smoothed) bigram transitions given in the table below. Take smoothing constant $\epsilon = 0.5$.

> $S_1$: *Maria* painted the *wall*.
> $S_2$: The *wall* was yellow.
> $S_3$: *Maria* admired the *wall* afterwards.
> $S_4$: *She* smiled.

| Bigram Pattern | Count |
|---|---|
| (+ , +) | 20 |
| (+ , -) | 15 |
| (+ , ∅) | 4 |
| (- , +) | 10 |
| (- , -) | 22 |
| (- , ∅) | 6 |
| (∅ , +) | 13 |
| (∅ , -) | 18 |
| (∅ , ∅) | 45 |
| **Total** | **153** |

**Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify unique entities** (after coreference resolution, *She* = *Maria*):
Entities $= \{$ `Maria`, `wall` $\}$.

**Step 2 — Determine grammatical roles per sentence:**

| Entity | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $S_3$ | $S_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `Maria` | + (subject) | ∅ | + (subject) | + (subject, *She*) |
| `wall` | - (object) | + (subject) | - (object) | ∅ |

**Step 3 — Extract bigram transitions:**

For `Maria`: $(+ \to \emptyset)$, $(\emptyset \to +)$, $(+ \to +)$.
For `wall`: $(- \to +)$, $(+ \to -)$, $(- \to \emptyset)$.

**Step 4 — Apply Laplace-style smoothing** with $k = 9$ patterns and $\epsilon = 0.5$:

$$P(p \mid e) = \frac{\text{count}(p, e) + 0.5}{153 + 9 \times 0.5} = \frac{\text{count}(p, e) + 0.5}{157.5}$$

**Step 5 — Compute transition probabilities for `Maria`:**

$$P(+ \to \emptyset) = \frac{4 + 0.5}{157.5} = \frac{4.5}{157.5} \approx 0.02857$$

$$P(\emptyset \to +) = \frac{13 + 0.5}{157.5} = \frac{13.5}{157.5} \approx 0.08571$$

$$P(+ \to +) = \frac{20 + 0.5}{157.5} = \frac{20.5}{157.5} \approx 0.13016$$

**Step 6 — Compute transition probabilities for `wall`:**

$$P(- \to +) = \frac{10 + 0.5}{157.5} \approx 0.06667$$

$$P(+ \to -) = \frac{15 + 0.5}{157.5} \approx 0.09841$$

$$P(- \to \emptyset) = \frac{6 + 0.5}{157.5} = \frac{6.5}{157.5} \approx 0.04127$$

**Step 7 — Aggregate (independence assumption):**

$$P(D) = \prod_{\text{transitions}} P(p \mid e)$$

Numerically:

$$P(D) \approx 0.02857 \times 0.08571 \times 0.13016 \times 0.06667 \times 0.09841 \times 0.04127$$

Step-by-step multiplication:

$$0.02857 \times 0.08571 = 0.002449$$

$$0.002449 \times 0.13016 = 0.000319$$

$$0.000319 \times 0.06667 = 0.0000212$$

$$0.0000212 \times 0.09841 = 2.087 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$2.087 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.04127 \approx 8.612 \times 10^{-8}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{P(D) \approx 8.61 \times 10^{-8}}$$

(Alternatively, log-coherence $\approx -16.27$.)

---

**(b)** Explain **Centering Theory** in detail. Define the four transition states and compute the centering-based coherence score for the text in part (a).

**Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Recap the definitions:**

Centering Theory models local coherence through the focus of attention. For each utterance $U_i$, we compute:
- $\text{Cb}(U_i)$: backward-looking center.
- $\text{Cf}_1(U_i)$: highest-ranked forward-looking center.

**Step 2 — Compute Cf and Cb per utterance:**

| $U_i$ | $\text{Cf}_1$ | Other Cfs | $\text{Cb}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $U_1$ | *Maria* | *wall* | ∅ (no prior) |
| $U_2$ | *wall* | — | *wall* (= Cf₁ of $U_1$) → SMOOTH-SHIFT |
| $U_3$ | *Maria* | *wall* | *Maria* (= Cf₁ of $U_2$) → ROUGH-SHIFT |
| $U_4$ | *Maria* | — | *Maria* (= Cb of $U_3$ = Cf₁ of $U_3$) → CONTINUE |

**Step 3 — Classify transitions:**

| Transition | Classification |
|---|---|
| $U_1 \to U_2$ | ROUGH-SHIFT (Cb = wall ≠ Cf₁ of U₁ = Maria) |
| $U_2 \to U_3$ | ROUGH-SHIFT (Cb = Maria ≠ Cf₁ of U₂ = wall) |
| $U_3 \to U_4$ | CONTINUE |

**Step 4 — Compute score:**

$$\text{Coherence}_{\text{Centering}} = \frac{0_{\text{CONT}} + 0_{\text{RET}} + 0_{\text{SMOOTH}} + 2_{\text{ROUGH}} \text{(wait: } 1_{\text{CONT}})}{3} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

Wait — corrected: 1 CONTINUE, 2 ROUGH-SHIFTS:

$$\text{Score} = \frac{1_{\text{CONT}} + 0_{\text{RET}} + 0_{\text{SMOOTH}} + 2_{\text{ROUGH}}}{3} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

This indicates *moderate* coherence — the focal entity shifts abruptly but partially recovers.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain **Rhetorical Structure Theory (RST)**. Draw the RST tree for the following text and identify all rhetorical relations:

> *John wanted to lose weight. **Therefore**, he started running every morning. **However**, the weather was cold, and he soon stopped. **As a result**, he gained weight.*

**Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify Elementary Discourse Units (EDUs):**

- EDU 1: *John wanted to lose weight.*
- EDU 2: *Therefore, he started running every morning.*
- EDU 3: *However, the weather was cold,*
- EDU 4: *and he soon stopped.*
- EDU 5: *As a result, he gained weight.*

**Step 2 — Identify rhetorical relations** using connectives:

- EDU 1 $\to$ EDU 2: **CAUSE / RESULT** (connective *therefore*); EDU 2 = Nucleus (the action), EDU 1 = Satellite (the reason).
- EDU 2 $\to$ EDU 3+4: **CONCESSION** (connective *however*); EDU 2 = Nucleus, EDU 3+4 = Satellite (the obstacle).
- EDU 3 $\to$ EDU 4: **CONTRAST / ADDITION** (connective *and*); EDU 4 = Nucleus, EDU 3 = Satellite.
- Whole (EDU 1+2) $\to$ EDU 5: **CAUSE / RESULT** (connective *as a result*); EDU 5 = Nucleus, EDU 1+2+3+4 = Satellite.

**Step 3 — RST Tree:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Top[ROOT: CAUSE]
    Top --> N5[EDU 5: He gained weight]
    Top --> S_all[S1234: Running failed]
    S_all --> C[CONCESSION]
    C --> N2[EDU 2: He started running]
    C --> S34[S34: Weather cold, he stopped]
    S34 --> A[ADDITION]
    A --> N4[EDU 4: He stopped]
    A --> S3[EDU 3: Weather was cold]
    N2 --> R[RESULT]
    R --> N2b[EDU 2]
    R --> S1[EDU 1: Wanted to lose weight]
```

**Step 4 — Inventory of relations used:**

`RESULT`, `CONCESSION`, `ADDITION`, `CAUSE`. Note that RST allows **multi-nuclear** relations (like ADDITION) where both spans are equally essential.

---

**(b)** With suitable examples, explain the **four top-level PDTB relation classes**. Discuss how an automatic discourse relation classifier could be built using the softmax probability model.

**Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Explain PDTB classes** with examples:

1. **Temporal**: relations between events in time.
   - *John arrived **after** the meeting started.*
2. **Comparison**: contrast or similarity.
   - *Riya is tall, **but** Anu is short.*
3. **Contingency**: cause, condition, or purpose.
   - *It rained, **because** the clouds were dark.*
4. **Expansion**: elaboration, instantiation, alternative.
   - *Anjali likes coffee, **and** she drinks it daily.*

**Step 2 — Discriminative classifier formulation:**

Given a pair of arguments $(Arg_1, Arg_2)$ represented by feature vector $\mathbf{f}$, the probability of class $R \in \{$Temporal, Comparison, Contingency, Expansion$\}$ is:

$$P(R \mid \mathbf{f}) = \frac{\exp(\mathbf{w}_R^\top \mathbf{f})}{\sum_{R' \in \mathcal{R}} \exp(\mathbf{w}_{R'}^\top \mathbf{f})}$$

**Step 3 — Features $\mathbf{f}$**:

- Connective string identity (e.g., *because*, *however*).
- Part-of-speech tags of words around the connective.
- Sentence position (intra/inter-sentential).
- Negation, modality, tense/aspect.
- Pairs of syntactic heads across the two arguments.
- Distributional / BERT embeddings of the argument pair.

**Step 4 — Training:** minimize cross-entropy loss:

$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(R_i \mid \mathbf{f}_i)$$

using stochastic gradient descent. The trained weights $\mathbf{w}_R$ then serve as the linear discriminative boundaries between relation classes.

**Step 5 — Engineering utility:** PDTB classifiers are deployed in summarization (to identify key RESULT/CONCESSION relations), dialogue generation (to choose appropriate contrast/causal connectives), and essay scoring.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**:
> 1. **Confusing cohesion with coherence** — Cohesion is *surface* (pronouns, conjunctions); coherence is *semantic/logical*. Many students write one for the other and lose full marks.
> 2. **Wrong centering transitions** — Students often confuse CONTINUE (Cb same AND Cb = Cf₁) with RETAIN (Cb same but Cb ≠ Cf₁). Memorize the precise conditions.
> 3. **Entity Grid errors** — Forgetting coreference resolution (collapsing pronouns to antecedents) leads to wrong grids and incorrect probabilities.
> 4. **Skipping smoothing** — When computing PD/PDTB probabilities, omitting smoothing yields zero probabilities for unseen patterns. Always show smoothing explicitly.
> 5. **Single-relation RST trees** — Forgetting that some relations (ADDITION, SEQUENCE) are *multi-nuclear* (no nucleus-satellite distinction) is a frequent error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Discourse Coherence**

- **Discourse Coherence** = semantic/logical unity of multi-sentence text.
- **Cohesion** = surface linguistic links (reference, conjunction, lexical repetition).
- **Four PDTB top-level classes**: Temporal, Comparison, Contingency, Expansion.
- **RST** uses **Nucleus–Satellite** trees with **~30 canonical relations**.
- **Centering Theory** transitions: CONTINUE > RETAIN > SMOOTH-SHIFT > ROUGH-SHIFT.
- **Entity Grid**: $N \times E$ matrix with $\{+, -, \emptyset\}$ cells; coherence via bigram transitions.
- **Lexical Cohesion**: Wu-Palmer similarity uses WordNet depth and LCS.
- **Coherence scoring** = weighted sum of relation strengths, often normalized to $[0,1]$.
- **Coreference resolution** is a prerequisite for centering, entity grid, and lexical chain methods.
- **Softmax formulation** is the standard for automatic PDTB-style relation classifiers.
- **Laplace smoothing constant** $\epsilon \in [0.001, 0.1]$ avoids zero-probability issues in entity grid.
- **Rough-shift detection** is the key cue for identifying *incoherent* discourse in narrative texts.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machine Translation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT741 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Machine Translation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Machine Translation (MT)** is a sub-field of computational linguistics and artificial intelligence that focuses on the automatic conversion of text or speech from a **Source Language (SL)** into an equivalent **Target Language (TL)** while preserving the meaning, syntactic structure, and semantic fidelity of the original content. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus for Natural Language Processing (PEITT741), Machine Translation is defined as the algorithmic process of using computational models to translate natural language without human intervention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Machine Translation is studied as the canonical sequence-to-sequence (Seq2Seq) problem in NLP. The module requires a thorough understanding of statistical, neural, and transformer-based paradigms, with emphasis on the encoder-decoder framework and the attention mechanism.

The mathematical abstraction of MT is formulated as:

$$\hat{Y} = \arg\max_{Y \in \mathcal{Y}} P(Y \mid X; \theta)$$

Where:
- $X$ = sequence of tokens in the source language
- $Y$ = sequence of tokens in the target language
- $\mathcal{Y}$ = the entire hypothesis space of valid target sentences
- $\theta$ = parameters of the translation model
- $\hat{Y}$ = the best candidate translation (highest conditional probability)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are a **UN Simultaneous Interpreter** standing in a glass booth during an international summit. You listen to the French delegate (Source Language), and within milliseconds you must speak the equivalent meaning in Mandarin (Target Language).

**Rule-Based Era (1950s–1990s):** You carry a giant bilingual dictionary and memorized grammar books. You look up every French word, apply hand-crafted grammar rules, and assemble Mandarin sentences.

**Statistical Era (1990s–2010):** Instead of grammar books, you analyze millions of past UN transcripts where French and Mandarin appear together. You compute probabilities: "Given that I heard this French phrase, what is the most statistically likely Mandarin phrase?"

**Neural Era (2014–Present):** You have internalized the deep semantic patterns of both languages through a massive artificial neural network. You don't look up words or count statistics — you *comprehend* the French meaning in a continuous vector space and *generate* Mandarin from that understanding.

## 1.3 Types of Machine Translation Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** A common Part A question is to list and differentiate MT approaches. Memorize this taxonomy.

| # | MT Approach | Era | Core Idea |
|---|------------|-----|-----------|
| 1 | **Direct MT** | 1950s | Word-for-word substitution using bilingual dictionaries |
| 2 | **Transfer-Based MT** | 1970s | SL → Syntactic Transfer → TL using linguistic rules |
| 3 | **Interlingua-Based MT** | 1980s | SL → Abstract Meaning Representation → TL |
| 4 | **Statistical MT (SMT)** | 1990s | Probability-driven translation from parallel corpora |
| 5 | **Neural MT (NMT)** | 2014+ | End-to-end deep learning with encoder-decoder |
| 6 | **Transformer MT** | 2017+ | Self-attention based parallel sequence modeling |

## 1.4 The Vauquois Triangle

The progression of MT systems is best understood through the **Vauquois Triangle (1973)**, which visualizes the trade-off between linguistic depth and portability.

```
                 Interlingua
                   /  \
                  /    \
                 /      \
                / ABSTRACT \
               /  TRANSFER  \
              /              \
             /  SYNTACTIC      \
            /    TRANSFER       \
           /                     \
          /    DIRECT TRANSLATION \
         /________________________\
            SOURCE         TARGET
```

Higher up the triangle = more abstraction = more portable across languages but harder to build. Lower = simpler but less accurate.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Statistical Machine Translation (SMT) Paradigm

Statistical Machine Translation, pioneered by IBM (Brown et al., 1990s), treats translation as a probabilistic inference problem. The system learns from **parallel corpora** — aligned sentence pairs $(X, Y)$ drawn from both languages.

### 2.1.1 The Noisy-Channel Model

SMT decomposes the translation probability using **Bayes' Theorem**:

$$P(Y \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid Y) \cdot P(Y)}{P(X)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula:** Since $P(X)$ is constant for a given source sentence, we maximize:
> $$\hat{Y} = \arg\max_{Y} P(X \mid Y) \cdot P(Y)$$
> - $P(Y)$ → **Language Model** (fluency in target language)
> - $P(X \mid Y)$ → **Translation Model** (faithfulness to source meaning)

### 2.1.2 The IBM Alignment Models

IBM proposed a sequence of 5 increasingly complex word alignment models:

| Model | Capability | Direction |
|-------|-----------|-----------|
| **IBM Model 1** | Bag-of-words alignment (no word order) | Both |
| **IBM Model 2** | Adds absolute alignment positions | Both |
| **IBM Model 3** | Adds fertility (how many target words per source word) | Source→Target |
| **IBM Model 4** | Relative alignment based on word classes | Source→Target |
| **IBM Model 5** | Handles deficient alignments (null word generation) | Source→Target |

The fundamental alignment probability in IBM Model 1:

$$P(X \mid Y) = \frac{\epsilon}{(J+1)^I} \prod_{i=1}^{I} \sum_{j=0}^{J} t(x_i \mid y_j)$$

Where $I$ = length of source sentence, $J$ = length of target sentence, $\epsilon$ = normalization constant, $t(x_i \mid y_j)$ = lexical translation probability.

### 2.2 Neural Machine Translation (NMT) — The Encoder-Decoder

NMT is built on the **Sequence-to-Sequence (Seq2Seq)** architecture introduced by Sutskever, Vinyals, and Le (2014).

#### 2.2.1 Encoder

The encoder reads the source sentence $X = (x_1, x_2, ..., x_T)$ and compresses it into a fixed-dimensional **context vector** $C$:

$$h_t = f(h_{t-1}, x_t)$$

$$C = h_T$$

Where $f$ is typically an **LSTM** or **GRU** cell, and $h_T$ is the final hidden state.

#### 2.2.2 Decoder

The decoder generates the target sentence $Y = (y_1, y_2, ..., y_{T'})$ one token at a time, conditioned on $C$:

$$s_t = g(s_{t-1}, y_{t-1}, C)$$

$$P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, X) = \text{softmax}(W_o \cdot s_t)$$

### 2.3 Attention Mechanism

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Topic:** Bahdanau Attention (2014) and Luong Attention (2015) are essential board exam topics.

The fixed-context-vector bottleneck is solved by the **Attention Mechanism**, which allows the decoder to dynamically focus on different parts of the source sentence at each decoding step.

**Bahdanau (Additive) Attention:**

$$e_{t,s} = v_a^T \tanh(W_a s_{t-1} + U_a h_s)$$

$$\alpha_{t,s} = \frac{\exp(e_{t,s})}{\sum_{s'=1}^{T_x} \exp(e_{t,s'})}$$

$$C_t = \sum_{s=1}^{T_x} \alpha_{t,s} h_s$$

**Luong (Multiplicative) Attention:**

$$\alpha_{t,s} = \frac{\exp(s_{t-1}^T \cdot W_a \cdot h_s)}{\sum_{s'} \exp(s_{t-1}^T \cdot W_a \cdot h_{s'})}$$

### 2.4 Transformer Architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017)

The Transformer eliminates recurrence and relies entirely on **self-attention**, enabling parallel training.

**Scaled Dot-Product Attention:**

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{Q K^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$?** Because large dot-product magnitudes push the softmax into regions with vanishingly small gradients. The square root of the key dimension keeps the variance of the dot product at 1, ensuring stable gradients.

**Multi-Head Attention:**

$$\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, ..., \text{head}_h) W^O$$

$$\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(Q W_i^Q, K W_i^K, V W_i^V)$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Application |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Bayes Translation | $\hat{Y} = \arg\max_Y P(X \mid Y) P(Y)$ | SMT decoding |
| 2 | IBM Model 1 | $P(X \mid Y) = \frac{\epsilon}{(J+1)^I} \prod_i \sum_j t(x_i \mid y_j)$ | Word alignment |
| 3 | Bahdanau Score | $e_{t,s} = v_a^T \tanh(W_a s_{t-1} + U_a h_s)$ | Additive attention |
| 4 | Luong Score | $\alpha_{t,s} = \text{softmax}(s^T W_a h)$ | Multiplicative attention |
| 5 | Scaled Dot-Product | $\text{softmax}(Q K^T / \sqrt{d_k}) V$ | Transformer attention |
| 6 | **BLEU Score** | $\text{BLEU} = BP \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | MT evaluation metric |
| 7 | Brevity Penalty | $BP = \begin{cases} 1 & c > r \\ e^{1 - r/c} & c \le r \end{cases}$ | Penalizes short translations |
| 8 | Modified n-gram Precision | $p_n = \frac{\sum_{\text{n-gram} \in C} \min(\text{count}_{clip}, \text{count}_{ref})$ | BLEU n-gram matching |
| 9 | Self-Attention | $\text{Attention}(X, X, X)$ | Context within same sentence |
| 10 | Positional Encoding | $PE_{(pos, 2i)} = \sin(pos / 10000^{2i/d_{model}})$ | Inject token order |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations form the bedrock of modern MT systems powering Google Translate, DeepL, Microsoft Translator, and Meta's No Language Left Behind (NLLB). Understanding them is essential for any NLP engineering role.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Search Engines:** Google's cross-lingual information retrieval uses MT to index pages in 100+ languages.
- **E-Commerce:** Amazon and Alibaba use MT for product listing localization across markets.
- **Legal/Medical:** Domain-adapted MT with human post-editing is standard in healthcare record translation.
- **Low-Resource Languages:** NLLB (Meta, 2022) brought MT to 200+ languages including endangered ones.
- **Simultaneous Interpretation:** Real-time speech-to-speech MT (e.g., Meta's SeamlessM4T).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: BLEU Score from First Principles

**Problem Setup:** Given a candidate translation $C$ produced by an MT system and a set of reference translations $R = \{R_1, R_2, ..., R_N\}$ written by human translators, we want a numerical score between **0** and **1** that correlates with translation quality.

### Step 1: Define Modified n-gram Precision

For each n-gram (sequence of $n$ consecutive tokens) in the candidate, count how many times it appears, but **clip** it to the maximum count in any single reference.

**Example Walk-through:**

- Candidate: `the the the the the the the`
- Reference 1: `the cat is on the mat`
- Reference 2: `there is a cat on the mat`

Unigram counts in candidate: `the` appears **7** times.
Maximum count of `the` in any single reference = **2** (in Reference 1).

$$p_1 = \frac{\min(7, 2)}{7} = \frac{2}{7}$$

This prevents the "the the the the the the the" attack from scoring 7/7.

### Step 2: Compute Geometric Mean of n-gram Precisions

$$\text{BLEU}_{N} = \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$$

With standard weights $w_n = \frac{1}{N}$ (typically $N = 4$).

### Step 3: Apply Brevity Penalty (BP)

Without BP, a candidate `the cat` would score perfectly against reference `the cat is on the mat`. The BP penalizes overly short candidates.

$$BP = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } c > r \\ \exp\left(1 - \frac{r}{c}\right) & \text{if } c \le r \end{cases}$$

Where $c$ = candidate length, $r$ = reference length (closest match).

### Step 4: Final BLEU Formula

$$\boxed{\text{BLEU} = BP \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Candidate (C):** `the cat sat on the mat`
**Reference (R):** `the cat is on the mat`

**Step-by-step computation:**

| n | n-grams in C | Matched in R (clipped) | Precision $p_n$ |
|---|-------------|----------------------|------------------|
| 1 | the×2, cat×1, sat×1, on×1, mat×1 | the×2, cat×1, sat×0, on×1, mat×1 | 6/6 = 1.0000 |
| 2 | the cat×1, cat sat×0, sat on×1, on the×1, the mat×1 | the cat×1, cat sat×0, sat on×0, on the×1, the mat×1 | 3/5 = 0.6000 |
| 3 | the cat sat×1, cat sat on×0, sat on the×1, on the mat×1 | the cat sat×0, cat sat on×0, sat on the×0, on the mat×1 | 1/4 = 0.2500 |
| 4 | the cat sat on×0, cat sat on the×0, sat on the mat×1 | the cat sat on×0, cat sat on the×0, sat on the mat×0 | 0/3 = 0.0000 |

**Compute geometric mean with $w_n = 1/4$:**

$$\sum_{n=1}^{4} \frac{1}{4} \log p_n = \frac{1}{4}(\log 1 + \log 0.6 + \log 0.25 + \log 0)$$

Wait — $\log 0 = -\infty$! This is the **zero-count problem**. The standard fix: replace 0 counts with a tiny $\epsilon$ (e.g., $10^{-7}$) or smooth with sentence-level n-gram matching.

$$\sum = \frac{1}{4}(0 + (-0.5108) + (-1.3863) + (-16.1181)) = -4.5038$$

$$\exp(-4.5038) = 0.0110$$

**Brevity Penalty:**
- $c = 6$, $r = 6$ → $c = r$, so $BP = 1$.

$$\boxed{\text{BLEU} = 1 \times 0.0110 = 0.0110 \approx 1.1\%}$$

This low score reflects the missing `is` and grammatical mismatch.

## 3.3 Derivation: Self-Attention Computational Complexity

For a sequence of length $n$, with embedding dimension $d$:

1. **Project to Q, K, V:** Three matrix multiplications of $n \times d$ → cost $O(n d^2)$ each, total $O(3 n d^2)$.
2. **Compute $Q K^T$:** Matrix multiply $n \times d$ by $d \times n$ → cost $O(n^2 d)$.
3. **Softmax over $n \times n$ matrix:** $O(n^2)$.
4. **Multiply attention weights by $V$:** $O(n^2 d)$.

**Total self-attention complexity:** $O(n^2 d)$ — **quadratic in sequence length**.

**Comparison with Recurrence:** RNN has $O(n d^2)$ — linear in $n$ but sequential (cannot parallelize). Transformer trades $O(n^2)$ for **full parallelism**, which wins on modern GPUs.

## 3.4 Derivation: Positional Encoding

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Expected Topic:** Transformer has no recurrence or convolution, so position must be injected externally.

For position $pos$ and dimension $i$:

$$PE_{(pos, 2i)} = \sin\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{model}}}\right)$$

$$PE_{(pos, 2i+1)} = \cos\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{model}}}\right)$$

**Why sine and cosine?**
1. They are bounded in $[-1, 1]$, compatible with embeddings.
2. They satisfy the linear translation property: $PE_{pos+k}$ can be expressed as a linear function of $PE_{pos}$, allowing the model to learn relative positions.
3. Different frequencies allow the model to attend to different position ranges — low dimensions handle nearby tokens, high dimensions handle distant tokens.

**Verification of Relative Position Property:**

$$PE_{pos+k, 2i} = \sin(\omega_i(pos+k)) = \sin(\omega_i pos)\cos(\omega_i k) + \cos(\omega_i pos)\sin(\omega_i k)$$

$$= \cos(\omega_i k) \cdot PE_{pos, 2i} + \sin(\omega_i k) \cdot PE_{pos, 2i+1}$$

Which is a **linear combination** of $PE_{pos, 2i}$ and $PE_{pos, 2i+1}$ — provably encoding relative position.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | SMT | RNN-Based NMT | Transformer NMT |
|---------|-----|---------------|-----------------|
| **Architecture** | Log-linear models | Encoder-Decoder + LSTM/GRU | Self-Attention + FFN |
| **Training Parallelism** | Yes (alignment) | No (sequential) | Full parallelism |
| **Long-range Dependency** | Weak | Weak (vanishing gradient) | Strong (direct attention) |
| **Context Handling** | Fixed window | Sequential | Global (all positions) |
| **Training Speed** | Fast | Slow | Very Fast |
| **BLEU Score (WMT14 EN-DE)** | ~20 | ~24 | **~28.4** |
| **Interpretability** | High | Low | Medium |
| **Memory Complexity** | Linear | Linear | Quadratic in $n$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Machine Translation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Sentence X] --> B[Tokenizer]
    B --> C[Embedding Layer]
    C --> D[Encoder Stack]
    D --> E[Context Representations]
    E --> F[Decoder Stack]
    F --> G[Linear Projection]
    G --> H[Softmax Layer]
    H --> I{Beam Search}
    I --> J[Target Sentence Y]
    J --> K[BPE Detokenizer]
    K --> L[Final Translation]
```

## 4.2 SMT Noisy Channel Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Target Language Model P of Y] --> M[Argmax Decoder]
    B[Translation Model P of X given Y] --> M
    C[Sentence Length Penalty] --> M
    M --> D[Best Translation Y-hat]
```

## 4.3 Encoder-Decoder with Bahdanau Attention

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Encoder
        E1[h1 from x1]
        E2[h2 from x2]
        E3[h3 from x3]
        E4[h4 from x4]
    end
    subgraph Attention
        AT[Compute Alignment Scores e sub t,s]
        SOFT[Softmax to get alpha]
        CTX[Context Vector C sub t = sum alpha h sub s]
    end
    subgraph Decoder
        D1[s sub t-1]
        D2[GRU or LSTM Cell]
        D3[Output Projection]
        D4[Softmax over Vocabulary]
    end
    E1 --> AT
    E2 --> AT
    E3 --> AT
    E4 --> AT
    AT --> SOFT
    SOFT --> CTX
    D1 --> D2
    CTX --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> D4
```

## 4.4 Transformer Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input Embedding] --> B[Positional Encoding Addition]
    B --> C[Multi-Head Self Attention]
    C --> D[Add and Norm 1]
    D --> E[Feed Forward Network]
    E --> F[Add and Norm 2]
    F --> G[Encoder Block Output]
    H[Encoder Output] --> I[Multi-Head Cross Attention]
    G --> I
    I --> J[Add and Norm 3]
    J --> K[Feed Forward Network]
    K --> L[Add and Norm 4]
    L --> M[Decoder Block Output]
```

## 4.5 Vauquois Triangle (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Language Sentence] --> B[Lexical Level]
    B --> C[Syntactic Level]
    C --> D[Semantic Level]
    D --> E[Interlingua Representation]
    E --> F[Semantic Generation]
    F --> G[Syntactic Generation]
    G --> H[Lexical Generation]
    H --> I[Target Language Sentence]
```

## 4.6 BLEU Score Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Candidate Translation] --> B[N-gram Extraction]
    C[Reference Translations] --> B
    B --> D[Clipped Count Matching]
    D --> E[Modified Precision per N]
    E --> F[Geometric Mean of Log Precisions]
    G[Candidate and Reference Lengths] --> H[Brevity Penalty]
    F --> I[BLEU Score]
    H --> I
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]
**Define Machine Translation. Differentiate between Rule-Based and Statistical Machine Translation.**

**Model Answer:**

Machine Translation (MT) is the automated process of converting text from a source natural language to a target natural language using computational algorithms, preserving semantic meaning and syntactic structure.

| Feature | Rule-Based MT | Statistical MT |
|---------|--------------|---------------|
| **Knowledge Source** | Hand-crafted linguistic rules | Parallel corpora |
| **Components** | Dictionary + Grammar | Language Model + Translation Model |
| **Adaptability** | Poor | Learns from data |
| **Linguistic Resources** | Heavy requirement | Minimal |
| **Example** | SYSTRAN (1968) | Google Translate (early versions) |

[3 Marks: 1 for definition + 2 for comparison]

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]
**What is BLEU score? Explain the role of Brevity Penalty in BLEU evaluation.**

**Model Answer:**

**BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy)** is a precision-based metric for evaluating MT output by computing modified n-gram overlap between the candidate translation and one or more human reference translations. Score ranges from 0 to 1 (often reported as percentage 0–100).

**Brevity Penalty (BP):** Without BP, a system could artificially inflate BLEU by producing short translations (e.g., outputting only `the cat` against reference `the cat is on the mat` would yield perfect unigram precision). The BP penalizes candidates shorter than the reference:

$$BP = \begin{cases} 1 & c > r \\ \exp(1 - r/c) & c \le r \end{cases}$$

[3 Marks: 1 for BLEU definition + 2 for BP explanation]

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the Encoder-Decoder architecture for Neural Machine Translation with a neat diagram. Discuss its limitations. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Describe the Bahdanau Attention Mechanism in detail. How does it overcome the limitations of the basic Encoder-Decoder model? **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Encoder-Decoder Architecture:**

The Encoder-Decoder (Sutskever et al., 2014) is a Sequence-to-Sequence (Seq2Seq) architecture with two main components:

**1. Encoder:** Processes the source sentence $X = (x_1, x_2, ..., x_T)$ token-by-token, updating hidden state at each step:

$$h_t = f(h_{t-1}, x_t)$$

Typically $f$ is an LSTM cell with forget gate, input gate, output gate. The final hidden state $h_T$ is treated as a **thought vector** or context vector $C$ summarizing the entire source.

**2. Decoder:** Generates target tokens one at a time, initialized from $C$:

$$s_t = g(s_{t-1}, y_{t-1}, C)$$

$$P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, X) = \text{softmax}(W \cdot s_t)$$

**Limitations:**
- **Bottleneck:** Entire source compressed into single fixed vector $C$ — loses information for long sentences.
- **Vanishing Gradient:** LSTM/GRU still suffer over very long sequences.
- **Sequential Decoding:** Cannot parallelize generation.
- **No Alignment Learning:** Decoder treats all source positions equally.

[7 Marks: 2 architecture + 3 process explanation + 2 limitations]

**Part (b) — Bahdanau Attention Mechanism:**

Bahdanau, Cho, and Bengio (2014) proposed **soft attention** that allows the decoder to dynamically attend to all source positions at each decoding step.

**Step 1 — Alignment Score:**
$$e_{t,s} = v_a^T \tanh(W_a s_{t-1} + U_a h_s)$$

This computes how well the source position $s$ aligns with the decoder state at time $t$.

**Step 2 — Attention Weights via Softmax:**
$$\alpha_{t,s} = \frac{\exp(e_{t,s})}{\sum_{s'=1}^{T_x} \exp(e_{t,s'})}$$

The weights $\alpha_{t,s}$ sum to 1 over all source positions.

**Step 3 — Context Vector:**
$$C_t = \sum_{s=1}^{T_x} \alpha_{t,s} h_s$$

The context vector $C_t$ is now **time-dependent**, replacing the static $C$.

**Step 4 — Decoder Update:**
$$s_t = g(s_{t-1}, y_{t-1}, C_t)$$

**How it overcomes limitations:**
- **Eliminates bottleneck:** Each decoding step has its own context vector derived from a weighted combination of all encoder states.
- **Provides interpretability:** Attention weights $\alpha_{t,s}$ can be visualized to show word alignments.
- **Better long-sentence translation:** Direct access to all source tokens mitigates gradient issues.

[7 Marks: 3 for mechanism + 2 for equations + 2 for overcoming limitations]

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the Transformer architecture for Machine Translation. Discuss Multi-Head Self-Attention and Scaled Dot-Product Attention with equations. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Compute the BLEU score for the following case and explain each step:

- **Candidate (C):** `It is a nice day today`
- **Reference 1 (R1):** `It is a nice day today`
- **Reference 2 (R2):** `Today is a beautiful day`
- **Reference 3 (R3):** `It is a good day today`

Use $N = 4$ and standard uniform weights. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Transformer Architecture:**

The Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) is the first transduction model relying **entirely on attention**, with no recurrence or convolution. It consists of an **encoder stack** and a **decoder stack**, each with $N$ identical layers.

**Encoder Layer Components:**
1. Multi-Head Self-Attention sublayer
2. Position-wise Feed-Forward Network (FFN)
3. Residual connection + Layer Normalization around each sublayer

**Decoder Layer Components:**
1. Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention (prevents looking at future tokens)
2. Multi-Head Cross-Attention (attends to encoder output)
3. FFN
4. Residual + LayerNorm

**Scaled Dot-Product Attention:**

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{Q K^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

- $Q$ (Query), $K$ (Key), $V$ (Value) are linear projections of input embeddings.
- $d_k$ = dimensionality of keys.
- The $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling prevents softmax saturation when dot products grow with dimension.

**Multi-Head Attention:**

$$\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, ..., \text{head}_h) W^O$$

$$\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(Q W_i^Q, K W_i^K, V W_i^V)$$

Multiple heads allow the model to **jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces** at different positions — analogous to having multiple convolution filters.

**Positional Encoding** injects token order information using sinusoidal functions (since attention is permutation-invariant).

[7 Marks: 2 architecture + 3 equations + 2 multi-head explanation]

**Part (b) — BLEU Score Computation:**

**Step 1: Tokenization and N-gram extraction**

Candidate: `[It, is, a, nice, day, today]` — length $c = 6$

**Step 2: Modified Unigram Precision (n=1)**

Candidate unigrams: `It(1), is(1), a(1), nice(1), day(1), today(1)` — 6 total

Clip counts against max occurrence in any reference:
- `It`: max in R1, R2, R3 = 1 → clip 1
- `is`: max = 1 → clip 1
- `a`: max = 1 → clip 1
- `nice`: max = 1 (only in R1) → clip 1
- `day`: max = 1 → clip 1
- `today`: max = 1 → clip 1

Total clipped = 6, total candidate = 6

$$p_1 = \frac{6}{6} = 1.0000$$

**Step 3: Modified Bigram Precision (n=2)**

Candidate bigrams: `It is, is a, a nice, nice day, day today`

Match against any reference:
- `It is` → in R1, R3 ✓
- `is a` → not in any reference ✗
- `a nice` → not in any reference ✗
- `nice day` → in R1 ✓
- `day today` → in R1 ✓

Clipped count = 3, Total = 5

$$p_2 = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6000$$

**Step 4: Modified Trigram Precision (n=3)**

Candidate trigrams: `It is a, is a nice, a nice day, nice day today`

- `It is a` → in R1 ✓
