- `is a nice` → ✗
- `a nice day` → ✗
- `nice day today` → in R1 ✓

$$p_3 = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5000$$

**Step 5: Modified 4-gram Precision (n=4)**

Candidate 4-grams: `It is a nice, is a nice day, a nice day today`

- `It is a nice` → in R1 ✓
- `is a nice day` → ✗
- `a nice day today` → ✗

$$p_4 = \frac{1}{3} = 0.3333$$

**Step 6: Geometric Mean (uniform weights $w_n = 0.25$)**

$$\log p_1 = \log(1.0000) = 0$$

$$\log p_2 = \log(0.6000) = -0.5108$$

$$\log p_3 = \log(0.5000) = -0.6931$$

$$\log p_4 = \log(0.3333) = -1.0986$$

$$\sum_{n=1}^{4} 0.25 \cdot \log p_n = 0.25 \times (0 - 0.5108 - 0.6931 - 1.0986) = -0.5756$$

$$\exp(-0.5756) = 0.5623$$

**Step 7: Brevity Penalty**

Candidate length $c = 6$. Closest reference length $r = 6$ (R1 matches exactly).

Since $c = r$, $BP = 1$.

**Step 8: Final BLEU**

$$\boxed{\text{BLEU} = 1 \times 0.5623 = 0.5623 \approx 56.23\%}$$

[7 Marks: 0.5 per n-gram + 1.5 geometric mean + 1 BP + 1 final answer]

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting Brevity Penalty (BP):** Many students compute only the geometric mean and forget BP. Examiners specifically test this in part (b) questions — **always state BP = 1 explicitly when $c = r$**.
> 2. **Using |x| in tables:** The vertical pipe inside a markdown equation breaks the table parser. Use `\vert x \vert` or $\lvert x \rvert$ instead.
> 3. **Confusing additive vs multiplicative attention:** Bahdanau uses $\tanh$ (additive); Luong uses dot-product (multiplicative). Mixing them up is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling:** Always include the scaling factor in Transformer attention equations. Examiners mark this explicitly.
> 5. **Skipping the softmax in attention:** The alignment weights must be a probability distribution — always show the softmax normalization.
> 6. **Zero-count BLEU:** If any $p_n = 0$, you must show smoothing or epsilon handling — otherwise log(0) makes BLEU undefined and you'll lose 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MT Goal:** Automatically translate source language to target language while preserving meaning.
- **Vauquois Triangle:** Direct → Transfer → Interlingua; higher = more abstraction, more portable.
- **SMT Formula:** $\hat{Y} = \arg\max_Y P(X \mid Y) P(Y)$ — Bayes decomposition.
- **IBM Models 1–5:** Progressive word alignment complexity, foundational to SMT.
- **Encoder-Decoder:** Compresses source into context vector $C$, then decodes target autoregressively.
- **Bahdanau Attention:** Additive alignment with $\tanh$ — solves the context vector bottleneck.
- **Luong Attention:** Multiplicative alignment using dot products — faster but less expressive for high dimensions.
- **Transformer:** Replaces recurrence with self-attention; enables full parallelism.
- **Scaled Dot-Product:** $\text{softmax}(Q K^T / \sqrt{d_k}) V$ — scaling prevents gradient vanishing.
- **Multi-Head Attention:** Multiple attention heads capture different subspace relationships.
- **Positional Encoding:** Sinusoidal vectors added to embeddings since attention is order-agnostic.
- **BLEU Formula:** $BP \cdot \exp(\sum_n w_n \log p_n)$ with $N = 4$ typically.
- **Brevity Penalty:** Penalizes overly short translations to prevent gaming the metric.
- **Modern Systems:** Google Translate, DeepL, Meta NLLB all use Transformer-based NMT.
- **Evaluation Beyond BLEU:** chrF, TER, METEOR, COMET, and human evaluation remain important complements.
- **Low-Resource MT:** Back-translation, multilingual models, and transfer learning are key research frontiers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Question Answering and Information Retrieval
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT741 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Question Answering and Information Retrieval

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Information Retrieval (IR): The Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **Information Retrieval (IR)** is the science of searching for information in a document collection, searching for documents themselves, and also searching for metadata that describes data. The documents are typically unstructured or semi-structured text.

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):** Information Retrieval is the activity of obtaining information resources relevant to an information need from a collection of unstructured or semi-structured data. In the context of NLP, IR deals with the representation, storage, organization, and access to textual information items.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Components of an IR System:**
> - **Document Collection (Corpus)** – The source data repository.
> - **Query Interface** – Accepts user information needs in natural language or keywords.
> - **Indexing Engine** – Builds inverted indices for fast lookup.
> - **Ranking Module** – Scores and orders documents by relevance.
> - **Retrieval Engine** – Returns the ranked list of matching documents.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a massive library with **billions** of books but no librarian, no card catalog, and no computer. You walk in and ask, *"Where can I find everything about black holes?"* An Information Retrieval system is essentially a hyper-intelligent digital librarian who has pre-read (or rather, pre-indexed) every word of every book. When you ask your question, the librarian instantly hands you the most relevant books in the order of how likely they are to satisfy your curiosity. Instead of just matching words, modern IR understands *meaning* — so asking about "stars that collapsed" also returns books about "supernovae."

---

### 1.2 Question Answering (QA): The Next Evolution

> [!NOTE]
> **Question Answering (QA)** is an NLP task where the system automatically answers questions posed by humans in natural language. Unlike IR which retrieves documents, QA aims to deliver precise answers — a sentence, phrase, or specific entity.

**Formal Definition:** QA is the task of building systems that can answer questions posed in natural language by extracting information from a structured database, semi-structured data, or unstructured text collections.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Classical Paradigms of QA Systems (KTU 2024 Module 4 Standard):**
> 1. **IR-Based Open-Domain QA** – Retrieves documents first, then pinpoints the answer span.
> 2. **Knowledge-Based QA** – Converts questions into structured queries over knowledge bases (e.g., DBpedia, Freebase).
> 3. **Hybrid / Neural QA** – Uses deep learning (e.g., BERT, RoBERTa) to perform Reading Comprehension directly over passages.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
If IR is a librarian who hands you *books*, QA is a knowledgeable expert who reads those books and gives you the *exact answer* — for example, replying *"The Eiffel Tower is in Paris"* to your question *"Where is the Eiffel Tower?"* It is the difference between giving someone a fishing net (IR) and giving them a cooked fish (QA). The expert not only *finds* the information but *understands* the question and *formulates* a direct response.

---

### 1.3 Closed-Domain vs. Open-Domain QA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Domain Restriction Spectrum:**
> - **Closed-Domain QA**: Operates within a specific subject area (e.g., medical QA, legal QA). Higher accuracy because vocabulary and context are constrained.
> - **Open-Domain QA**: Can answer questions on any topic. Lower precision but broader coverage (e.g., IBM Watson, Google Search featured snippets).
> - **Accuracy Threshold Metric:** Modern open-domain QA systems aim for **Top-1 Exact Match (EM)** scores above **70%** on benchmarks like SQuAD 2.0.

---

### 1.4 Reading Comprehension (RC) – The Modern Backbone

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Comprehension (RC)** is the task of reading a passage of text and answering questions about it. It is the most common evaluation framework for modern neural QA models.

**Standard Benchmarks:**
- **SQuAD (Stanford Question Answering Dataset)** – Contains **100,000+** question-answer pairs over Wikipedia articles.
- **Natural Questions (NQ)** – Real user queries from Google Search.
- **TriviaQA** – Trivia-style questions with evidence documents.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Classical IR Pipeline

The IR pipeline can be broken down into **5 sequential stages**:

1. **Document Collection & Preprocessing**
   - Tokenization, stop-word removal, stemming/lemmatization.
2. **Indexing**
   - Construction of an **Inverted Index** mapping each term to the documents containing it.
3. **Query Processing**
   - User query is transformed using the same preprocessing steps.
4. **Matching & Scoring**
   - Each document is scored against the query using a similarity metric.
5. **Ranking & Output**
   - Top-$k$ documents are returned in descending order of relevance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Inverted Index Structure:** A hash-map-like structure where the **key** is a term and the **value** is a sorted list of document IDs (the **posting list**). It enables $O(\log n)$ lookup instead of $O(n)$ scanning.

---

### 2.2 The Boolean Retrieval Model

The Boolean model represents documents as sets of terms and supports logical operators (**AND**, **OR**, **NOT**).

- **Query Form**: A Boolean expression over terms.
- **Example**: $q = \text{("machine learning" AND "neural networks") OR "deep learning"}$
- **Relevance**: Binary — a document is either relevant (1) or not (0).

> [!WARNING]
> The Boolean model suffers from **no ranking** and **all-or-nothing** retrieval, making it unsuitable for large-scale web search.

---

### 2.3 The Vector Space Model (VSM)

Documents and queries are represented as vectors in a high-dimensional term space.

**Cosine Similarity Formula (Core Equation):**

$$
\text{sim}(q, d) = \cos(\theta) = \frac{\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d}}{\vert \vec{q} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{d} \vert}
$$

Where:
- $\vec{q}$ and $\vec{d}$ are the query and document term-weight vectors.
- $\vert \vec{q} \vert$ is the Euclidean norm of the query vector.
- $\vert \vec{d} \vert$ is the Euclidean norm of the document vector.

---

### 2.4 TF-IDF Weighting (The Classic Scoring Mechanism)

**Term Frequency (TF):** Measures how often a term $t$ appears in document $d$.

$$
\text{tf}(t, d) = f_{t,d}
$$

Often normalized as:

$$
\text{tf}_{\text{norm}}(t, d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}
$$

**Inverse Document Frequency (IDF):** Measures how *rare* a term is across the corpus.

$$
\text{idf}(t, D) = \log \frac{N}{\vert \{ d \in D : t \in d \} \vert}
$$

Where $N$ is the total number of documents.

**TF-IDF Weight:**

$$
w_{t,d} = \text{tf}(t, d) \times \text{idf}(t, D)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of TF-IDF:**
> - Used as a feature in classical machine learning classifiers (e.g., SVMs, Naive Bayes).
> - Foundation of search engines like **Apache Lucene** and early Google.
> - Still serves as a strong baseline for keyword-based retrieval.

---

### 2.5 Probabilistic Relevance Models (BM25)

BM25 (Best Match 25) is a probabilistic ranking function widely used in modern search systems.

$$
\text{score}(q, d) = \sum_{t \in q} \text{idf}(t) \cdot \frac{f_{t,d} \cdot (k_1 + 1)}{f_{t,d} + k_1 \cdot \left(1 - b + b \cdot \frac{\vert d \vert}{\text{avgdl}} \right)}
$$

Where:
- $k_1 \in [1.2, 2.0]$ – term frequency saturation parameter.
- $b \in [0, 1]$ – document length normalization parameter (typically **0.75**).
- $\vert d \vert$ – length of document $d$.
- $\text{avgdl}$ – average document length.

---

### 2.6 Evaluation Metrics for IR

**Precision:**

$$
P = \frac{\vert \text{Relevant} \cap \text{Retrieved} \vert}{\vert \text{Retrieved} \vert}
$$

**Recall (Sensitivity):**

$$
R = \frac{\vert \text{Relevant} \cap \text{Retrieved} \vert}{\vert \text{Relevant} \vert}
$$

**F1-Score (Harmonic Mean):**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}
$$

**Mean Average Precision (MAP):**

$$
\text{MAP} = \frac{1}{\vert Q \vert} \sum_{q \in Q} \text{AP}(q)
$$

**Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (nDCG):**

$$
\text{DCG}_p = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \frac{2^{\text{rel}_i} - 1}{\log_2(i + 1)}
$$

---

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Key Variable | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cosine Similarity** | $\frac{\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d}}{\vert \vec{q} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{d} \vert}$ | $q$, $d$ vectors | Range $[-1, 1]$ |
| **TF (Raw)** | $f_{t,d}$ | frequency | Integer |
| **IDF** | $\log \frac{N}{\text{df}(t)}$ | df = document freq. | $\geq 0$ |
| **TF-IDF** | $\text{tf} \times \text{idf}$ | combined weight | $\geq 0$ |
| **BM25 Score** | $\sum \text{idf} \cdot \frac{f(k_1+1)}{f + k_1(1-b+b\frac{\vert d \vert}{\text{avgdl}})}$ | $k_1$, $b$ | $k_1=1.5$, $b=0.75$ |
| **Precision** | $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | TP, FP | $[0,1]$ |
| **Recall** | $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | TP, FN | $[0,1]$ |
| **F1-Score** | $\frac{2PR}{P+R}$ | $P$, $R$ | $[0,1]$ |
| **nDCG** | $\frac{DCG}{IDCG}$ | ranked rel. | $[0,1]$ |
| **Exact Match (QA)** | $\frac{1}{N}\sum \mathbb{1}[\hat{a}=a]$ | $\hat{a}$, $a$ | $[0,1]$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Field Utility:**
> - **Search Engines** (Google, Bing): Use BM25 + neural re-rankers.
> - **E-commerce** (Amazon, Flipkart): Product search ranking.
> - **Legal Tech**: Case-law retrieval using TF-IDF + dense embeddings.
> - **Healthcare**: Clinical decision support QA systems (e.g., BioBERT in PubMed QA).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: TF-IDF Computation

**Corpus $D$ (3 documents):**
- $d_1$ = "the cat sat on the mat"
- $d_2$ = "the dog sat on the rug"
- $d_3$ = "cats and dogs are pets"

**Query $q$:** "cat"

**Step 1 – Compute Document Frequencies:**

$$
\text{df}(\text{the}) = 2, \quad \text{df}(\text{cat}) = 1, \quad \text{df}(\text{sat}) = 2
$$

$$
\text{df}(\text{dog}) = 1, \quad \text{df}(\text{on}) = 2, \quad \text{df}(\text{cats}) = 1
$$

**Step 2 – Compute IDF for "cat" (assuming $N = 3$):**

$$
\text{idf}(\text{cat}) = \log \frac{N}{\text{df}(\text{cat})} = \log \frac{3}{1} \approx 1.0986
$$

*Explanation:* The term "cat" appears in only 1 document out of 3, so it has a high IDF — meaning it is highly discriminative.

**Step 3 – Compute TF for "cat" in $d_1$:**

$$
\text{tf}(\text{cat}, d_1) = 1 \quad (\text{appears once})
$$

**Step 4 – Compute TF-IDF weight for "cat" in $d_1$:**

$$
w_{\text{cat}, d_1} = 1 \times 1.0986 = 1.0986
$$

**Step 5 – Cosine Similarity:**

For simplicity, using a single-term query, the cosine similarity equals the TF-IDF weight divided by the document norm:

$$
\text{sim}(q, d_1) = \frac{1.0986}{\sqrt{1.0986^2}} = 1.0
$$

This means $d_1$ is perfectly aligned with the query "cat" in the vector space.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: BM25 Scoring

Given:
- $N = 1000$ documents
- $\text{df}(\text{quantum}) = 50$
- $f_{\text{quantum}, d} = 3$ in document $d$
- $\vert d \vert = 200$ words
- $\text{avgdl} = 250$
- $k_1 = 1.5$, $b = 0.75$

**Step 1 – Compute IDF:**

$$
\text{idf}(\text{quantum}) = \log \frac{1000 - 50 + 0.5}{50 + 0.5} \approx \log \frac{950.5}{50.5} \approx 2.944
$$

**Step 2 – Compute the term-frequency saturation factor:**

$$
\frac{f_{t,d}(k_1+1)}{f_{t,d} + k_1 \left(1 - b + b \frac{\vert d \vert}{\text{avgdl}} \right)}
$$

Plugging in:

$$
= \frac{3 \cdot 2.5}{3 + 1.5 \cdot \left(0.25 + 0.75 \cdot \frac{200}{250}\right)}
$$

$$
= \frac{7.5}{3 + 1.5 \cdot (0.25 + 0.6)} = \frac{7.5}{3 + 1.5 \cdot 0.85}
$$

$$
= \frac{7.5}{3 + 1.275} = \frac{7.5}{4.275} \approx 1.754
$$

**Step 3 – Final BM25 Score:**

$$
\text{score} = 2.944 \times 1.754 \approx 5.164
$$

*Interpretation:* A higher BM25 score means stronger relevance. Document $d$ ranks high for the term "quantum" because the term is moderately rare (good IDF) and appears 3 times with reasonable document length normalization.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Classical vs. Neural QA

| Parameter | Classical IR-Based QA | Neural QA (BERT-style) |
|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | Retrieve-then-Extract pipeline | End-to-end encoder |
| **Knowledge Source** | TF-IDF/BM25 + heuristics | Pre-trained transformers |
| **Answer Type** | Span / entity | Span / free-form |
| **Training Data** | Small labeled QA datasets | Large SQuAD-like corpora |
| **Inference Speed** | Fast (index lookup) | Slower (deep inference) |
| **Accuracy (SQuAD F1)** | $\sim 70\%$ | $\geq 90\%$ |
| **Interpretability** | High | Low (black-box) |
| **Cold Start** | Works with no training | Requires fine-tuning |
| **Best Use Case** | Enterprise search, legal docs | Customer support chatbots |

---

### 3.4 Neural QA Architecture (BERT-style Span Prediction)

The architecture consists of:

$$
\text{Input} = [\text{CLS}] \, q_1 \, q_2 \, \ldots \, q_m \, [\text{SEP}] \, c_1 \, c_2 \, \ldots \, c_n
$$

The output includes two learned vectors for span start and end:

$$
P_{\text{start}}(i) = \frac{e^{W_{\text{start}} \cdot T_i}}{\sum_j e^{W_{\text{start}} \cdot T_j}}
$$

$$
P_{\text{end}}(i) = \frac{e^{W_{\text{end}} \cdot T_i}}{\sum_j e^{W_{\text{end}} \cdot T_j}}
$$

The final answer span is:

$$
\hat{a} = \arg\max_{i \leq j} \, P_{\text{start}}(i) \cdot P_{\text{end}}(j) \quad \text{subject to} \quad j - i + 1 \leq L_{\max}
$$

Where $L_{\max}$ is the maximum allowed answer length (typically **30** tokens).

*Explanation:* The model computes a probability distribution over every position being the start of the answer, and similarly for the end. The valid span with the maximum joint probability is selected as the answer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classical IR Pipeline Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[user1: Document Collection] --> B[stepB: Tokenization & Preprocessing]
    B --> C[stepC: Inverted Index Construction]
    C --> D[stepD: Index Storage]
    E[user2: Query Input] --> F[stepF: Query Processing]
    F --> G[stepG: Matching against Inverted Index]
    G --> H[stepH: Scoring TF-IDF or BM25]
    H --> I[stepI: Ranking Top-k Documents]
    I --> J[stepJ: Retrieved Documents Output]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style E fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style J fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** This flowchart shows the complete classical IR pipeline. Documents flow downward through preprocessing and indexing on the left branch, while queries flow on the right branch and merge at the scoring/ranking stage.

---

### 4.2 QA System Architecture (Retrieve-then-Read)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[userA: User Question] --> B[stepB: Question Analysis Module]
    B --> C[stepC: Query Reformulation]
    C --> D[stepD: Document Retriever BM25 or Dense]
    D --> E[stepE: Top-k Candidate Passages]
    E --> F[stepF: Reading Comprehension Model BERT]
    F --> G[stepG: Answer Span Extractor]
    G --> H[stepH: Confidence Scorer]
    H --> I[stepI: Final Answer Output]

    style A fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style I fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
    style F fill:#e1d5ff,stroke:#6f42c1
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** This is the standard *retrieve-and-read* architecture used by modern open-domain QA systems. The retriever narrows down millions of documents to a handful, then a neural reader extracts the precise answer span.

---

### 4.3 QA Paradigm Classification Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[nodeA: Question Answering Systems] --> B[nodeB: Open-Domain QA]
    A --> C[nodeC: Closed-Domain QA]
    A --> D[nodeD: Knowledge Base QA]

    B --> E[nodeE: IR-Based Pipeline]
    B --> F[nodeF: Neural End-to-End]

    C --> G[nodeG: Vertical Specific e.g. Medical]
    C --> H[nodeH: Enterprise Internal]

    D --> I[nodeI: SPARQL Query over Knowledge Graph]
    D --> J[nodeJ: Semantic Parsing Approach]

    E --> K[nodeK: Retriever plus Reader]
    F --> L[nodeL: BERT GPT style Transformers]

    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style F fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    style K fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460
    style L fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** This block diagram maps the three main paradigms of QA — Open-Domain, Closed-Domain, and Knowledge-Base — and their respective sub-architectures. This aligns with the KTU 2024 Module 4 taxonomy.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
> Define Information Retrieval. List any two differences between Information Retrieval and Question Answering systems.

**Model Answer:**
Information Retrieval (IR) is the process of searching and retrieving relevant documents or information from a large collection of unstructured or semi-structured data based on a user's query. IR systems return entire documents or document lists as results.

**Two Differences:**

| Aspect | Information Retrieval | Question Answering |
|---|---|---|
| **Output** | List of ranked documents | Precise answer string |
| **Granularity** | Document-level | Sentence / phrase / entity |
| **Understanding** | Keyword matching primarily | Deep semantic understanding |
| **User Effort** | User must read documents | Direct answer obtained |

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
> What is TF-IDF? Explain the role of IDF in information retrieval.

**Model Answer:**
TF-IDF (Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency) is a statistical measure used to evaluate the importance of a word in a document relative to a collection of documents (corpus). It is the product of two statistics: Term Frequency (TF) and Inverse Document Frequency (IDF).

**Role of IDF:**
The IDF component assigns higher weight to terms that appear in fewer documents, thereby reducing the importance of common words like "the", "is", "and" which carry little discriminative power. It enhances retrieval precision by surfacing rare and meaningful terms. Mathematically:

$$
\text{idf}(t) = \log \frac{N}{\text{df}(t)}
$$

where $N$ is the total number of documents and $\text{df}(t)$ is the number of documents containing term $t$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Full Question with Sub-Parts**

> **[KTU University Exam – KTU 2024 Scheme Model Paper]**
> **(a)** Explain the Vector Space Model for Information Retrieval with a neat diagram. Derive the cosine similarity measure used for ranking documents. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the architecture of a modern Question Answering system. Differentiate between open-domain and closed-domain QA systems. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to (a):**

The **Vector Space Model (VSM)** represents documents and queries as vectors in an $n$-dimensional space, where $n$ is the number of unique terms in the vocabulary.

**Step 1 — Vector Representation:**
Each document $d_j$ is represented as $\vec{d_j} = (w_{1j}, w_{2j}, \ldots, w_{nj})$ and the query $q$ as $\vec{q} = (w_{1q}, w_{2q}, \ldots, w_{nq})$, where $w_{ij}$ is the TF-IDF weight of term $i$ in document $j$.

**Step 2 — Cosine Similarity Derivation:**

Given two vectors $\vec{q}$ and $\vec{d}$, the dot product is:

$$
\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i d_i
$$

The magnitudes are:

$$
\vert \vec{q} \vert = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i^2}, \quad \vert \vec{d} \vert = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i^2}
$$

The cosine of the angle between them is:

$$
\cos(\theta) = \frac{\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d}}{\vert \vec{q} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{d} \vert}
$$

This value ranges from **0 (orthogonal / irrelevant)** to **1 (identical / highly relevant)**. Documents are ranked in descending order of cosine similarity.

**Advantages of VSM:**
- Supports partial matching and ranked retrieval.
- Term-weighting (TF-IDF) improves precision.
- Mathematically elegant and computationally tractable.

---

**Solution to (b):**

A modern QA system follows the **retrieve-and-read** pipeline:

1. **Question Analysis** — Identify question type (factoid, definitional, descriptive).
2. **Query Reformulation** — Expand or rewrite query for better retrieval.
3. **Document Retrieval** — Use BM25 or dense embeddings to fetch top-k passages.
4. **Reading Comprehension** — A neural model (e.g., BERT) reads passages and predicts answer span.
5. **Answer Validation** — Confidence scoring and optional answer re-ranking.

**Open-Domain vs. Closed-Domain QA:**

| Aspect | Open-Domain QA | Closed-Domain QA |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Any topic | Specific vertical (e.g., medical) |
| **Knowledge Source** | Web-scale corpus | Curated database |
| **Examples** | IBM Watson, Google SGE | BioBERT, LegalQA |
| **Accuracy** | Moderate ($\sim$70% F1) | High ($\geq$90% F1) |
| **Complexity** | Very High | Lower |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – KTU 2024 Scheme Model Paper]**
> **(a)** Explain the BM25 ranking algorithm with its mathematical formulation. Why is it preferred over traditional TF-IDF in modern search engines? **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the components of an Inverted Index. With a suitable example, explain how it speeds up document retrieval. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to (a):**

**BM25 Mathematical Formulation:**

For a query $q$ containing terms $t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_k$, the relevance score for document $d$ is:

$$
\text{score}(q, d) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{idf}(t_i) \cdot \frac{f_{t_i, d} \cdot (k_1 + 1)}{f_{t_i, d} + k_1 \cdot \left(1 - b + b \cdot \frac{\vert d \vert}{\text{avgdl}} \right)}
$$

**Why BM25 over TF-IDF:**
1. **Term Frequency Saturation** — BM25 has a saturating curve for TF (controlled by $k_1$), preventing long documents from dominating purely due to term count. The parameter $k_1$ typically lies in $[1.2, 2.0]$.
2. **Document Length Normalization** — The parameter $b$ (default **0.75**) normalizes for varying document lengths, preventing bias toward longer docs.
3. **Probabilistic Foundation** — Derived from the **Binary Independence Model**, providing theoretical grounding.
4. **Empirical Superiority** — Outperforms raw TF-IDF on standard IR benchmarks like TREC.

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Components of an Inverted Index:**

1. **Vocabulary (Dictionary)** — The set of all unique terms in the corpus.
2. **Postings List** — For each term, a sorted list of document IDs where it appears.
3. **Postings Entries** — Optional frequency and positional information per document.
4. **Skip Pointers** — Allow fast traversal of long postings lists.

**Example Inverted Index:**

| Term | Postings List (DocID : Frequency) |
|---|---|
| cat | (1 : 1), (3 : 1) |
| dog | (2 : 1), (3 : 1) |
| mat | (1 : 1) |

**How It Speeds Up Retrieval:**
In a forward index (scanning every document), retrieval takes $O(N \cdot L)$ time where $N$ is the number of documents and $L$ is average length. With an inverted index, lookup of a single term is $O(\log V)$ where $V$ is vocabulary size, and intersecting postings lists for multi-term queries is $O(P)$ where $P$ is postings length — yielding orders-of-magnitude speedup.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing TF with IDF** — Students often swap the formulas. TF = frequency in one document; IDF = rarity across corpus.
> 2. **Forgetting normalization in cosine similarity** — Always divide by $\vert \vec{q} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{d} \vert$, not just the dot product.
> 3. **Mixing BM25 parameters** — $k_1$ controls TF saturation; $b$ controls length normalization. Do not interchange them.
> 4. **Missing components of inverted index** — Must list vocabulary, postings list, AND frequency/positional info.
> 5. **No diagram in VSM answer** — KTU examiners expect a diagram for 7-mark questions. Always include one.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **IR** = retrieves documents; **QA** = extracts precise answers.
- ✅ **Boolean Model** = no ranking, binary relevance.
- ✅ **Vector Space Model** = TF-IDF vectors + cosine similarity for ranking.
- ✅ **TF-IDF** = TF × IDF; IDF uses log to dampen common terms.
- ✅ **BM25** = Probabilistic ranking with $k_1$ and $b$ parameters; default $b = 0.75$.
- ✅ **Inverted Index** = Term → List of Document IDs (with optional frequencies).
- ✅ **Precision** = relevant retrieved / total retrieved.
- ✅ **Recall** = relevant retrieved / total relevant.
- ✅ **F1-Score** = Harmonic mean of precision and recall.
- ✅ **Open-Domain QA** uses retrieve-and-read (BERT + BM25).
- ✅ **Closed-Domain QA** = higher accuracy due to constrained vocabulary.
- ✅ **Knowledge-Base QA** uses SPARQL queries over knowledge graphs.
- ✅ **SQuAD F1** is the standard neural QA benchmark.
- ✅ **Exact Match (EM)** = strict string-match metric for QA evaluation.
- ✅ **MAP** = Mean Average Precision over multiple queries.
- ✅ **nDCG** = Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (graded relevance).
- ✅ **Neural QA span prediction** uses softmax over start and end positions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chatbots and Dialogue Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT741 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Chatbots and Dialogue Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Chatbot** is a software agent that engages in conversational interaction with a human user using natural language (text or speech), attempting to simulate human-like dialogue to accomplish specific tasks or provide information. A **Dialogue System** is the broader computational framework that powers conversational agents, encompassing the entire pipeline from input interpretation to response generation.

In the KTU 2024 NLP framework, a Dialogue System is formally modeled as a tuple:

$$\mathcal{D} = \langle U, S, A, R, T \rangle$$

Where:
- $U$ = User utterance space
- $S$ = System state space (dialogue state)
- $A$ = Set of possible system actions
- $R$ = Response generation function
- $T$ = Dialogue turn transition function

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** A Dialogue System is a conversational agent architecture composed of three principal modules — **Natural Language Understanding (NLU)**, **Dialogue Manager (DM)**, and **Natural Language Generation (NLG)** — operating in a continuous loop to maintain coherent multi-turn interaction with users.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a chatbot like a **waiter at a restaurant** 🧑‍🍳:

| Real-World Waiter | Chatbot Equivalent |
|------------------|-------------------|
| Listens to your order | **NLU** (Speech/Text Recognition) |
| Remembers your preferences & course | **Dialogue State Tracker** |
| Decides what to suggest (main, dessert?) | **Dialogue Policy Manager** |
| Speaks the order back to you | **NLG** (Response Generator) |
| Coordinates with the kitchen | **Knowledge Base / Backend APIs** |

Just as a beginner waiter might forget your order after two items, a chatbot struggles with **long-term context**. A skilled waiter (like a great dialogue system) keeps the entire conversation history in mind.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Modern Dialogue Systems:**
> 1. **NLU** — "What did the user mean?"
> 2. **Dialogue Manager** — "What should I do next?"
> 3. **NLG** — "How do I say it back?"

### 1.3 Classification of Chatbots (Taxonomy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Classification:**

**A. By Technology Paradigm:**
- **Rule-Based Chatbots** — operate on handcrafted if-then rules, pattern matching (e.g., ELIZA, 1966)
- **Retrieval-Based Chatbots** — select best response from a predefined repository using similarity matching
- **Generative Chatbots** — generate novel responses using sequence-to-sequence neural models, transformers, or LLMs

**B. By Application Domain:**
- **Task-Oriented (Closed-Domain)** — booking flights, ordering food, customer support
- **Open-Domain (Chitchat)** — general conversation, entertainment, companionship

**C. By Modality:**
- **Text-Based** — purely textual interaction
- **Voice-Based (Spoken Dialogue Systems - SDS)** — speech input/output (e.g., Alexa, Siri, Google Assistant)

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bolded Constants/Standards)

- **Word Error Rate (WER)**: Standard metric for ASR in spoken dialogue systems, target: **< 5%** for production
- **Task Success Rate**: Percentage of dialogues completing the user's goal
- **BLEU Score**: Measures n-gram overlap between generated and reference responses (range: **0 to 1**)
- **Perplexity**: Measures how well a probability model predicts samples (lower = better)
- **F1 Score**: For intent classification, standard target: **> 0.85**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Architecture of a Task-Oriented Dialogue System

A modern task-oriented dialogue system follows a modular pipeline architecture. The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates coverage of this canonical structure:

#### **Module 1: Natural Language Understanding (NLU)**

The NLU component performs three sub-tasks:

1. **Domain Classification** — Identifies the broad area (e.g., "restaurant", "weather")
2. **Intent Detection** — Determines user goal (e.g., "book_table", "find_restaurant")
3. **Slot Filling (Semantic Parsing)** — Extracts entities/parameters

For an utterance like *"Book a table for 4 at 7 PM in Bangalore"*, the NLU produces:

| Component | Output |
|-----------|--------|
| Domain | Restaurant |
| Intent | BookTable |
| Slots | $\{$party_size: 4, time: 7PM, location: Bangalore$\}$ |

**Slot Filling is formalized as a sequence labeling problem:**

$$P(y \mid x) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(y_t \mid x, y_{1:t-1})$$

Where $x$ is the input token sequence, $y$ is the BIO-tag sequence, and $T$ is sequence length.

#### **Module 2: Dialogue Manager (DM)**

The DM consists of two sub-components:

**a) Dialogue State Tracker (DST)** — Maintains a belief state $b_t$ summarizing conversation history:

$$b_t = f(b_{t-1}, u_t)$$

Where $u_t$ is the current user utterance representation. The belief state is a probability distribution over possible slot-value pairs.

**b) Dialogue Policy / Decision Maker** — Selects the next system action $a_t$:

$$\pi(a_t \mid b_t) = \arg\max_{a \in A} Q(b_t, a)$$

This is typically a Markov Decision Process (MDP) where:
- **State** = dialogue belief state
- **Action** = system response action
- **Reward** = task success + efficiency penalty

#### **Module 3: Natural Language Generation (NLG)**

Translates the abstract system action into natural language. The semantic input to NLG is a meaning representation $\mathcal{M}$:

$$\mathcal{M} = \langle \text{intent}, \{\text{slot}_i : \text{value}_i\}_i, \text{response\_type} \rangle$$

**Modern NLG approaches:**
- **Template-Based** — Handcrafted sentence templates with slot substitution
- **Statistical NLG** — n-gram models, class-based language models
- **Neural NLG** — Seq2Seq, SC-LSTM (Semantically Conditioned LSTM), T5, GPT-based

### 2.2 Frame-Based Dialogue Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** The **Frame** is a hierarchical knowledge structure representing the information the system needs to fulfill the user's goal.

A **Frame** is a collection of **slots** (attributes) and **values** (filled entries). Example frame for restaurant booking:

```
FRAME: RestaurantBooking
├── SLOTS:
│   ├── cuisine (TYPE: string, REQUIRED: yes)
│   ├── party_size (TYPE: integer, REQUIRED: yes)
│   ├── date (TYPE: date, REQUIRED: yes)
│   ├── time (TYPE: time, REQUIRED: yes)
│   └── location (TYPE: city, REQUIRED: yes)
└── CONSTRAINTS:
    └── time must be between 11:00-23:00
```

The system uses **slot-filling dialogue strategies:**
- **Slot-by-Slot** — System asks for one missing slot at a time
- **Top-Down** — User provides multiple slots; system confirms
- **Bottom-Up** — User initiates; system extracts and asks for missing

### 2.3 Dialogue Acts Theory

Dialogue Acts classify the *communicative function* of utterances, inspired by speech act theory (Austin, Searle). Common taxonomy:

| Dialogue Act | Example Utterance |
|---------------|-------------------|
| **Greeting** | "Hello!" |
| **Inform** | "I want Italian food" |
| **Request** | "Book me a table" |
| **Confirm** | "Did you say 7 PM?" |
| **Acknowledge** | "Okay, got it" |
| **Clarify** | "Which cuisine?" |
| **Bye** | "Thanks, goodbye" |
| **Offer** | "How about Pizza Hut?" |

**Formal Definition:**

A dialogue act is a function $d: U \to \mathcal{A}$ mapping utterance $U$ to act category $\mathcal{A} \in \{\text{Inform, Request, Confirm, ...}\}$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory for KTU 2024 ESE — Memorize These Formulas:**

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units / Range |
|---|-------------------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $P(y \mid x) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(y_t \mid x, y_{1:t-1})$ | Slot filling (BIO tagging) | Probability in $[0,1]$ |
| 2 | $b_t = f(b_{t-1}, u_t)$ | Belief state update | Vector of slot-value confidences |
| 3 | $\pi(a_t \mid b_t) = \arg\max_{a} Q(b_t, a)$ | Optimal dialogue policy | Action selection |
| 4 | $\mathcal{M} = \langle \text{intent}, \{\text{slot}_i : \text{value}_i\}, \text{type} \rangle$ | Meaning representation for NLG | Structured object |
| 5 | $\text{BLEU} = \text{BP} \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | BLEU score for NLG evaluation | Score in $[0,1]$ |
| 6 | $\text{BP} = \min\left(1, e^{1 - r/c}\right)$ | Brevity Penalty in BLEU | $r$ = ref length, $c$ = cand length |
| 7 | $R = r_{\text{success}} - \alpha \cdot T - \beta \cdot C$ | Dialogue reward | $T$ = turns, $C$ = confusion cost |
| 8 | $\text{WER} = \frac{S+D+I}{N}$ | Word Error Rate (ASR) | $S,D,I$ = subs, dels, ins; $N$ = words |
| 9 | $PPL = 2^{H(p)}$ or $e^{H(p)}$ | Perplexity for LM evaluation | Lower = better |
| 10 | $H(p) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p(w_i)$ | Cross-entropy for perplexity | In bits or nats |

**Key Engineering Utilities:**
- **Rule-based chatbots** excel in narrow domains with predictable queries (banking IVRs, FAQ bots)
- **Retrieval-based** systems are safe for customer service — no hallucination risk
- **Generative chatbots** power modern LLM-based assistants (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini) — flexible but require guardrails
- **Task-oriented systems** integrate with backend APIs and databases
- **Open-domain systems** leverage large language models trained on conversational corpora

### 2.5 Modern Evolution: From Rules to LLMs

The historical progression that KTU examiners love to test:

1. **1966: ELIZA** — Joseph Weizenbaum, pattern matching, DOCTOR script
2. **1990s: ALICE** — AIML (Artificial Intelligence Markup Language), rule-based
3. **2000s: Commercial IVRs** — Statistical ASR + handcrafted dialogue flows
4. **2010s: Virtual Assistants** — Siri (2011), Alexa (2014), Google Assistant — hybrid systems
5. **2015-2019: Neural Chatbots** — Seq2Seq, VHRED, Persona-Based Models
6. **2020-Present: LLM-Powered** — GPT-3/4, LaMDA, BlenderBot, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Worked Example: Complete Dialogue Pipeline Trace

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example — Trace through a complete dialogue system:**

**Scenario:** User interacts with a restaurant booking chatbot.

**User Utterance 1:** "I want to book a table for two at an Italian restaurant tonight."

#### **Step 1: NLU Processing**

**Tokenization:**
$$x = [\text{I}, \text{want}, \text{to}, \text{book}, \text{a}, \text{table}, \text{for}, \text{two}, \text{at}, \text{an}, \text{Italian}, \text{restaurant}, \text{tonight}]$$

**Domain Classification (Softmax output):**
$$P(\text{domain} \mid x) = \text{softmax}(W_d \cdot h_{\text{CLS}})$$

Result: $P(\text{Restaurant}) = 0.97$, $P(\text{Flight}) = 0.02$, $P(\text{Hotel}) = 0.01$

**Intent Classification:**
Result: $P(\text{BookTable} \mid x) = 0.94$

**Slot Filling (BIO Tagging):**

| Word | I | want | to | book | a | table | for | two | at | an | Italian | restaurant | tonight |
|------|---|------|---|------|---|-------|-----|-----|----|----|---------|-----------|---------|
| Tag | O | O | O | O | O | O | O | B-party_size | O | O | B-cuisine | O | B-time |

**Extracted Slots:**
$$\text{cuisine} = \text{Italian}, \quad \text{party\_size} = 2, \quad \text{time} = \text{tonight (relative)}$$

#### **Step 2: Dialogue State Tracker (Belief State Update)**

Initial belief state: $b_0 = \emptyset$ (empty)

After utterance 1:
$$b_1 = \{ \text{cuisine} = \text{Italian}, \text{party\_size} = 2, \text{time} = \text{tonight} \}$$

**Identifying Missing Required Slots** (from frame definition):

```
REQUIRED SLOTS: cuisine, party_size, date, time, location
FILLED: cuisine, party_size, time (partially)
MISSING: date, location
```

#### **Step 3: Dialogue Policy Decision**

$$\pi(b_1) = \arg\max_{a \in A} Q(b_1, a)$$

Available actions: $\{$Request(date), Request(location), Request(both), Confirm, Search$\}$

The policy learns via RL that the highest Q-value action is to **request the most important missing slot first**:

$$a_1 = \text{Request}(\text{location})$$

#### **Step 4: NLG — Surface Realization**

The semantic frame $\mathcal{M} = \langle \text{Request}, \{\text{slot}: \text{location}\}, \text{question} \rangle$ is passed to NLG.

**Template-based output:**
"Which city would you like the restaurant in?"

**Neural NLG output (variation):**
"Sure thing! What city are you looking to dine in?"

#### **User Utterance 2:** "Bangalore."

**Quick trace:**
- Slots extracted: $\text{location} = \text{Bangalore}$
- Updated belief: $b_2 = b_1 \cup \{\text{location} = \text{Bangalore}\}$
- Missing: $\text{date}$
- Policy action: $\text{Request}(\text{date})$
- NLG: "Great! For which date would you like to book?"

#### **User Utterance 3:** "Tomorrow."

- Slots: $\text{date} = \text{2024-XX-XX}$ (resolved from relative)
- $b_3$ = full belief state, no missing required slots
- Policy: $\text{ExecuteSearch}$
- NLG: "Let me find Italian restaurants in Bangalore for 2 people tomorrow evening..."

### 3.2 Mathematical Formulation: BLEU Score (Detailed Derivation)

The **BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy)** score is the standard metric for evaluating NLG quality. Step-by-step derivation:

#### **Step 1: Modified n-gram Precision**

For n-grams of size $n$, count the maximum number of times an n-gram appears in any single reference translation (the **clipped count**):

$$\text{Count}_{\text{clip}}(n\text{-gram}) = \min(\text{Count}_{\text{cand}}(n\text{-gram}), \max_{\text{ref}} \text{Count}_{\text{ref}}(n\text{-gram}))$$

#### **Step 2: Compute Modified Precision**

$$p_n = \frac{\sum_{\text{ngram} \in \text{cand}} \text{Count}_{\text{clip}}(\text{ngram})}{\sum_{\text{ngram} \in \text{cand}} \text{Count}(\text{ngram})}$$

#### **Step 3: Apply Brevity Penalty**

To penalize overly short candidates:

$$\text{BP} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } c > r \\ e^{1 - r/c} & \text{if } c \leq r \end{cases}$$

Where $c$ = candidate length, $r$ = closest reference length.

#### **Step 4: Final BLEU Score**

$$\text{BLEU} = \text{BP} \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$$

Typically $N = 4$ and $w_n = \frac{1}{4}$ (uniform weights).

#### **Numerical Example:**

**Candidate:** "the the the the the the the"
**Reference:** "the cat is on the mat"

| n-gram | Cand Count | Max Ref Count | Clipped |
|--------|-----------|---------------|---------|
| "the" | 7 | 2 | 2 |

$$p_1 = \frac{2}{7} \approx 0.286$$

The brevity penalty will also penalize the excessively long candidate, demonstrating how BLEU captures both accuracy and length fidelity.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Chatbot Paradigms

| Feature | Rule-Based | Retrieval-Based | Generative (Neural/LLM) |
|---------|-----------|-----------------|------------------------|
| **Response Source** | Handcrafted rules | Pre-stored responses | Model-generated text |
| **Flexibility** | Very Low | Low | Very High |
| **Consistency** | 100% | 100% | Variable (may hallucinate) |
| **Development Cost** | Low initially, high maintenance | Medium | High compute, low per-skill |
| **Domain Coverage** | Narrow | Medium | Broad |
| **Handles Novel Inputs** | Poor | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Evaluation** | Manual testing | Recall@k, MRR | BLEU, perplexity, human eval |
| **Examples** | ELIZA, ALICE | Many customer service bots | ChatGPT, BlenderBot |
| **Failure Mode** | Falls out of rule scope | Returns irrelevant response | Hallucinates facts |
| **Latency** | <10ms | 50-200ms | 500ms-several seconds |

### 3.4 Dialogue State Tracking — Bayesian Update

For a single slot, the belief update is:

$$P(s_t = v \mid u_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot P(u_t \mid s_t = v) \cdot P(s_t = v \mid u_{1:t-1})$$

Where:
- $\eta$ = normalization constant
- $P(u_t \mid s_t = v)$ = observation likelihood
- $P(s_t = v \mid u_{1:t-1})$ = prior belief

Expanding the prior (temporal smoothing):

$$P(s_t = v \mid u_{1:t-1}) = \sum_{v'} P(s_t = v \mid s_{t-1} = v') \cdot P(s_{t-1} = v' \mid u_{1:t-1})$$

This forms a **Hidden Markov Model (HMM)** style tracking, modernized with neural networks as $f(\cdot)$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Canonical Dialogue System Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    userA[USER UTTERANCE] --> asrA[ASR MODULE\nSpeech to Text]
    userB[TEXT INPUT] --> nluA[NLU PIPELINE]
    asrA --> nluA

    nluA --> nluB[DOMAIN CLASSIFIER]
    nluA --> nluC[INTENT CLASSIFIER]
    nluA --> nluD[SLOT TAGGER\nBIO Schema]

    nluB --> dstA[DIALOGUE STATE TRACKER]
    nluC --> dstA
    nluD --> dstA

    dstA --> dstB[Belief State BT]
    dstB --> polA[DIALOGUE POLICY\nDecision Engine]

    polA --> polB{ACTION TYPE}
    polB -->|Query| kbA[KNOWLEDGE BASE\nBackend APIs]
    polB -->|Clarify| nlgA[NLG MODULE]
    polB -->|Confirm| nlgA
    polB -->|Execute| kbA

    kbA --> nlgA
    nlgA --> respA[SYSTEM RESPONSE\nText Output]
    respA --> ttsA[TTS MODULE\nText to Speech]
    ttsA --> outA[SPOKEN OUTPUT]

    polA -.feedback.-> dstA

    style userA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nluA fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style dstA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style polA fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nlgA fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style respA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Frame-Based Slot-Filling Dialogue Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[USER INITIATES\nCONVERSATION] --> greetA[System Greets\nDialogue Act GREET]
    greetA --> q1A{Frame Has\nMissing Slots}

    q1A -->|Yes| askA[Policy Chooses\nSlot to Request]
    askA --> nlgAskA[NLG Generates\nQuestion]
    nlgAskA --> userRespA[User Provides\nInformation]
    userRespA --> nluProcA[NLU Extracts\nSlot Value]
    nluProcA --> updateA[Update Belief\nState]
    updateA --> q1A

    q1A -->|No complete| searchA[Execute Search\nQuery Backend]
    searchA --> resultA[Retrieve Results\nfrom KB]
    resultA --> nlgResA[NLG Generates\nResponse with Results]
    nlgResA --> offerA[Present Options\nDialogue Act OFFER]
    offerA --> userChoiceA{User Confirms\nor Modifies}

    userChoiceA -->|Confirm| bookA[Execute Booking\nAction]
    userChoiceA -->|Modify| updateA
    bookA --> byeA[Dialogue Act BYE\nEnd Conversation]

    style startA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style greetA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    style askA fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style searchA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style offerA fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style bookA fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style byeA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Classification Taxonomy of Chatbots

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[CHATBOT TAXONOMY] --> techA[BY TECHNOLOGY]
    rootA --> domainA[BY DOMAIN]
    rootA --> modalA[BY MODALITY]

    techA --> ruleA[Rule Based\nELIZA, ALICE]
    techA --> retrA[Retrieval Based\nSimilarity Matching]
    techA --> genA[Generative\nSeq2Seq, LLM, GPT]

    domainA --> taskA[Task Oriented\nGoal Driven]

    modalA --> spkA[Spoken\nVoice Assistants]

    taskA --> frameA[Frame Based\nSlot Filling]
    taskA --> pipeA[Pipeline\nNLU DM NLG]

```

### 4.4 Reinforcement Learning Loop for Dialogue Policy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    envA[USER SIMULATOR\nReal or Synthetic] -->|Provides\nUtterance| agentA[DIALOGUE\nAGENT]
    agentA -->|System\nResponse| envA
    envA -->|Reward Signal\nSuccess or Failure| agentA

    agentA --> stateA[State Encoder\nBelief State BT]
    stateA --> qnetA[Q Network\nor Policy Network]
    qnetA --> actA[Action Sampler\na_t equals argmax Q]
    actA --> execA[Execute Action\nin Environment]
    execA --> envA

    qnetA -.updates.-> replayA[Experience\nReplay Buffer]
    replayA -.samples.-> qnetA

    style envA fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style agentA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style stateA fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style qnetA fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: ELIZA-Style Pattern Matcher

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[User Input Text] --> prepA[Preprocessor\nLowercase, Strip]
    prepA --> matchA{Pattern Matcher\nKeyword Detection}

    matchA -->|Match Found| ruleA[Rule Selection\nPriority Based]
    matchA -->|No Match| fallA[Fallback Rule\nGeneric Response]

    ruleA --> transA[Transformation\nRules Apply]
    fallA --> transA
    transA --> outA[Generate Response]
    outA --> outputA[Output Text to User]

    ruleA --> memoryA[Context Memory\nStores Keywords]
    memoryA --> matchA

    style inputA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style matchA fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style ruleA fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style transA fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style outputA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**
*Define a chatbot. Differentiate between a rule-based chatbot and a retrieval-based chatbot with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **chatbot** is a computer program designed to simulate human conversation through text or voice interactions, allowing users to communicate with machines using natural language.

| Aspect | Rule-Based Chatbot | Retrieval-Based Chatbot |
|--------|-------------------|------------------------|
| **Mechanism** | Uses handcrafted if-then rules and pattern matching | Selects best response from a predefined repository using similarity matching |
| **Response Generation** | Fixed or templated | Retrieved from a corpus of possible responses |
| **Flexibility** | Low — only handles anticipated inputs | Medium — handles inputs similar to training data |
| **Example** | ELIZA (DOCTOR script) | Customer service FAQ bots using cosine similarity over TF-IDF vectors |
| **Advantage** | Full control, deterministic | Consistent, no hallucination |
| **Limitation** | Brittle, fails on unseen inputs | Cannot handle truly novel queries |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]**
*What is a dialogue act? List any four common dialogue acts with examples.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **dialogue act** is a label representing the communicative function or intent behind an utterance in a conversation, drawn from speech act theory. It characterizes *what the speaker is doing* with their words rather than the literal meaning.

Four common dialogue acts:

| # | Dialogue Act | Example |
|---|-------------|---------|
| 1 | **Greeting** | "Hello, how can I help you?" |
| 2 | **Inform** | "I want to book a flight to Mumbai" |
| 3 | **Request** | "Can you show me the menu?" |
| 4 | **Confirm** | "Did you say 7 PM tonight?" |

> [!NOTE]
> Additional acts include **Acknowledge, Clarify, Offer, Bye, Thank**, and **Reject**.

---

### 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Expected Pattern]**
*With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a task-oriented dialogue system. Describe the functions of NLU, Dialogue Manager, and NLG modules in detail. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A task-oriented dialogue system helps users accomplish specific goals such as booking tickets, ordering food, or making reservations. The canonical architecture consists of three main modules:

**1. Natural Language Understanding (NLU):**
The NLU module converts user input (text or speech) into a structured semantic representation. It performs:
- **Domain Classification**: Identifies the area of the user's query (e.g., restaurant, travel)
- **Intent Detection**: Determines what the user wants to achieve (e.g., BookTable, SearchFlight)
- **Slot Filling (Semantic Parsing)**: Extracts parameters using BIO tagging

For example, the utterance *"Book a table for 4 at 7 PM in Bangalore"* yields:
- Domain: Restaurant
- Intent: BookTable
- Slots: {party_size=4, time=7PM, location=Bangalore}

**2. Dialogue Manager (DM):**
The DM is the central decision-making component with two sub-modules:
- **Dialogue State Tracker (DST)**: Maintains a belief state summarizing the conversation history. At each turn, the belief is updated as $b_t = f(b_{t-1}, u_t)$, accumulating confirmed slot-value pairs.
- **Dialogue Policy**: Decides the next system action by selecting $a_t = \arg\max_{a} Q(b_t, a)$. It chooses whether to ask for missing information, query the database, confirm details, or terminate.

**3. Natural Language Generation (NLG):**
NLG converts the abstract system action into natural language output. It receives a meaning representation $\mathcal{M} = \langle \text{intent}, \{\text{slot}_i : \text{value}_i\}, \text{type} \rangle$ and produces a surface form. Modern neural NLG uses sequence-to-sequence models, while simpler systems use templates.

**Block Diagram Reference:** See Section 4.1 above.

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam - Module 4 Common Pattern]**
*Explain the concept of frame-based dialogue systems. Describe slot-filling strategies with a suitable example. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A **frame** is a structured knowledge representation that encodes all the information (slots) the system needs to collect to fulfill a user's goal. Frame-based dialogue systems are the standard approach for task-oriented bots.

**Structure of a Frame:**

A frame contains:
- **Frame Name** (the goal/activity, e.g., RestaurantBooking)
- **Slots** (attributes to be filled, e.g., cuisine, time, location)
- **Slot Constraints** (valid value ranges)
- **Required vs Optional Slots**

**Example Frame — Hotel Booking:**

```
FRAME: HotelBooking
  Slot: city (string, required)
  Slot: checkin_date (date, required)
  Slot: checkout_date (date, required)
  Slot: num_guests (integer, required)
  Slot: room_type (enum: single/double/suite, optional)
  Slot: price_range (range, optional)
```

**Slot-Filling Strategies:**

1. **Slot-by-Slot (System-Initiated):** The system asks for one missing slot at a time, in a predetermined order. Simple and predictable, but can be slow.

2. **Top-Down (Mixed-Initiative):** The user can provide multiple slots in a single utterance (e.g., "I want a deluxe room in Mumbai for 2 guests from 15th to 18th December"). The system extracts all and only asks for genuinely missing ones.

3. **Bottom-Up (User-Initiative):** The user provides partial information; the system asks for the rest as needed.

**Example Trace (Slot-by-Slot):**

```
User:    I want to book a hotel.
System:  Which city?           (Request: city)
User:    Mumbai.
System:  Check-in date?        (Request: checkin_date)
User:    15th December.
System:  Check-out date?       (Request: checkout_date)
User:    18th December.
System:  How many guests?      (Request: num_guests)
User:    2.
System:  Searching hotels in Mumbai from 15-18 Dec for 2 guests...
```

**Advantages of Frame-Based Approach:**
- Modular and easy to extend
- Clear state representation
- Works well with template-based NLG
- Easy integration with backend databases

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme]**
*Discuss the various types of chatbots with their characteristics, advantages, and limitations. Compare rule-based, retrieval-based, and generative chatbots. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

Chatbots can be classified based on their underlying technology into three primary categories:

**1. Rule-Based Chatbots:**
- **Mechanism:** Use handcrafted if-then rules, pattern matching, and regular expressions. The earliest example is **ELIZA (1966)** by Joseph Weizenbaum, which used keyword detection and transformation rules.
- **Advantages:** Deterministic, full control, easy to debug, predictable behavior, no training data required.
- **Limitations:** Cannot handle unseen inputs, brittle, requires manual expansion, limited naturalness.
- **Use Cases:** FAQ bots, IVR systems, narrow-scope customer service.

**2. Retrieval-Based Chatbots:**
- **Mechanism:** Maintain a repository of pre-written responses. For each user input, compute similarity (e.g., TF-IDF cosine, BERT embeddings) against the repository and retrieve the best match.
- **Advantages:** Consistent responses, no hallucination, fast inference, easy to update.
- **Limitations:** Cannot generate novel responses, requires curated dataset, may return irrelevant matches.
- **Use Cases:** Customer support, FAQ assistants, e-commerce query handlers.

**3. Generative Chatbots:**
- **Mechanism:** Use sequence-to-sequence neural networks, transformer architectures, or Large Language Models to generate novel responses token by token.
- **Advantages:** Highly flexible, can handle novel inputs, produces natural responses, supports open-domain conversation.
- **Limitations:** May hallucinate facts, requires massive training data, high compute cost, harder to control.
- **Use Cases:** Modern AI assistants (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini), open-domain chatbots, creative writing aids.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Rule-Based | Retrieval-Based | Generative |
|---------|-----------|----------------|------------|
| Flexibility | Very Low | Low | Very High |
| Consistency | 100% | 100% | Variable |
| Maintenance | High (manual) | Medium | Low (per task) |
| Novel Input | Poor | Moderate | Excellent |
| Compute Cost | Negligible | Low | High |
| Hallucination Risk | None | None | Present |

**Hybrid Systems:** Modern production chatbots often combine all three — retrieval for known queries, generative for open-domain, and rules for safety guardrails.

---

**Q4(b). [KTU University Exam - Model Paper]**
*Explain the concept of Natural Language Generation (NLG) in dialogue systems. Discuss evaluation metrics used for NLG with the BLEU score formula. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Natural Language Generation (NLG):**

NLG is the final stage of a dialogue system pipeline. It converts the abstract meaning representation produced by the Dialogue Manager into a natural language utterance that the user can understand.

**Meaning Representation Input:**

The NLG module receives a structured semantic frame:

$$\mathcal{M} = \langle \text{intent}, \{\text{slot}_i : \text{value}_i\}_{i=1}^{n}, \text{response\_type} \rangle$$

For example: $\mathcal{M} = \langle \text{Inform}, \{\text{cuisine}: \text{Italian}, \text{location}: \text{Bangalore}\}, \text{statement} \rangle$

**NLG Approaches:**

1. **Template-Based NLG:** Handcrafted sentence templates with slot placeholders. Example: `"I found {count} {cuisine} restaurants in {location}."` Simple, reliable, but lacks variety.

2. **Class-Based Language Models:** n-gram models conditioned on semantic classes representing slots and values.

3. **SC-LSTM (Semantically Conditioned LSTM):** Neural model that incorporates semantic information at each decoding step to ensure slot values appear in the output.

4. **Transformer-Based NLG:** T5, BART, GPT models fine-tuned for dialogue response generation. Most flexible and natural.

**Evaluation Metrics for NLG:**

1. **BLEU Score (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy):**
   - Measures n-gram overlap between candidate and reference responses
   - Formula:

$$\text{BLEU} = \text{BP} \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$$

   Where $p_n$ is the modified n-gram precision and BP is the brevity penalty.

2. **METEOR:** Considers synonyms and word stems, addressing BLEU's limitations.

3. **ROUGE:** Recall-oriented metric, used more in summarization.

4. **Perplexity:** Measures language model quality; lower is better.

5. **Human Evaluation:** Gold standard — judges rate responses on naturalness, informativeness, and task success.

**BLEU Limitations in Dialogue:**
- Dialogue often has multiple valid responses, hurting BLEU scores
- Does not measure semantic correctness
- Word-order insensitive in some cases
- Human evaluation often remains the gold standard for dialogue systems

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up dialogue acts with intents** — Dialogue acts are *generic* communicative functions (greet, inform, request), while intents are *task-specific* (BookTable, FindFlight). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
>
> 2. **Confusing slot-filling with NER** — Slot filling is task-specific semantic parsing tied to a frame's slots, while NER identifies generic named entities. Frame slots may include non-entity values like dates and numbers.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Dialogue Manager split** — Many students describe the DM as a single block. Always split it into **State Tracker** and **Policy** components.
>
> 4. **Skipping the reward function in RL** — When discussing dialogue policy, the KTU rubric mandates mention of the **reward function** $R = r_{\text{success}} - \alpha T - \beta C$ balancing task success with dialogue length and clarification cost.
>
> 5. **Wrong attribution of ELIZA** — ELIZA was created by **Joseph Weizenbaum in 1966** at MIT, not Alan Turing or any modern AI lab. Examiners deduct marks for misattribution.
>
> 6. **Ignoring brevity penalty in BLEU** — Always include the BP term; candidates often forget to penalize short outputs.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 KTU 2024 Rapid Revision Checklist — Chatbots & Dialogue Systems**

- ✅ A **chatbot** is a conversational agent; a **dialogue system** is the broader architectural framework.

- ✅ **Three core modules** of a task-oriented system: **NLU → Dialogue Manager → NLG**.

- ✅ **NLU** performs **Domain Classification, Intent Detection, and Slot Filling (BIO tagging)**.

- ✅ **Dialogue Manager** = **Dialogue State Tracker (DST) + Dialogue Policy**.

- ✅ Belief state update: $b_t = f(b_{t-1}, u_t)$.

- ✅ Optimal policy: $a_t = \arg\max_{a} Q(b_t, a)$ (RL formulation).

- ✅ **A frame** = structured knowledge representation with **slots** (attributes) and **values** (filled entries).

- ✅ **Three slot-filling strategies**: Slot-by-Slot, Top-Down (Mixed-Initiative), Bottom-Up (User-Initiative).

- ✅ **Dialogue acts** classify communicative functions: Greet, Inform, Request, Confirm, Clarify, Offer, Acknowledge, Bye.

- ✅ **Three chatbot types**: Rule-based (ELIZA), Retrieval-based (FAQ bots), Generative (LLMs, ChatGPT).

- ✅ **Task-oriented vs Open-domain**: Task-oriented = specific goals; Open-domain = general chitchat.

- ✅ **BLEU formula**: $\text{BLEU} = \text{BP} \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ with $\text{BP} = e^{1 - r/c}$ when $c \leq r$.

- ✅ **WER formula**: $\text{WER} = \frac{S+D+I}{N}$ — used for ASR evaluation in spoken dialogue systems.

- ✅ **Perplexity** = $2^{H(p)}$ — lower is better, measures LM quality.

- ✅ **ELIZA (1966, Weizenbaum)** = first chatbot, pattern matching, DOCTOR script.

- ✅ **Modern NLG** uses T5, BART, GPT-based architectures; older systems used templates and SC-LSTM.

- ✅ **Hybrid systems** (combining retrieval + generative + rules) dominate production deployments.

- ✅ **Reward function** for RL policy: $R = r_{\text{success}} - \alpha T - \beta C$ balances success, turn count, and clarification cost.

- ✅ **Evaluation hierarchy**: Automatic metrics (BLEU, perplexity) → Human evaluation is the gold standard for dialogue.

- ✅ **Spoken Dialogue Systems (SDS)** add ASR (front-end) and TTS (back-end) to the text-based pipeline.

> **🎯 KTU Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions, always include a **block diagram** (worth ~2-3 marks) plus **examples** for every concept. Examiners reward structure, labels, and clarity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT741 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Relation and Event Extraction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Relation Extraction (RE)?

**Relation Extraction** is a sub-task of Information Extraction (IE) in Natural Language Processing that focuses on identifying and classifying the semantic relationships that exist between pairs of **named entities** (or concepts) mentioned in unstructured natural language text.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition**
> Given a sentence $S$ and two entity mentions $e_1$ and $e_2$ within $S$, Relation Extraction is the task of producing a relation label $r \in \mathcal{R}$ (where $\mathcal{R}$ is a predefined relation set) such that the ordered tuple $(e_1, r, e_2)$ correctly captures the semantic association expressed by the linguistic context surrounding the entities.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Family Tree of Sentences"
Imagine a sentence as a **family gathering photograph**. You can clearly see people (entities), but the photograph itself doesn't tell you *who is married to whom*, *who is the child of whom*, or *who works for whom*. A relation extraction system acts like a **detective studying the photograph** — it examines visual cues (clues in the text), body language (syntactic dependencies), and nearby interactions (contextual words) to deduce relationships such as *"daughter-of"*, *"employed-by"*, or *"headquartered-in"*. Without RE, the computer just sees a list of names; with RE, it understands the **connective tissue** of meaning.

### Types of Semantic Relations (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

| Relation Class | Example Relations | Example Sentence |
|---|---|---|
| **Geographic / Location** | *Located-In, Capital-Of, Headquartered-In* | Apple is **headquartered in** Cupertino. |
| **Employment / Affiliation** | *Employed-By, Founder-Of, Subsidiary-Of* | Sundar Pichai is the **CEO of** Google. |
| **Personal / Social** | *Spouse-Of, Parent-Of, Sibling-Of* | Aarav is the **son of** Meera. |
| **Part-Whole** | *Part-Of, Member-Of, Component-Of* | The wheel is a **part of** the car. |
| **Temporal** | *Before, After, During* | The meeting happened **before** lunch. |
| **Causal** | *Causes, Results-In, Leads-To* | Rain **causes** flooding. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Automatic Content Extraction (ACE)** program and the **TAC-KBP** (Text Analysis Conference — Knowledge Base Population) relation taxonomy are the two industry-academic standards frequently cited in KTU examinations. ACE defines 7 major relation types and 18 subtypes. TAC-KBP expands this to 41 relation types.

---

## 1.2 What is Event Extraction (EE)?

**Event Extraction** is the NLP task of automatically identifying **structured representations of events** described in unstructured text. An *event* in linguistics refers to an occurrence or action involving a change of state, and event extraction converts free-form text into a structured record of *what happened, who did it, to whom, where, when, and how*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition**
> An **Event Mention** is a structured tuple $\mathcal{E} = (T, C, A, M, P)$ where:
> - $T$ = **Trigger** (the word/phrase that most clearly expresses the event)
> - $C$ = **Event Type / Subtype class** (from the ACE taxonomy)
> - $A = \{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n\}$ = **Arguments** (participants of the event)
> - $M$ = **Argument Roles** (e.g., *Agent, Patient, Time, Location, Instrument*)
> - $P$ = **Properties** (polarity, modality, tense, genericity)

### Conceptual Analogy — "The CCTV of Language"
Think of an event extraction system as a **linguistic CCTV camera** monitoring a news broadcast. A regular camera records the video (raw text), but the *smart* CCTV automatically generates a **logbook entry**:
- *What* happened? → A **bombing** (trigger: "detonated")
- *Who* did it? → A **terrorist group** (Agent argument)
- *Who* was affected? → **Civilians** (Victim argument)
- *Where*? → **Market square** (Place argument)
- *When*? → **Tuesday morning** (Time argument)
- *Was it real or hypothetical?* → **Real, past-tense** (Modality/Polarity)

Without EE, the text is just a stream of sentences; with EE, the text becomes a **queryable database of facts**.

### The ACE 2005 Event Taxonomy (Industry Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ACE 2005 defines **8 broad event types** and **33 subtypes**. Students are expected to memorize these for KTU exams:
>
> 1. **Life** (Be-Born, Marry, Divorce, Die, Injure)
> 2. **Movement** (Transport)
> 3. **Transaction** (Transfer-Ownership, Transfer-Money)
> 4. **Business** (Start-Org, Merge-Org, Declare-Bankruptcy, End-Org)
> 5. **Conflict** (Attack, Demonstrate)
> 6. **Contact** (Meet, Phone-Write)
> 7. **Personnel** (Start-Position, End-Position, Nominate, Elect)
> 8. **Justice** (Arrest-Jail, Release-Parole, Trial-Hearing, Charge-Indict, Sentence, Fine, Convict, Acquit, Appeal, Execute)

### Trigger vs Argument — The Heart of Event Extraction

| Component | Definition | Example in *"The bomb detonated in the market at noon."* |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | The word/phrase that most clearly expresses the event occurrence | `detonated` |
| **Event Type** | The class label assigned to the trigger | `Conflict.Attack` |
| **Argument** | An entity mention participating in the event | `The bomb`, `the market`, `noon` |
| **Argument Role** | The semantic function of the argument | `Instrument`, `Place`, `Time` |
